**Thematic framing:** *ice-storm squalls on hail-driven cliffs*. Depth 7-8 Frozen Shore adds weather to the previously-static cold. The zone was silent and still at depths 1-6; at depths 7-8 it gains violence while keeping its cold emotional register.

**Thunder Peaks (Storm + Flame).**

Depth 1-6 palette anchored in grey cliff faces, lightning-struck skies, continuous vertical elevation (per §1.2a). Depth 7-8 layers magmatic orange into the palette — volcanic vents between the rocks, red glow visible through cracks in cliff faces, lightning strikes occasionally revealing lava-glow underneath the stone.

**Ambient audio layering:** depth 1-6 soundscape (rumbling mid-bass, light static crackle, thunder cracks) gains at depth 7-8 a thin magmatic layer (deep sub-bass rumbles from vents, occasional hiss of superheated steam, faint crackle that could be either lightning or lava). The two elements blend in the sound — a player can't always tell which rumble is thunder and which is magma. This ambiguity is the signature sonic detail.

**Signature depth 7-8 detail:** the magmatic-lightning intersections. Moments where a lightning strike hits near a volcanic vent and produces a brief combined flash — bright white lightning against the underlying orange lava glow, creating a momentary color combination unique to Thunder Peaks depth 7-8.

**Thematic framing:** *magmatic storm cells, lightning over volcanic vents*. Depth 7-8 Thunder Peaks reveals that the zone's geology is actively volcanic beneath the storm-scoured surface. The two forces (lightning from above, magma from below) are the zone's double identity at depth.

**Deep Hollows (Stone + Venom).**

Depth 1-6 palette anchored in near-dark with small visibility radius, claustrophobic rock tunnels, sub-bass drones (per §1.2a). Depth 7-8 layers sickly green into the palette — toxic mineral seeps visible as stained patches on walls, bioluminescent fungal blooms in the darker corners, phosphorescent trails where contaminated water has dripped.

**Ambient audio layering:** depth 1-6 soundscape (sub-bass drone, dripping water, echo-amplified footsteps) gains at depth 7-8 a thin bio-toxic layer (faint squelching, occasional spore-release pops, subtle hissing from mineral fissures). The toxic layer is oddly organic — the zone gains a sense of being alive in a way it wasn't at depths 1-6, and that aliveness is unsettling.

**Signature depth 7-8 detail:** the luminous toxin pools. Shallow basins of contaminated water glowing faint green, providing small lighting islands in the otherwise-dark zone. A player without a torch can navigate between these pools; they become landmarks as much as visibility aids.

**Thematic framing:** *toxic mineral seeps, contaminated rock formations*. Depth 7-8 Deep Hollows reveals that the deep stone is not inert — it's decomposing, leaching, and the decomposition is biological. The zone's claustrophobia gains a new dimension of contamination.

**Sky Reaches (Wind + Frost).**

Depth 1-6 palette anchored in pale blue-white emptiness, sparse ground, altitude silence with 2-3 second silence gaps (per §1.2a). Depth 7-8 layers ice crystallization into the palette — frozen winds visible as glittering particles at high altitude, ice-rime on exposed rock, crystalline ground textures that weren't present at lower depths.

**Ambient audio layering:** depth 1-6 soundscape (airy high-frequency wash, band-pass wind whistle, periodic silence gaps) gains at depth 7-8 a thin ice-crystal layer (faint crystalline ringing, occasional ice-rime fracture sounds, the sonic texture of frost forming on metal in an open wind). The silence gaps remain — they're the zone's signature — but the surrounding ambient gains cold-weather texture.

**Signature depth 7-8 detail:** the rime-formations. Crystalline ice structures growing on exposed rock faces, catching the pale zone light and casting faint rainbow refractions. They accumulate over time — the longer a player stays in depth 7-8, the more rime-detail becomes visible.

**Thematic framing:** *glacial wind channels, freezing altitude jets*. Depth 7-8 Sky Reaches reveals that the loss-at-altitude the zone represents (per §33.1 Wind = Loss) has a temperature — the thin air gets cold, and the cold takes form in ice that accumulates slowly on the zone's few solid surfaces.

**Tidal Caves (Water + Venom).**

Depth 1-6 palette anchored in slow rhythmic tide pulses, bubbling underwater texture, patience-emotional register (per §1.2a). Depth 7-8 layers poisoned water — deeper pools gain a dark-green tint, bioluminescent algae bloom in the stagnant corners, toxic currents visible as faint trails moving through the water.

**Ambient audio layering:** depth 1-6 soundscape (slow rhythmic wash, bubbling, tide surges on 12s cycle) gains at depth 7-8 a thin bio-contamination layer (slow squelches, occasional gurgles from poisoned pools, a faint pressure-wave hum that suggests something submerged). The tide rhythm remains — it's the zone's signature — but the water itself is visibly compromised at depths 7-8.

**Signature depth 7-8 detail:** the toxic plumes. Slow-moving clouds of dark-green water drifting through otherwise-clear pools, visibly separable from clean water, carrying visual weight. Players avoid swimming through plumes even though the mechanical effect is just being-in-Venom territory.

**Thematic framing:** *brackish toxic pools, poisoned underwater currents*. Depth 7-8 Tidal Caves reveals that the zone's patience-teaching (wait for tide, time your movement) has another layer — what you're waiting in has been contaminated. The rhythm is the same; the water is worse.

**Verdant Wilds (Flora + Water).**

Depth 1-6 palette anchored in overwhelming green, bioluminescent fungi, vine walls that grow and block paths (per §1.2a). Depth 7-8 layers flooded undergrowth into the palette — shallow standing water underfoot visible as reflective surfaces, water-draped vegetation with drooping wet leaves, marsh vegetation species that thrive on waterlogged ground.

**Ambient audio layering:** depth 1-6 soundscape (dense mid-frequency hum, insect chirps, slow wood-creaking) gains at depth 7-8 a thin marsh-water layer (splash-sounds from player movement through shallow water, frog-croaks at irregular intervals, the subtle bubble of water through saturated plants). The overwhelming-abundance register remains — it's the zone's signature — but gains a water-logged heaviness.

**Signature depth 7-8 detail:** the reflection surfaces. Standing water pools that catch light and vegetation reflections, creating doubled-imagery effects that add visual complexity to an already-dense zone. A player crossing a reflection surface briefly sees their own sprite mirrored below their feet.

**Thematic framing:** *wetland swamp thickening, flooded undergrowth*. Depth 7-8 Verdant Wilds reveals that the zone's suffocating abundance (§1.2a) is not just upward-growing vegetation — it's also ground-saturating water. The zone gains a different axis of overwhelm: not just surrounded by plants, but wading through what those plants have consumed.

**Shadow Sanctum and Radiant Heights — no hybrid.**

These two zones are deliberately excluded from the late-depth hybrid treatment. Shadow Sanctum remains pure Dark at all depths; Radiant Heights remains pure Light. The exclusion reasoning is thematic: both zones are alignment-endgame zones (Dark is the Sovereign's destination, Light is the Hero's), and their emotional weight depends on clarity of element. A hybrid-treated Shadow Sanctum would feel like a combination-zone; the zone's identity requires that it be *only* Dark. Same for Radiant Heights. See GDD §14.4's exclusion note.

**Cross-zone creative thesis.** The 8 hybrid zones share one design discipline: *the secondary element makes the primary element more itself, not less.* Ember Fields depth 7-8 isn't "Flame diluted with Stone" — it's "Flame revealing its geological bedrock, giving the lava a container." Frozen Shore depth 7-8 isn't "Frost diluted with Storm" — it's "Frost gaining weather, adding violence to its indifference." Each hybrid combination is framed as the zone deepening into its own identity rather than losing it. The secondary element is a natural extension of what the primary element is doing, not a contrast to it.

**This produces pedagogical coherence.** A player who finishes their Ember Fields depth 7-8 exploration has experienced Flame+Stone collisions in a zone that thematically justifies those collisions. The Codex's Flame+Stone entries (Magma, Forge) feel natively Ember Fields when discovered there, rather than feeling like "Stone collisions the player had to import from Deep Hollows." The zone's pedagogical promise (teach its signature collision curriculum) is now fulfilled by the zone itself, through late-depth natural encounters.

### 1.3 The Grand Slam
Critical kills trigger scaled cinematic micro-events:
- Fodder: small snap
- Brute/Sentinel: dramatic kill slowmo (150-300ms)
- Boss: full treatment with screen shake + particle burst
- **Lunge kills:** Grand Slam can trigger on lunge arrival damage. When a lunge kills a Fodder enemy, the Grand Slam fires mid-dash — the kill happens during the approach, creating a "didn't even slow down" moment. A lunging Greatsword can Grand Slam through a Swarm cluster in one dash, with small snaps per kill. Brute/Sentinel lunge kills are not possible at appropriate levels (LUNGE_DAMAGE_MULT prevents one-shotting heavy enemies).

### 1.4 UI Philosophy
Glassmorphism panels with backdrop blur. Minimal chrome — game canvas is primary. HUD elements fade when not relevant. Action bar at bottom with emoji buttons. Building panels as overlay cards.

#### §1.4.1 Resource Bar Behaviors (v12.26)

The three resource bars (HP, Stamina, Mana) have distinct idle and stressed animations so peripheral vision can read their state without direct attention. **The bars behave like three different organs with three different emotional registers.**

**HP — impact response.** HP shakes on damage taken. Shake is horizontal, brief (~0.2s), with magnitude scaling linearly with damage taken as a fraction of max HP. A light hit produces a subtle ~2-pixel wobble; a near-death hit produces a noticeable ~8-pixel jarring shake. On critical damage (>25% HP in a single hit), the shake is accompanied by a brief red edge glow on the bar that fades over ~0.5s. **The HP bar should feel like a body flinching.**

**Stamina — breath rhythm.** Stamina pulses in a breathing rhythm that matches the character's movement state. Standing still: bar is visually still, no pulse. Walking: slow, steady pulse at ~0.8Hz (one breath every ~1.3s), amplitude ~1 pixel. Running: faster pulse at ~1.8Hz (rapid breathing), amplitude ~2 pixels. When stamina is drained below 25%, the pulse becomes irregular — syncopated, labored, as if gasping. **The Stamina bar should feel like lungs.**

**Mana — wellspring.** Mana ripples outward during collision mana restore events. The ripple is a single radial pulse that travels from the bar's fill line outward, lasting ~0.4s, with the ripple's color matching the element that triggered the collision (e.g., frost-blue ripple for a Frost collision). When mana is at or near full capacity, a subtle standing ripple oscillates at ~0.5Hz — gentle, continuous, like a calm pond. The standing ripple fades when mana drops below ~90%. **The Mana bar should feel like a spring — still at rest, pulsing with each draw.**

**Intensity dampening.** When 2+ bars are actively animating simultaneously (e.g., taking damage while running), each animation runs at 60% intensity to prevent visual overwhelm. The dampening is symmetric — no single bar "wins"; all simultaneously-active animations share the reduced intensity. This prevents the "HUD shaking at me" feeling during chaotic moments while preserving the individual bar identities.

**Audio accents.** Each bar animation has a matching audio cue at low volume:
- HP: brief soft thump on damage-taken shake (pitched lower for heavier damage)
- Stamina: subtle exertion breath sound on run-pulse transitions (not on every pulse — only when transitioning between walk and run states)
- Mana: gentle chime on collision ripple, element-inflected (Frost chime is higher-pitched glass-like; Flame chime is warmer; each element has its own harmonic)

Audio respects the same simultaneous-activity dampening rule — when multiple bars are animating, audio layers are mixed down proportionally.

**Creative thesis.** A UI that looks identical whether the player is thriving or dying is chrome; a UI that behaves differently in different body-states is presence. The three bars become three emotional registers the player reads without attention — *I am fine* (stamina calm, mana steady, HP still) versus *I am struggling* (stamina gasping, mana drained, HP shaking). The peripheral-vision legibility is the design's point: players should rarely need to *look at* their bars; the bars should tell them everything through motion.

#### §1.4.2 Damage Number Resistance Tier Presentation (v12.26)

Damage numbers carry resistance-tier information through shape and color, not through explicit labels.

**Neutral (standard damage).** Damage number rendered at standard size, standard color (white with slight drop shadow). Impact sound is the standard hit sound. This is the baseline — no special treatment.

**Resistant (reduced damage).** Damage number rendered ~15% smaller than standard, with a subtle grey tint (~70% desaturation from neutral). The size reduction is visible in peripheral vision even without reading the number. Impact sound is a muffled version of the standard hit — low-pass filtered at ~800Hz, slightly quieter. The audio register suggests *hit, but the impact was soaked*.

**Immune (heavily reduced damage).** Damage number rendered with a diagonal strikethrough shape (a single line crossing the number from upper-left to lower-right at ~30° angle), same size as neutral. The strikethrough is the primary identifier — readable without color. A grey tint accompanies the strikethrough (similar to Resistant) but the shape is the load-bearing signal. Impact sound is distinctly flat — a dull thunk at low frequency (~200Hz fundamental), shorter than a normal hit, with no overtones. The audio register suggests *the hit found nothing to catch on*.

**Symmetric application.** The resistance-tier presentation applies to **both** outgoing damage (the player's own hits on enemies with resistances) **and** incoming damage (enemy hits on the player, where the player's armor attunement or other resistances are in play). A player learns enemy resistance patterns through their own damage taken, not just through their own damage dealt. This symmetry turns every combat encounter into a passive tutorial on elemental relationships.

**Accessibility.** Shape-based (strikethrough, size reduction) + audio-based differentiation gives resistance tier information through two channels independent of color vision. A colorblind player can identify Immune hits by the strikethrough alone; an audio-first player can identify Resistant hits by the muffled impact sound alone.

**Creative thesis.** Resistance tier is information the player should *feel* rather than read. A resistant hit should register as "that didn't quite land" in peripheral feel before the player parses the number; an immune hit should register as "that bounced" through sound alone. Converting resistance information from UI chrome to sensory feedback makes elemental strategy a felt system rather than a lookup table.

#### §1.4.3 Mana Entropy Overlay and Grace Tick (v12.39)

The v12.39 mana entropy visibility layer (GDD §3.5a) surfaces the previously-invisible entropy mechanic on the mana bar itself — a red overlay showing accumulated entropy, and a green tick indicating an active grace window. The creative direction governs how both surfaces feel: *resource dynamics as ambient information*, read peripherally alongside normal HUD scanning rather than requiring deliberate attention.

**The red entropy overlay.** Rendered as a semi-transparent red layer (~30% alpha, warm red — `#C0392B` matching the Flame palette entry per §10.1) on the *right portion* of the mana bar. The overlay grows from invisible (at entropy = 1.0, no recent collisions) to a layer covering ~30% of the bar's width (at entropy = 0.30 floor). The overlay's growth is smooth — each recent collision adds a small increment to the overlay's width, and as collisions drop out of the 15-second entropy tracking window, the overlay shrinks back down. Players watching their mana bar during sustained combat see the overlay *breathe* with their collision cadence.

**Rendering position — right-aligned, not full-bar tint.** The overlay is deliberately rendered on the right side of the mana bar (where the "effective maximum mana restore" would be) rather than tinting the full bar or covering the left-aligned filled portion. This visual choice reads as *"your mana's effective cap for the next collision's restore is reduced"* rather than *"your mana is contaminated"* — the former is an accurate description of the mechanic (per-collision restore is scaled down), the latter would imply a different mechanic (mana pool degradation). The right-side overlay preserves the left-side mana fill as the primary indicator and layers the entropy cost as a secondary ceiling.

**No numeric readout.** The overlay does not display a percentage or numeric entropy value. The mechanic's feel is conveyed through the overlay's *size* rather than through a readable number. A new player who spams collisions sees the red grow; a player who paces collisions sees the red stay absent. Neither player needs to know the entropy formula (collision count × 0.20) to understand the pattern. The visibility makes the system legible without requiring arithmetic.

**The green grace tick.** During an active grace window (2 seconds after any collision consumes its setup status), a soft green icon appears above the mana bar with a countdown-arc animation. The icon is a small pulse-shape (~14px diameter at standard HUD scale) in a warm mint green (`#2ECC71` matching the Flora palette entry). The arc around the icon starts at full circle at window open and depletes clockwise to empty at 2.0 seconds elapsed.

**Color coupling — green as "ready" not as "safe."** Green is deliberately chosen to read as *ready/available* rather than *safe/mild*. In most games green signifies low-threat; in Bro Town's grace-tick context green signifies *an available opportunity*. The same player who saw green as "healthy HP" now sees green as "next collision gets full restore." The color-mapping shifts contextually because the shape (pulse ring with countdown arc) and position (above mana bar) make it clear the green relates to the mana system, not to HP.

**Countdown-arc rendering.** The arc begins rendering at the 12 o'clock position of the icon and depletes clockwise. At 0.5 seconds elapsed, ~75% of the arc remains; at 1.0 seconds, ~50%; at 1.5 seconds, ~25%; at 2.0 seconds, the icon fades out over ~200ms. The fade-out is *gentle* — no popping or sudden disappearance. A player watching the arc can time their next collision to land before the arc completes, experiencing the 2-second window as a *breath rhythm* rather than as a strict timer.

**Tick reset on grace collision.** When the player triggers another collision within the grace window, the arc resets to full and a subtle pulse animation (brief +10% scale bounce over 100ms) confirms the reset. The reset-pulse is peripheral — it catches attention without demanding it. A rhythm-paced player chaining grace collisions sees a steady sequence of pulse-resets that communicates "you're in flow" through the icon's behavior rather than through any announcement.

**No audio on grace-tick appearance or reset.** The grace tick is a visual signal only. Adding audio to every collision's grace-tick appearance would accumulate into an auditory clutter during sustained combat. The visual channel is sufficient; the audio channel is reserved for higher-priority signals (collision impacts, resonance pulse, combat cues). This discipline preserves the audio mix for events that genuinely need audio attention.

**Interaction with §2.4 haptic layer (grace-collision tick).** The v12.38 Haptic Feedback Layer (§2.4) added 12 canonical haptic events. v12.39 adds one supplementary haptic: a subtle additional micro-tick (5ms, light intensity) that fires alongside the existing collision-triggered haptic when the collision is a grace collision. The double-event rhythm (existing collision pulse + subtle micro-tick) reads in the fingers as *"that one got the bonus"* without adding a distinct new haptic pattern. Players with haptics disabled don't miss the information — the visual green-tick already communicates the grace state. Hearing-impaired players particularly benefit from the haptic supplement during extended sessions where visual HUD scanning fatigue sets in.

**Accessibility register.**

- **Toggleable.** The red entropy overlay and green grace tick can be hidden via Settings → Accessibility → Combat Hints → Mana Entropy Display. Default on. Players who find the overlay visually noisy (or who prefer minimal HUD) can disable it without losing any mechanical effect — the mechanic continues to apply to their mana restoration; only the visual surfacing is toggled. Disabled state reverts the mana bar to its pre-v12.39 appearance.
- **Shape-first supplement.** For players on the colorblind-safe palette (§58), the red overlay uses the Flame shape glyph (§10.1 diamond) as a secondary texture within the overlay, visible as a subtle diamond-pattern edge. The green tick uses the Flora leaf glyph as its icon shape rather than a generic pulse-ring, preserving shape-based identification without color.
- **Reduced-motion respect.** Settings → Accessibility → Motion → Reduced Motion disables the grace-tick's countdown-arc animation; the icon simply appears at window open and disappears at window close. The arc's motion can be replaced with a static fill-level indicator (half-full at 1 second elapsed, empty at 2 seconds) for players sensitive to moving UI elements.

**Creative thesis — resource dynamics as flow-state signature.** The mana bar's *behavior* becomes the flow-state surface. A rhythm-paced player watches the green tick appear and reset smoothly, the red overlay stay absent, and the mana fill stay consistently high — a visual rhythm that reads as *"you're in flow"* without any explicit flow-state indicator. A spam-paced player watches the red overlay accumulate, the green tick rarely appear, and the mana fill drain faster than it refills — a visual pattern that reads as *"you're draining yourself"* without any punishment language. The mechanic self-teaches: rhythm-paced play looks different than spam-paced play at the mana bar level, and players learn which pattern they want to cultivate through the visibility alone.

Together with the v12.38 Resonance Streak badge (§5.9 UI area, small numeric counter next to combo count), the mana-region HUD now articulates two flow-state axes simultaneously: *rhythm* (via entropy overlay / grace tick dynamics) and *resonance precision* (via streak badge). A fully-engaged player reading their own HUD peripherally experiences the full flow-state textural display through resource dynamics alone — the kind of lived-in HUD awareness that ARPG veterans develop naturally, now visible to all players through deliberate visual design rather than through hidden-mechanic inference.

### 1.5 Desktop UX Direction

Desktop detected via `(pointer:fine)` media query. The desktop experience strips all touch UI and replaces it with keyboard+mouse paradigms.

**Visual changes:** Crosshair cursor on canvas. Touch joysticks hidden. Stat HUD (horizontal HP/Mana/Stamina bars) appears at bottom-left with `Space Mono` monospace values. Semi-transparent keybind hints fade in below the HUD. Building buttons show `[E]` key badges.

**Aim reticle:** Subtle crosshair drawn at mouse world position on the game canvas — lightweight, non-intrusive. Only renders in desktop mode.

**Feel target:** Desktop should feel like a native PC ARPG — direct, responsive, no trace of mobile UI. Mouse aim should feel as natural as the right joystick. WASD movement should have the same analog feel via key combinations (W+D = northeast).

#### §1.5.1 Power-User Controls — Flow, Not Menu (v12.51)

The v12.51 §34.1 addition formalizes desktop power-user controls (weapon stash keybinds 1-8, Tab target cycling, hover-tooltips) as first-class desktop features rather than plugin-like conveniences. The creative direction governs how these controls should *feel* at the keypress level — specifically, **how each affordance extends the player's combat flow rather than interrupting it.**

**The load-bearing creative choice — keybinds as continuation of combat, not pause-and-swap.** A weapon-swap via menu breaks combat flow — the player's attention shifts from the fight to the menu, and even a fast menu-swap feels like a pause. Keybinds 1-8 must feel like *continuation*: the player decides to switch weapons, presses the key, the swap happens, combat continues. No menu flash, no inventory overlay, no "Swapped to Bow" toast notification, no cooldown animation. The visual feedback is the swap itself — the weapon changes in the character's hand, and that is the entire acknowledgment. Audio feedback is minimal: a single soft swap-click, identical per slot, calibrated to be present but not distracting from combat audio.

**Tab target cycling — camera-aware, predictable.** The `Tab` cycle feels right when the targeting order matches camera framing expectations — closest enemy first, then next-closest by angle, repeating. Players should not need to think about *which* enemy Tab will pick; they should press Tab and know which one it will be. When Tab picks an enemy, the lock-on indicator appears on that enemy immediately, no transition animation, no interstitial "cycling" state. `Shift+Tab` reverses direction with the same immediacy. The cycling feel is surgical, not circular — each Tab is a direct target selection, not a rotation through a list.

**Hover-tooltips — information on patience.** The HOVER_TOOLTIP_DELAY(0.6s) is calibrated to the point where a player has *decided* to inspect an item, not where they are still scanning their inventory. A tooltip that appears instantly on hover feels aggressive; a tooltip that never appears feels broken. 0.6s captures the moment between arrival and commitment. Tooltip visual treatment: floating panel with dark translucent background (matching the inspect-card panel style), item stats in a compact two-column layout, crafter attribution in subdued text at the bottom. No animation on appear beyond a 0.15s fade-in; no animation on disappear beyond a 0.15s fade-out. Respects the player's focus — the tooltip is information, not a UI event.

#### §1.5.2 Screenshot Mode — Framing as Creative Intention (v12.51)

Screenshot Mode's four framing options (centered, left-third, top-third, free-roam) are presented as **composition choices**, not optimization presets. Each framing is appropriate to different gameplay moments, and the player chooses based on what they are trying to capture.

**The load-bearing creative choice — framing options as authored positions, not as filters.** Many games' screenshot modes offer "presets" that blend toward generic-social-media-friendly output. v12.51 rejects this framing in favor of *composition*: each position is a specific creative choice with a specific visual logic. Centered is portraiture — the character is the subject, surrounding environment is context. Left-third is action-implied — the character is arriving from off-frame or departing toward the rightward expanse. Top-third is landscape-emphasis — the environment dominates, the character is a figure in it. Free-roam is player-as-photographer — the player composes the shot deliberately within the 40-unit bound. Each positioning is appropriate to different moments, and the player's choice reflects what they want the capture to say.

**Filter register — restrained intensification.** Four filter options (none / vintage / high-contrast / dreamy) are designed as **register shifts**, not dramatic transformations. The default "none" is the baseline; each filter shifts the image into a specific aesthetic register without obscuring gameplay content. Vintage desaturates and warms — appropriate for reflective moments, quiet zones, lore-adjacent captures. High-contrast pushes darks and lights — appropriate for dramatic moments, boss reveals, high-stakes combat frames. Dreamy softens foreground and bumps saturation — appropriate for peaceful moments, farm shots, aesthetic compositions. Filters should never distort the rendered image beyond recognition — a player should see a Greatsword in the final screenshot that clearly reads as a Greatsword regardless of filter.

**Mobile parity through tap-and-hold — respecting platform conventions.** Mobile Screenshot Mode deliberately limits to "tap-and-hold UI hide + use OS native screenshot." This respects the mobile platform's UX conventions: phone screenshots are OS-level actions, not app-level actions, and players expect to use the volume+power button combo or the native OS gesture for screen capture. Adding an in-app screenshot pipeline on mobile would fight the OS's existing capability. The creative direction: mobile-provides-the-capture-surface, the-game-provides-the-clean-frame.

#### §1.5.3 Replay Library — Sharing the Moment, Not the Match (v12.51)

The v12.51 §34.3 Replay Clip Library is the larger of v12.51's two features, and its creative direction is substantively different from Screenshot Mode's — replay clips are *motion*, and motion communicates things that stills cannot. The direction governs how clips feel, what they capture, and how they appear in the player's library and in shared contexts.

**The load-bearing creative choice — clips are moments, not footage.** Most in-game recording systems produce "footage" (undifferentiated video that requires the player to curate). The Replay Library produces "moments" — auto-captured, auto-trimmed, event-specific. A Godly reveal clip is the reveal and its lead-up; an Apex kill clip is the killing blow and its consequence; an Arena win clip is the match-deciding sequence. Each clip is *about something specific*, and the player knows what it is without having to watch through irrelevant gameplay to find it. This is the discipline: every clip has a subject, and the subject is the reason the clip exists.

**Clip trim windows — authored per-moment.** The trim windows specified in §34.3 (Godly reveal -5/+10, Apex kill -10/+5, Arena win -15/+5, Hardness 5 -3/+9, collision -5/+5, Grand Slam -7/+3) are not generic windows — each is calibrated to what the specific moment needs. The Godly reveal needs lead-up to establish the drop happening, plus the full reveal animation, plus post-reveal reaction. The Apex kill needs the fight context before the killing blow. The Hardness 5 success needs minimal lead-up but a full "yes, it worked" reaction. These windows are authored creative decisions, not uniform rules. When new triggers are added to `replay-triggers.json`, their trim windows should be authored with the same per-moment specificity.

**Watermark discipline — unobtrusive but present.** Clip watermarks are a deliberate compromise: the game appears in the player's shared content (organic marketing), but discreetly enough that the clip feels like the player's own. Specific discipline:

- Watermark is thin-weight text, low-opacity (~60% white with subtle shadow), 14pt font
- Position is fixed bottom-right corner, ~16px margin from edges
- Watermark text: `"{GAME_NAME} · {PLAYER_NAME} · Day {PLAYER_DAY_COUNT}"` — game first for brand identification, player name second for attribution, day count third for context
- Watermark is NOT logos, emblems, or graphic elements — text-only. This keeps the visual weight minimal.
- Watermark is NOT removable through any in-game path. A player cannot share an unbranded version.

The "not removable" discipline is critical. If players could strip the watermark, most would, and the organic-marketing value would evaporate. If the watermark is too prominent, players would stop sharing clips because the share feels like advertising the game rather than their gameplay. The balance: present-but-quiet — the game's presence is visible in shared content, and that presence is the point.

**What players want to share.** The clip trigger list (Godly reveal, Apex kill, Arena win, Hardness 5, first collision, Grand Slam) reflects observed social-media behavior in adjacent ARPGs: players overwhelmingly share *peak moments*, not general gameplay. Within that, they prefer *motion* (animations, impacts, reveals) over *state* (inventory screens, stats). The auto-capture trigger list is calibrated to these observed preferences. Future trigger expansions should ask: is this a peak moment? Does it carry motion? Does the clip tell a coherent small story? If not, the trigger probably isn't worth the storage cost.

**Library UI — personal archive, not social dashboard.** The Replay Library appears under the player's inspect panel Clips tab, visible only to the player themselves. Clips are not broadcast to friends, not ranked, not upvoted, not commented on within the game. This matches the identity-acknowledgment discipline (§8.51 / §8.54 / §8.56 / §8.57) — the game notices the player's moments, the player notices them, social visibility is optional and player-initiated through the share flow. The library is an archive of personal highlights, not a social performance surface.

**What this rejects.**

- No clip-rating system (no thumbs-up, no "player voted best clip of the week"). Clips are personal records, not ranked content.
- No automated highlight reels or "top moments" compilations — players curate their own sharing.
- No branded overlay kits (sponsor logos, ad placements, affiliate watermarks). Watermark is game-presence only.
- No real-time clip notifications to friends ("Bro Alice just got a Godly clip!"). Sharing is player-initiated, not auto-broadcast.
- No "private-only" clips below the level of the library itself — all clips in the library are private by default; sharing is an explicit action that makes a clip public.
- No "premium clip quality" upsell. Clip resolution and framerate are uniform across free-tier and Supporter players per v12.51 §34.3 discipline.

**Twenty-sixth consecutive CR ship (v5.9-v5.34).** v5.34's specific contribution: articulating **keybinds as continuation of combat flow** for desktop power-user controls, **framing options as authored composition choices** for Screenshot Mode, and **clips as moments not footage** for the Replay Library. The thread through v5.15 / v5.25 / v5.27 / v5.28 / v5.29 / v5.30 / v5.31 / v5.32 / v5.33 continues. v5.34 is distinctive within the sequence as the first explicit focus on **desktop platform identity + social-sharing infrastructure** — prior ships have focused on in-game systems and their creative registers; v5.34 opens the question of how the game's presence extends *outward* into the player's own social-media sharing. The watermark-as-unobtrusive-presence discipline is the creative infrastructure for this outward extension, and it is specified with the same care as the in-game registers.

### 1.6 Cosmetic Masks — Visual Identity

15 canvas-drawn masks that overlay the character head composite. Each mask has a unique `colors` object and is drawn with fillRect/arc calls — no sprites. Masks are expressive, silly, and social. They're the first thing another player notices.

**Design language:** Masks range from playful (Clown, Cat, Pumpkin) to edgy (Skull, Demon, Ninja) to memetic (Cool Shades, Anonymous, Rage Face). The Royal Crown at 2,000g and Level 50 is the aspirational flex item.

**Canvas rendering:** Mask draw functions receive the character's head position and scale. Each mask defines feature-specific geometry — horns for Troll/Demon use triangles, Robot Visor uses a horizontal rect with glow, Ghost Sheet is a rounded rect that obscures the entire head.

### 1.7 Stat-Based Visuals — Build Identity at a Glance

Characters visually evolve based on the player's trained stat distribution. Six archetypes (Berserker, Tank, Mage, Rogue, Healer, Leader) each produce distinct visual signatures: body scale changes, ambient glow colors, floating particles, and frame adjustments. Because stats are trained through use (GDD §1), the archetype a character drifts toward is a *visual readout of how the player has actually played* — not a label they declared. The Berserker visual emerges from hundreds of hours of aggressive melee play; the Mage visual emerges from a Mind-trained, collision-focused playstyle.

**Creative direction:** Visual changes should be subtle at low investment and unmistakable at high investment. A Berserker at 150+ score should have noticeably bulkier arms and a red ambient glow. A Mage at max intensity should have 3 floating arcane particles orbiting the character. The changes reinforce social identity — you should be able to guess someone's build from across the room.

**Intensity curve:** `min(1, dominantScore / 150)`. Below 15 total score: no visual changes. This prevents low-level characters from showing confusing partial visuals.

#### 1.7 Training Moment Feedback — Visual Identity (v12.48)

The v12.48 §1.6.1 addition extends the stat legibility infrastructure with continuous-fill bars (moment-to-moment sub-integer training visibility) and threshold celebrations (first-productive-use character glow + floating label). The creative direction governs how this feedback feels across both layers — specifically, **how training milestones are acknowledged without interrupting the moment they belong to.**

**The load-bearing creative choice — acknowledgment, not reward.** A threshold crossing in a use-trained stat system is not a *reward* in the loot-box sense. The player already earned the progression through play; the threshold is the moment their accumulated work becomes mechanically visible. The celebration is acknowledgment of what already happened, not a prize arriving from outside. The visual register must match this — **no popup, no modal, no screen interruption, no game-state pause**. The player fights through the moment; the game quietly marks it.

**Character-glow register — presence, not spectacle.** The 0.5-second glow treats the character as the subject of the acknowledgment. The stat's accent color surrounds the character briefly — a red halo for Ferocity, a blue halo for Mind, a gold halo for Power, matching the §1.7 archetype accent palette exactly. The glow pulses once softly rather than flashing or strobing. Other nearby players see the glow with the same intensity the celebrating player sees — no "your screen shows more" privacy asymmetry. Witnessing a friend's Ferocity 150 moment is the same visual as having one's own.

**Floating label — quiet typography.** The "FEROCITY 150" label appears ~1 unit above the character's head, in the stat's accent color, at a size matching floating damage numbers but with a slight weight bump for readability. Label appears over 0.15s (not instant — flash-in would read as damage), holds for 0.35s, fades over 0.15s. Total duration ≈0.65s. Label motion is a gentle upward drift (~0.3 units over the display window) — neither static nor dramatically rising. The label reads once, then is gone; it does not linger or persist in the combat log.

**The first-productive-use trigger discipline.** Celebration fires when the new tier is *used*, not when it is *crossed*. A player whose Ferocity ticks to 150 mid-combo sees no effect on that tick; their next crit produces the glow and label. A player whose Fortification ticks to 100 during idle sees nothing until their next successful block. This ties the celebration to a felt moment — the player experiences the threshold through their own action, not through a background tick they didn't notice. **The trigger must be invisible-until-used**: no popup warning that a threshold is imminent, no waiting-period notification, no "your next crit will celebrate" tooltip. The moment arrives when it arrives.

**Stat-name pedagogy — teach, then trust.** For a character's first 10 celebrations per stat, the label shows the stat name alongside the value ("FEROCITY 150"). After the 10th trigger for a given stat, the label drops to value-only ("150"). This teaches new players what each accent color represents through explicit naming; veterans who have internalized the color-to-stat mapping get a cleaner visual signature at the celebration moment. The transition is silent — no "you have mastered this stat, labels now simplified" acknowledgment. The game trusts the player to have learned.

**Continuous-fill bars — animation restraint.** Inspect-card fill bars animate smoothly between states over TRAINING_FILL_BAR_SMOOTHING(0.3s) windows. The bar fill is not literal XP accumulation — it is a smoothed render of the underlying continuous counter. Animation uses ease-out curves (fast start, gentle settle) so the bar feels responsive without feeling jittery. When combat generates multiple rapid XP events, the bar absorbs them into a continuous fill motion rather than discrete jumps. When the bar completes and ticks over to the next integer, the transition is a soft snap — brief pause at full, then reset to empty for the next integer. No celebration fires from the bar-completion moment itself; the threshold celebration is its own separate event driven by the productive-use trigger.

**What this rejects.**

- No popup or modal acknowledgment — crossing a threshold is not a game-state event.
- No achievement chime, no level-up jingle, no audio sting — celebration is visual-only.
- No broadcast beyond local view — party members in different zones do not see notifications about a friend's threshold.
- No leaderboard or ranking tied to celebration count — this would create optimization pressure for farming threshold moments.
- No "your next action" predictive tooltip — the trigger must be experienced, not previewed.
- No persistent log entry beyond Journey Log session recap — celebrations do not accumulate into a visible history that players could treat as a completion list.

**Twenty-fourth consecutive CR ship (v5.9-v5.32).** v5.32's specific contribution is articulating **acknowledgment-not-reward** as the governing register for use-trained stat milestones, and **invisible-until-used** as the trigger discipline for milestone-moment feedback. The thread through v5.15 / v5.25 / v5.27 / v5.28 / v5.29 / v5.30 / v5.31 continues — the stat-system legibility layer gets its own explicit register statement, extending the identity-acknowledgment thread to its eleventh surface. The v12.48 contribution is distinctive because it closes the legibility coverage for the §1 stat system: moment-to-moment micro-feedback (§1.6), sub-integer fill bars (§1.6.1 continuous), threshold celebration (§1.6.1 discrete), session recap (Journey Log), lifetime archetype emergence (§1.7). All five time scales are now explicitly articulated.

#### §1.7a Weapon Quality and Hardness Visuals

Quality grade and hardness level (GDD §4.6b, §4.6c) are visible identity dimensions, not just stat modifiers. Other players see them at a glance.

**Quality borders.** Each quality grade adds a colored border to the weapon's nameplate and inventory icon. Normal has no border (the absence is the signal — most weapons are Normal). Rare uses a thin silver border. Elite uses a thicker gold border. Godly uses a prismatic shimmering border that cycles through the element spectrum slowly — the same prismatic treatment as Shift element tier, but on the border rather than the weapon body. A Godly weapon is visible from across a room. The animation is slow and dignified, not flashy — the weapon is special, not screaming for attention.

**Hardness badges.** Hardness level 1–5 displays as a small numeric badge in the corner of the weapon icon and on the inspect card. Hardness 0 is hidden (the absence is the signal — most weapons have never been hardened). Hardness 1–3 use a simple white numeral. Hardness 4 uses a gold numeral. Hardness 5 uses a prismatic shimmering numeral with a faint glow halo around the badge — visible at any zoom level. A Hardness 5 weapon is a *named object* in the game's social fabric, and its badge is the visual marker of that identity.

**The drop moment.** Quality dictates how a weapon drop is presented to the player. Normal drops appear as their resolved weapon icons immediately on the ground — no fanfare, the player walks over and picks them up. **Drops that roll Rare or above appear as question mark icons** instead of resolved weapons. The grade is hidden until the player picks up the mystery item, and the reveal animation **plays out in stages, with each stage visualizing the underlying probability distribution in real time**.

The question mark icon shows the weapon type silhouette underneath (Greatsword, Sword, Bow, or Staff) so the player knows what *kind* of weapon they're approaching, but not the quality. The icon pulses subtly, calibrated to the *minimum* grade — players see a question mark and know the floor is Rare. They do not know the ceiling. Every question mark carries the theoretical possibility of being a Godly. That possibility is what makes question marks magnetic.

**Tiered RNG reveal animation (v12.6 CANONICAL).** The reveal is not a single resolved animation per grade. It is a multi-stage RNG visualization that *honestly shows the underlying probability distribution playing out*. The icon — a weapon-type silhouette matching the actual drop — flashes through real RNG ticks, with each visible color tick representing an actual roll the server has performed. The frequency of each color reflects the *true conditional probability* of that outcome. The player isn't watching a 50/50 oscillation; they're watching the lottery happen in real time at honest odds.

**Stage 1 — Rare check (~9 seconds total):** ~8 seconds of fast spin at ~12 ticks per second (~96 visible roll ticks) where the icon flashes between **silver** (Rare floor) and **gold** (escalation to Elite stage). The conditional probability of escalating past Rare is ~9%, so the player sees gold flash several times during the spin — each gold tick is a small "wait, did it just" moment, but the animation keeps rolling and most ticks are silver. The final ~1 second is the deceleration: the last 3 ticks happen slowly and visibly, each one clearly silver or gold. **If the final tick is silver,** the reveal ends. The weapon resolves as Rare. **If the final tick is gold,** the animation transitions immediately into Stage 2 — the gold tick is the bridge.

**Stage 2 — Elite/Godly check (~9 seconds total):** Same structure as Stage 1, but the icon now cycles between **gold** (Elite floor) and **prismatic** (Godly). The conditional Godly probability within this stage is ~1 in 3,600, so the player sees a prismatic flash during the animation roughly 1 in 36 reveals. Most Stage 2 reveals show 99 gold ticks in a row with no prismatic at all. Final ~1 second is the deceleration with the last 3 ticks slow and visible. **If the final tick is gold,** reveal ends as Elite. **If the final tick is prismatic,** Stage 3 fires immediately.

**Stage 3 — Godly finale (~5 seconds):** The screen-hijacking moment. The icon explodes outward in a prismatic burst, the screen flashes prismatic for ~0.5 seconds, the dedicated motif plays (4 seconds — see §4.6c.5 below), the weapon's name appears in massive text in the center of the screen, and **the player's input is fully locked for the entire animation**. The moment is theirs to witness, not to interrupt. After the animation completes, the weapon enters their inventory bound to them forever.

**Total reveal times:** Rare ~9 seconds, Elite ~18 seconds, Godly ~23 seconds. The escalation in time matches the escalation in rarity — most reveals are quick, the rare ones take longer because the player is watching something rarer happen.

**Hybrid input model (CANONICAL).** Different reveal stages have different levels of player intrusiveness, matching the escalation in importance:

- **Rare reveals happen in a non-blocking corner of the screen.** The player can keep fighting while the animation plays out. They glance at the corner occasionally, see silver ticking, and either watch it or ignore it depending on whether they're in combat. If the deceleration trends toward gold, they pay closer attention. The Rare reveal is background dopamine — present but not interrupting. (At high levels, players see ~18 Rare reveals per hour. A 9-second hijacking reveal on every one would mean ~3 minutes per hour spent watching forced reveals; the corner placement prevents this.)
- **Elite escalations move to a more prominent screen position** — the panel grows or migrates to center-ish, demanding attention without fully blocking the screen. The player has already passed Rare; the question of whether they'll pass Elite into Godly is now interesting enough that they should be watching. Combat is still possible but the player's attention is meaningfully divided.
- **Godly escalations fully hijack the screen with input lock.** The player witnesses the moment with no possibility of interruption. The escalation in *visual prominence* matches the escalation in *the player's interest*. By the time Stage 3 fires, the player has been preparing for this moment through Stage 1 and Stage 2 deceleration, and the full hijack is the cosmological climax.

**Server-streamed reveal protocol (anti-cheat).** The reveal animation is *server-authoritative* not just at the result level but at the *per-tick* level. The server streams the actual color sequence to the client for each stage; the client renders what the server tells it. A client cannot fabricate a fake escalation to Godly because the server is feeding the visible color ticks frame by frame. See GDD §17.4 for the cryptographic provenance system that protects the *outcome* and the streaming protocol that protects the *animation*.

**The mystery moment is private.** The reveal animation plays only for the player who is owed the drop. Other players in the same room see a question mark on the ground (because the §4.11 loot rules are per-player-instanced), but they cannot see the reveal — the dramatic moment of grace is *between the player and the gift*. The server-room broadcast for Godly drops fires when the reveal completes, so the *social* moment of celebration comes after the *personal* moment of revelation. This separation is intentional. The player has a few seconds alone with their gift before the world finds out.

**Border shapes for accessibility.** Each grade has both a color and a shape: Rare uses a thin line border, Elite uses a notched border (corner cuts), Godly uses a radiant border (small rays extending outward) with a prismatic shimmer animation. The shape is the primary signal for colorblind players; the color is reinforcement. This mirrors the §10.1 element shape system where shapes are the canonical identifier and color is supplementary.

**Gem and composite visuals (v12.6).** Gems are first-class inventory items with their own icons. Each element type has a distinct gem shape (matching the §10.1 element shape system: Flame as a diamond, Frost as a hexagon, etc.) rendered in the element's canonical color, with a small numeral indicating the gem's level (1–10). Higher-level gems have a subtle inner glow that intensifies with level — Level 1 gems are matte, Level 10 gems shimmer noticeably from across the inventory grid.

**Composites are visually distinct from individual gems.** A composite icon shows both gems side by side within a unifying frame: a Flame+Frost composite displays the Flame diamond and Frost hexagon together inside a thin border that hints at the composite's volatile/stable status. **Volatile composites have a faint crackle effect at the boundary between the two gem icons** — the visual reads as "these elements are in tension, the player has chosen this." Stable composites blend smoothly. The player can identify a volatile composite at a glance without opening any tooltip.

**Shift gems are visually unique.** A Shift gem icon is prismatic — cycling through all 10 element colors slowly (matching the v12.5 Godly border treatment, but on the gem itself rather than a weapon border). When applied to a weapon, the weapon's nameplate gains a prismatic shimmer to indicate it is currently a Shift weapon. Shift gems carry their three rolled elements as small icons inside the gem's frame, so the player can see at a glance which elements they granted at drop time.

**The Enchanter UI.** The Enchanter's interface is the central interaction surface for all gem operations: enchanting a weapon, combining gems, creating composites, applying composites, decoupling composites, flipping orientation. The UI is organized as a workbench — gems and weapons sit on the bench, the player drags them into combination/application slots, and the Enchanter NPC narrates the operations briefly. The narration is functional, not flowery — *"Two Flame gems. Combining."* — and the player can skip past it once they've seen it a few times. The first enchanting operation triggers a brief tutorial moment where the Enchanter explains the concept of vessel and gem in a single line: *"A weapon holds. A gem speaks. The weapon doesn't speak until you give it something to say."* This is the only time in the game the Enchanter delivers a thematic line; all subsequent interactions are purely functional.

**Reveal animations escalate dramatically:**

- **Rare reveal:** Stage 1 only. ~9 seconds in a corner panel. Silver-mostly cycling with occasional gold flashes, deceleration, final silver tick, weapon resolves as Rare.
- **Elite reveal:** Stage 1 + Stage 2. ~18 seconds. The Stage 1 deceleration lands gold and the panel moves to a more prominent position as Stage 2 begins. Gold-mostly cycling with very rare prismatic flashes (~1 in 36 reveals shows any prismatic at all), deceleration, final gold tick, weapon resolves as Elite.
- **Godly reveal:** Stage 1 + Stage 2 + Stage 3. ~23 seconds. The Stage 2 deceleration lands prismatic, the Godly finale fires, screen hijack, motif, weapon name in massive text, full input lock until the animation completes.

**The hardening attempt.** Hardening at the Blacksmith is a deliberate ritual, not a button press. The player places the weapon on the anvil, the Blacksmith works it for several seconds (the duration scales with the hardness level being attempted — Hardness 0→1 takes 2s, Hardness 4→5 takes 8s of held suspense), and the result is revealed with a distinct sound and visual cue. **Success** plays a rising chime, the anvil flashes, the weapon glows briefly, and the new hardness badge appears. **Failure** plays a sharp metallic crack, the anvil dims, the hardness badge resets to 0 (or disappears entirely if it was at 1), and the weapon visually "settles" — no destruction, just loss. The failure animation is long enough to register as a real loss (about 1.5 seconds before the player can interact with the weapon again) but not punishingly so. The player should feel the weight of the failure without being held in it.

**The Hardness 5 reveal.** When a Hardness 4→5 attempt succeeds — the 0.1% roll — the game treats it as a server-defining moment. Every player in the same room receives a private chat notification: "A weapon has reached Hardness 5 in [zone]." (The broadcast is *zone-vague*, not coordinate-precise — see GDD §4.6c.2 for the security reasoning.) The weapon's owner sees a screen-filling animation of the weapon glowing prismatic, the anvil radiating light, and the dedicated musical motif plays (about 4 seconds). After the animation, the weapon enters the world with its Hardness 5 badge, and its owner walks out of the Blacksmith's shop carrying something that may not exist again on the server for months or years. The owner receives 30 seconds of PvP invulnerability immediately after the success — enough time to walk to safety or simply log off if they want to savor the moment privately.

**The shared motif and the differentiating notes.** Godly drop reveals and Hardness 5 successes share the same 4-second base motif. Both are "the lottery hit" and players learn to associate the cue with "something almost-impossible just happened to someone." When they hear it for someone else, they remember every other time they heard it for someone else, and the moment carries the weight of the entire history. **However**, the two events are distinguishable by a single additional note layered on top of the shared base:

- **Godly drop reveal:** Base motif + a high resolving note suggesting *grace* — a chime from above. The cosmological direction is *downward* — something descending into the player's hand.
- **Hardness 5 success reveal:** Base motif + a deep resolving note suggesting *forge* — a strike from below. The cosmological direction is *upward* — something rising from accumulated effort.

Players will learn both notes by ear within their first dozen exposures and will be able to identify the event from the audio alone, even if they don't see the visual. **The shared base motif builds the social mythology of the lottery layer; the differentiating notes preserve the asymmetry between grace and grit.** This is the most carefully composed audio in the game and it carries the weight of the entire lottery system — players will hear it many times before they hear it for themselves, and every prior hearing builds anticipation for the eventual personal one.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Quality and hardness are loot dopamine systems, but they only work as dopamine if the moment of receiving them feels special. The drop moment and the hardening reveal are where the system either lands or doesn't. Normal drops with no fanfare protect the special moments — if every drop had a chime, nothing would feel rare. The escalation from Normal (silence) to Godly (5-second screen-hijacking reveal with input lock) is exponential, matching the rarity curve. Hardness 5 sharing the audiovisual signature with Godly drops is intentional: both are "the lottery hit." The differentiating notes give the player ear-recognition for *which* lottery hit, without breaking the shared identity. The mystery reveal model added in v12.5 is the structural improvement that makes all of this work — the question mark is the anticipation, the reveal is the moment of identity. Players run to question marks because they're tiny lottery scratch-offs. Most are Rare (silver shimmer, satisfying). Some are Elite (gold ring, real upgrade). And once in a player's lifetime, the prismatic explosion fires for them.]`

#### §1.7b Zone Marks on Players (v12.26)

Extended presence in a zone visually marks the character sprite with zone-specific visual effects. Marks accumulate gradually while the player is in-zone, then fade after the player leaves. The effect is subtle, atmospheric, and never obstructs combat readability — zone marks layer *over* the archetype visuals (§1.7) without conflicting.

**Per-zone visual direction.**

- **Frozen Shore (Frost):** Snow dusting accumulates on the character's shoulders and hair. Appears ~30s after zone entry, pronounced at ~2 minutes. Visual register: soft white flecks with slight sparkle, settling onto the character like real snowfall. Clears over ~60s after zone exit (snow melts visibly during that time, with tiny water droplet particles).
- **Ember Fields (Flame):** Faint soot smudge darkens the character's face and hands. Same timing as Frozen Shore. Visual register: thin grey-black smudges, irregular placement, as if the character has been near a fire. Clears over ~45s after zone exit (soot wipes off gradually).
- **Tidal Caves (Water):** Water droplets cling to the character's edges and hair. Appears ~20s after zone entry (water clings faster than snow accumulates), pronounced at ~90s. Visual register: small highlights that catch light, occasional drip particles falling from the character. Clears faster than other marks — within ~30s of leaving (the character dries off visibly).
- **Deep Hollows (Stone):** Faint torch-glow residue warms the character's silhouette — an amber halo that persists only while the character is holding or recently held a torch. Visual register: soft amber light around the character's outline, as if recent torch-heat lingers. Clears instantly on zone exit (torch is extinguished; the glow dies).
- **Mistwood (Venom):** Subtle green mist clings to the character's feet and lower body. Appears ~40s after zone entry (mist takes time to find its host), pronounced at ~2 minutes. Visual register: soft green vapor trailing behind the character as they move. Clears over ~60s after zone exit.
- **Thunder Peaks (Storm):** Occasional static crackle particles appear around the character's hair and shoulders — small blue-white electrical arcs, firing every 10-20 seconds. Appears ~45s after zone entry. Visual register: brief flashes rather than continuous presence. Clears within ~30s of leaving.
- **Sky Reaches (Wind):** Windblown hair and cape animation intensifies. The character's hair strands and any cloth elements (cape, scarf) animate with stronger wind motion while in-zone. Effect appears gradually — wind-affected animation increases from ~20% amplitude at zone entry to ~100% at 2 minutes. Clears over ~30s after zone exit as the wind-animation amplitude returns to baseline.
- **Verdant Wilds (Flora):** Small leaf particles trail the character as they move. Appears ~30s after zone entry, pronounced at ~2 minutes (multiple leaf trails). Visual register: tiny green leaves in wake behind the character's sprite. Clears over ~60s after leaving.

**Social visibility.** Zone marks are visible to all players, not just the marked player. A player walking into town with Frozen Shore snow dusting still visible is a social cue — *"I just came from Frozen Shore"* — that other players register without UI. This creates ambient "where have you been" moments in town without any explicit tracking system. The ~30-60 second mark-persistence into town after zone exit is the key design choice: long enough that other players see it, short enough that it doesn't accumulate into cross-contamination when the player visits multiple zones.

**Guardian zone intensification.** Guardian zones (§55) use intensified versions of their base element's mark. A Flame Guardian zone's soot mark is darker and more pronounced than the standard Ember Fields version; a Frost Guardian zone's snow dusting is heavier and has a faint shimmer. Guardian-zone marks fade at the same rate as their base-element equivalents but arrive faster (within ~15s of zone entry).

**The Convergence exception.** The Convergence (zone with two elemental identities) applies both Dark and Light marks simultaneously — a faint dark aura around the lower body, a faint light halo around the upper body. The dual application signals the zone's duality. Cleared on zone exit at the rate of whichever mark is longer-lasting (dark aura).

**Archetype visual compatibility.** Zone marks layer *additively* over the archetype visuals established in §1.7 (Berserker, Scholar, Duelist, etc.). A Berserker with aggressive stance visuals + Frozen Shore snow dusting reads as both at once — the archetype is the character's identity; the zone mark is the character's recent travel. No conflict because the two use different visual channels (archetype uses body language, posture, equipment emphasis; zone marks use surface-texture accumulation).

**Accessibility.** Zone marks are subtle enough not to interfere with combat readability. A player with reduced-effects accessibility setting (planned) can disable zone marks entirely without affecting other game systems. Zone marks are purely atmospheric.

**Creative thesis.** Players passing through zones should leave the zone having absorbed something of it. Zone marks make this literal — the zone's visual essence briefly rides along with the character into their next encounter. A player who's spent an hour in Ember Fields carries the soot into town; the soot fades as they spend time in town; by the time they leave for Frozen Shore, the soot is gone. This is ambient world-weight: the player is *from somewhere* at all times, not a blank sprite reset between zones.

### 1.8 Anniversary Items — The OG Bro Cape

The Year 1 anniversary cape is the game's first discontinued cosmetic. Black body (#1a1a1a) with gold accent (#d4a030) and trim (#f5c542). Animated gold shimmer on the accent stripe.

**Rendering:** Cape drawn as a trailing rectangle behind the character, slightly narrower than shoulder width. Gold "OG" text rendered in 3-pixel font on the cape body. Glow halo (rgba(212,160,48,.3)) pulses subtly.

**Social signal:** The OG Cape says "I was here from the start." It cannot be bought, earned, or replicated — only claimed during the drop window. Over time, as the player base grows, the cape becomes rarer and more meaningful.

### 1.9 UI/UX Feel Direction

#### §1.9.1 Panel Motion Language

All UI panels share a consistent motion vocabulary. Panels slide in from the edge nearest their trigger button (bottom bar panels slide up, side panels slide from their side). Duration: 200ms with ease-out curve. Closing reverses the animation at 150ms (closing is faster than opening — snappy exit, gentle entry). Backdrop blur animates in over 100ms (faster than the panel to create a "world dims, then panel arrives" sequence).

No panel scales or fades. The motion is always translational — panels are objects that move into position, not objects that materialize. This creates a consistent spatial model: the UI exists just off-screen and slides in when summoned.

#### §1.9.2 Codex Browsing Feel

90 collisions organized by setup element (10 tabs — one per element). Within each tab, collisions listed by trigger element. Each entry shows: collision name, one-line effect, volatile indicator (crackle icon) if applicable, and the Codex hint question in italics.

The Codex should feel like a spellbook — something you consult, not something you read cover-to-cover. The dominant interaction is: "I just triggered a new collision → what was that? → tap the notification → Codex opens to that entry." Discovery-driven, not browse-driven.

**Undiscovered entries** show as "???" with the setup+trigger element glyphs visible but the name and effect hidden. This creates visible gaps — the player can see how many collisions they haven't found for each element pair. The gaps are the motivation to explore new weapon combinations.

**Entry animation on first discovery:** The "???" text dissolves into the collision name with a brief shimmer (150ms). The Codex hint question fades in 500ms after the name. This two-beat reveal mirrors the collision itself: setup → trigger. The name is the setup. The question is the trigger.

#### §1.9.3 Inspect Card Hierarchy

The inspect card is the game's social business card. Information hierarchy (what the player sees first → last):

1. **Name + clan tag + title** — top of card, largest text. The name IS the identity. Prismatic color on "The Unlikely One" title. Alignment color on the name background.
2. **Level + alignment indicator** — immediately below name. Level is a number. Alignment is a color-coded bar with dot markers showing individual Good/Neutral/Evil choices.
3. **Equipment names** — center of card. Weapon creative names with element shape glyphs. Armor name. The gear IS the build at a glance.
4. **Stat archetype glow** — subtle background tint matching the player's dominant stat archetype (§49). A Berserker's card has a faint red ambient. A Mage's has purple. This is felt rather than noticed — the card "looks like" a Berserker card before you read the stats.
5. **Action buttons** — bottom of card. TP, Trade, Duel, Threat, Friend, Block, Mute. Secondary information — the card is for reading, the buttons are for acting.

**Emotional impression targets:** A Radiant-aligned Level 90 Berserker's card should feel *dangerous and noble* — gold name background, red archetype glow, high-tier weapon names with flame/storm glyphs. A Shadow-aligned Level 40 Mage's card should feel *mysterious and concerning* — dark red name background, purple archetype glow, dark/venom element glyphs.

**Dual-title display (v12.36).** The v12.36 §54.1 title-showcase feature adds a secondary title slot alongside the active title. Typography treatment:

- **Active title** renders in its established canonical weight and color. No typographic change from pre-v12.36.
- **Showcase title** (when set, per §54.1 rules) renders below the active title at ~80% of the active title's font weight — visibly secondary without being diminished. Color matches the showcase title's semantic register (Hero gold, Sovereign dark red, etc.), independent of the active title's color.
- **Nameplate (compact display)** — horizontal rendering with middot separator: *"Hero · The Unlikely One"*. Both titles in single line, active on the left, showcase on the right.
- **Full inspect card** — two-line rendering when vertical space allows: active title on line 1, showcase on line 2 at slightly reduced size. Fallback to middot-separator single line on narrower viewports.
- **No showcase set** — card displays only the active title, identical to pre-v12.36 presentation. The dual-title treatment is an additive layer; players who don't engage with showcase selection see no visual change.

**Pairing read as story, not résumé.** The two-slot ceiling is load-bearing typographically: a player seeing "Hero · The Unlikely One" reads a character arc (*"they earned the Guardian title and also walked the unlikely path"*). A player seeing three or more titles reads a list. The dual-title register produces narrative legibility; more slots would produce inventory legibility. The v12.36 design chose narrative.

**Showcase selection interaction.** The character-info panel (Inventory → Character → Titles → Set Showcase) presents the player's earned titles as a scrollable list with the current showcase marked. Tapping an earned title sets it as showcase (replacing any prior selection); tapping the current showcase un-sets it (returning to active-only display). No confirmation dialog — the selection is immediate and reversible.

**Progressive derivative reveal (v12.42).** The inspect card's stat derivatives section (GDD §2.7) reveals additional derivative rows as the underlying stat crosses thresholds. The reveal animation is specifically designed to feel like *earning visibility into depth* rather than *unlocking a UI feature*.

When a player opens their inspect card after a stat has crossed a threshold that reveals a new derivative (e.g., first open after Elemental Mastery crosses 50, revealing Status Duration), the new derivative row animates in with a ~0.3s slide-from-right motion followed by a ~0.5s subtle highlight glow on the row. The animation fires once per newly-revealed derivative per character; subsequent inspect-card opens show the derivative as a standard row without highlight. No audio cue, no popup, no notification — the animation is the sole acknowledgment, and it happens inside the inspect card the player chose to open.

The *tonal register* of the reveal is earned visibility rather than unlocked content. The language in any associated tooltip uses observational framing: *"Status Duration is now visible on your inspect card"* — a statement of what changed, not a congratulation. A player who has just watched their EM cross 50 and opens their card to see Status Duration slide in feels the small recognition of "my investment in EM has reached the level where this matters" — the game acknowledging their depth without announcing.

**Typography for revealed derivatives matches primary derivatives exactly.** A newly-revealed Status Duration row at EM 50+ renders in the same typography, color, and layout as the always-visible Collision Damage Mult row. The reveal is not a "new player acquires advanced UI" register; it is "the card now reflects the character's depth." Visual parity reinforces that the derivative was always applying mechanically — only its inspect-card presence was gated.

**No opt-out toggle for progressive reveal.** Players cannot disable the threshold-gated reveal to see all derivatives immediately. This is deliberate: the mechanic's value is in the reveal *being* a structural pedagogical signal, not a preference setting. A toggle would undermine the recognition — a player who opted to see all derivatives from the start would miss the small moment of "the card just grew richer with my investment." The discipline is non-configurable because the configurability would destroy what it's configuring.

#### §1.9.4 Loadout Menu Feel

The element orientation swap (choosing which element is auto-attack vs swipe) should feel like flipping a coin — a decisive, physical motion. The two collision names displayed for the current orientation update immediately. The volatile indicator (crackle icon) appears/disappears as orientations swap, creating a visual "this direction has power" signal.

When the player taps a collision name in the preview, the Codex opens to that entry. The Codex hint question appears. The player goes back to combat and experiments. The preview → Codex → combat loop (§8.31) is the full learning path and the UI should make each transition feel like a single tap, not a navigation journey.

#### §1.9.5 Friend List Activity Display (v12.25)

The friend list (GDD §30.1 extended in v12.25) shows each friend's current location and activity. The visual direction: **clean, quiet, instantly readable.**

Each friend entry renders as a single line. Layout:
- Online indicator (small circle, green/yellow/grey for online/idle/offline)
- Friend name
- Current location on its own line below, in smaller lighter text: *"Frozen Shore (Depth 4)"*
- Time-in-location appears for stays ≥30min: *"Town — 45min"* — tiny text, right-aligned

No icons for locations (no fire emoji for Ember Fields, no tree emoji for Flora). Text alone. The list should scan as a simple readable list, not a dashboard.

Friends in Lawless Land render their location line in a slightly warmer red tint — *"Lawless Land (15 min)"* — a subtle warning that doesn't shout. This lets social players quickly see "oh, Alice is in the wastes, I might not want to TP to her right now."

Teleport request is a small icon button at the end of each row, subtle. Not prominent — TP is a secondary action, not the primary purpose of the list.

The privacy toggle ("Appear offline to friends") lives in the settings menu, not on the friend list itself. The UX respects that this is a private decision that should not be made publicly visible or easily triggered accidentally.

#### §1.9.6 Leaderboard Scope Presentation (v12.25)

The leaderboard tabs now support scope filters (GDD §37.2). Visual treatment:

The three scope filters — Friends, Clan, Global — render as three smaller tabs above the main leaderboard. The default scope is visually distinguished with a small "default" pip indicator, not just the active-tab highlight. This signals to the player that the opened scope is the system's chosen default for them, not their own last choice.

The nearby-neighbors section is the dominant visual element when the leaderboard opens. The player's own row is highlighted. Two rows above, two rows below. Top 10 of the scope renders as a secondary section below the nearby-neighbors. The UI's emphasis: *this is where you are in this scope, and who is immediately near you.*

Global scope, when active, adds a small reminder at the top: *"Global leaderboard — all players on the server."* This is to reassure the player that switching to Global isn't a different feature; it's the same leaderboard with a different scope. Similar reminders for Friends ("Your friends, ranked") and Clan ("Your clan, ranked").

**Creative thesis.** Personal-scale competition is the leaderboard's real engagement surface. The UI should reinforce this at every decision point: defaults, visual emphasis, header text. Global is available but quiet; Friends and Clan are foregrounded.

#### §1.9.7 Price Guidance UI (v12.31)

Marketplace listings (GDD §39.9.1) surface recent-sales data inline on every listing. The creative direction governs how this information renders so it reads as *helpful context* rather than *market editorial*.

**Typography register — body text, informational.** Price guidance renders in the listing's standard body-text color, same size as listing description text. No highlight, no color emphasis, no font change. The information is there for buyers who want it; it does not demand attention from buyers who don't. A listing's price remains the most prominent number on the listing; recent-sales data sits below it as secondary information.

**Layout.**

Standard listing display:

```
Steel Greatsword · Hardness 3 · Temper 47
Listed at 1,100g
Recent sales (last 30 days or 30 sales, whichever is fewer): 950g, 1,020g, 880g
This listing is 15% above recent average.
[Inspect] [Buy]
```

Price guidance takes 1-2 lines of text. Recent sales render as comma-separated values, most-recent-first (left to right). The delta percentage line is rendered below the raw sample sales; together they form a two-line informational block.

**Delta presentation — neutral numeric.** The delta line always reads:

> *"This listing is [N]% above recent average."*
> *"This listing is [N]% below recent average."*
> *"This listing matches recent average."* (for deltas within ±2%)

Never: *"Good deal!"* *"Overpriced!"* *"Best price!"* *"Fair!"* The language is strictly factual-numeric. The marketplace presents the number; the buyer draws the conclusion.

**Color treatment — none.** The delta line renders in the same body-text color as everything else. Not green for below-average, not red for above-average. Neutral coloration is the load-bearing choice here: colored text implies "good/bad," which is editorial. A neutral black/grey delta is factual-numeric. Colorblind-safety and tonal-neutrality both argue for no color.

**No-data state.** When no recent sales match the listing's key, the guidance renders as:

```
Recent sales: No recent sales of this exact match.
```

No italics, no fallback numerical suggestion. A clean acknowledgment that the system has no data for this listing.

**Fallback-match annotation.** When the system falls back to a broader matching key (e.g., Hardness-matched but Temper-insensitive), the guidance makes the fallback explicit:

```
No recent sales at this exact spec.
Recent sales of Hardness-4 Mithril Bows (any Temper): 8,400g, 9,100g, 7,800g
This listing at 12,000g is 35% above recent average for the broader match.
```

The *"for the broader match"* tail is essential — without it, buyers might think the comparison is precise. Being explicit about fallback breadth is part of the factual-framing discipline.

**Creative thesis.** Price guidance exists to help new players participate in the marketplace without being exploited by their own inexperience. The visual treatment deliberately underplays the information so that browsing experienced players who already know the market don't feel crowded by it, while newer players who need the context can read it and learn.
