Over many marketplace sessions, even a player who started as a novice internalizes the rough price ranges for categories they care about — at which point the guidance becomes reinforcing confirmation rather than essential information. This is the right arc: training wheels that disappear naturally as the player's own judgment develops. The marketplace never forces anyone to rely on guidance; it simply makes guidance available.

#### §1.9.8 Flip Indicator (v12.31)

Flipped listings (GDD §39.9.2) display a small indicator identifying that the current listing follows a recent marketplace purchase of the same item by the same seller. The creative direction keeps the indicator *disclosure-not-accusation*.

**The glyph — ↻ (clockwise open circle arrow).** The Unicode symbol U+21BB renders as a clean single-character indicator. Small, neutral, visually quiet. It does not pulse, glow, flash, or animate. Just a small glyph in the listing's informational strip.

**Position and layout.**

```
Steel Greatsword · Hardness 3 · Temper 47
Listed at 1,100g
Recent sales: 950g, 1,020g, 880g. This listing is 15% above recent average.
↻ Recently flipped. Previous sale: 800g · purchased 6 hours ago.
[Inspect] [Buy]
```

The flip line sits below the price-guidance block as a single additional line. The ↻ glyph starts the line; the rest is plain text describing the previous sale price and the time since purchase.

**Text register — informational, past tense.**

> *"Recently flipped. Previous sale: [N]g · purchased [T] ago."*

Past-tense framing (*"flipped," "previous sale," "purchased"*) matters. It establishes that the marketplace is stating a *fact about what happened* rather than making a claim about the seller's character or intent. "Flipped" is a description of a transaction pattern, not a character judgment.

**No color, no highlight, no warning register.** The flip line renders in the same body-text color as the price guidance. Not red, not yellow, not in a warning-icon-adjacent color scheme. Neutral coloration consistent with the marketplace's overall factual-framing discipline. A flip flag is data, not an alarm.

**Hover/tap for context.** Buyers who want more detail about the flip can tap the flip line to see a small popover:

> *This seller purchased this exact item on the marketplace 6 hours ago for 800g. They are now listing it for 1,100g without meaningfully changing the weapon's state (no Hardness change, no composite applied, no significant combat use).*
>
> *Flip indicators disappear 24 hours after the original purchase.*

The popover is plainspoken. It explains what the flag means in human language without editorializing. Buyers can close the popover and make their own judgment.

**What the flip indicator is NOT.**
- Not a warning — no alarm icon, no color, no visual interrupt.
- Not a block — flipped listings can still be purchased normally.
- Not a permanent flag — it disappears 24 hours after the original purchase.
- Not a social judgment — the language is descriptive, not evaluative.

**Creative thesis.** Flip disclosure serves players who want to know about flipping activity; it does not punish sellers who flip. Some buyers will avoid flipped listings on principle — fine, the information lets them do so. Other buyers will consider flips reasonable market behavior and buy anyway — also fine. The marketplace's role is to surface the information; it is not to take a side. The neutral visual register (↻ glyph, no color, past-tense text) enforces this neutrality at every level. A buyer reading the indicator understands *what happened* without feeling the marketplace is urging them to react a particular way. Over time, players develop their own flip-tolerance norms, and the marketplace accommodates all positions by refusing to hold one.

#### §1.9.9 Collision Opportunity Glow (v12.38)

The v12.38 weapon edge glow (GDD §9.2.1) is a peripheral-vision signal that communicates collision compatibility between the player's equipped weapon and nearby enemies' active statuses. The creative direction for this signal is *subtle tool awakening* — the player's weapon reads as *coming alive* when there's a collision opportunity nearby, without adding markers to the battlefield.

**The rendering — 2-pixel outer edge ring.** The weapon sprite's outer silhouette gains a thin outer ring (2 pixels at standard zoom, scaling with weapon icon size at higher zoom) in the setup element's canonical color per §10.1. The ring follows the weapon's sprite contour — it is an edge-outline effect, not a rectangular halo around the icon. On a Greatsword the ring traces the blade's outer curve; on a Bow the ring follows the curve of the bow limb; on a Staff the ring outlines the crystal end. The effect is *weapon-shaped awareness* — the specific tool glows, not a generic area around it.

**Intensity coupling.** Glow alpha starts at ~15% when the most-urgent nearby compatible status has ≥80% duration remaining, and scales up linearly to ~60% alpha as that status approaches expiry. The intensity ramp communicates urgency without numeric timer — a player sees their weapon getting brighter and knows the window is closing without having to look at a status duration bar. This is the single most important property of the glow: **intensity encodes time**, allowing the player to prioritize among multiple opportunities by looking at which glow is brightest.

**Color coupling.** The glow uses the setup element's palette entry per §10.1 (Flame red, Frost blue, Venom green, Storm purple, Stone brown, Wind grey, Flora green, Water blue, Dark dark-blue, Light gold). The color is the element, and the element is legible at a glance. In a fight where the player has a Flame weapon and sees a red edge glow, they know there's a Burn status ready to trigger Melt (Flame + Frost setup) or Explosion (Flame + Flame setup) nearby — the color names the element, and the player's combat knowledge provides the specific collision.

**Multiple compatible targets.** When multiple nearby enemies carry compatible statuses, the glow intensity tracks the *most-urgent* (lowest-remaining-duration) compatible status among them. The glow does not split into multiple rings or shift color to indicate multiple opportunities — it remains a single edge outline tuned to the most-pressing target. This preserves the signal's simplicity: *one glow, one urgency, one color*. Players in crowded combat feel the signal as a continuously-urgent peripheral awareness rather than as a multi-layered information display.

**The shape-first supplement for colorblind palette.** Players using the colorblind-safe palette (§58 Accessibility) lose some of the color-distinction signal. The glow supplements color with a *shape-edge texture* matching the setup element's shape glyph per §10.1 — the glow's rendering pattern traces Flame-diamond edges, Frost-hexagon edges, Storm-star points, etc. The shape texture is present for all players but becomes the primary channel when color distinction is degraded. A colorblind player sees their weapon's edge glow with Frost's hexagonal pattern and knows a Freeze setup is available without needing to distinguish the blue hue.

**Never exceeds 60% alpha.** The glow's maximum intensity is deliberately capped below a range where it would compete for attention with central-vision combat elements (enemy sprites, telegraphs, the player character). At 60% alpha the glow is *noticeable peripherally* but does not draw the eye from the fight's focal point. This is the discipline — a player's attention should remain on enemy telegraphs and positioning; the glow augments combat awareness without redirecting attention.

**Comparison to the not-shipped alternative.** The original plan draft placed a small arrow on the enemy's status icon indicating collision compatibility. That approach adds one marker per compatible enemy, producing visual noise that scales with combat density. The shipped v12.38 design places the signal on the player's own weapon, which is peripheral to attention by design (the hand in the foreground) and produces a signal that stays constant regardless of enemy count. The visual discipline ("my tool glows") also reads as empowering rather than as training-wheels ("the enemy has been identified for me"). The weapon-edge framing treats the player as the combat protagonist whose instrument is sharp, not as a learner who needs the game to highlight opportunities on the enemies.

**What the glow is NOT.**
- Not a battlefield marker — nothing renders on the enemy sprite.
- Not an attention-grab — 60% alpha max, always peripheral.
- Not a mechanical bonus — purely informational, zero damage effect.
- Not required — toggleable in Accessibility settings, default on.
- Not a progression surface — does not level up, expand, or become more pronounced for veterans.

**Creative thesis.** Information can be placed in the foreground of a scene without cluttering it. The player's weapon is the most-peripherally-visible object in combat — the hand in the corner of the eye — and making it responsive to combat state turns it into an information display that the player feels rather than reads. The glow does not announce; it reacts. A weapon that lights up when there's something to do is a tool that seems to know about the world, and a tool that seems to know about the world turns the player into an instrument-wielder rather than a heads-up-display-reader. This is a small feel-state for a small mechanic, but the register choice matters: empowerment over guidance, reaction over announcement, peripheral over focal.

#### §1.9.10 Threat Direction Arrows (v12.42)

The v12.42 threat direction arrows (GDD §11.1) place a small arrow above each aggroed enemy, pointing at the player the enemy is currently targeting. The creative direction governs the arrow's tonal register: *peripheral threat indicator*, not *combat HUD element*. The arrow is a subtle piece of information layered over the combat scene rather than a UI component that competes with enemies for attention.

**Muted orange — threat neutral, not danger.** The color choice (`#D68A3C`) sits between Flame red (which would read as "you're on fire") and Flora green (which would read as "safe"). The muted-orange register is neither urgency nor reassurance — it is *attention* in the peripheral sense. A player catching the orange arrow in peripheral vision registers "threat direction is marked on this enemy" without feeling alarm. When the arrow points at them specifically, the information is actionable; when the arrow points elsewhere, the information is contextual. Neither reading triggers the adrenaline response that a red arrow would produce.

**Semi-transparent, peripheral-grade rendering.** The arrow renders at ~70% alpha — visible enough to catch peripherally, faint enough to never compete with the enemy sprite itself. This matches the v12.38 weapon edge glow's peripheral discipline: information channels layered over combat should be registerable at a glance, not demand foveal focus. A player looking at an enemy sees the enemy first and the arrow second; the arrow is decoration on top, not a UI element beside.

**Shape-based, not pure-color.** The triangular arrow shape carries the directional information regardless of color perception. A colorblind player reads arrow direction from the pointed triangle shape alone; a player on a bright screen in sunlight reads the direction from shape contrast even when color distinction is low. This is the same shape-first discipline that §10.1 element palette uses: color is the primary channel for non-impaired players, shape is the universal backup.

**Rendering position — above the sprite, not overlapping it.** The arrow renders at a fixed offset ~8px above the sprite's top edge. This keeps the arrow visually attached to the enemy (tracks enemy position) while never occluding the enemy's body (no ambiguity about enemy art vs arrow art). Players reading the enemy's telegraphs — attack wind-ups, status icons, resistance-tier damage numbers — see all those signals on the enemy's body unobscured by the arrow layer above.

**Fade-on-transition feel — the event is visible.** When a monster's aggro shifts from Player A to Player B, the arrow fades to ~0 alpha over 0.3s and then resolves at full pointing toward the new target. Player A sees the arrow fade as threat leaves them; Player B sees it solidify at them as threat arrives. The fade-and-re-resolve makes aggro transitions *legible as events* rather than silent snaps. Without the fade, a Tank playing against a boss whose aggro snaps instantly to the DPS would not see the transition — they'd just notice later that the boss is now facing the DPS. The 0.3s fade window gives the transition a visible moment, which is what Tank-style play needs in order to react.

**No audio component.** The arrow has no sound on appearance, transition, or disappearance. Audio layered on threat arrows would produce constant clicking during active combat as arrows appear, transition, and fade across many enemies. The visual channel alone is sufficient; the audio channel is reserved for higher-priority signals (collision impacts, resonance pulses, combat cues). This discipline preserves the audio mix for events that genuinely benefit from audio attention.

**Solo play — every aggroed enemy marks you.** A solo player fighting 5 aggroed enemies sees 5 arrows converging on their position. The visual accumulation produces a visceral peripheral signal of threat concentration — at 1 enemy, the arrow is informational; at 5 enemies, the convergent arrows produce the sense of being surrounded. This scales the solo combat feel naturally with enemy count, without any explicit "you are surrounded" UI treatment.

**Party play — the Tank's read.** A Tank holding aggro on 3 monsters sees 3 arrows pointing at them. When one arrow flips to the DPS, the Tank sees the transition visible as an event (the fade, then the re-resolve at the DPS) and knows to re-taunt *before* the DPS takes damage. This is the core Tank-role interaction that was previously invisible — the server knew about the aggro shift, but the Tank had no signal until damage landed on someone else. The arrow's fade-and-resolve makes the aggro-management loop tight and responsive.

**What the arrow is NOT.**
- Not a HUD marker. Does not appear in any UI panel, minimap, or character sheet.
- Not a warning. The muted orange is not an alarm color; the arrow does not pulse or flash to demand attention.
- Not a progression surface. The arrow is consistent across character levels; it does not grow in size, intensity, or specificity as the player advances.
- Not stat-gated. Every player sees threat arrows regardless of stat investment, gear, or level.
- Not per-alignment. Heroes, Sovereigns, and Arbiters all see identical threat arrow rendering.

**Creative thesis.** Threat direction is information the server has and the player needs, and the gap between those two facts has historically been bridged by painful learning (taking damage to find out an enemy was targeting you). The arrow is the smallest, most peripheral-friendly way to close that gap. It is not a UI feature in the conventional sense — it does not have a menu, a toggle button labeled "enabled," or a tutorial explaining what it means. It is a piece of information layered onto the combat scene, visible in the same peripheral zone as weapon edge glow and combo posture. Together these three peripheral channels (weapon edge for collision compatibility, posture for combo tier, arrow for threat direction) form a complete combat-awareness layer that expert players read simultaneously without conscious attention. That's the goal: combat legibility through peripheral vision, not through heads-up display.

#### §1.9.11 One-Handed Auto-Attack Feel (v12.43)

The v12.43 One-Handed Control Preset (GDD §58.4) replaces manual attack input with continuous auto-attack on the locked-on enemy. This is a meaningful change to combat feel — the tap-rhythm that shapes two-thumbed play disappears, replaced by a steadier automated cadence. The creative direction governs how that change reads: **the auto-attack should feel like the character handling the routine rhythm so the player can focus on movement and swipes, not like a game that plays itself.**

**The distinction between "auto-attack" and "idle game."** A conventional idle-game auto-attack reads as the player stepping back from the action — the game runs and the player watches. One-Handed auto-attack is the opposite register: the player is *more* engaged with movement, positioning, and swipe-timed abilities because the trivial rhythm input has been automated away. The creative intent is "character handles the routine; player handles the interesting decisions," not "character plays while player observes." The feel should lean into the character's agency — the character is deciding to attack because an enemy is locked on, not because the game is driving them.

**Attack visual cadence — identical to manual attack.** Auto-attack uses the same attack animation, the same damage numbers, the same combo-count progression, the same collision opportunity glow that manual attack uses. A spectator watching a One-Handed-mode player fight looks at the weapon swing, hit flash, and impact particles and sees identical output to a two-thumbed player. Only the input source differs — the visual output is unchanged. This preserves the core combat feel and lets One-Handed players participate in the game's visual register without feeling like they're watching a different game.

**Auto-lock framing — "the character picks a target."** The auto-lock selection (nearest enemy) reads most naturally when the character's orientation or posture subtly indicates the target choice. When the lock shifts from Enemy A to Enemy B (A dies, B is now nearest), the character's facing direction and weapon-ready stance rotate smoothly toward B over a short interpolation (~150ms). The player reads this as the character noticing the next threat, not as a menu-driven target switch. This is a creative direction discipline: auto-lock feels like character initiative, not like system automation.

**What the mode does NOT do.**
- Does not display "AUTO-ATTACK" or any mode-indicator UI. The preset is invisible; the character just fights.
- Does not use a distinct color, aura, or animation variant that marks "this is auto-attack mode." The attack output is visually identical to manual attack.
- Does not play an "auto-attack enabled" sound cue or notification. The mode is ambient, not announced.
- Does not adjust monster difficulty, spawn rates, or rewards. One-Handed play is the same game on the same content.

**The swipe layer carries the expressiveness.** With attack rhythm automated, the player's expressive input shifts to swipes — dodge direction, lunge choice, retreat shot timing. One-Handed mode's full-screen swipe zone gives these inputs more surface area to work with, and the swipe resolution becomes the primary interactive register. The player's tactical voice speaks through *when* and *where* they swipe, not through *when* they tap attack. This is a deliberate design redistribution — automating the input with the least tactical depth (tap rhythm) to preserve full fidelity on the input with the most (directional swipes).

**Block long-press — held pressure, released relief.** Block via long-hold (0.3s activation) produces a distinct feel from tap-to-block. The 0.3s hold creates a "committed stance" register — the player is holding a defensive intention, not toggling a state. When the block releases, the character's posture relaxes from the defensive lean back to neutral. This posture arc is slightly slower than manual block's instant-toggle feel (~200ms relaxation vs instant), reinforcing the sense that One-Handed Block is a deliberate hold rather than a quick tap. The trade is appropriate — the single thumb cannot easily execute quick tap-block-tap-unblock rhythms, so the mode leans into held-stance feel instead of tap-toggle feel.

**Creative thesis.** One-Handed Control Preset is an accessibility surface where the creative direction must compensate for input constraints *without* signaling those constraints to the player. A player enabling One-Handed mode should feel that their character is just as engaged, expressive, and visually alive as a two-thumbed player's — the constraint is on the input layer, not on the character's presence. Auto-attack doesn't look automated; auto-lock doesn't look systemic; the swipe layer carries full expressive weight; the block register leans into held-pressure feel. The net effect is that One-Handed play reads as a different *input style* for the same character in the same game, not as a "lite" version. This is the same creative discipline that threat arrows follow — accessibility surfaces layer over the shared combat scene at the peripheral-information level, never as mode-indicator UI that divides players into visible strata. Every One-Handed player sees the same combat register as every two-thumbed player; the difference is entirely in how the player's intentions reach the character.

---

## 2. AUDIO DIRECTION

All audio via Web Audio API (`BT_AUDIO` module). No external audio files — everything synthesized.

**Sound palette:**
- Combat hits: short triangle/sawtooth bursts (80-400Hz)
- UI interactions: clean sine beeps (400-800Hz)
- Collect/reward: ascending sine arpeggio
- Level up: 6-note celebratory arpeggio
- Death: low sawtooth descend
- Block: square wave thud (300Hz)
- Zone ambience: per-zone synthesized soundscapes — see §1.2a Zone Mood Guides for full per-zone direction. Each zone has a unique ambient drone, texture layer, and event sound palette.
- **Echo Stone:** soft crystalline chime — two sine tones a fifth apart, gentle attack, long decay. Should feel like finding something ancient. Not a reward sound — a discovery sound.
- **Alignment choice:** low resonant bell (Good = ascending major third, Evil = descending minor second, Neutral = single sustained tone). Plays once. Should feel permanent.
- **Ferryman quest unlock:** unique — reversed sawtooth swell into silence. The absence of a resolution note is the point.
- **True Eclipse collision:** Extended version of standard collision sound — adds a harmonic overtone series that resolves into a major chord. The only collision that sounds like resolution instead of impact.
- **Pet evolution:** Rising arpeggio with shimmer overtone — three ascending notes accelerating, then a sustained harmonic. Feels like transformation, not reward.
- **Furniture craft:** Warm wooden thunk (low sine + noise burst) followed by a brief settlement chime. Satisfying, tactile.
- **Arena match start:** War horn — low brass synth swell with reverb tail. Urgent, not celebratory.
- **Arena champion:** Full fanfare — 8-note triumphant arpeggio with sustained final chord. The most elaborate sound in the game.
- **Clan war declaration:** Distant thunder roll — filtered noise with descending pitch. Ominous, exciting.
- **Guild rank up:** Gentle ascending bell tone, one note higher per rank. Rank 8 (Transcendent) adds a shimmering overtone series.
- **Mask equip:** Quick comedic "boing" — sine sweep 200→600Hz with fast decay. Playful.
- **Minigame countdown:** Three descending beeps (G, E, C) then a start chime (high C). Clean, arcade-like.
- **Underwater ambient:** Filtered low oscillator with slow LFO modulation. Muffled, immersive. Air meter warning: accelerating beeps as air depletes below 25%.
- **Flora/Thorn collision:** Organic creak-and-snap — layered sine tones with subtle pitch bend simulating wood stress, followed by a sharp high click (thorn piercing). Distinct from other collision sounds because it sounds alive.
- **Reverse palette collisions:** Same base collision sound as the original direction but with a **reversed envelope** — the attack and decay are swapped. Where Steam (Flame→Frost) has a sharp attack and quick decay (explosion), Meltwater (Frost→Flame) has a slow swell and sustained tail (pooling). The player hears "same ingredients, different shape" without consciously analyzing it. The reverse pairs feel like the echo of the original.
- **Dark corruption collisions (palette→Dark):** The base collision sound plays normally, then a **low reverberant shadow tone** fades in underneath — a sub-bass swell that lingers 0.5s after the collision sound ends. The shadow tone is the same for all 7 Dark corruption collisions. It says "something wrong just happened." Shadowfire's invisible Burn has no ongoing sound — the absence is the point.
- **Light redemption collisions (palette→Light):** The base collision sound plays, then resolves into an **ascending harmonic chime** — two notes rising a major third, gentle attack, warm decay. Same for all 7 Light redemption collisions. It says "something was healed." Judgment is the exception — no ascending chime, just the loudest single-hit collision sound in the palette→Light category. Judgment sounds like verdict, not mercy.
- **Mend (Stone→Light):** The collision crack of Stone followed by a warm resonant tone — not the Light healing chime (Mend doesn't heal the enemy), but a deeper, self-directed version. The sound says "the cracks filled in *me*." A brief rising hum after the crack, like armor settling into place. Distinct from other Light collisions because the benefit flows to the attacker, not the target.
- **Umbra (Light→Dark):** Eclipse's sound played in reverse — the harmonic resolution that normally ends Eclipse instead *begins* Umbra, then decays into the Dark sub-bass shadow tone. Where Eclipse resolves, Umbra unravels. The most unsettling collision sound in the game. 3 seconds, same length as Eclipse.
- **Guardian zone entry:** Low sustained drone with a golden bell overtone — the drone is the zone's standard ambient pushed lower in register, the bell is the Guardian signal. Should feel like crossing a threshold into something sacred and dangerous.
- **Apex boss phase transition:** Descending power chord (three stacked square waves) into silence, then the next phase's theme begins. The silence between phases is intentional — 0.5s of nothing creates tension.
- **Ascendant title:** Extended level-up fanfare with an additional sustained harmonic resolution. Longer than any other title sound — 3 seconds. Should feel like arrival.
- **Final ending sequence:** Ambient — not a single cue but a 60-second evolving soundscape. Hero ending: warm sine harmonics that slowly build into a resolved major chord. Arbiter ending: neutral sustained tones that neither resolve nor conflict — stillness. Sovereign ending: low reverberant tones that grow in intensity, then cut to silence at the title reveal. The silence IS the sound.
- **Final title bestowed:** Single deep bell strike with long decay (8+ seconds). Same for all three endings. The bell is the last sound of the narrative. It should linger in the room after it plays.
- **Resonance pulse tick:** Faint audible tick per pulse, using the status element's audio identity. Not a beep — a tick. Like a clock. Accelerates with pulse rate. Flame: tiny crackle (filtered noise burst, warm). Frost: crystalline click (high sine, cold). Water: soft drip (pitched sine with fast decay). Venom: wet pop (noise + sine, organic). Storm: static snap (white noise burst, sharp). Stone: mineral tap (low sine, hard). Wind: whispered hiss (band-passed noise, airy). Dark: muffled thud (sub-bass sine, felt more than heard). Light: bell ping (high sine with overtone, clear).
- **Resonance collision (timed):** Standard collision sound with an added ascending harmonic layer. The ascending tone rises higher based on resonance depth at trigger time. Collision triggered at resonance entry: barely-audible ascending hum. At peak resonance: clear, bright ascending chime that harmonizes with the standard collision sound. The audio reward for timing — the collision sounds *better*.
- **Failed resonance (status expires without trigger):** Soft descending tone — two notes, falling minor second, quick fade. Not punishing. A quiet "you waited too long." Should feel like a missed opportunity, not a mistake.
- **Lunge impact:** Short sharp forward sound — a compressed whoosh that's the opposite of the dodge roll's evasive swoosh. The dodge sound spreads out (moving away from danger). The lunge sound compresses in (collapsing distance). Hit on arrival has a slightly meatier impact than a standard auto-attack — not more damaging, but more forceful in feel. Status application chime plays immediately after impact.
- **Retreat shot:** Two layered sounds: the dodge roll's standard swoosh PLUS a quiet projectile-release click underneath it. The click is subtle — the player hears the dodge first, the shot second. If the status applies, the chime plays 100–200ms after the click, creating a three-part sound sequence: swoosh → click → chime. Dodge, shot, setup.
- **Combo glow transition:** No sound on count increment — the weapon glow is purely visual. Sound would create audio clutter on every auto-attack. The combo chain's audio identity lives in the swipe, not the buildup.
- **Combo burst (count 1+ swipe):** A subtle bass undertone added to the standard collision sound. Not louder — deeper. The collision sounds fuller when it has the burst bonus behind it. The player doesn't hear a separate "combo" sound — they hear the collision sound with more weight.
- **Combo spread (count 2+ swipe):** The status application chime plays on the spread target 100–200ms after the collision sound. The slight delay creates an "and also..." feeling. The collision landed... and also reached the next one. No unique sound — just the standard chime, delayed, from a different spatial position.
- **Combo investment active (count 3 flag):** When the extended status applies on the next auto-attack, the status application chime plays at a slightly lower pitch than normal — warmer, heavier. The player hears "this one matters more" without being told why. The pitch difference is subtle enough to be felt rather than consciously noticed.
- **Brute collapse:** Three-phase sound. Stagger: silent. Hold: a structural creak (low sine with slow pitch bend, like weight shifting). Impact: deep resonant thud scaled to sprite size — 16×16 is a tap, 176×176 endgame-scale is a boom (v12.40). Dust settle: soft hiss (filtered noise, 0.3s decay). The collapse sound has more acoustic weight than any standard kill sound.
- **Swarm flinch:** Silent. The flinch is visual only. The kill sound plays on the dead member. The living members react without sound. The silence of the flinch makes it more noticeable through contrast.
- **Death screen killing blow text:** No sound. The text appears silently below the respawn timer. The death's comedic animation and sound have already played. The killing blow line is informational aftermath — adding a sound would compete with the existing death audio.

### 2.1 Zone Soundscape Reference Table

Quick lookup for ambient audio direction per zone. Each entry summarizes the base tone, texture layer, and event sounds. Full creative direction (first impression, signature detail) lives in §1.2a Zone Mood Guides.

| Zone | Base Tone | Texture Layer | Event Sounds | Mood Ref |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------------|:--------:|
| Town | Warm mid-frequency hum (major key) | Distant crowd murmur (filtered noise), anvil taps | Bird chirps (random sine arpeggios, 2–8s) | §1.2a |
| Ember Fields | Low rumbling drone (40–80Hz sawtooth) | Continuous crackling (filtered noise bursts) | Distant eruption booms (sub-bass hits, 15–30s) | §1.2a |
| Mistwood | Mid-range hum with detuned second oscillator (dissonance) | Irregular dripping (pitched sine plinks) | Distant animal call (detuned saw note, 20–40s) | §1.2a |
| Frozen Shore | Thin high whistle (filtered sine, 2kHz) | Ice creaking (slow pitch-bent sine, irregular) | Wind gusts (noise sweeps, 10–20s) | §1.2a |
| Thunder Peaks | Rumbling mid-bass (oscillating volume) | Continuous light static crackle | Thunder cracks (noise burst + reverb tail, 8–20s) | §1.2a |
| Deep Hollows | Sub-bass drone at threshold of hearing (25–35Hz) | Dripping water (sine plinks with delay echo) | Footstep echoes amplified (feedback on player movement) | §1.2a |
| Sky Reaches | Airy high-frequency wash (filtered bright noise) | Whistling wind (band-pass swept sine) | Silence gaps — 2–3s dropouts of all sound | §1.2a |
| Tidal Caves | Slow rhythmic wash (LFO-modulated noise, 6s cycle) | Bubbling (randomized sine plinks) | Tide surge (volume swell on base tone, 12s) | §1.2a |
| Verdant Wilds | Dense layered mid-frequency hum (multiple oscillators) | Insect-like chirping (high sine pulses, randomized) | Creaking wood (slow pitch-bend, 15–25s) | §1.2a |
| Shadow Sanctum | Reversed reverb tails on silence | Heartbeat-rate sub-pulse (0.8Hz, felt not heard) | Player's own collision sounds echoed back 2s later, quieter | §1.2a |
| Radiant Heights | Sustained high harmonic chord (pure sine overtone series) | Continuous crystalline ringing (high sine, long decay) | Brightness surges — uncomfortable volume swell, 3–5s | §1.2a |
| Convergence | Crossfade between Shadow Sanctum and Radiant Heights based on player position | Both ambiences play simultaneously at the boundary | Rare consonance — both ambiences align into resolved chord (60–120s, random) | §1.2a |
| Lawless Land | Dead air. No ambient tone. No texture. | Player footsteps only | Other players' combat sounds audible at 2× normal range | §1.2a |

### 2.2 Zone Emotional Audio Layer (v12.26)

Each zone's ambient soundscape subtly shifts toward the emotional register of its element aspect (GDD §33.1) after extended play. The shift is below conscious notice for most players; dedicated players with long sessions feel zones differently than quick-visit players.

**Timing.** The emotional layer fades in over the player's first ~3 minutes in-zone. For the first minute, only the standard ambient (§2.1) plays. Between 1-3 minutes, the emotional layer fades in at low volume (~20% of standard ambient). After 3 minutes, the emotional layer sits at its target volume (~30% of standard ambient — audible but not dominant). The layer persists as long as the player remains in-zone; it does NOT fade back out if the player stands still or stops engaging (this is ambient immersion, not engagement reward).

**Zone exit behavior.** The emotional layer fades out over ~30 seconds after the player leaves the zone. During the fade-out, the layer carries briefly into the next zone's ambient — a brief cross-contamination that registers as "I came from somewhere else" before the new zone's audio takes over.

**Per-zone specification.**

- **Frozen Shore (Frost / Grief):** A minor-key chord sustained on low strings (viola + cello register). Chord quality: suspended second, resolving occasionally to a minor triad. Dynamic level: pp (pianissimo), just audible. Instrument choice evokes *something crying without words*.
- **Ember Fields (Flame / Rage):** A low drum pulse at ~0.6Hz (every ~1.6 seconds), single tom-drum struck softly. Occasional subtle bass drum hit every ~15-30 seconds. Dynamic level: mp-p. Feel: *something patient and angry*.
- **Tidal Caves (Water / Memory):** A slow descending melodic fragment on an instrument with long decay (harp, marimba, or similar). Fragment repeats with slight variation every ~45 seconds — never exactly the same twice. Dynamic level: pp. Feel: *something remembering and forgetting*.
- **Mistwood (Venom / Betrayal):** A distant whisper-like sound, pitched so low it's felt more than heard (~50-80Hz fundamental). Occasional brief crescendos that resolve into silence. Dynamic level: pp, felt through subwoofers more than heard through speakers. Feel: *something confiding, then withdrawing*.
- **Thunder Peaks (Storm / Chaos):** Distant, irregular thunder rolls at ~2-5 minute intervals, mixed with occasional sustained dissonant chord in brass (horn section). The dissonance never resolves. Dynamic level: variable — silent most of the time, with occasional ~mf crescendos that fade. Feel: *something that will not settle*.
- **Deep Hollows (Stone / Defiance):** A sustained low drone (~80Hz fundamental with slow harmonic shifts), unchanging in pitch for long periods (~5-10 minutes at a time). Occasional stone-percussion hits (~15-30 second intervals). Dynamic level: p, steady. Feel: *something that refuses to move*.
- **Sky Reaches (Wind / Loss):** A distant choral voice (single sustained soprano note) that fades in and out at ~1-2 minute intervals. The note is never the same pitch twice — it drifts. Dynamic level: pp-p. Feel: *something that was here and is gone*.
- **Verdant Wilds (Flora / Yearning):** Multiple overlapping mid-frequency tones (woodwind register — oboe, clarinet), each playing slow glissandos that never reach their target. The tones layer and overlap without resolving. Dynamic level: p. Feel: *something reaching upward without shape*.
- **Shadow Sanctum (Dark / Fear):** Reversed reverb tails on silence — silent stretches broken by reverse-playback whispers that seem to emerge from nothing and disappear. Occasional ~0.8Hz sub-pulse (heartbeat rate). Dynamic level: silent most of the time, with brief ~mf whisper-events. Feel: *something unable to face itself*.
- **Radiant Heights (Light / Truth):** A sustained high harmonic chord — pure sine-wave overtone series on a tonic pitch. Occasional uncomfortable volume swells that make the listener want to turn down the volume. Dynamic level: mp but psychologically intense. Feel: *something that is too much to look at*.

**Convergence exception.** The Convergence uses its existing alignment-based ambient crossfade (§1.2a) as the emotional layer. Dark and Light emotional layers crossfade based on the player's current alignment-leaning — a Hero-path player in the Convergence hears mostly the Radiant Heights emotional layer; a Sovereign-path player hears mostly the Shadow Sanctum emotional layer; the player at an alignment neutral point hears both simultaneously, creating the same harmonic consonance described in §1.2a.

**Guardian zone treatment.** Guardian zones use intensified versions of their base element's emotional layer — louder, slightly more pitch-varied, with the layer fading in faster (within ~90 seconds of zone entry rather than 3 minutes). A Guardian zone's emotional presence should feel like the player has walked into the element's *pure distillation* rather than just visiting its standard zone.

**Accessibility.** Players with audio off are unaffected — all gameplay information is visual and text-based. The emotional layer is pure atmosphere.

**Creative thesis.** The emotional layer converts zones from *biomes* to *emotional residue*. Players playing long sessions in a single zone should feel the zone's aspect seep into their play — not because gameplay changes, but because the audio environment does. A player who spends an hour in Frozen Shore doesn't consciously register the minor chord they've been hearing for 40 minutes, but they leave the zone carrying some of its melancholy with them. Over many sessions, the zones develop felt identities that match their GDD §33.1 aspect labels — zones become the *emotions they are*, not just the biomes they look like.

### 2.3 Combat Audio Cues — Resistance Tier and Bless Heal (v12.26)

**Resistance tier audio cues.** Damage number resistance tier (§1.4.2) is reinforced with audio differentiation at hit-impact. The three tiers have distinct impact sounds:

- **Neutral:** standard hit impact — weapon-and-material specific (a steel greatsword hitting flesh sounds different from an ironwood bow striking armor, and so on). This is the baseline.
- **Resistant:** same base hit sound, but low-pass filtered at ~800Hz (cuts high-frequency content, leaving a muffled impact) and reduced in volume by ~3dB. The hit *feels soaked* — the impact is there but the high-frequency snap is missing. A player running with visuals off can identify "resistant hit" by the muffle alone.
- **Immune:** distinctly flat dull thunk. Low-frequency (~200Hz fundamental), short duration (~150ms), no overtones, no weapon-specific character. The sound is the same regardless of weapon/material combination because the hit essentially *didn't land*. A player running with visuals off can identify "immune hit" instantly — the flat thunk is unmistakable.

**Symmetric application.** The resistance tier audio plays for outgoing hits (player hits on resistant/immune enemies) AND incoming hits (player takes damage with their own resistances in play). A player takes a Flame hit while wearing Flame Attunement armor and hears the muffled variant of the Flame impact sound — their own armor's resistance is audible.

**Mix considerations.** Resistance audio cues are mixed so they don't compete with combat music or other gameplay sounds. The cues are close to the player's position in the 3D audio mix, and they duck (temporarily reduce volume of) ambient music by ~2dB during the impact moment. This ensures the resistance information is always audible even in busy combat.

**Bless Heal audio.** The Light swipe trigger's Bless heal (GDD §9.4) plays a specific chime distinct from any standard healing sound. Specification: a single bell-like chime with a warm fundamental (~440Hz, A natural) and a shimmer overtone one octave up. Duration: ~1.2 seconds from attack to full decay. Volume: audible but not dominant — similar dynamic range to the standard collision chime (~mf). The chime plays on the caster's position and is audible to all players in the ~20-unit Bless radius (including opposing PvP players — the Bless is a tactical signal, not stealth).

**Bless chime vs. standard heal.** Standard healing sounds (from potions, food, etc.) play a simpler higher-frequency chime (~660Hz, E natural). The Bless chime is distinguished by its lower fundamental and the octave overtone — players learn to recognize "Bless just happened" by ear within their first few exposures, and can localize it in 3D space to identify which ally cast it.

**Creative thesis.** Combat information that exists only visually is incomplete — players with partial visual attention (peripheral-vision combat, accessibility needs, or just eyes on movement) lose critical information. Layering resistance tier and heal type into audio gives the same information through a second channel. A veteran player playing at high speed with visuals scanning for movement can still hear their damage tiers and their heals landing, which keeps them tactically informed without requiring full visual attention on numbers and animations.

### 2.4 Haptic Feedback Design (v12.38)

The v12.38 mobile haptic layer (GDD §15.2a) provides tactile feedback for 12 key combat events, using the device's haptic motor (iOS Taptic Engine, Android haptic actuators). The creative direction governs how each event *feels* — pattern rhythm, duration, intensity, and the emotional register each haptic conveys. The load-bearing principle: **haptics are additive sensory confirmation, never primary signaling.** Every haptic event corresponds to an existing visual or audio event; no event is haptic-only. Players without haptic hardware or with haptics disabled lose nothing informationally or mechanically.

**Accessibility-first framing.** The haptic layer's primary audience is hearing-impaired players. Audio-critical gameplay signals (Perfect Block timing, resonance pulse, Godly reveal motif) gain tactile counterparts so the game remains fully playable without audio. This framing shapes every design decision: haptic patterns that duplicate audio rhythm where possible, intensity scaling that matches audio volume dynamics, duration pacing that mirrors audio event lengths. A hearing-impaired player feeling the Perfect Block haptic click should experience the same confirmation that a hearing player experiences through the audio click.

**The three duration tiers.**

- **Combat-event tier (10-120ms):** Rapid feedback for actions and hits. Auto-attack hits, criticals, collisions, status applications, Perfect Block successes. These haptics must not accumulate fatigue during sustained combat — a player landing 10 auto-attack hits per second gets 10 light pulses per second, which the Taptic Engine handles cleanly at low intensity but would become unpleasant at higher intensity. Low-intensity light pulses for high-frequency events.
- **Moment tier (50-120ms):** Medium-frequency consequential events. Grand Slam, Death. These events occur a few times per minute during active combat and warrant stronger haptics without risking fatigue.
- **Cinematic tier (up to 3.5s):** Rare sustained events with their own audio-cinematic framing. Godly reveal Stage 3, Hardness 5 success. These are once-per-session-or-less events; their haptics can extend beyond typical combat-event duration because they won't cause fatigue through repetition.

**Per-event pattern rationale.**

**Auto-attack hit — single short pulse (10ms, light).** The simplest haptic in the game. Confirms the hit landed. The short duration and light intensity let the haptic fire repeatedly during combo sequences without accumulating into a continuous vibration. A player landing a 5-hit combo feels 5 discrete ticks, each corresponding to a specific hit.

**Critical hit — double pulse (10ms gap, 30ms total, medium).** The double-pulse pattern distinguishes criticals from regular hits through rhythm, not just intensity. A player feels the tactile difference between a regular hit (single tick) and a critical (double tick) even in the middle of chaotic combat, which lets them track crit frequency as a textural sense rather than as a visual number on the damage counter.

**Collision triggered — triple pulse (15ms spacing, 60ms total, medium-strong).** Collisions are the game's primary skill-payoff moments. The triple-pulse pattern is distinctive and memorable — a player learns to associate the specific rhythm with collision success, and feeling the triple tick becomes a tactile confirmation that pairs with the visual collision explosion. The slightly stronger intensity (medium-strong) emphasizes collision as a *weighted event* without crossing into fatigue territory.

**Status applied by player — single short pulse (10ms, light).** Identical intensity to auto-attack hits but fires on status application rather than damage landing. The identical-intensity choice is deliberate: from the player's perspective, applying a status is a *setup* action, tactically equivalent to a hit that is also a setup for a future collision. The haptic reflects this — status application feels like a hit, because mechanically it serves the same combat-state-transition purpose.

**Status applied to player — single longer pulse (25ms, medium).** When a status is applied *to* the player, the haptic is *longer* and *stronger* than status-by-player. This inversion creates an immediate tactile signal that "something happened to you" vs "something happened to them." In intense combat where visual status icons might be missed, the haptic intensity difference catches attention at the tactile level — a player feels a longer buzz and knows *they* are now burned/frozen/poisoned.

**Grand Slam (player-dealt) — sustained rolling pulse (120ms, strong, escalating).** The Grand Slam is the game's rare critical-kill spectacle (§4.5 and §1.3). The haptic matches the visual and audio register: a sustained escalating vibration that starts medium-strong and ramps to strong over 120ms, mirroring the screen-shake and particle burst. Unlike combat-event haptics, the Grand Slam earns its duration because Grand Slams are rare — typical players feel maybe 3-5 Grand Slams per hour of active combat, not per second.

**Perfect Block success — double-pulse "click" (20ms total, light-medium).** The v12.27 Perfect Block is a precision-timing action (§5.3b). The haptic is deliberately designed as a *click* — two quick pulses separated by a 5ms gap, reading as a sharp tactile "snap" in the fingers. The click metaphor matches the mental register of Perfect Block: a moment of precise contact, like the snap of a latch. A hearing-impaired player cannot hear the Perfect Block audio confirmation (which is a critical signal for the mechanic's learn-curve); the click haptic provides the same confirmation through touch. This is the single most important accessibility-driven haptic in the registry.

**Godly reveal — Stage 3 sustained vibration (3.5s, medium, wave-modulated).** The Godly reveal is the game's most cinematic moment (§4.6b.2). The 3.5-second sustained haptic matches the Stage 3 audio motif's duration exactly — the haptic and audio swell together and release together. Wave modulation (intensity oscillating in a slow pulse pattern over the 3.5s) produces a *resonating* tactile texture rather than a flat vibration, which matches the reveal's transcendent emotional register. A player experiencing a Godly reveal feels their device *holding* the moment in their hands, which is a deliberate sensory emphasis for the rarest lottery outcome in the game.

**Hardness 5 success — distinctive 4-pulse celebratory pattern (80ms total, medium-strong).** The Hardness 5 forge success (§4.6c) is the mortal counterpart to the Godly reveal. The haptic is a distinctive 4-pulse pattern — four rapid pulses with varying intensity (light-medium-medium-strong) — producing a forge-hammer-like rhythm that feels *constructive* rather than *gifted*. The rhythm is memorable and specific enough that players who witness multiple Hardness 5 successes over their playtime will recognize the pattern and feel the "that's the Hardness 5 haptic" tactile recognition. This specific-pattern approach parallels the differentiated audio motif between Godly (chime from above, grace) and Hardness 5 (strike from below, grit) established in §8.12a.

**Death — single heavy pulse, then silence (50ms, strong).** Player death is the game's most personal failure state (§5.5). The haptic is a single heavy pulse at maximum strength, followed by complete haptic silence throughout the respawn sequence. The silence-after-pulse is as important as the pulse itself — after death, the device goes tactilely quiet while the respawn UI fades in, giving the player a moment of *tactile absence* that matches the narrative weight of losing the character's run. No other haptic event in the registry uses the silence-after pattern; it's reserved for death because death is the only event where the silence carries dramatic meaning.

**Mastery Level crossing (v12.33) — subtle single pulse (15ms, light).** Mastery Level advancement is a v12.33 recognition moment. The haptic is deliberately subtle — a single short light pulse, matching the v12.33 notification register's "acknowledgment, not celebration" discipline. A player crossing a Mastery Level tier feels the light tactile confirmation alongside the visual notification; the haptic does not escalate for higher tiers, preserving the design intent that Mastery Level advancement is informational rather than ceremonial.

**Act-transition acknowledgment (v12.36) — subtle single pulse (15ms, light).** Identical pattern to Mastery Level crossing. When Mayor Bro delivers an act-transition line (§53.6), the haptic fires at the moment the line begins, providing a tactile marker that matches the line's "quiet recognition" register. A player who is not directly looking at Mayor Bro when the line triggers feels the light haptic and knows to turn attention toward the NPC's dialogue bubble.

**No haptic for:** routine movement (walking, running, joystick input), ambient events (zone atmosphere, NPC wandering), UI interactions (menu open/close, inventory drag), economy events (marketplace buy/sell, gold gain), or social events (friend list updates, leaderboard rank changes). Reserving haptics for combat and rare cinematic moments preserves their signal value; haptics on every tap would cause the tactile channel to become indistinct and eventually fatiguing.

**Per-event content authoring.** All 12 haptic patterns live in `haptic-patterns.json` with per-event specifications including the pulse sequence (array of `{start_ms, duration_ms, intensity}` entries), total duration, and accessibility metadata (which audio event the haptic duplicates, for hearing-impaired player context). Content refinement through iteration rather than tuning — the patterns are sensory-output rather than balance-mechanics, so they don't belong in §20 tuning.

**Creative thesis.** The tactile channel is the sense most games neglect, and when mobile devices have perfectly capable haptic motors, neglecting the channel is a missed accessibility opportunity. Bro Town's haptic layer treats the player's hand as a genuine feedback surface — the phone in their grip *responds* to gameplay, not as a gimmick but as a fourth sensory channel alongside visuals, audio, and UI text. Hearing-impaired players gain a path to the game's timing-critical depth. All players who opt in feel combat's recognition moments in their fingers. The discipline — additive sensory confirmation, never primary signaling, never fatiguing, always opt-out-able — ensures the layer enhances without imposing, which is the consistent register of the integration-sequence's recognition-moments design thread applied to the tactile layer.

#### v12.39 addition — grace-collision micro-tick

The v12.39 mana entropy grace period (GDD §3.5a) adds a subtle supplementary haptic: when a collision is a *grace collision* (triggered within the 2-second grace window and thus bypasses entropy for full mana restore), the existing collision-triggered haptic pattern fires as usual AND is immediately followed by a small additional micro-tick (5ms duration, light intensity) with a ~20ms gap between the main pulse and the micro-tick.

The double-event rhythm reads in the fingers as *"that collision got the bonus"* — the main collision triple-pulse lands, followed by a tiny tactile echo that confirms the grace bypass. Players feel the difference between a grace collision (double-event rhythm) and a non-grace collision (single collision pulse, no echo) within their first few sustained combat sessions.

**Why a micro-tick rather than a distinct new pattern.** Adding a fully-distinct 13th haptic pattern for grace collisions would expand the registry at the cost of signal coherence — players would have to learn "grace collision feels like X" separately from "collision feels like Y." The micro-tick approach extends the existing collision haptic rather than replacing it, reading as *"collision + confirmation"* which is exactly the mechanical relationship (collision happened, and it was a grace collision). Pattern-coherence is preserved; the tactile channel communicates the additional state through rhythm extension rather than through pattern substitution.

**Duration budget impact — minimal.** The 5ms micro-tick with a 20ms gap adds ~25ms to the haptic event duration when grace collisions fire. At typical grace-collision frequency (sustained rhythm-paced play produces grace collisions every 2-3 seconds at most, given the COLLISION_MANA_CD(3.0) lower bound), the additional haptic load is well within the duration budget established for combat-event-tier haptics.

**Hearing-impaired player benefit.** During extended gameplay, visual HUD scanning fatigue can cause rhythm-paced players to miss the §1.4.3 green grace tick's countdown arc on occasion. The haptic micro-tick gives hearing-impaired players a secondary confirmation channel that the grace bypass applied — a rhythm-precise player feels the double-event pattern and knows the mana-restore was full without needing to glance at the mana-region HUD to verify.

**Haptic toggle respect.** Players with haptics disabled via Settings → Controls → Haptics lose the micro-tick alongside all other haptic events. The visual grace-tick (§1.4.3) remains visible as the primary channel. Players with haptics enabled can additionally toggle the grace micro-tick specifically via a future per-category toggle if player feedback indicates the additional pattern causes fatigue for any users — for the v12.39 launch, the micro-tick is included under the master haptic toggle without a separate per-event setting.

---

## 3. ZONE PERSONALITY (The Mario Principle)

> "Each zone has highly varied terrain and enemies with distinct personality. No reskins. Different environments demand different strategies."

### 3.1 Implemented Zone Mechanics

**Frozen Shore — Play differently because:**
- Ice physics add slide momentum to movement
- Snowballs give free ranged attacks with stun
- Snowman decoys create tactical positioning (draw aggro while you flank)
- Sled gives burst mobility + damage (3× speed, collision damage)
- Snow auto-collects from walking on drifts — encourages exploration
- *Strategy:* Kite enemies, use snowman as tank, sled for burst/escape

**Tidal Caves — Play differently because:**
- Tide cycle creates time pressure — paths flood and open on 60s rhythm
- Swimming at 40% speed makes water areas dangerous (can't flee effectively)
- Raft removes the penalty — crafting it is an investment in zone safety
- **Underwater diving** adds a vertical dimension — dive below the surface for treasure, manage air meter, risk drowning. Three parallax depth layers create visual immersion.
- *Strategy:* Time your exploration around tide, craft raft early, fish during low tide, dive for treasure during high tide (more water = more dive area)

**Deep Hollows — Play differently because:**
- Near-zero visibility without torch (40px radius)
- Torch extends to 80px but costs wood and burns out in 2 minutes
- Echo mechanic doubles monster aggro range during combat
- *Strategy:* Choose when to fight carefully, torch management is a resource decision, avoid multi-pulls

### 3.2 Planned Zone Mechanics
- Ember Fields: Lava rivers (damage), cooling bridges (frost element interaction)
- Mistwood: Toxic fog (DoT), mushroom trampolines, antidote flowers
- Thunder Peaks: Lightning strikes, updraft gliding, chain lightning
- Sky Reaches: Wind bridges (appear/disappear), paraglider crafting
- Shadow Sanctum: Shadow clones, dark portals, curse altars
- Radiant Heights: Blinding zones, crystal prisms, healing auras
- **Verdant Wilds:** Vine walls that grow and block paths over time (must be cut or burned), carnivorous plants that grab and hold (break free by mashing), bioluminescent fungi that reveal hidden paths in deeper depths, pollen clouds that apply Thorn status to player if walked through
- **Guardian Zones (all 8):** Environmental hazards unique per element (§55.4). Each hazard adds constant pressure — the zone fights back while you fight monsters. Guardian zones feel like the element has become hostile to everything, not just the player.

### 3.3 Zone-Specific Lunge & Retreat Shot Interactions

**Frozen Shore + Lunge:** Ice physics interact with the lunge. The forward dash on ice carries further — ice slide momentum extends the lunge distance by approximately 20%. A Greatsword Bruiser lunging on ice covers enormous ground. This isn't a balance-critical bonus — it's a feel bonus that makes the Frozen Shore's ice physics feel rewarding rather than punishing for melee players.

**Tidal Caves + Retreat Shot:** Retreat shots fired over water tiles produce a small splash VFX at the point where the projectile crosses the water surface. Purely cosmetic. Makes the retreat feel like it's interacting with the zone's reality.

**Deep Hollows + Lunge:** The lunge's arrival impact produces a brief sound echo that matches the zone's echo mechanic. The lunge tells the cave you're here. This is the same aggro-range-doubling echo from §26.3 — the lunge's impact sound triggers it. Lunging in the Deep Hollows is louder than walking in. The zone punishes aggression, which creates a genuine decision: lunge for the setup advantage, but every monster nearby heard you arrive.

**Sky Reaches + Retreat Shot:** Wind-element zones could eventually add a wind-assisted retreat distance bonus (matching the ice-physics lunge bonus). Not specified for v1 — flagged as a future feel enhancement.

### 3.4 Archetype Arrival per Zone (v12.37)

GDD §6.3b defines a progressive per-depth archetype introduction schedule: depth 1 Fodder only, with each subsequent depth adding one archetype (Brute → Swarm → Sentinel → Volatile → Stalker → Hexer) until depth 7, when all seven archetypes are in rotation. Creative direction governs how each zone *feels* as new archetypes arrive at each depth — the archetype's first appearance in a zone is a small atmospheric moment that the zone's tonal palette should support without announcing.

**The design principle: the archetype belongs here.** When a new archetype arrives at its introducing depth, the zone's existing environment should make the arrival feel thematically consistent rather than imported from elsewhere. Ember Fields welcoming its first Brute at depth 2 should read as "the volcanic heat naturally produces a creature with this mass"; Frozen Shore's first Stalker at depth 6 should read as "the silence always had something moving in it; now we see it." The archetype's combat identity (Brute's mass, Stalker's stealth) connects to the zone's atmospheric identity (Ember Fields' geology, Frozen Shore's silence). Each zone × archetype combination should feel native, not universal.

**Per-zone arrival framing.**

**Ember Fields (Flame primary, Stone hybrid at depth 7-8).**
- *Depth 2 — Brute arrives:* a massive lava-crusted Imp-form that moves with the zone's volcanic weight. The Brute's slow patience-teaching gameplay fits the zone's "hostility you have to commit against" register.
- *Depth 3 — Swarm arrives:* Ember Swarm creatures moving in 3-5 packs through heat channels. The Swarm's group-movement fits the zone's vertical heat currents — the pack moves with the atmosphere.
- *Depth 4 — Sentinel arrives:* Flame-attuned Sentinel with Frost resistance (counter-element). A player needing to swap to Frost weapons to defeat it introduces the element-swapping skill against a backdrop of lava.
- *Depth 5 — Volatile arrives:* Magma Volatile explodes into a 30% maxHP Flame AOE on death. Thematically perfect — the zone's volcanic nature produces creatures that embody volcanic behavior.
- *Depth 6 — Stalker arrives:* Heat-shimmer Stalker uses the zone's existing heat-shimmer distortion as visual cover. The zone's heat-shimmer particle effect now has a predator hidden in it.
- *Depth 7 — Hexer arrives:* Ember Hexer applies Burn status at range. Combined with the §14.4 Stone-hybrid content introducing Fracture statuses, depth 7 Ember Fields becomes the zone's status-application peak.

**Frozen Shore (Frost primary, Storm hybrid at depth 7-8).**
- *Depth 2 — Brute arrives:* Ice-Titan form, slow and immense, breath visible as frost clouds. Fits the zone's abandonment register — a huge, patient, indifferent thing.
- *Depth 3 — Swarm arrives:* Ice-Shard Swarm, small frost fragments that move in coordinated waves. Spatial awareness teaching against the zone's ice-slick movement.
- *Depth 4 — Sentinel arrives:* Frost-attuned Sentinel with Flame resistance. Element-swapping taught in the coldest zone against the hottest counter.
- *Depth 5 — Volatile arrives:* Frost-Bloom Volatile explodes into a Frost AOE on death. The zone's cold produces creatures that embody cold.
- *Depth 6 — Stalker arrives:* the silence's shape. Frozen Shore's audio-design already uses 2-3 second silence gaps; the Stalker's arrival makes those silences suspicious. A player learning to lock-on proactively matches the zone's patience discipline.
- *Depth 7 — Hexer arrives:* Hail Hexer applies Slow and Shock (via the §14.4 Storm hybrid) at depth 7-8. Combined with the zone's ice physics already slowing movement, the Hexer compounds positional difficulty.

**Mistwood (Venom primary, Wind hybrid at depth 7-8).**
- *Depth 2 — Brute arrives:* Fungal-Mass Brute, thick with spores and heavy. Mistwood's muffled dense atmosphere produces a heavy thing to match.
- *Depth 3 — Swarm arrives:* Spore Swarm, drifting clusters of small creatures riding fog currents. The zone's fog already moves; the Swarm moves with it.
- *Depth 4 — Sentinel arrives:* Venom-attuned Sentinel with Flora resistance.
- *Depth 5 — Volatile arrives:* Spore-Bomb Volatile releases a Venom cloud on death. Mistwood's already-contaminated atmosphere gets a creature that spreads the contamination further.
- *Depth 6 — Stalker arrives:* Mist-Cloaked Stalker uses the zone's fog as native cover. The zone's fog curtain now actively hides predators.
- *Depth 7 — Hexer arrives:* Miasma Hexer applies Poison status at range.

**Thunder Peaks (Storm primary, Flame hybrid at depth 7-8).**
- *Depth 2 — Brute arrives:* Stone-Colossus Brute, carved of cliff rock, telegraphs attacks with ground-crack VFX. Matches the zone's vertical stone aesthetic.
- *Depth 3 — Swarm arrives:* Static-Flit Swarm, small lightning creatures moving in rapid coordinated flashes. The zone's storm atmosphere produces electric pack behavior.
- *Depth 4 — Sentinel arrives:* Storm-attuned Sentinel with Stone resistance.
- *Depth 5 — Volatile arrives:* Charge-Bloom Volatile releases a Shock AOE on death.
- *Depth 6 — Stalker arrives:* Cloud-Shadow Stalker uses the zone's dense cloud cover to disappear. When storm-cloud shadows pass overhead, something may be in them.
- *Depth 7 — Hexer arrives:* Storm Hexer applies Shock status at range, combined with the §14.4 Flame-hybrid content introducing Burn. Depth 7 Thunder Peaks becomes a two-status-application peak.

**Deep Hollows (Stone primary, Venom hybrid at depth 7-8).**
- *Depth 2 — Brute arrives:* Stone-Bound Brute, slow and massive with rock-scraping movement sound. The zone's claustrophobic acoustic space amplifies the Brute's already-present weight.
- *Depth 3 — Swarm arrives:* Crystal-Scuttle Swarm, 3-5 small crystalline creatures. The zone's sub-bass drone has a new high-frequency texture when a Swarm is present.
- *Depth 4 — Sentinel arrives:* Stone-attuned Sentinel with Wind resistance.
- *Depth 5 — Volatile arrives:* Crystal-Burst Volatile releases a Fracture AOE on death.
- *Depth 6 — Stalker arrives:* Dark-Form Stalker uses the zone's near-zero visibility as native cover. In a zone the player can barely see anyway, the Stalker is the embodiment of the zone's dread.
- *Depth 7 — Hexer arrives:* Rockblight Hexer applies Fracture status at range, combined with §14.4 Venom-hybrid content introducing Poison.

**Sky Reaches (Wind primary, Frost hybrid at depth 7-8).**
- *Depth 2 — Brute arrives:* Altitude-Heavy Brute, grounded movement despite the zone's sparse-air theme. Ironic presence — the Brute is the zone's one heavy thing in a palette of lightness.
- *Depth 3 — Swarm arrives:* Wind-Rider Swarm, small creatures riding air currents between exposed rock platforms.
- *Depth 4 — Sentinel arrives:* Wind-attuned Sentinel with Stone resistance.
- *Depth 5 — Volatile arrives:* Gust-Burst Volatile releases a Wind AOE on death, pushing the player back on the zone's already-narrow paths.
- *Depth 6 — Stalker arrives:* Cloud-Drift Stalker moves through the zone's silence gaps. The zone's 2-3 second silence gaps become predator windows.
- *Depth 7 — Hexer arrives:* Altitude Hexer applies Slow status at range combined with §14.4 Frost-hybrid Freeze.

**Tidal Caves (Water primary, Venom hybrid at depth 7-8).**
- *Depth 2 — Brute arrives:* Tidal-Mass Brute, massive and wave-aligned. Tide cycle enhances the Brute's presence — at high tide the Brute is at its strongest; at low tide it's partially exposed and more vulnerable.
- *Depth 3 — Swarm arrives:* School Swarm, 3-5 small creatures moving in fluid coordinated curves. The zone's rhythm-teaching fits the Swarm's wave-motion.
- *Depth 4 — Sentinel arrives:* Water-attuned Sentinel with Flame resistance.
- *Depth 5 — Volatile arrives:* Bubble-Burst Volatile releases a Soak AOE on death.
- *Depth 6 — Stalker arrives:* Depth-Stalker uses underwater cover, visible only when it breaks the surface to attack.
- *Depth 7 — Hexer arrives:* Tide-Hexer applies Soak + §14.4 Venom-hybrid Poison.

**Verdant Wilds (Flora primary, Water hybrid at depth 7-8).**
- *Depth 2 — Brute arrives:* Growth-Mass Brute, rooted movement with slow telegraphs. The zone's suffocating abundance produces a creature whose mass is vegetative growth.
- *Depth 3 — Swarm arrives:* Pollen-Spire Swarm, small plant-creatures emerging from the ground in coordinated patterns.
- *Depth 4 — Sentinel arrives:* Flora-attuned Sentinel with Venom resistance.
- *Depth 5 — Volatile arrives:* Seedpod-Burst Volatile releases a Flora AOE on death.
- *Depth 6 — Stalker arrives:* Thicket-Stalker uses the zone's dense canopy cover. The zone's abundant vegetation is now actively hiding predators.
- *Depth 7 — Hexer arrives:* Vinemaster Hexer applies Root status at range combined with §14.4 Water-hybrid Soak.

**Shadow Sanctum and Radiant Heights (Dark/Light endgame zones).** These zones exist at depths 9 and 10 respectively per §14. By the time a player enters them, all seven archetypes have been introduced per §6.3b's depth-7 cutoff. No per-depth archetype arrivals occur in these zones — they operate as the all-archetype endgame encounters. Creative framing: Shadow Sanctum's archetypes are all visually shadow-cast, Radiant Heights' archetypes are all visually light-saturated, emphasizing the zones' alignment-endgame identity. No individual archetype gets its own arrival moment in these zones because the archetype system is fully established by depth 7.

**First-encounter notification tonal register.** When GDD §6.3b fires the one-time Codex notification for a player's first encounter with each archetype, the notification's register follows the v12.33 surfaced-mastery-ladder discipline: *brief, observational, informational rather than celebratory, opt-in hint rather than instruction*. Example phrasing:

> *New archetype discovered: Stalker.*
> *Invisible until attacking. Lock-on is the counter — it reveals position early. The Codex has a new entry.*

**What the notification is NOT.** Not a tutorial popup that blocks gameplay. Not a cutscene. Not a celebratory banner. Not a reward. It is a notification that new Codex content is available and a single-sentence tactical hint that respects the player's capacity to internalize the hint without further instruction. The notification fires once per archetype per character, never interrupts active combat (queues if combat flag is set), and is dismissible by tap or 4-second timeout.

**Creative thesis.** The v12.37 progressive archetype introduction is pedagogy through zone-atmospheric native-arrival. Each archetype belongs to each zone it appears in — the Ember Fields Brute feels volcanic, the Frozen Shore Stalker feels like the silence's shape, the Deep Hollows Hexer feels like the rock itself applying Fracture. The player encountering each archetype for the first time in a specific zone gets two simultaneous registrations: "this is a new kind of monster" (system-level) and "this monster is native to this zone" (atmospheric-level). The combination produces a teaching moment that feels authored rather than procedural — the zone and the archetype arrive together as a coherent encounter design, not as two unrelated systems crossing paths.

---

## 4. SOCIAL DESIGN

### 4.1 Core Social Loop
Town is the social hub. Players see each other, chat, emote, trade, duel, inspect. The town is where identity lives — your clan tag, your gear, your reputation.

### 4.2 Emotes as Social Language
20 emoji + 12 text emotes. Key identity emotes:
- "67" — community callsign
- "Gritty" — signature
- "GG" / "RIP" / "F" — universal gaming vocabulary
- 🕺💪🫡🤝 — physical expression

### 4.3 Clan System
Clans are identity groups, not power groups. No stat bonuses. Benefits are social:
- Shared name tag visibility
- Custom 8×8 pixel logo (creative expression hook)
- Shared farm access (social spaces)
- Clan XP / leaderboard (bragging rights)
- **Clan Wars** (§42) — 30-minute PvP events between clans. Zone-based, scored by kills, MVP rewarded. Creates inter-clan rivalries and social narrative.

### 4.3a Life Skill Guilds
10 guilds with NPC guildmasters. Each guild has 9 ranks and 8 quests. The guild system creates sub-communities within the game — the Angler's Circle, the Anvil Brotherhood. Guild titles display on inspect cards, creating another layer of social identity beyond combat stats.

### 4.3b Gladiator Arena + Spectator Betting
The Arena is the game's competitive PvP centerpiece. 100g entry, 10 rounds, single elimination, no healing. The Champion title and 2,000g + 500 AP reward make it aspirational. Spectator betting (blind, 3× payout) turns every match into a communal event — players gather to watch and wager.

### 4.3c Community Feedback
In-game feedback system (📝 button) with 6 categories and 15 topics. Players submit, view, and vote on feedback. Server-side Durable Object stores everything. This closes the feedback loop without leaving the game — players feel heard, devs get structured input.

### 4.3d Leaderboards
Seven categories (Level, Life Skills, AP, Kills, Dungeons, Gold, Playtime) stored in a server Durable Object. Leaderboards create aspiration and social narrative — "who's the highest level?" becomes a community talking point.

### 4.4 Inspect Card
Tap any player to see their full RPG card. This creates a "checking each other out" social moment. Equipment, stats, combat record, clan — everything visible. Transparency builds community.

**Alignment display (v13, v14.0.5 UPDATED).** Below the name/clan row, four independent register counters display — **Responsible / Mischievous / Cool / Ruthless** — each as a small labeled number with no ranking between them and no aggregate alignment label. The four registers are not stages on a gradient; they are separate dimensions of who the character has been. A player might read as "Responsible 47 · Mischievous 12 · Cool 8 · Ruthless 23" and the inspect card presents that as four facts side-by-side rather than as a computed aggregate. The earned register title (Hero / Trickster / Arbiter / Sovereign — or Weaver, v14.0.5, for characters whose registers remain balanced at True Guardian time) renders as a small italic label beneath the register row. Compound and capstone titles earned through branch choices display beneath the register title in small italic text. A player's full story at a glance might read: *"Responsible 47 · Mischievous 12 · Cool 8 · Ruthless 23 — Hero — Forgemaster, Guardian, Virtuoso"* vs *"Responsible 9 · Mischievous 8 · Cool 6 · Ruthless 44 — Sovereign — Scrapper, Vulture, Predator"* — two different characters with different histories and no moral hierarchy rendered between them.

**The Unlikely One title** (Ferryman quest reward) renders in prismatic shifting color — cycling through all nine element colors over 3 seconds. It is the only title with animated color. When other players see it, they know.

**The Weaver title (v14.0.5 NEW).** The Weaver register title — earned by True Guardian characters with no single register strictly exceeding all others per GDD §33.4 — renders in a soft interleaved-gradient color treatment that cycles through the four register colors across a 4-second period (Responsible warm-amber → Mischievous warm-violet → Cool cool-slate → Ruthless dark-crimson → back to Responsible). The gradient transitions are smooth crossfades rather than the Unlikely One's crisp element-cycling, signaling *holding all four registers* rather than *performed versatility*. The Weaver title is visually distinct from each of the four register titles (which render in their single register's color) and from the Unlikely One (which cycles through element colors, not register colors).

**HISTORICAL RECORD — pre-v13 alignment display (superseded by v13, preserved per Editing Protocol step 6):**

> *Alignment display:* Below the name/clan row, a subtle bar shows moral choices as colored dots: gold (●) for Good, grey (○) for Neutral, dark red (●) for Evil. The alignment label (Radiant/Good/Neutral/Dark/Shadow) renders in its corresponding color. Titles earned from capstone branches display beneath the alignment bar in small italic text. This tells the player's moral story at a glance — "Radiant ●●●●●●●● — Forgemaster, Guardian, Virtuoso" vs "Shadow ●●●●●●●● — Scrapper, Vulture, Predator" paints two very different pictures.

The pre-v13 alignment display encoded a ranked-gradient moral hierarchy (Radiant > Good > Neutral > Dark > Shadow) expressed as colored dots and color-coded labels. The v13 alignment rework replaced the five-tier ranked gradient with the four-register model (independent counters, no ranking, no aggregate label) per GDD §0.3 and §33. The v14.0.5 ending expansion added the Weaver title for balanced characters. The pre-v13 body is preserved here for design-provenance and migration-understanding (pre-v13 save data's Radiant/Good counts map to `responsibleCount`; Shadow/Dark map to `ruthlessCount`; Neutral maps to `coolCount`; Mischievous is v13-new and has no pre-v13 equivalent, per Appendix A migration spec).

### 4.5 Friend / Block / Mute
- **Friends:** Online indicators, TP to friends, social panel management
- **Block:** Full interaction shield in lawful zones. **Intentionally disabled in lawless zones** — you chose to enter, you accept all risks.
- **Mute:** Selective chat filtering without full interaction block

---

## 5. ECONOMY PHILOSOPHY

### 5.1 Gold Sinks
- Crafting costs (ore + gold at every tier)
- Gem extraction fees (scales with tier)
- Reforge/harden material costs
- Gambling den (60/40 house odds — net negative for players)
- Jackpot pool (10% house cut)
- Clan creation (500g)
- Ferryman travel (100g)
- **Arena entry fee (100g per tournament)**
- **Spectator bets (10–5,000g, redistribution)**
- **Marketplace order placement (escrow)**
- **Furniture crafting (30–300g per piece)**
- **Cosmetic masks (150–2,000g)**
- **Pet enchantment (50–500g per enchant)**

**HISTORICAL RECORD — Gold Sinks removed/superseded (preserved per Editing Protocol step 6):**

> - *Minigame entry (25g per game)* — superseded v12.45. Farm minigames (Fishing / Cooking / Elemental-Matching Puzzle) are zero-entry hangout activities with no fees and no rewards beyond an earn-once badge. Per §8.54, the hangout framing rejects entry fees because they would reframe the activity as a destination with stakes rather than as social-visit texture.
> - *Dungeon packs (200–1,000g for premium packs)* — superseded v13 §36 rework. The pre-v13 premium-pack model is not part of current canonical dungeon design; the v13 Dungeon Workshop treats packs as content authored by players through friend-validated flows rather than sold through a premium-gold tier.

### 5.2 Gold Sources
- Monster kills (scales with level)
- Quest rewards
- Salvage returns (60% gold recovery)
- Gambling wins (40% chance to double)
- Player trading

### 5.3 Anti-Dumping
The salvage system prevents high-level players from flooding the market with cheap gear. Instead of selling/trading endgame items, players recycle them back into materials at a 40% loss. This keeps materials scarce and crafting relevant at every tier.

---

## 6. MONETIZATION — COST RECOVERY, NOT EXTRACTION

### 6.0 Authority Note

This section is the **creative** companion to GDD §32 (mechanics authority). GDD §32 owns the model specifications — price points, time caps, payment architecture, the no-pay-to-win commitment list, the published cost FAQ, the Paying-Player-Never-Worse principle.
