The ten element signatures visible as distant constellations — each element a loose cluster of stars forming its shape, distributed across the sky in their elemental-wheel configuration but at cosmological scale. The player's gaze up reveals that the chamber is a microcosm of the sky above; the floor's tracery and the sky's constellations mirror each other. This is the only view of the full elemental cosmos in the game.

**The Ferryman.** The Ferryman stands at the chamber's center when the player enters. Oar resting against the floor beside him — not held. He does not greet the player. He waits until they have crossed to the center. When the player arrives, he speaks the framing line without preamble and without dramatization. His dialogue is dry-observational per §8.5a, not grave — he is naming an act, not conducting a ritual. His sprite is the canonical Ferryman at full detail; no special ritual-chamber variant.

**The Rise — ten elements as standing presences.** Per GDD §57.3, the ten elements rise from the floor around the player as standing forms in a ring. Each element's canonical shape and color (§10.1) appears at roughly apex-boss sprite scale (240×240 range), but the elements are NOT combatants — no health bars, no target reticles, no threat indicators. They stand as witnesses. The ring is centered on the player's position with approximately 25u radius; the elements are close enough to feel present but far enough to leave room for the player's gesture.

**The rising sequence — how the ritual teaches its own grammar (v14.0 revised §57.3).** The ten elements do NOT rise simultaneously. They rise **one at a time, in sequence**, each resolving to a standing form before the next begins. The inner eight rise first, in order: Flame, Frost, Water, Venom, Storm, Stone, Wind, Flora. Each element takes approximately 0.8 seconds to resolve — floor tracery brightens in the element's color, the element's canonical shape extrudes upward with the element's signature animation grammar (Flame's licking rise; Frost's crystalline materialization; Water's coalescing rise; etc.), the element's signature audio fires (the zone ambient motif from the element's canonical zone, reduced to a single sustained tone for this moment), the element settles into its ring position. The full eight-element rising sequence runs approximately 6-7 seconds.

After the eight have risen, the ring **holds still for approximately 2 seconds**. The player stands at the center surrounded by the eight inner elements in their elemental-wheel configuration. This is the first canonical moment of eight-at-rest-together — the derivation visible, the fracture's inner shape present but incomplete.

Then **Darkness rises** on one side of the ring — not opposite Light (yet); just settling into its ring position. The Darkness presence is rendered as a standing column of structured shadow, matching the §8.9b canonical treatment of darkness-as-substance (weight and depth rather than absence). Its audio is the Shadow Sanctum deep resonant tone, held sustained. Rise duration matches the inner elements (approximately 0.8 seconds).

Then **Light rises** on the other side — again, settling into its ring position. The Light presence is rendered as a standing column of structured luminance, matching the §8.9b canonical Light treatment (warm-steady, not harsh). Its audio is the Radiant Heights high clarity-tone, held sustained.

The ring is now complete: eight inner elements plus the two primordial poles. The ten are all present at rest together. **Hold for approximately 2 seconds** — the canonical "ten at rest" image, allowed to exist long enough to be felt. Every Crossed player will see this exact configuration. No one else ever will.

Visual grammar of the rising sequence: the player has seen each of the ten elements individually across 100 hours of play (in the eight elemental zones and in the Shadow Sanctum / Radiant Heights endgame zones), and the ritual assembles them into the one configuration the world has never shown. By the third or fourth rising, the player understands what they are inside. The teaching is instantaneous and lived — no UI explanation, no textual hint, just the elements rising one at a time until the ring completes.

Then the elements begin to drift inward. This triggers the painful three-phase recombination.

---

**The painful three-phase recombination — visual direction (v14.0 revised §57.3).**

The 30-second act is NOT a uniform gesture. It is three phases of increasing visual and physical resistance, with the player's input layer progressively working against them. Each phase has distinct visual treatment across all four register gestures.

**Phase A (seconds 0-10): The Eight.** The eight inner elements drift toward the center at uniform, measured speed. Their individual colors remain distinct. The ring contracts smoothly. The player's silhouette per the gesture variant begins to change (translucent for Opening; dense for Consuming; trail-laying for Dancing; sharp-edged for Watching). Darkness and Light stand in their ring positions but do not yet move — they are witnessing what they will become part of. Audio: the voices (§8.17 system) are present as a low layered drone, words not yet clear. The player's input at this phase is steady and achievable; the camera is stable.

Visual register of Phase A: **the familiar work at larger scale**. The player has trained on pairing elements for 100 hours; Phase A asks them to hold all eight at once. Difficult but within the grammar they know.

**Phase B (seconds 10-22): The Band.** At second 10, the eight inner elements visibly collapse into two bands — a band of darkness forming on one side of the player, a band of light forming on the other. The eight elemental colors fold into these two primordial poles: Flame and Venom and Stone's heavier palettes pull into the Darkness band; Water, Wind, Flora, Storm's brighter palettes pull into the Light band; Frost's neutral cold threads between. The two bands begin rotating around the player, resisting each other — their rotation speed is approximately 0.3 revolutions per second, and they visibly want to fly apart.

The player's input now drifts. On mobile, a small visible indicator shows the "true center" and the player's touch point — they have to continuously adjust to hold center. On desktop, the reticle slides off-target under visible pressure; counter-aiming is required to hold. The physics of the input layer itself is now working against the player.

The character's silhouette visibly strains — posture change, shoulders set, the gesture signature still present but under audible and visible duress. Audio: the voices become clear and specific; the player's Journey Log content is pulled into persuasive argument; the drone under the voices thickens with ten-element signatures now fully present. Camera: small, subtle shake at the input layer — the camera drifts in sync with the input drift, reinforcing the struggle at the visual layer.

Visual register of Phase B: **the work the grammar doesn't cover**. Pair-collisions the player trained on do not apply; they are holding two enormous things the world was built by failing to hold.

**Phase C (seconds 22-30): The Closing.** The two bands begin approaching the fold point at the player's center. This is where resistance peaks. The bands' rotation accelerates to approximately 0.6 revolutions per second; their approach speed is visibly accelerating. The input drift is severe — on mobile, the touch point is being pushed hard toward the edges; on desktop, the reticle is yanked strongly enough that untrained players will lose hold.

One of the voices, during Phase C, makes the walkout offer audibly: *"You can still walk out. The Ferryman has said so."* This is the ritual's last genuine offer of refusal, at the moment refusal is hardest.

The character's silhouette has disappeared into the light-and-dark gyre at the center. Only the gesture signature remains visible — the luminous-translucent body of Opening; the dense-absorbing mass of Consuming; the color-trail body of Dancing; the precise-edged body of Watching. These signatures are the only thing the player and observers can see of the character during Phase C's peak.

Audio: voices peak in volume and specificity. The ten-element drone underneath reaches its fullest density — all ten signatures present at clear levels, building toward the recombination chord. Camera: visible shake, proportional to the bands' approach intensity; small chromatic aberration at screen edges signaling the physical scale of what is being held together.

Visual register of Phase C: **the world holding its fracture together through a single committed act**. The player is the thing holding. The bands want to fly apart. The moment is almost unsustainable.

**The Explosion (second 30).** At second 30, if the player has maintained contact through Phase C, the two bands meet at the fold point and detonate. This is NOT a gentle fold-inward. It is a violent recombination — the visual rendering of the force of two things that repel meeting anyway. For one frame, the player's silhouette at the center is visible as the origin point of the explosion — the specific character, the specific Bro, visible at the instant the recombination happens through them. Then the screen whites out.

The white flash is the *force* of the recombination rendered as pure brightness. Hold for approximately 2 seconds. Then the white fades to pure black over 1-2 seconds. The Naming (Phase 8, §8.17 audio direction) proceeds in the pure black.

Visual direction continuity: the explosion's white is NOT a decorative transition — it is the force of recombination rendered as visual event. Authoring discipline: the one frame of the player's silhouette visible before white-out must show the specific character, not a generic silhouette. The specific Bro, the specific gesture, the specific moment they held the bands together — visible for one frame as the origin. This is the game's most intensive single-frame authoring requirement.

---

**Visual direction per register gesture (§57.3), revised for three-phase structure.**

The four register gestures are preserved from initial v14.0 spec, now operative through all three phases:

**Opening (Responsible → Light).** *Phase A:* the player stands still; the eight inner elements flow toward and through the player's silhouette, which becomes increasingly translucent and luminous as each element enters. *Phase B:* the player remains open while the two bands rotate — the silhouette holds translucent but visibly strains to stay open under pressure. *Phase C:* at the moment the bands approach, the silhouette holds open at the center of the gyre; Opening under peak resistance reads as *holding the door open while the world pushes it closed*. The visual register is *suffused with warmth, strained but sustained* — the player is lit from within even as the light shakes.

**Consuming (Ruthless → Dark).** *Phase A:* the player reaches toward each of the eight in sequence as the ring contracts; each element is pulled toward the player deliberately and absorbed. *Phase B:* consumption becomes effortful — the bands resist being taken, and the silhouette visibly *reaches harder* to keep taking them. *Phase C:* consumption peaks at the moment the bands approach; the silhouette becomes a compressed form of shadow-that-has-substance, actively drawing in the collision rather than allowing it. The visual register is *gravitational, intensifying through the phases* — the player becomes a heavier and heavier weight.

**Dancing (Mischievous → Eclipse-warm).** *Phase A:* the player moves through the ring, tracing a pattern of their choosing at their chosen pace. *Phase B:* the dance has to continue under the bands' rotational pressure — the trail of ten-color light behind the player visibly warps and recovers as the bands push at the motion. *Phase C:* dancing at peak resistance reads as *leading a dance against the world's resistance* — the pattern held, the rhythm sustained, the chamber floor now traced with a ribbon of ten-color light that records the player's chosen choreography under pressure. The visual register is *playful and luminous, holding its play under strain* — the Mischievous register's essential quality preserved even in the hardest moment.

**Watching (Cool → Eclipse-reserved).** *Phase A:* the player stands still; the elements converge on a focused point approximately 3u in front of the player, folding together there while the player observes. *Phase B:* watching continues as the bands rotate — the player must not look away even as the pressure mounts; the silhouette remains sharp and distinct, unmoving. *Phase C:* watching at peak resistance is the hardest Cool discipline — the silhouette holds precise stillness while the gyre of light and dark fold together 3u in front of them; the player is the witness the recombination required, unflinching through the moment the world rejoins. The visual register is *architecturally precise, preserved through all three phases* — the Cool player's discipline made visible.

---

**Input-loss during Phase B or C — visual direction (v14.0.2 revised §57.3).**

When a player loses sustained input during Phase B or C (either because they let go or because the resistance overcame their ability to sustain), the ritual visibly stalls. The bands stop rotating. They do not recombine. They slowly drift back outward toward the ring — the ten elements visibly separating as the recombination unwinds. The player's silhouette returns to standard form. No explosion.

The Ferryman approaches the player at the center of the chamber. His line depends on the phase where sustain was lost (per GDD §57.3):

- Phase B input-loss: *"Not this time. Some fractures push harder. Take this."*
- Phase C input-loss (after the voice-offered walkout): *"Walked after all. Some days. Take this."*

He produces an **Unfinished Fragment** — the same artifact produced by a chosen-exit walkout. See §8.15a Unfinished Fragment visual direction. The Fragment's visual treatment does not distinguish chosen-exit from input-loss; both walkout causes produce the same amorphous-neutral crystalline object with the same resume semantics.

**v14.0.2 creative-direction note — Overwhelmed Fragment spec superseded.** An earlier revision (v14.0.1) specified a distinct "Overwhelmed Fragment" visual with single-element-dominant color and broken crystalline pattern, distinct from the Unfinished Fragment. v14.0.2 removed this specification because the underlying GDD mechanic was cut (design review: a consolation "tried and failed" artifact creates one-directional player motivation — "I must go back and replace this" — absent from the Unfinished and Soul Fragments, undermining walkout's genuine-choice framing by offering a face-saving alternative at Phase C's hardest moment). The single Unfinished Fragment now covers both walkout causes; the Ferryman's dialogue carries the distinction at the narrative layer without the visual layer needing to.

---

**The beach — visual direction.** The tutorial beach, reconstructed exactly. Same sprite work, same water motion, same horizon. The player is face-down when they appear; they stand after one second. The view is the same view the tutorial opened on. This visual rhyme is the ending's core move — the player ends where they began, but now the beginning means something it couldn't have meant when they first arrived.

**The walk home — visual direction (v14.0 revised §57.5, §57.9).** The Crossed character's items are NOT on the beach. They are at the farm's home chest (§19.2). The visible journey from beach to farm is the ritual's aftermath rendered as actual travel. The player walks from the tutorial beach through the Meadow (low-level monsters do not aggro a tutorial-visible character — this is quiet terrain for the walk), through town (Mayor Bro does not recognize them, because the Crossed property's NPC recognition beats are tuned to later in the new life per §57.6), through the farm portal. The route is exactly the route a new Bro would take, but performed by someone who has crossed. The atmospheric register of this walk should be *quiet-steady-specific* — no ceremony, no special audio, no camera treatment. The world looks as it always looked. Only the Crossed character is different, and only they know it.

At the farm, the home chest is full. The player's pre-ritual gear is there. The preserved NPCs are there. The museum items from §31 v13 ADDITIVE are on display. The farm is the one place that remembers who they were in full.

A single Journey Log entry appears at the moment the player enters the farm portal post-ritual:

> *You came home. Everything is where you left it. You are not.*

This is the only authored line the game delivers at the farm in the new life. No NPC approaches the Crossed character at the farm with ritual-specific dialogue. The farm is canonically private space per §31, and the game respects that privacy at the moment of return. The farm visit is the player's own — they read what they want to read, they take what they want to take, they leave when they want to leave. The walk home was the ritual's aftermath made physical; the farm visit is the aftermath made personal.

**Mayor Bro on the beach (continued).** Same sprite, same canonical "Another one washed up. They never last." line. He does not recognize the Crossed player — his Phase 1 dialogue register is the default. The almost-remembering moment (GDD §57.6) happens later, around level 30 of the new life, not at this moment. This is correct — the beach is the moment of *complete* reset; any recognition at this moment would soften the sacrifice.

**Authoring scope (v14.0 revised).**

- **Chamber assets.** One zone worth of art: floor tracery, wall textures, ceiling sky with ten-constellation configuration, Ferryman standing animation, entry-from-corridor transition. Estimated one zone-art pass.
- **Element rise animations.** Ten element-presence animations (one per element). Each element's canonical visual (§10.1) in a "standing at rest" state plus a "rise from floor" entry animation. Estimated one week of animator time.
- **Three-phase recombination treatment (v14.0 revised).** Band-formation animation (eight inner elements folding into two bands); two-band rotation-around-player system with escalating rotation speed; fold-point gyre animation; one-frame player-silhouette-at-origin pre-white-out authoring; explosion-as-white-out transition. Estimated 3-4 weeks VFX artist + animator time.
- **Four register gesture treatments, revised for three-phase operation.** Four distinct visual sequences, now with per-phase variations (Phase A steady, Phase B strained, Phase C peaked). Each has per-element and per-band behavior relative to the player. Estimated 4-5 weeks animator + VFX time (up from 2-3 in initial v14.0 spec).
- **Sustain-failure stall sequence (v14.0.2).** When input is lost during Phase B or C, the ritual stalls: bands stop rotating, ten elements drift back outward, player silhouette returns to standard, Ferryman approaches and delivers the phase-appropriate line. Estimated one week animator time. v14.0.1's "Overwhelmed state animation" authoring is folded into this sequence with the walkout-visual-continuity constraint (the stall produces an Unfinished Fragment, not a distinct visual artifact).
- **Pre-elemental whole — three-second beat.** The ten-element interlocked form, rendered at the chamber center for three seconds at highest-fidelity single-asset quality; the art director authors this directly. Estimated one week art director time.
- **White flash and beach transition.** Standard cross-fade infrastructure; estimated 1-2 days.
- **Walk-home atmospheric continuity.** The Meadow / town / farm portal route during the Crossed character's post-ritual walk. No new assets required; existing zone art continues to serve. Atmosphere is the existing zones rendered as they always are; the only new authoring is the Journey Log entry ("You came home. Everything is where you left it. You are not.") appearing at farm portal entry. Estimated minimal (single Journey Log template + event trigger).
- **Chamber authorship traces (v14.0.2 new — see GDD §57.13).** Procedural rendering of past-Guardian authored marks on the chamber's surfaces. Per-mark palette is per-pole (Light marks are gold-warm; Dark marks are shadow-weighted; Eclipse-warm marks shift subtly; Eclipse-reserved marks are precisely-edged). Mark placement is semi-random within chamber surface constraints (no blocking of ritual-critical sprite regions like the Ferryman standing position or the ten-element ring positions). Year 1 chambers have 0-5 marks total; Year 5 chambers have ~50-200 marks; Year 10 chambers approach saturation at ~500-1000 marks. Each mark is small (~1-3u in visual size), signed on close inspection with the Crossed character's name and their pole and server day. Estimated one week engineering + one week art direction for procedural mark rendering and placement.

**The chamber's multi-generational authorship (v14.0.2 new — creative direction for visible traces).** Per GDD §57.13, the chamber was built by past Guardians and continues to accumulate Crossed characters' authored marks in their new lives. The creative direction for these traces:

- **Mark visual vocabulary.** Four canonical mark types chosen at authoring time: a small tracery segment (~1-2u gold line in the author's pole color), a single placed stone in the floor pattern, a curve added to a specific wall section, or a small elemental sigil at a chosen position. Each mark type has four pole-color variants (Light-gold, Dark-shadow, Eclipse-warm-iridescent, Eclipse-reserved-precise-edge). The palette is constrained; the author chooses mark type and location but does not choose color (determined by their own pole).
- **Visible signing.** Each mark is signed on close inspection — tapping/hovering a specific mark reveals the Crossed character's name, their pole, and the server day they authored the mark. This parallels the Godly weapon lineage (§17.4.1) and the Soul Fragment inscription — records of player action are visibly attributable to specific people on the server.
- **Visible history.** A player walking into the chamber for their own ritual walks into a space marked by specific past Crossed characters of their server. They can inspect the marks before beginning their ritual — reading names, seeing when other Bros crossed, noticing which poles dominate their server's authoring history.
- **Aesthetic continuity.** The authored marks must integrate with the chamber's base stonework. They should read as *accumulated authorship* rather than as ego-plaques. The art direction is "maker's trade marks in a cathedral built over generations" — small, earnest, specific, not ornamental. A mark that calls attention to itself is wrong; a mark that rewards careful looking is right.
- **Year 10+ saturation.** When the chamber approaches saturation density (~500-1000 marks depending on chamber surface metrics), the Keeper rejects new authoring requests with the line: *"The chamber is full, this year. Come back if you like."* The saturation cap is a soft limit tuned per server; designer-triggered expansion epochs may add new chamber surface area in future major ships (not specified in v14.0.2).

The chamber authorship visual direction addresses the v14.0.2 lore revision's creative implementation: past Guardians built the chamber, current Crossed characters add to it in their new lives, and the chamber's visible growth over server lifetime is the accumulated contribution of specific crossers. This is the chamber's expression of Brotown's server-memory thread (same pattern as Growing Crystal, Hall of Fragments accumulation, Godly lineage chains).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v14.0 revised): The chamber's "liturgical without being religious" register is the load-bearing creative choice. The game's cosmology is animistic (§8.12b) — no gods except the Ferryman's single acknowledgment — and the ending cannot be a religious rite because there is no religion. But the ending IS a sacred act; reducing it to "a quiet room" would lose the weight. The resolution is ceremonial-without-reverence — the chamber is made, not divine; the Ferryman names the act, doesn't conduct it; the elements stand as witnesses, not as objects of worship. 

The rising-sequence grammar is the ritual's own teaching — eight inner elements rising individually (each with canonical zone audio signature and animation grammar), held in place, then Dark rises, Light rises, held complete for two seconds, then drift-inward begins. No UI explains the structure; the player learns what they are inside by watching the shapes they have spent 100 hours fighting assemble into the configuration the world has never shown. This addresses the structural risk that the ten-at-rest-together image was unprecedented at the ritual — the ritual teaches its own grammar in ~10-12 seconds of rising sequence, so the payoff of ten at rest is both new *and* legible.

The three-phase painful recombination (Phase A, Phase B, Phase C) is the v14.0 revised ending's central authoring payoff and largest departure from the initial v14.0 spec. The initial spec treated the 30-second hold as a uniform gesture with emotional weight supplied by the voices; the revised spec makes the 30 seconds *physical work* at the input layer. Phase A is the familiar work at scale (the player's trained grammar applied to eight elements at once). Phase B is the work their grammar does not cover (eight collapsing into two; the two bands rotating around the player and resisting each other; the input drifting under visible pressure). Phase C is peak resistance (the bands approaching the fold, input severely drifted, voices at peak, walkout offer audibly made). The painful physicality is what makes the voices' arguments land — persuasion against a player whose hands are tired and whose attention is held compounds with persuasion against emotional content alone.

The four per-register gesture treatments are now operative through all three phases rather than as single-phase animations — Opening holds open through strain, Consuming reaches harder through strain, Dancing leads its dance against strain, Watching does not look away through strain. The cosmological pole is legible from the visual alone (warmth for Light, weight for Dark, playful motion for warm Eclipse, architectural precision for reserved Eclipse) at each phase, with the strain-quality progressively increasing. A player watching another player's ritual in a video would be able to tell which register and pole from ten seconds of footage at any phase.

The three-second pre-elemental whole (Phase 7) is preserved as the game's key visual moment — but it is now preceded by 30 seconds of sustained physical work rather than 30 seconds of sustained hold-without-resistance. The explosion at second 30 is earned by the preceding work; the white flash is the *force* of recombination, not a decorative transition. The one-frame player silhouette visible at the explosion's origin is the game's most intensive single-frame authoring requirement — the specific Bro, the specific gesture, the specific moment they held the bands together, visible for one frame as the origin. 

The sustain-failure stall sequence (input-loss during Phase B or C) resolves to the walkout path per v14.0.2 revision — bands stop rotating, elements drift back outward, Ferryman delivers the phase-appropriate line, Unfinished Fragment granted. An earlier revision (v14.0.1) specified a distinct Overwhelmed Fragment visual here with single-element dominant color and broken crystalline pattern. v14.0.2 removed this because a distinct "tried and failed" artifact creates a consolation-participation token that undermines walkout's genuine-choice framing. The Unfinished Fragment's amorphous-neutral visual now covers both chosen-exit walkouts and input-loss walkouts; the Ferryman's dialogue carries the distinction at the narrative layer without the visual layer needing to. This simplification saves ~1.5 weeks of art/animator time while producing a cleaner player-facing artifact set — two Fragments (Unfinished and Soul), not three.

The walk-home atmospheric direction (beach → Meadow → town → farm portal) renders the ritual's aftermath as actual travel rather than instant retrieval. The initial v14.0 spec had items "at the bank" with instant beach retrieval, which produced a theatrical reset; the revised spec places items in the farm's home chest (§19.2, canonical v13 home storage) and makes the walk home the new life's opening beat. The world looks as it always looked; only the Crossed character is different. The Journey Log entry "You came home. Everything is where you left it. You are not." is the single authored line at the farm — no NPC greets the Crossed character with ritual-specific dialogue because the farm is canonically private space (§31). The farm visit is the player's own.

The Mayor Bro Phase 1 line on the beach is the cruelest preserved authoring choice — the character the player was is gone, and the world does not remember them. This is the ritual's sacrifice made concrete. The almost-remembering beat (§57.6) happens later in the new life, at level 30, not at the beach. The Ferryman's "Oh. You're back. Of course you are." on first post-ritual Ferryman interaction is the first recognition the world gives the Crossed character. Recognition is sparse and well-placed.]`

---

#### §8.15 HISTORICAL RECORD — Primordial Visual Direction (SUPERSEDED by v14.0)

The pre-v14.0 canonical Primordial visual direction is preserved below as historical record per Editing Protocol step 6. The Primordial entities (Primordial of Darkness, Primordial of Light) do not exist in v14.0 and are replaced by the ten-element standing ring. The visual philosophy below is NOT canonical in v14.0; it documents the design generation that preceded the ritual framing.

The Primordial encounter was the game's final creative act. Everything built over hundreds of hours — the elemental system, the fracture lore, the alignment choices, the Eclipse mechanic — converged into one solo fight that meant something different to every player based on how they played.

**Visual philosophy (pre-v14.0):** The Primordials are NOT humanoid. They are not demons, angels, gods, or monsters. They are abstract emotional forces given barely-physical form. They should feel like encountering something that doesn't belong in a game — something from a different visual language than anything else the player has seen.

**Primordial of Darkness (Fear):** A void with depth. Imagine a hole in the pixel art itself — not black pixels but the *absence* of pixels. Tendrils of shadow reach out from the void, testing, retracting. Eyes open and close across its surface — not monster eyes but abstract circles of awareness that appear and vanish. At 320×320 it fills a quarter of the visible screen. The player should feel small. The color palette is anti-color — deep purples and blacks that seem to absorb the surrounding pixel art's warmth.

**Primordial of Light (Truth):** A geometric impossibility. Crystalline, folding, rotating — like a 4D object projected into pixel art. Beams radiate from every facet. No face, no features, no anatomy — just form and light. At 320×320 it is overwhelming. The player should feel exposed. The palette is white-gold but pushed to overexposure — pixels that seem brighter than the screen should allow.

**The arenas (pre-v14.0):**
- Hero arena: starts as a void. The player's Light attacks paint the ground with warmth and color. Filling the arena with light literally paints the world back into existence. The visual payoff of 70% illumination is seeing a beautiful, warm space emerge from nothing.
- Sovereign arena: starts as blinding white. The player's Dark attacks paint shadows — cool, deep, protective. Creating shadow cover is literally carving meaning out of noise. Too much truth is chaos. Darkness gives it structure.
- Arbiter arena: split down the middle. One half void, one half blinding. The center line shimmers. Eclipse collisions at the boundary create expanding rings of the dark-and-gold Eclipse palette — the only balanced color in the space. Each Eclipse makes the center zone more livable. By the end, the center is the most beautiful area in the game — a strip of wholeness between two extremes.

`[DESIGN INTENT PRESERVATION NOTE (v14.0): The pre-v14.0 Primordial visual direction was genuinely well-authored — the anti-color Darkness and overexposed Light framings are specifically memorable, and the three alignment arena treatments (void-painted-into-warmth, light-shadowed-into-structure, split-arena-bridged-by-Eclipse) contained strong visual ideas. v14.0's ritual chamber replaces this framing not because the prior direction was bad but because the ending's design thesis changed. The v14.0 ritual chamber inherits one visual idea from the prior direction: the Arbiter arena's "center boundary of Eclipse color as the most beautiful strip in the game" maps to the v14.0 ritual chamber's "pre-elemental whole — white with depth, all ten colors at equal weight" — the principle of "wholeness is the rarest visual register" carries forward.]`

### 8.16 Soul Fragment Sentence Authoring Guide (v14.0 REPLACES Final Echo Stone guide; v14.0.3 NOTE)

`[v5.41 NOTE]` §8.16's inscription authoring guide carries forward to v14.0.3 unchanged in its structural content. The inscription flow, the "Someone" template discipline, the 120-character limit, the canonical-four-reserved-for-Naming rule, and the register-voiced examples all apply to v14.0.3's Fragment exactly as authored for v14.0. The only relevant update is at the Fragment-visual layer: v14.0.3 collapses v14.0's four-variant Fragment shapes into the unified grip-at-base + crystalline-upper structure (see §8.15a v14.0.3 direction). The inscription is still rendered in Echo Stone typography on the Fragment's interaction overlay; the interaction surface, visual treatment, and speech-act framing are unchanged. The "preview as Fragment inscription" button in the authoring UI now previews the sentence against the unified Fragment structure rather than against a pole-specific visual variant — small UI update, no content change.

The Soul Fragment's one-sentence inscription is the last piece of content each player will ever produce OR discover about themselves. Per GDD §57.5, the player at ritual completion chooses between authoring their own inscription (120 character max, Echo Stone discipline) or accepting a game-generated sentence drawn from their Journey Log in their register's voice. Both paths are equally valid; the guide below covers authoring discipline for both.

**Rules for Soul Fragment inscriptions (both player-authored and game-generated):**

- **One sentence.** Same rule as every Echo Stone (§8.3). No exceptions. No paragraphs, no multi-sentence constructions, no numbered lists.
- **It must be specific.** Generic aphorisms ("I lived well" / "The journey was long") do not qualify. The sentence must reference a specific journey — a specific zone, a specific choice, a specific register's voice, a specific moment the player can point to.
- **It must be quiet.** No exclamation marks. No grandiosity. The quieter the sentence, the harder it hits. Per Echo Stone discipline (§8.3): "One sentence. No exclamation marks. Understatement over overstatement."
- **It must be legible to strangers.** The inscription will be read by players who never met the Crossed player. The sentence must communicate meaning to someone who does not know the specific journey — the specificity should evoke the journey's shape without requiring knowledge of its details.

**Player-authored inscriptions.** The player at ritual completion is offered a text input with 120-character limit and a preview of the Fragment's visual (the player sees their authored sentence on the Fragment they are about to produce). The authoring UI includes:

- Character counter showing remaining characters.
- Register-specific tonal hint based on the player's dominant register: Responsible players see "Your voice is earnest with self-aware edge"; Mischievous players see "Your voice is warm-witted"; Cool players see "Your voice is dry-observational"; Ruthless players see "Your voice is hard-direct."
- A "preview as Fragment inscription" button rendering the sentence on the Fragment's visual.
- Content-filter validation per §58 discipline (no profanity, no impersonation of NPCs, no obvious test-strings like "test" or "asdf").

Once confirmed, the inscription is frozen per INV-34 (GDD §22). The player has one chance to write it; editing after creation is not permitted (except for the 30-day v14.0-migration window for pre-v14.0-Crossed characters, §57.11).

**Game-generated inscriptions.** For players who prefer not to write or who feel uncertain, the game generates a candidate sentence from their Journey Log, drawing specific nouns and moments and framing them in the player's register voice. The player can accept or decline the generated sentence; if they decline, they author their own. If they also decline authoring, the ritual simply produces an "Unsigned" Fragment with a safely-generic register-voiced template sentence drawn from the Journey Log pool — the fallback is always graceful, never punitive. The canonical four v13 sentences are explicitly NOT in the fallback pool; they are reserved for the Naming delivery (Phase 8, see GDD §57.4a and §8.17) and cannot be produced as Fragment inscriptions.

**Register-voiced examples (game-generated):**

*Responsible (Light pole).* Drawing from: protection-heavy capstones, consistently-chosen "help the stranger" branches, Herbalist quest completions.
- *"Someone walked through here refusing to leave anyone unhelped. You can feel that here."*
- *"Someone held the line at the Deep Hollows until every child got home. It's remembered."*
- *"Someone's shadow is still at the Blacksmith's door, waiting to see if the hammer swung true."*

*Ruthless (Dark pole).* Drawing from: PvP records, hard-choice capstone branches, Lawless Land traversals.
- *"Someone came back from the wasteland each time. They didn't say why. This is where they didn't say it."*
- *"Someone made the hard choice at the Herbalist's door. The right one. They never spoke of it."*
- *"Someone did not flinch. You can feel where they did not flinch. It is here."*

*Mischievous (Eclipse-warm pole).* Drawing from: player-generated Echo Stones, emote-heavy social surfaces, mischief-capstone branches, Trickster Mimicry moments.
- *"Someone left half the town laughing. The laugh is still traveling."*
- *"Someone took the long way through the Frozen Shore. They are still laughing about it."*
- *"Someone taught the Ferryman a joke. He did not laugh. But he remembered."*

*Cool (Eclipse-reserved pole).* Drawing from: observational-branch capstones, non-engagement-of-bait choices, long-distance travels, witness-register moments.
- *"Someone walked past the same door you'll walk past. They didn't go in either. They were right."*
- *"Someone kept their distance from the fight until it mattered. When it mattered, they were there."*
- *"Someone saw what needed to be seen. They did not announce it. It was seen."*

**The canonical v13 Final Echo Stone sentences are NOT available as inscription options (v14.0 REVISION per GDD §57.4a Naming addendum).** The four canonical sentences —

- *She held the dark until it remembered it was part of something.* (Hero / Light)
- *He stared into the light until it blinked.* (Sovereign / Dark)
- *They held both hands until the hands remembered they were the same body.* (Arbiter / Eclipse-reserved)
- *They laughed until the dark forgot why it was serious.* (Trickster / Eclipse-warm)

— are delivered exclusively once, in the Naming (Phase 8, GDD §57.4a), as the game's own voice about the player's ritual in the pure black between white flash and beach. The canonical four are not in the Fragment inscription pool, neither as selectable options nor as fallback defaults. A player whose journey closely rhymes with a canonical register pattern can author a close variant themselves — distinct words, same register — as their Fragment inscription; the direct canonical four are reserved for the game's voice, not the player's. This is a structural separation of speech acts: the Naming is the game speaking about the player in third person (the world naming what the ritual did); the Inscription is the player speaking in "Someone" register to future strangers (the Fragment as externalized record). Conflating the two by allowing the canonical four as inscriptions would blur both. A player who wants their own voice to land like *"He stared into the light until it blinked"* writes something in the same register — say, *"Someone walked toward the light and did not look away"* — which rhymes, acknowledges the canonical sentence's authority, and is the player's own act of authorship rather than an appropriation of the game's.

**Authoring notes for game-generation templates.**

The generated-sentence templates (authored by the narrative designer) follow a consistent pattern:

1. **"Someone" as the actor.** Not "I" (too direct), not "you" (breaks the Fragment's stranger-reading legibility), not "they" (ambiguous referent). "Someone" carries the right distance — the Crossed player is a specific person who did specific things, but the Fragment is being read by a stranger, and "someone" honors both.
2. **A specific reference to a Journey Log event.** The noun is pulled from the Journey Log: zone name, NPC name, item name, or event type. The template's job is to make the specific reference feel inevitable in context rather than bolted-on.
3. **A closing beat that recontextualizes.** The second half of the sentence reframes the first — "they did not say why. This is where they didn't say it" — the Fragment's location becomes part of the sentence's meaning.

**Authoring scope.** Approximately 100 register-voiced sentence templates across the four registers, each with Journey-Log fill-in slots. The narrative designer produces the 100-template set plus the register-voicing guide. Generation-time composition is O(1) — template selection weighted by Journey Log density in relevant fields, fill-in from current values, no blocking work on the ritual-completion pipeline. **The canonical four sentences are not in this pool**; they are authored separately for the Naming delivery and do not appear in Fragment inscriptions.

**The deepest-register rule.** The generated sentence should draw from the register the player *lived* most, not merely the register their capstone choices resolved to. A player who nominally resolved to Responsible but whose Journey Log is dense with Mischievous moments should get a Responsible-voiced sentence that *acknowledges* the Mischievous thread in its fill-ins ("Someone did the serious work with a grin. You can feel that here.") rather than a generic Responsible sentence. This is the discipline that makes the Fragment feel earned rather than assigned.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v14.0): The Soul Fragment inscription is the ending's most player-expressive surface. Two paths are provided because different players have different relationships to authoring — some want to write their own last sentence and want the blank page; others want the game to produce something good for them and not have to perform the last authoring act. Providing both with equal dignity is the correct move. The "Someone" template discipline is the strongest authoring choice in the v14.0 ending — it carries the correct distance for a Fragment-being-read-by-strangers and preserves Echo Stone's third-person-quiet register. The canonical v13 sentences are NOT preserved as inscription options (v14.0 REVISION per GDD §57.4a Naming addendum). The initial v14.0 spec had preserved them in the pool, which buried the game's best writing as optional content a player might or might not encounter. The Naming addendum corrects this: the canonical four are delivered once, in the game's own voice, in the pure black between white flash and beach — their authored role as the ending's last words, restored. Separating Naming from Inscription as two distinct speech acts (game voice vs player voice; third-person-"they" vs third-person-"Someone") makes both stronger than either could be if conflated. The deepest-register rule prevents the ending from feeling mechanical — the Fragment reflects who the player *was* in play, not just who they resolved to at the capstone level, and the inscription should carry the evidence. The 120-character limit matches Echo Stone discipline (§8.3) and prevents the common "player writes a paragraph" failure mode; the constraint is structural rather than stylistic, and the creative direction is that the constraint produces better writing by forcing selection. The content-filter validation is thorough because the Fragment is permanent and public — inscription is the one author-time decision that must be held to high-quality standard because it cannot be retracted later per INV-34. Post-success silence matches the v12.44 creative direction — the ritual completes, the Naming sentence is delivered once, the Fragment is created, the player is on the beach. No congratulations. No "your Fragment has been placed!" announcement. The Fragment simply IS, and the player discovers it on their own terms, by visiting the zone they chose or walking the Hall. The game does not narrate its own achievement of producing the Fragment; the Fragment is the achievement.]`

---

#### §8.16 HISTORICAL RECORD — Final Echo Stone (SUPERSEDED by v14.0)

The Final Echo Stone is preserved below as historical record per Editing Protocol step 6. The v14.0 Soul Fragment supersedes the Final Echo Stone's discover-and-read-a-stone mechanic; the four canonical v13 sentences themselves are preserved in their authored role as the ending's last words via the Naming (Phase 8, GDD §57.4a) — delivered once, in the game's voice, in the pure black between white flash and beach.

The Final Echo Stone is the last piece of content the player will ever discover. It must be the best-written sentence in the game.

**Rules for the Final Echo Stone:**
- One sentence. Same rule as every other Echo Stone. No exceptions.
- It must recontextualize the entire game. The player should think of the very first zone they explored and see it differently.
- It must be specific to the player's alignment path — not a generic platitude.
- It must be quiet. No exclamation marks. No grandiosity. The quieter the sentence, the harder it hits.

**The three Final Echo Stones (canonical pre-v13):**

**Hero:** *"She held the dark until it remembered it was part of something."*

The "she" is unnamed — could be the player, could be a previous Guardian, could be the world itself. "Held" not "fought" — the Hero doesn't destroy darkness, they embrace it. "Remembered it was part of something" — the fracture was forgetting. The Hero's gift is reminding.

**Sovereign:** *"He stared into the light until it blinked."*

The Sovereign doesn't look away. Doesn't cover their eyes. Doesn't submit. They stare at truth itself until truth flinches. "Blinked" — the light didn't go out. It just acknowledged, for one moment, that something could meet it without breaking. The Sovereign's gift is endurance.

**Arbiter:** *"They held both hands until the hands remembered they were the same body."*

"Both hands" — the two Primordials, the two halves. "Held" — not forced, not commanded. Held. The Arbiter's role was never to fight either side. It was to hold them together long enough for them to recognize each other. "The same body" — the fracture was always one thing pretending to be two. The Arbiter's gift is patience.

`[DESIGN INTENT PRESERVATION NOTE (v14.0): The pre-v13 three Final Echo Stone sentences are among the best-written lines in the game, and the v13 Trickster addition extended the set to four with "They laughed until the dark forgot why it was serious." All four are fully preserved in v14.0 in their authored role as the ending's last words — delivered via the Naming (Phase 8, GDD §57.4a), in the game's own voice, in the pure black between white flash and beach, pole-matched to the player's register. The initial v14.0 spec had relegated them to "inscription options in the Soul Fragment pool," which the v14.0 Naming addendum corrected: the sentences were authored as the ending's last words and must land as the ending's last words, not as filler options a player might or might not select. The v14.0 rework supersedes the discover-and-read-a-single-stone mechanic in favor of a delivered-statement mechanic — the player does not find the sentence; the sentence is spoken to the player, once, by the game. This is the stronger framing because the ending's register is sacrificial-cosmological, not archaeological. The writing quality of the canonical four serves as the quality benchmark for both the 100 auto-generated template set used for Fragment inscriptions and the player-authored inscription discipline. The canonical four are not in the Fragment inscription pool — that separation keeps the Naming (game voice) and the Inscription (player voice) as structurally distinct speech acts.]`

### 8.15a Soul Fragment — The Ritual's Public Artifact (v14.0.3 REPLACES v14.0 four-variant direction)

The Soul Fragment is the ending's public artifact — the permanent record of a Crossed player's ritual completion, visible to every player on the server forever. Per GDD §57.7a and §57.9, v14.0.3 collapses the v14.0 four-variant visual direction (four distinct Fragment shapes per pole) into a **single unified visual structure**: a rusted grip at the base, embedded in the chamber stone or the Hall floor, with a crystalline form growing upward through the corroded metal in the Fragment-owner's pole color, taking the silhouette of the player's lifetime-dominant weapon type. Pole is expressed through the crystalline form's color and living behavior; structure is shared across every Fragment.

This structural unification is load-bearing for the §0.4 unification thesis at the memorial layer. A Year-5 Hall with four distinct Fragment shapes per pole would read as four categories of Bro; a Year-5 Hall with one shape per pole-color would read as *every completer held the same thing and each one made it their own*. The GDD v14.0.3 §57.9 states this directly: *"we all arrived with nothing; we all held our origin alongside what we became; we all crystallized the holding into this form."* The visual direction must honor the shared structure.

**Visual philosophy.** The Soul Fragment is NOT a trophy, a relic, or an achievement icon. It is the externalized form of the player who produced it — what the ritual made of them, rendered in rust-and-crystal-and-pole-color. A player walking past a Fragment should feel they are walking past someone specific who crossed; the Fragment's presence carries the same register as a gravestone but without the register of death. The player who produced the Fragment is *still here*, continuing to play; the Fragment is what they left behind of who they were before the ritual.

**The unified Fragment structure (v14.0.3 canonical).**

Every Fragment is built from three visual elements, rendered in the same structural relationship across all completers:

- **The grip (base).** The rusted first-weapon grip from the ritual, exactly as it appeared in the player's off-hand during the chest-cradle hold. Material visible (bronze / iron / copper), repair-count notches visible (or pristine per the untouched-grip whimsy, §8.15b). The grip sits at floor level, embedded in the stone — not resting on it, but continuous with it, as if the floor has grown up around the grip's lower inch or two. Height: approximately 0.6u. The grip's register is *specific origin* — this is a specific Bro's specific first weapon, and the material/notches/pristine-or-notched state is the Fragment's identity layer.
- **The crystalline form (upper).** A crystal growing upward through the corroded rust, taking the silhouette of the player's lifetime-dominant weapon type (greatsword-broad, sword-narrow, bow-arced, staff-tall-and-slim, etc., per `weapon_type_hours` dominant). Height: approximately 1.4-1.6u depending on weapon type silhouette, so the full Fragment (grip + crystal) totals roughly 2.0-2.2u — about half the player's character height. The crystal's color is the player's pole color per §10.1 canonical palette (Light = warm gold; Dark = deep red-violet; Eclipse-warm = shifting gold-and-dark; Eclipse-reserved = held gold-and-dark boundary). The register is *what the holding became* — the specific weapon silhouette the player's hours produced, rendered in the register they resolved to.
- **The seam (continuity layer).** Where the crystal meets the grip, the crystal visibly emerges *through* the corroded metal rather than sitting adjacent to it. The rust is pitted at the seam; mineral creep is visible across the first 2cm above the grip's top edge — fine filaments of pole-colored crystal webbing into the rust's surface. Per GDD §57.7a: *"the Fragment is not adjacent to the grip — the crystal visibly emerges through the corroded metal."* The seam is the Fragment's load-bearing creative detail. A Fragment rendered with a clean boundary between grip and crystal fails the direction; a Fragment rendered with visible mineral-through-metal continuity succeeds.

`[v5.41 INTERPRETIVE CALL — review needed]` The GDD §57.7a describes the seam's structural requirement but does not specify its visual rendering density. The v5.41 proposal: mineral creep is visible across the first 2cm above the grip's top edge as *fine filaments* (1-2 pixel-width veins of pole-colored crystal spreading into the rust's surface), with approximately 6-10 visible filaments per Fragment at close inspection (one-meter range in the Hall). The filaments should be individually distinct rather than a uniform band of color — the register is "crystal growing into metal across specific points" rather than "crystal dipped into metal uniformly." This interpretive call is load-bearing because it affects how the Fragment reads at close range: uniform-band rendering would read as "crystal and grip are adjacent, joined by a color transition zone," while per-filament rendering reads as "crystal has grown into and through this specific grip's specific surface." The distinction is what the GDD design intent requires. Alternative renderings (uniform band, sharp boundary with glow, progressive color gradient) should be evaluated against "the crystal visibly emerges through the corroded metal" — emerges is the operative word, and per-filament rendering is the emergence made visible.

**The five pole-color treatments (v14.0.3 REVISED from v14.0 four-variant direction, v5.42 EXPANDED with Convergence).**

The crystalline upper form carries pole-specific color and living behavior. Structure is identical across poles; color and behavior differentiate.

- **Light Fragment (Responsible / Hero pole).** Crystal is warm gold-white, semi-transparent, with steady inner glow. Light passes through the crystal's interior and catches in the upper silhouette's geometry, producing a faint warm halo of illumination around the Fragment — the Fragment is a small held source of light in the zone. The glow is *steady*, not pulsing — the crystal does not blink, flicker, or vary; it holds. The register is *sustained warmth*. Color reference: the Radiant Heights high clarity-tone rendered at sustainable brightness levels (not the v13 Primordial overexposure).
- **Dark Fragment (Ruthless / Sovereign pole).** Crystal is deep red-violet with slow internal light. The exterior is opaque — not empty black but substantial deep-color, like obsidian with mass. Internal light is a slow pulse of deep red-violet, traveling through the crystal's interior volumes every 3-4 seconds. The register is *weight* — the Fragment sits heavily in the zone's space, drawing the eye by substance rather than brightness. Color reference: the Shadow Sanctum deep resonant palette integrated into a sustainable artifact.
- **Eclipse-warm Fragment (Mischievous / Trickster pole).** Crystal shimmers between gold and dark in a living rhythm. Not pulsing on a timer — the colors *drift* through the crystal like clouds moving across a surface, producing a visual texture that is always slightly different moment to moment. The shimmer is warm: the dark passages carry a gold edge; the gold passages carry a dark undercurrent; neither pole fully wins. The register is *playful vitality* — the Fragment is alive in a way the other three pole-treatments are not, and a player watching for ten seconds notices the shimmer movement's irregularity. Signature for players who brought the warm-mischievous register through.
- **Eclipse-reserved Fragment (Cool / Arbiter pole).** Crystal carries a precise gold-dark boundary held perfectly still. The crystal's interior is split by an exact mathematical boundary between gold and dark — one half one color, one half the other — and the boundary does not move. The register is *architectural clarity* — the Fragment is held in a specific configuration, and the player knows by looking that nothing will shift in this Fragment's presentation regardless of how long they watch. Signature for players who brought the dignified-observational register through; the boundary is not a conflict but a held distinction.
- **Convergence Fragment (balanced / Weaver pole) — v5.42 NEW.** The Convergence pole is not a fifth color treatment; it is the **sequential cycle** of the four pure treatments across the Fragment's standard 15-second rotation, each treatment holding for 3.75 seconds before transitioning to the next in canonical order: Light → Dark → Eclipse-warm → Eclipse-reserved → (loop). During each 3.75s window the Fragment renders *exactly as* the corresponding pure pole-treatment — the Light window is a Light Fragment (warm gold glow, halo, steady inner light); the Dark window is a Dark Fragment (deep red-violet, substantial, slow pulse); the Eclipse-warm window drifts with warm shimmer; the Eclipse-reserved window holds its precise boundary. The transitions between windows are **crisp cuts, not crossfades** — the Fragment does not blend Light into Dark; it holds Light fully, then holds Dark fully. A visitor watching a Convergence Fragment for a full 15-second cycle sees each pole's identity delivered in sequence and knows, without the overlay opening, that this Fragment belongs to a Bro who held all four registers rather than resolving to one. The register is *visible refusal to collapse* — the Fragment refuses, moment to moment, to settle into any single pole, and the refusal itself is the Fragment's identity. This matches the §33.5 v14.0.5 Complete Eclipse direction at the Fragment scale: sequential holding rather than synthesis. Signature for Weaver-ending Bros.

**Why the Convergence pole uses sequential cycle rather than a fifth unique color.** A fifth pole-color (a new prismatic or blended hue) would have been the natural instinct but would have failed the thesis the same way the v13 "four variants layered together" Complete Eclipse failed it — a blended fifth color resolves the four registers into a composite, subsuming them into a single new identity that is not any of the four. Sequential cycling preserves each pole's full identity across its 3.75s window and expresses the Weaver's balance as a temporal structure rather than a spatial blend. This is also a significant authoring-scope saving: no fifth pole-color asset is required; the existing four treatments are composed into the cycle at render time. The only new engineering is a scene-graph timing layer that switches the rendered treatment every 3.75s. See GDD §57.12 v14.0.5 for the canonical additive rendering note.

`[v5.42 INTERPRETIVE CALL — review needed]` Transition rendering between the four treatments within a Convergence Fragment's cycle. The v5.42 proposal: the transition is a single-frame cut (one render frame at ~16.67ms, below the threshold of visual blending) with no intermediate state. Alternatives considered but rejected: (a) a short crossfade between treatments (rejected because it would reintroduce the blending the thesis rules out, producing a brief moment where the Fragment is "half Light and half Dark" rather than "holding Light, then holding Dark"); (b) a visible "reset pulse" between treatments (rejected as ceremonial and heavy — the cycle should read as natural holding-then-holding, not as an announced transition). The crisp-cut approach produces the cleanest "refusal to collapse" reading. If playtest finds the crisp cut jarring (early-review framing: "is this a rendering bug?") rather than legible (target framing: "oh, this Fragment is cycling through the poles"), revisit with a minimum-viable transition (~80ms linear interpolation, no mid-blend dwell). Do not exceed 150ms of transition time under any revision — beyond that threshold, the transition reads as blend and the thesis is violated at the Fragment layer.

**Shared rotation behavior (v14.0.3 unified from v14.0's per-pole variation, v5.42 extended for Convergence).** All five pole-color Fragments share one rotation behavior: slow rotation on a vertical axis, approximately one full rotation per 15 seconds. The rotation is steady across Light and Dark Fragments. Eclipse-warm Fragments carry slight rotation irregularity (small pauses and accelerations, approximately ±10% period variation). Eclipse-reserved Fragments rotate with mechanical precision (exactly 15-second period, no variation). Dark Fragments rotate steadily but with the deep internal pulse adding a second temporal layer. Light Fragments rotate steadily with the steady halo. **Convergence Fragments rotate on the same 15-second axis, with the axial rotation providing a second temporal layer orthogonal to the 3.75s pole-cycle.** Through one 15-second rotation a Convergence Fragment passes through all four pole treatments in sequence; through a second rotation the cycle begins again in the same canonical order. The axial rotation itself is steady (no per-pole variation in rotation speed during the cycle) — the pole cycling is the Convergence Fragment's temporal signature; the rotation is its structural continuity. Visitors reading a Convergence Fragment at any single moment see one pure pole-treatment rotating steadily; visitors reading it for 15+ seconds see the cycle unfold. This two-layer temporal structure (steady rotation + cycling pole) is the Convergence Fragment's unique living-behavior and is its load-bearing rendering detail.

`[v5.41 INTERPRETIVE CALL — review needed]` The v5.41 proposal unifies rotation behavior to a common baseline (15s period, steady) with per-pole minor variations (Eclipse-warm irregularity, Eclipse-reserved precision) rather than preserving v14.0's more differentiated per-pole rotation direction. This interpretive call serves the v14.0.3 structural unification — a Year-5 Hall full of Fragments rotating at broadly the same rate reads as *shared structure, individual color*, while rotation differentiation would re-introduce the four-variant register v14.0.3 collapsed. If team review judges the v14.0 per-pole rotation direction (15s shared period but with signature motion per pole) more structurally correct for the unified direction, the variations above can be tuned to preserve more per-pole motion identity. The direction should err toward unification rather than differentiation — motion is a secondary differentiator; color and living-behavior are the primary.

**The five pole-color treatments are instantly distinguishable at a glance.** A player in a zone with multiple Fragments present can tell which pole each Fragment resolved to from peripheral vision alone — by color first, motion second. This is load-bearing — the Fragment is a record of a specific person's specific completion, and pole legibility is the first layer of that record. The four pure poles (Light / Dark / Eclipse-warm / Eclipse-reserved) are distinguished by color, motion, and boundary-behavior; the Convergence pole is distinguished at the cycle layer — a visitor watching a Fragment for more than a few seconds notices that its color register has changed, which is the Convergence pole's unmistakable signature. A player who has seen one Fragment of each pole develops an immediate visual vocabulary: four pure identities plus one cycling identity, five legible reads.

**Inscription display — interaction surface.** Fragments are interactable objects. Approaching a Fragment (within 3u) highlights it with a faint aura in the pole's color; interaction button reveals the inscription overlay. The overlay is minimalist:

- **Signed name or "Unsigned"** — top line, in the player's chosen display text if signed, or the explicit "Unsigned" if anonymous.
- **The inscription sentence** — center, in Echo Stone typography (matching §8.3). The sentence is the visual focus; the overlay is framed around it.
- **Metadata line** — bottom, small: server day number, register, cosmological pole, walkouts-before-completion if any. Example: *"Day 847 · Mischievous · Eclipse (warm) · 2 walkouts before."*
- **Cohort readout (v14.0.3 addition).** A small indicator of grip material — *"bronze starter"* / *"iron starter"* / *"copper starter"* — naming the completer's cohort per the Hall's grip-material-as-server-timestamp structure (§8.15b, GDD §57.9 non-negotiable constraint). This is not framed as rank or prestige; it is a historical marker like the server day number.

No stat display, no gear history, no achievement list. The Fragment is a memorial, not a profile. Players who want stat/achievement data have other surfaces; the Fragment does not compete with those.

**The visit log and signing (preserved from v14.0).** After reading an inscription, the visitor is offered the opportunity to sign their visit. The prompt is simple: *"Leave your mark? (Name will appear in the owner's visit log.)"* — Yes / No / Cancel. No is the default. Yes records the visitor's display name in the Fragment's visitor log (per GDD §57.10). Anonymous visits still log but without identifying data.

**Living-Fragment behaviors (preserved from v14.0, per GDD §57.10).**

- **Dim on owner logout (FRAGMENT_DIM_ON_LOGOUT = 0.85).** The Fragment's brightness multiplier drops to 85% when the Crossed player is not currently online. The dim is subtle — not an alarming "is the player dead?" shift, but a noticeable-if-you-know-to-look softening. The visual register is: the Fragment is *present* when its owner is present, and *quiet* when its owner is elsewhere.
- **Pulse on owner death.** When the Crossed player dies in normal combat (not the ritual), their Fragment produces one soft pulse in the pole's color. The pulse is silent — no sound accompanies it. The pulse is visible to any player in the Fragment's zone. The pulse duration is approximately 2 seconds, fading smoothly back to normal brightness. This is the only Fragment behavior that can happen without the Crossed player being online in the specific zone.
- **Owner-proximity brightening.** When the Crossed player stands within interaction range (3u) of their own Fragment, the Fragment brightens slightly and its rotation pattern gains a small flourish (rotation rate briefly accelerates and settles). This is the "Fragment recognizes its owner" behavior — a moment of private acknowledgment between the Crossed player and their Fragment.

**Authoring scope (v14.0.3 REVISED, reduced from v14.0).**

- **Unified Fragment visual structure.** One Fragment geometry (grip at base + crystalline upper silhouette), parameterized by: grip material (bronze / iron / copper), grip notch count (0-N per repair history), grip pristine-vs-notched state, upper silhouette (per weapon type, ~10-15 variants per the weapon taxonomy), pole color (4 variants). Total authored geometry: one base structure + parametric variation. Estimated 1.5-2 weeks animator + VFX time (**reduced from v14.0's 2 weeks for four distinct variants**).
- **Seam rendering.** Per-filament mineral-creep authoring per the interpretive call above. 6-10 filaments per Fragment, individually rendered rather than procedural-band. Estimated 1 week VFX artist time.
- **Four pole-color treatments.** Light (warm halo, steady glow), Dark (deep internal pulse), Eclipse-warm (drifting shimmer), Eclipse-reserved (precise boundary). Each treatment is a shader/VFX layer applied to the unified geometry. Estimated 2 weeks VFX artist time for all four.
- **Interaction aura and overlay UI.** Standard interactable-object treatment with Fragment-specific overlay layout, including v14.0.3 cohort readout. Estimated 1 week UI + animator time.
- **Living-Fragment behaviors.** Dim-on-logout (server-driven state), pulse-on-death (event-driven), owner-proximity-brightening (client-driven proximity check). Estimated 1 week engineering + animator time.
- **Fragment placement in zones.** Each zone needs designated Fragment-compatible placement spots (`[OPEN ITEM]` per GDD §57.9) — estimated one art-pipeline pass per zone.

**Total §8.15a authoring estimate (v14.0.3).** Approximately 5-7 weeks animator + VFX + UI + engineering time. **Reduced from v14.0's 5-7 weeks estimate only because the structural unification allowed the authoring effort to shift toward the seam rendering quality (the new load-bearing detail) rather than toward four-variant differentiation. The Fragment construction visual (§8.15's Fragment-construction-during-completion sequence, 3-4 weeks VFX estimated) and the Fragment's at-rest visual (§8.15a) together form the Fragment's full visual layer; both must ship at high quality for the Fragment to land per GDD §57.13 Gate 3 (Fragment construction VFX).**

`[DESIGN INTENT (v5.41): The Soul Fragment is the v14.0.3 ending's most visible creative asset — the one piece of the ending that is rendered constantly throughout the world for the rest of the server's lifetime. Getting its visual register right is therefore more important than any other v14.0.3 creative surface. v14.0.3's collapse from four distinct Fragment shapes to one unified shape with four pole-color treatments is the ending's sharpest structural commitment to the unification thesis at the memorial layer — the Hall is legible from five meters as *every completer held the same thing* precisely because every Fragment shares one structural shape. Pole identity is expressed through color and living-behavior, not through shape, so the Hall reads unified at distance and individual at close inspection. The seam — crystal emerging through corroded metal — is the load-bearing creative detail per §57.7a design intent; the per-filament rendering interpretive call is the v5.41 proposal for making "emerges" visible. The unified rotation behavior is the v5.41 proposal for unifying motion at the pole layer consistent with the structural unification; team review should evaluate whether per-pole motion differentiation serves or fights the overall unification direction. The cohort readout at the inscription overlay is small but important — it closes the legibility loop between Fragment (grip material visible in the base) and metadata (material named in the overlay), so visitors who don't know what bronze-vs-iron-vs-copper starters mean can read the cohort from the overlay and then notice it in the base material. This is the Hall's demographic readability made accessible per §8.15b and GDD §57.9 non-negotiable constraint. The living-Fragment behaviors are preserved from v14.0 because their relationship-to-owner register is unchanged by v14.0.3's structural simplification — the Fragment's subtle aliveness is what distinguishes memorial from trophy, regardless of whether there are four Fragment shapes or one. The authoring-scope reduction from v14.0 is real (four geometries → one parameterized geometry) but smaller than it looks; the recovered authoring time should be reinvested in the seam rendering, which is the v14.0.3 ship's creative focus.]`

---

#### §8.15a HISTORICAL RECORD — v14.0 Four-Variant Fragment Direction (SUPERSEDED by v14.0.3)

The v14.0 Soul Fragment creative direction (four distinct Fragment visual variants, one per cosmological pole) is preserved below as historical record per Editing Protocol step 6. The four-variant structure is superseded by v14.0.3's unified grip-at-base + crystalline-upper structure with pole-color differentiation. The v14.0 direction does NOT govern v14.0.3 Fragments. The v14.0 per-pole rotation variations, per-pole shape characters, and per-pole living behaviors contained strong creative choices that informed the v14.0.3 pole-color treatments (above); the historical record below documents the design generation that preceded the unification ship.

The v14.0 → v14.0.3 progression on the Fragment is the ending's clearest single example of the unification thesis tightening at the visual layer. v14.0's four-variant direction was register-correct at the per-pole level — each Fragment expressed its pole through a distinct shape and behavior, and the pole-to-shape mapping was well-authored. But the four-variant direction read at Hall scale as *four categories of Bro*: a Year-5 Hall would group Darkness Fragments, Light Fragments, Eclipse-warm Fragments, and Eclipse-reserved Fragments as four distinct visual families, and the thesis *"we all arrived with nothing, we all held our origin, we all crystallized the holding"* would be readable only by walking across families rather than within them. v14.0.3's structural unification renders the thesis at first glance — the Hall reads unified because the Fragments are structurally unified, and pole-difference is the per-Fragment individuality within that unification rather than four separate categories.

**Pre-v14.0.3 content begins below (v14.0 canonical, now historical):**

The Soul Fragment is the ending's public artifact — the permanent crystalline record of a Crossed player's ritual completion, visible to every player on the server forever. Per GDD §57.5, four visual variants correspond to the four cosmological poles: Darkness (Ruthless), Light (Responsible), Eclipse-warm (Mischievous), and Eclipse-reserved (Cool). The creative direction governs Fragment visual treatment, inscription display, and the living-Fragment behaviors specified in GDD §57.7.

**Visual philosophy:** The Soul Fragment is NOT a trophy, a relic, or an achievement icon. It is the externalized form of the player who produced it — what the ritual made of them, rendered in stone-and-light-and-space. A player walking past a Fragment should feel they are walking past someone specific who crossed; the Fragment's presence carries the same register as a gravestone but without the register of death. The player who produced the Fragment is *still here*, continuing to play; the Fragment is what they left behind of who they were before the ritual.

**The four Fragment variants.**

**Darkness Fragment (Ruthless → Dark pole).** A dark crystal with slow, deep internal light. The exterior is opaque-black — not empty black but substantial black, like obsidian with mass. Internal light is a slow pulse of deep red-violet, traveling through the crystal's interior volumes every 3-4 seconds. The crystal rotates slowly on a vertical axis — approximately one full rotation per 15 seconds. Height: approximately 2u (about half the player's character height). The visual register is *weight* — the Fragment sits heavily in the zone's space, drawing the eye by substance rather than brightness. The color palette draws from the v13 Primordial of Darkness (anti-color, deep purples and blacks) but integrated into a sustainable object rather than an overwhelming encounter entity.

**Light Fragment (Responsible → Light pole).** A gold-white crystal with steady inner glow. The exterior is semi-transparent — light passes through and catches in the interior geometry, producing a faint halo of warm illumination around the Fragment. The inner glow is *steady*, not pulsing — the Fragment does not blink, flicker, or vary; it holds. The crystal rotates slowly at the same rate as Darkness Fragments, maintaining visual parity. Height: 2u. The visual register is *sustained warmth* — the Fragment is a small held source of light in the zone. The color palette draws from the v13 Primordial of Light (white-gold) but at sustainable brightness levels rather than the prior encounter's overexposure.

**Eclipse-warm Fragment (Mischievous → Eclipse-warm pole).** A crystal shimmering between gold and dark in a living rhythm. Not pulsing on a timer — the colors *drift* through the Fragment like clouds moving across a surface, producing a visual texture that is always slightly different moment to moment. The shimmer is warm — the dark passages carry a gold edge; the gold passages carry a dark undercurrent; neither pole fully wins. The crystal's rotation is slightly irregular — approximately 15-second period but with small pauses and accelerations, as if the Fragment is dancing slowly in place. Height: 2u. The visual register is *playful vitality* — the Fragment is alive in a way the other three variants are not, and a player watching it for ten seconds notices the rotation irregularity and the shimmer movement. This is the signature Fragment for players who brought the warm-mischievous register through to the end.

**Eclipse-reserved Fragment (Cool → Eclipse-reserved pole).** A crystal with a precise gold-dark boundary held perfectly still. The Fragment's interior is split by an exact mathematical boundary between gold and dark — one half one color, one half the other — and the boundary does not move. The crystal rotates with mechanical precision — exactly 15-second period, no variation, no irregularity. Height: 2u. The visual register is *architectural clarity* — the Fragment is held in a specific configuration, and the player knows by looking that nothing will shift in this Fragment's presentation regardless of how long they watch. This is the signature Fragment for players who brought the dignified-observational register through to the end; the boundary is not a conflict but a held distinction.

**The four variants are instantly distinguishable at a glance.** A player in a zone with multiple Fragments present can tell which pole each Fragment completed from peripheral vision alone. This is load-bearing — the Fragment is a record of a specific person's specific completion, and the pole/register legibility is the first layer of that record. Color, motion, and boundary behavior differentiate the four; a player who has seen one Fragment of each type develops an immediate visual vocabulary for reading pole from Fragment appearance.

**Inscription display — interaction surface.** Fragments are interactable objects. Approaching a Fragment (within 3u) highlights it with a faint aura in the pole's color; interaction button reveals the inscription overlay. The overlay is minimalist:

- **Signed name or "Unsigned"** — top line, in the player's chosen display text if signed, or the explicit "Unsigned" if anonymous.
- **The inscription sentence** — center, in Echo Stone typography (matching §8.3). The sentence is the visual focus; the overlay is framed around it.
- **Metadata line** — bottom, small: server day number, register, cosmological pole, walkouts-before-completion if any. Example: *"Day 847 · Mischievous · Eclipse (warm) · 2 walkouts before."*
