No stat display, no gear history, no achievement list. The Fragment is a memorial, not a profile. Players who want to see stat/achievement data have other surfaces; the Fragment does not compete with those.

**The visit log and signing.** After reading an inscription, the visitor is offered the opportunity to *sign their visit*. The prompt is simple: "Leave your mark? (Name will appear in the owner's visit log.)" — Yes/No/Cancel. No is the default. Yes records the visitor's display name in the Fragment's visitor log (§57.7). Anonymous visits still log but without identifying data.

**Living-Fragment behaviors (creative treatment).**

- **Dim on owner logout (FRAGMENT_DIM_ON_LOGOUT = 0.85).** The Fragment's brightness multiplier drops to 85% when the Crossed player is not currently online. The dim is subtle — not an alarming "is the player dead?" shift, but a noticeable-if-you-know-to-look softening. The visual register is: the Fragment is *present* when its owner is present, and *quiet* when its owner is elsewhere. Other players looking at Fragments develop an intuition for which Crossed players are currently online based on Fragment brightness.
- **Pulse on owner death.** When the Crossed player dies in normal combat (not the ritual), their Fragment produces one soft pulse in the pole's color. The pulse is silent — no sound accompanies it. The pulse is visible to any player in the Fragment's zone; a player in the zone for unrelated reasons may notice a Fragment pulse and have no idea why, which is correct — the pulse does not belong to them. It is the Fragment registering what just happened to its player. The pulse duration is approximately 2 seconds, fading smoothly back to normal brightness. This is the only Fragment behavior that can happen without the Crossed player being online in the specific zone.
- **Owner-proximity brightening.** When the Crossed player stands within interaction range (3u) of their own Fragment, the Fragment brightens slightly and its rotation pattern gains a small flourish (the rotation rate briefly accelerates and settles). This is the "Fragment recognizes its owner" behavior. Visible to others who happen to be watching, but small enough that most observers will miss it. This is a moment of private acknowledgment between the Crossed player and their Fragment — the ritual's record responding to the body that made it.

**Authoring scope.**

- **Four Fragment visual assets.** Each variant requires sprite work, rotation animation, inner-light effect, and pole-specific behavior (Darkness: slow pulse; Light: steady glow; Eclipse-warm: shimmer drift; Eclipse-reserved: precise boundary). Estimated 2 weeks animator + VFX time.
- **Interaction aura and overlay UI.** Standard interactable-object treatment with Fragment-specific overlay layout. Estimated 1 week UI + animator time.
- **Living-Fragment behaviors.** Dim-on-logout (server-driven state), pulse-on-death (event-driven), owner-proximity-brightening (client-driven proximity check). Estimated 1 week engineering + animator time.
- **Fragment placement in zones.** Each zone needs designated Fragment-compatible placement spots (`[OPEN ITEM v14.0]` per GDD §57.8) — estimated one art-pipeline pass per zone.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v14.0): The Soul Fragment is the v14.0 ending's most visible creative asset — the one piece of the ending that is rendered constantly throughout the world for the rest of the server's lifetime. Getting its visual register right is therefore more important than any other v14.0 creative surface. The four variants must be instantly distinguishable, register-congruent, and sustainable-at-quality-through-thousands-of-Fragments. The "weight for Dark, warmth for Light, vitality for Eclipse-warm, clarity for Eclipse-reserved" register mapping is the load-bearing creative choice — each visual is exactly the pole it represents, rendered in a way that reads without explanation. The Fragment is not a trophy: no gold frame, no particle fanfare, no "achievement unlocked" iconography. It is a memorial, and the memorial register is quiet-and-steady-and-specific. The living-Fragment behaviors (dim on logout, pulse on death, brighten at owner proximity) are the Fragment's relationship to its player expressed visually — the Fragment is subtly alive because the player who made it is alive, and the subtle liveliness is what distinguishes it from a trophy. The interaction surface is minimalist because adding stat/achievement/gear-history data would turn the memorial into a profile, and the memorial register cannot survive profile framing. The signing mechanic is soft-social infrastructure that supports Fragment pilgrimage as a real practice — friends of a Crossed player can visit, sign, and know their visit is recorded; strangers can visit anonymously and the Fragment honors both equally. The "Someone" inscription register from §8.16 pairs with the Fragment's visual register — the inscription is about a specific person but addressed to strangers, and the visual is a specific person's specific Fragment rendered so strangers can read it.]`

---

#### §8.15a HISTORICAL RECORD — Stability Fragments Creative Direction (SUPERSEDED by v14.0)

The v12.44 Stability Fragments creative direction is preserved below as historical record per Editing Protocol step 6. The Stability Fragments mechanic is superseded by the v14.0 ritual walkout path (Unfinished Fragment per GDD §57.2c) and Soul Fragment (per GDD §57.5); the creative discipline below does NOT govern v14.0 Soul Fragments, which have their own creative treatment (the primary §8.15a content above). The discipline IS preserved as historical record documenting the design generation that preceded the ritual framing.

The Stability Fragments mechanic (GDD §57.2b — v12.44) was a small mechanical register for an emotional truth: **the player who failed learned something, and the game sees it.** This creative direction governed every surface where fragments appeared — the inventory representation (silent, no UI chrome), the portal-entry prompt (observational, not ceremonial), the post-failure acknowledgment (absent — fragments earned silently), and the encounter's arena-entry sequence (the prefill visible in the stability bar, not announced). The overall tonal register: **persistent effort becomes a small shortening of the next attempt, without celebration or framing that would diminish the encounter's gravity.**

**The load-bearing creative choice — no celebration on earn (preserved historical).** When a player failed a Primordial attempt and was returned to the Convergence, the failure was not reframed. No "Stability Fragment Earned!" popup. No golden particle effect drifting toward the player's character. No Echo Stone offering observational commentary on the fragment's existence. The fragment appeared in the player's count silently — visible only on the next portal-entry prompt. The failure remained a failure; the fragment was a quiet credit against future attempts, not a consolation prize.

**Prompt language — observational, not encouraging (preserved historical).** The portal-entry prompt read: *"You have [N] Stability Fragments. Apply [0-N] to this attempt?"* The language was deliberately neutral. No "Use your hard-earned fragments!" No "Make the fight easier!" The player read the prompt, decided, confirmed. The decision was theirs.

`[DESIGN INTENT PRESERVATION NOTE (v14.0): The v12.44 Stability Fragments creative direction was a model of restrained mechanical acknowledgment — the "no celebration on earn" discipline, the "observational, not encouraging" prompt language, and the "post-success silence" treatment were all strong authoring choices. The direction is preserved in the historical record because the underlying discipline (mechanical acknowledgment should not require ceremony) carries forward even though the specific mechanic is replaced. The v14.0 Unfinished Fragment (GDD §57.2c) inherits the restraint: the Ferryman hands the walking-out player an object without ceremony; the Fragment carries the refusal silently; the player can return or not without the game narrating either choice. The Soul Fragment similarly inherits the "no celebration at creation" discipline — the beach appears, Mayor Bro's line plays, the Fragment is produced, no fanfare. The creative register established by v12.44 continues to shape the v14.0 ending's surface-level treatments even though the specific Stability Fragments mechanic is gone.]`

---

### 8.15b Hall of Fragments — The Server's Memorial Zone (v14.0 NEW; v14.0.3 UPDATED)

The Hall of Fragments is the server's collective memorial zone, introduced in v14.0 as the public-archive counterpart to the ritual chamber's personal-threshold space. Per GDD §57.8, the Hall contains a copy of every Soul Fragment every player on the server has ever produced, arranged spatially by cosmological pole. A Year 1 server has a sparse Hall; a Year 5 server has a dense Hall; a Year 10 server has a Hall that is a forest of rotating Fragments. The Hall's creative direction governs its spatial layout, ambient treatment, and the register of walking-among-the-dead-who-are-not-actually-dead.

---

**v14.0.3 Fragment-readings in the Hall (NEW subsection).**

Per GDD §57.9 (v14.0.3 canonical) and §8.15a (v14.0.3 rewritten above), every Soul Fragment shares one structural shape — a rusted grip at the base, embedded in the Hall floor, with a crystalline form growing upward through the corroded metal in the owner's pole color, taking the silhouette of their lifetime-dominant weapon type. The Hall's readings operate along two axes.

**From five meters — unity.** Every Fragment reads as the same silhouette: rust at the bottom, pole color at the top. The Hall is a forest of crystalline forms sharing one design language. New players walking the Hall understand immediately that *every completer started the same way.* This is the thesis at Hall scale: **we all arrived with nothing; we all held our origin alongside what we became; we all crystallized the holding into this form.** The structural unification per §8.15a is what makes this reading possible at distance — the prior v14.0 four-shape direction could not have produced this reading.

**From one meter — history and identity.** Five distinct readings become available at close inspection, each adding specificity without resolving the unity read from five meters:

- **Grip material reveals cohort.** Bronze starters are early-beta Bros (launched before the v12.x opening window); iron starters are launch-cohort Bros; copper starters are later-cohort Bros. The material palette is fixed per GDD §57.9 non-negotiable constraint — no future character-creation promotion, seasonal event, or cosmetic variation may alter starter weapon material. A Year-5 visitor walking the Hall can read the server's demographic history from the grip-material distribution: a Hall with many bronze grips toward the back (older Fragments, per §8.15b chronological layout below) carries the server's early-beta memory; a Hall with rising copper density toward the front (newer Fragments) carries the server's late-cohort growth.
- **Notch count on the grip reveals repair history.** Notches are physically modeled on the grip's surface — one per repair, small and handmade-looking, positioned where a hand would grip the weapon most often. A grip with 7-8 notches is a Bro who repaired their first weapon repeatedly before losing it; a grip with 1-2 notches is a Bro who repaired it a few times and then it was gone; a grip with many notches suggests a player who cared for their first weapon across a long opening stretch. The notch count is legible at close range and carries the register of *this specific Bro's specific relationship to their specific first weapon*.
- **Pristine grip — the untouched-grip whimsy (v14.0.3 explicit naming per GDD §57.9).** A minority of Fragments have pristine grips with no notches. These are the Fragments of players who never repaired their first weapon before losing it — players who salvaged their starter for materials in the first hour, or had it destroyed in early hardening failure, or simply never thought about it. The pristine grip appears in the Hall as a **quiet architectural joke**. The game, the Hall, and the grip itself refuse to comment. The grip is simply pristine, smooth, untouched, sitting at the base of a crystalline form that represents a hundred-plus hours of identity accumulation and a twenty-second ritual of holding origin and present simultaneously. **The Bro who produced this Fragment unified their identity in the most serious ritual the game performs, and the origin they held was trash they never bothered to take care of.** Per §0.1 and §0.4, this is the tonal rule at architectural scale. The Hall's memorial gravity is held in balance by the pristine grip's quiet absurdity. The memorial is not interrupted — it is *completed* by admitting its absurd register.

  **Creative direction for the pristine grip's visual treatment.** The pristine grip is NOT a lesser grip. It is NOT visually degraded, dimmer, smaller, or lower-quality. It is rendered at the same fidelity as a notched grip, same material palette, same embedded-in-the-floor integration, same crystal-emerging-through-rust seam. The only difference is the surface: pristine grips are smooth and undecorated. The crystalline upper form above a pristine grip is equally pole-color-correct, equally sized, equally present. Nothing in the visual treatment signals *"this one didn't care"* — the grip is simply the grip this Bro held, which happens to be pristine. The whimsy lives in the incongruity between pristine-grip-surface and full-cosmological-ritual-weight, not in any visual downgrading.

  **Why this is a whimsy anchor, not a comic relief.** Per GDD §57.9 design intent, a Hall full of only-notched grips would be grave-only and would fail the unification thesis at the architectural layer; a Hall full of only-pristine grips would be absurd-only and would fail equally. The real Hall, with both types coexisting, holds both registers permanently. The pristine grip is the absurd pole's architectural presence — small, specific, undeniable, and present in every server's Hall at roughly the rate players actually fail to repair their first weapons. The whimsy is structural rather than authored: it emerges from player behavior that the design does not police. Some Bros repair. Some don't. The Hall honors both.

  **Production defense (v5.41 structural requirement).** A well-intentioned production note proposing to add retroactive notches to pristine grips (to "honor all completers equally") would destroy the Hall's tonal balance and violate §0.4. Such notes must be refused on structural grounds, not debated on stylistic merits. The specific refusal language: *the pristine grip is a whimsy anchor per GDD §57.9 canonical; adding retroactive notches serves the grave pole and subordinates the absurd pole; this fails the unification thesis at the architectural layer.* The Bro who never repaired their first weapon earned their pristine grip through the same hundred hours and same twenty seconds as every other completer. The Hall honors them by showing their actual history — including the part where they didn't maintain their origin weapon. That honesty is the memorial's integrity.

- **The crystalline upper form reveals build.** The silhouette of the player's lifetime-dominant weapon type is the Fragment's build-identity readout — greatsword-broad (wide crystal form with a pronounced guard), staff-narrow (tall thin crystal with possible orb-like top), bow-arced (curved crystal in a horizontal arc), sword-balanced (medium crystal with distinct hilt-to-blade proportion), etc. A Bro who played 100 hours of greatsword has a broad Fragment; a Bro who played 100 hours of mixed weapons has a Fragment shaped by the dominant of that mix. The build-reading is available at one meter for all visitors and matches the `weapon_type_hours` dominant calculation per GDD §57.12.
- **The pole color reveals register.** Light (warm gold), Dark (deep red-violet), Eclipse-warm (shifting gold-and-dark), Eclipse-reserved (precise gold-and-dark boundary). Each color carries the §8.15a living-behavior treatment. Pole is legible at distance and close range.
- **The inscription (optional, authored by the player at completion per §8.16) reveals voice.** The one-sentence inscription in Echo Stone typography, addressed to future strangers. Accessible via interaction overlay.

Five readings at close range. No ranking, no hierarchy. Each Fragment is specific; every Fragment is structurally unified with every other. This is the Hall's v14.0.3 architecture at the reading layer.

---

**Visual philosophy:** The Hall is a *memorial*, not a cemetery. The Crossed players whose Fragments are in the Hall are still alive and still playing; the Fragments are the externalized records of who they were before the ritual, not records of deaths. The visual register is therefore *library* rather than *grave*: the Hall is a place where records are kept, and the records are actively responsive to their players' continued lives (dimming on logout, pulsing on death, etc.), rather than frozen artifacts of people who are gone.

**Spatial arrangement (per GDD §57.8).** Large circular chamber with a high domed ceiling. Floor is dark stone inlaid with gold tracery forming the ten elemental shapes in a ring around the center — matching the ritual chamber's floor treatment (§8.15), signaling visual continuity between the two sacred spaces. Ceiling is open to the same ten-element constellation sky as the ritual chamber. The two spaces are cosmologically linked: the ritual chamber is where a crossing happens; the Hall is where the crossings are kept.

**Fragments arranged by pole:**

- **Darkness Fragments** occupy the left quadrant. From the entrance looking forward, the Darkness quadrant is to the left, its boundary sharp against the center region. The quadrant's ambient carries a slight weight — the Darkness Fragment's pole-specific ambient (per §57.6) is layered into the zone here, producing a localized sense of substance.
- **Light Fragments** occupy the right quadrant, mirror-arranged. The ambient here carries a slight warmth.
- **Eclipse Fragments** occupy the center, with warm-Eclipse clusters on one side of center and Reserved-Eclipse on the other. The narrow shared boundary where the two Eclipse variants meet is the most visually active region in the Hall — the warm shimmer and the reserved clarity exist in immediate contrast, producing a strip of heightened aliveness in the zone's geometry.

**Chronological density within quadrant.** Older Fragments (earlier server days) are placed toward the back of the quadrant; newer Fragments appear at the front as completions accumulate. Walking from the entrance toward the back of a quadrant is walking backward through the server's history — the earliest completers whose Fragments opened the Hall are at the far wall; the most recent completer is near the entrance. This temporal depth is readable to players who know to notice it: Year 1 Fragments in a Year 5 Hall are visibly arranged at the back, carrying the register of "these were first."

**The Keeper of the Hall (new canonical NPC).** Stands at the Hall's entrance. A designer-authored NPC (per §8.12a framework boundary) comparable to Mayor Bro, the Ferryman, and the Blacksmith in register. Visual direction: the Keeper is older than most NPCs in the game (relative age readable in sprite work — more weathered, slower stance, deliberate movement). Color palette: unsaturated greys and muted earth tones — the Keeper is dressed for *keeping* rather than *doing*, and the palette signals this. No ceremonial robes, no glowing accessories, no register of mysticism; the Keeper is a *librarian of the dead-who-are-not-dead*, not a priest.

**Keeper greeting (canonical first-visit line):** *"These are the ones who crossed. Read what they wanted you to know. Walk among them. Leave when you're ready."*

The line is observational, instructional, and gently protective. "These are the ones who crossed" — naming the Fragments as specific people. "Read what they wanted you to know" — framing inscription as gift, not trophy. "Walk among them" — the verb "walk," not "visit," treating the Fragments as peers. "Leave when you're ready" — the Keeper does not hurry the visitor; the Hall is a place to stay as long as needed.

**The Keeper is a former Guardian (v14.0.2 expanded).** The Keeper has themselves crossed the ritual in a past life and chose, as their new-life vocation, to tend the memorial zone. This is the Keeper's most load-bearing creative property: the person who welcomes visitors to the Hall is *one of them*. They are not a designer-authored explainer; they are a former completer whose continued presence at the Hall is their own answer to "what did you do with the life that came after?"

**Per-server pole variation.** Each server generates its Keeper from one of four cosmological pole variants at server initialization: Light-Keeper (Responsible crossing), Dark-Keeper (Ruthless crossing), Eclipse-warm-Keeper (Mischievous crossing), or Eclipse-reserved-Keeper (Cool crossing). The Keeper's pole is determined by server seed and is immutable for that server's lifetime. Each pole produces a distinct creative treatment:

- **Light-Keeper.** Warm tonal register. Carries subtle gold tracery on their robes; the Hall's ambient around the entrance is slightly warmer when a Light-Keeper tends it; the floor tracery in the Hall's central region glows slightly gold. Their voice is steady, earnest, not effusive. Crossing-resonance line: *"I held a line too, once. Different line. Same kind of holding."*
- **Dark-Keeper.** Weightier tonal register. Carries subtle shadow depth; the Hall's ambient around the entrance has slightly more visible weight when a Dark-Keeper tends it; the floor tracery in the Hall's central region reads slightly deeper-shadowed. Their voice is grave, dry, unhurried. Crossing-resonance line: *"I took the weight, myself. Years ago. It taught me how to stand still."*
- **Eclipse-warm-Keeper.** Playful but subdued tonal register. Carries subtle iridescent shimmer; the Hall's ambient around the entrance has slightly more porous element boundaries when an Eclipse-warm-Keeper tends it (boundaries between tracery elements shift subtly); the center-region tracery animates with gentle playfulness. Their voice is warm-wry, small flickers of humor. Crossing-resonance line: *"I danced it once. The floor still remembers the pattern. I don't."*
- **Eclipse-reserved-Keeper.** Precise, architecturally-still tonal register. Carries subtle gold-dark edge patterning; the Hall's ambient around the entrance has slightly sharper-edged tracery when an Eclipse-reserved-Keeper tends it; the center-region tracery reads with mechanical clarity. Their voice is observational, exact, economical. Crossing-resonance line: *"I walked the line between once too. Different pole. Same line."*

The Keeper's crossing-resonance line is delivered casually during a longer interaction — once per visitor per day, usually in response to a question the visitor asks or a Fragment they stop at. The line is never announced. Most players will not decode it. Players who notice — and who see that the Keeper carries the Crossed property ambient (§57.6) matching their pole — may realize the Keeper is a former completer.

**The Keeper's sprite.** Standard NPC composite system, matched to a mid-40s-to-50s Bro design with the specific register markers of their pole. Light-Keeper's sprite palette is warm-gold-accented; Dark-Keeper's is heavier-shadow-accented; Eclipse-warm-Keeper's shifts subtly between gold and dark as the sprite animates (the only NPC sprite in the game with subtle palette drift); Eclipse-reserved-Keeper's is precisely-boundaried between gold and dark with geometric clarity. All four variants use the same NPC skeleton; per-pole variation is at the palette and animation-nuance level.

**Dialogue authoring scope (v14.0.2 expanded).** `[OPEN ITEM v14.0.2]` Keeper dialogue authoring across four pole variants. Greeting line is shared canonical and pole-independent. Crossing-resonance line is per-pole (four variants authored above, possibly more for tonal variety per pole). Register-varied responses for repeat visitors, Fragment-specific questions, and tonal variants per §0.1 need authoring per pole. Estimated: 30-40 dialogue lines × 4 pole variants = 120-160 total authored lines. Authored by the same narrative designer producing the ritual voice templates (§57.4).

**The Keeper's chamber-authoring-permission role (v14.0.2 new).** Per GDD §57.13, Crossed characters in their new life may request permission from the Keeper to contribute to the ritual chamber's ongoing construction. The Keeper grants this once per Crossed character with a pole-independent line: *"You may add to it. One hand. Make it count."* The mechanic opens the otherwise-closed chamber portal to Crossed characters in a restricted mode for a single authoring visit. See §57.13 for the full mechanic.

`[OPEN ITEM v14.0]` Keeper repeat-visit dialogue, register-varied responses for specific Fragment questions, and tonal variants per §0.1 (grave/wry/warm/dry) need authoring. Estimated 20-30 dialogue lines per pole (120 total across four pole variants per v14.0.2 expansion). Authored by the same narrative designer producing the ritual voice templates (§57.4).

**Hall ambient audio.** A sustained harmonic drone combining all ten elemental signatures at very low volume. The audio register is close to the ritual chamber's silence-plus-drone (§8.17) but slightly warmer — the Hall is a place of completion rather than threshold. See §8.17 for full audio specification. **Per-Keeper ambient variation (v14.0.2):** the Hall ambient is subtly colored by the server's Keeper pole. Light-Keeper Halls have slightly warmer drone tuning (consonant overtones favored). Dark-Keeper Halls have slightly deeper-register drone. Eclipse-warm-Keeper Halls have drone with subtle harmonic shifts (the ten signatures gently phase against each other). Eclipse-reserved-Keeper Halls have precisely-tuned drone with sharpest element separation. Each variation is small (within 3-5% of the canonical drone mix) but recognizable as a signature of that server's specific Keeper. Occasional faint voice echoes (per GDD §57.6 and §9.5.23.5 architecture) pass through the Hall at approximately half the frequency of zone voice echoes (1200s average vs 600s) — the echoes here are quieter, older, more distant.

**Walking-the-rows register.** A player in the Hall walks between Fragments that stand roughly 2u apart in dense regions and 4-5u apart in sparse regions. The pacing invites slowness — not because walking fast is blocked, but because each Fragment rewards attention, and the player naturally adjusts their pace to "read one, pause, read the next, pause." The Hall's floor-tracery pattern is subtly aligned to the Fragment arrangement — the gold tracery connects Fragments in their cosmological configuration, and walking the tracery is walking the ritual-chamber's inlaid pattern writ large.

**Year-5 and Year-10 scaling.** A Year 5 server's Hall is visibly denser — hundreds or thousands of Fragments arranged in the quadrant configuration, with walking between them requiring more deliberate navigation. A Year 10 server's Hall (`[OPEN ITEM v14.0]` scaling flagged per GDD §57.8 and §9.5.23.6) may require rendering-cost mitigation via density-based LOD, instancing, or time-sliced display. The creative direction for the scaled Hall is that *density itself is meaningful* — the forest of Fragments at Year 10 is a testament to the server's accumulated lives, and players walking that Hall should feel the weight of how many came before.

**Search and filter UI.** Per GDD §57.8, the Hall provides name search, pole filter, register filter, and date-range filter — navigation tools, not game mechanics. Creative direction for the UI: minimalist text inputs, no leaderboard aesthetics, no "most visited" or "most popular" sorts. The UI is a card catalog, not a scoreboard. Searching is private (no public indication that a specific visitor searched for a specific name). Filtering dims the non-matching Fragments to 30% brightness rather than hiding them — the unfiltered Fragments remain visible at low intensity, preserving the continuous spatial register. A filter that "only show Light Fragments from days 200-400" does not erase other Fragments, it quiets them.

**Authoring scope.**

- **Hall zone assets.** Floor tracery, wall geometry, domed ceiling with constellation sky (reused from ritual chamber §8.15). Estimated one zone-art pass.
- **Keeper of the Hall NPC.** Sprite work, standing animation, greeting-line delivery, register-varied dialogue set (`[OPEN ITEM v14.0]`). Estimated 1-2 weeks character + animator + narrative time.
- **Fragment placement layout engine.** Pole-quadrant arrangement, chronological density within quadrant, boundary regions between poles, scaling to thousands of Fragments. Estimated 2-3 weeks engineering + layout-design time.
- **Hall ambient audio.** Ten-element harmonic drone (composed from the same ten-element signatures used in the ritual's recombination chord, §8.17). Estimated 1 week audio director time (shared authoring with §8.17).
- **Hall UI for search and filter.** Minimalist card-catalog treatment per creative direction above. Estimated 1 week UI + design time.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v14.0): The Hall of Fragments is the v14.0 ending's most architecturally ambitious zone and the creative direction's most public-facing expression of the ending's thesis. The "library not cemetery" register is the load-bearing creative choice — Fragments are records of living players, and treating them as grave markers would misread what the Fragment is. The Keeper-as-librarian register matches: not a priest, not a guardian, not a guide, but someone whose role is to keep and welcome. The chronological-depth arrangement (old Fragments toward the back) is the Hall's most quietly powerful feature — a Year 5 server's Hall has layered historical depth visible in the walking path, and the player who walks to the back of a quadrant is literally walking backward through the server's collective memory. The spatial arrangement by pole rather than by any ranked metric (date, visits, achievement level) is an explicit rejection of leaderboard framing — the Hall is not a hall of fame, it is a hall of fragments, and the distinction must hold. Year 10+ scaling is deferred not because it's unimportant but because the decision requires actual population data to resolve sensibly; the three documented mitigation paths (density LOD, instancing, time-slicing) are all creatively tolerable, and the decision can be made post-launch without invalidating pre-threshold Fragments. The Hall's audio treatment is the game's quietest sustained ambient outside the underwater zones — the sound of a memorial library is almost-silence-with-undertone, and the ten-element drone at very low volume delivers this. The shared harmonic signature with the ritual's recombination chord (§8.17) creates cosmological continuity — the ritual chamber and the Hall are audibly the same key, rendered at different intensities. The Hall extends the doc's server-memory thread (Godly lineage, Recent Vows, Growing Crystal, player-generated Echo Stones) into its most ambitious expression yet: a room full of every person who crossed, arranged spatially by cosmological pole, each Fragment responsive to its living owner. The Hall is the ending's contribution to the server's ambient texture; every server's Hall is different, shaped by who crossed and when, and the shape is readable at a glance.]`

### 8.17 Sound — The Ritual (v14.0.3 REPLACES v14.0 ten-phase audio direction)

The v14.0.3 ritual's audio direction is substantially simpler than the v14.0 direction it replaces, because the v14.0.3 ritual's interior structure is substantially simpler: one uniform twenty-second hold rather than ten phases with per-register gesture treatments and a ten-element harmonic chord climax. The v14.0.3 audio direction covers: chamber ambient during threshold and Rise, the grip's gentle-descent audio cue, the chest-cradle hold's sustained near-silence, the voice structure (one primary line at 8-12s, optional second at 16-18s), the Fragment construction's particle-and-reassembly audio, the Naming note preserved from v13, the sky flash's server-wide perfect-fifth audio event, the Hall of Fragments ambient, and the Crossed-player ambient shifts in zones.

The v14.0 ten-element harmonic chord is deprecated along with the three-phase recombination structure it served. The v13 Final Echo Stone perfect-fifth sustained-note is preserved and canonicalized for the Naming (Phase 8 equivalent in v14.0.3 sequencing: the pure-black moment between Fragment construction and beach).

**Chamber ambient (threshold entry, Rise, hold).** A sustained low-frequency drone combining all ten elemental signature tones at very low volume. Each element has a canonical audio signature (per §10.1); the drone is those ten signatures layered at approximately -45dB below unity, producing a barely-audible harmonic bed. The drone does not change during the threshold line, the Rise, the grip's descent, or the twenty-second hold — it is the chamber's sound register, held constant across the entire ritual interior. Over the drone: the Ferryman's threshold line delivered at conversational volume (standard NPC dialogue register, no reverb, no ceremonial processing per §8.5a v5.41 direction); the player's footsteps on raw stone with natural echo matching the chamber's 60u diameter acoustic space.

`[v5.41 INTERPRETIVE CALL — review needed]` The v5.41 proposal preserves the v14.0 chamber-drone composition exactly (ten elemental signatures layered at -45dB) but removes the v14.0 Rise-enrichment cue. In v14.0 each element's rise played its signature at -15dB (discrete from the drone) for about 2 seconds before fading back. For v14.0.3's eight-light Rise with intensity scaling, the audio treatment should be proportional to the visual treatment: each light's rise plays its canonical zone audio motif as a **single sustained tone** at a volume scaled 30-100% per the player's lifetime usage share of that element (the same scaling that governs visual brightness). The 100%-light Flame motif fires at approximately -18dB; a 30%-light Flame motif fires at approximately -35dB — audible but quiet. This renders the player's elemental history audibly in the Rise, matching the visual intensity scaling. The full eight-element Rise audio runs approximately 6.5 seconds (matching the visual timing), after which the chamber drone resumes at steady -45dB. Alternative approaches (all elements at uniform volume, skip-audio for 30% elements entirely, uniform 2-second signature duration) should be evaluated against the "history-as-atmosphere" framing — proportional volume scaling is the audio analog of proportional brightness scaling and renders the same personal history in the same sensory grammar.

**The grip's gentle-descent audio cue (v14.0.3 NEW per GDD §57.5).** A small, domestic audio event accompanying the grip's half-second descent into the waiting off-hand. Audio signature: a single soft note in the grip's material register — a warm bronze note for bronze grips, a cooler iron note for iron grips, a neutral copper note for copper grips. Volume: low, approximately -22dB. Duration: approximately 0.6 seconds (slightly longer than the visual descent, with a trailing decay that lands as the grip settles into the off-hand). No reverb beyond the chamber's natural acoustic. No pitch bend, no vibrato, no sustain beyond the trailing decay.

`[v5.41 INTERPRETIVE CALL — review needed]` The audio cue must match the GDD §57.5 design intent's *"domestic, not sacred"* register. The v5.41 proposal: the grip's descent note should sound like a single soft struck chime in the grip's material timbre — similar to the note a silver spoon makes when set on a wood table, or a wooden bead when dropped onto a cloth-covered surface. Not bell-like (too ceremonial). Not sword-like (too combat-register). Just the sound of a small specific object arriving. Alternative interpretations (a breath sound, a held pad tone, a processed-voice "ah" vocal) should be rejected — they all push toward sacred grammar. A single soft material-specific note is the right register.

**The chest-cradle hold — the load-bearing near-silence (v14.0.3 REPLACES v14.0 per-register gesture audio).** Per GDD §57.6, the hold's silence is load-bearing — *"the ritual's weight is carried by not filling the space."* The audio direction for the twenty-second hold is therefore structurally scarier and structurally simpler than v14.0's per-register gesture audio. The chamber ambient continues at -45dB unchanged. The player's breathing is present in the mix at low volume (approximately -30dB) — the player's own body is audible as part of the ritual's private acoustic. No musical layer, no ambient enrichment, no chamber-response-to-hold, no building tension, no swelling toward climax. The hold is held, not built.

The v14.0 per-register gesture treatments (Opening suffused, Consuming compressed, Dancing reactive, Watching chromatic) do NOT carry forward to v14.0.3. The uniform chest-cradle pose removes the register-specific visible action that the gesture audio was authored to accompany. Per §8.15 v5.41 direction, the register differentiation lives in the voice templates (below) and the Fragment's pole-color at construction, not in the hold's audio layer.

**The voices (v14.0.3 per GDD §57.6).** One primary voice line plays approximately **8-12 seconds into the hold**, drawn from the player's Journey Log in register-specific templates per GDD §57.6. A second optional line may play approximately **16-18 seconds in** if the player is still holding — approximately 40% of rituals produce a second line based on Journey Log richness. Rich Journey Logs produce compelling second lines; sparse Journey Logs default to single-line delivery.

Audio treatment for the voices (preserved from v14.0 where compatible):

- **Unlocated positioning.** Voices are NOT positioned in 3D space — they appear to come from nowhere and everywhere simultaneously. Processing: slight reverb (matching the chamber's acoustic space but not quite), slight pitch variation (different voices for different lines — some lower, some higher, none identifiable as a specific NPC the player has met).
- **Volume.** Voice lines are quiet but central to the mix — approximately -12dB, well above the drone but below shouting. Delivery is conversational rather than declaimed.
- **The voices are IN the drone, not separate from it.** The drone continues under each voice line; the voices rise and fall within the ambient rather than displacing it.
- **Source ambiguity.** No voice in a single ritual should sound identifiable as a specific NPC the player has met. The designer authors 20-30 register-specific voice templates (per GDD §57.6 scope) and records with multiple voice actors such that a single ritual's 1-2 lines are drawn from a pool of 4-6 distinct voices across the narrative content. The player remembers the argument; the player does not remember the speaker.

**Voice authoring scope (v14.0.3 REDUCED from v14.0 80-120 templates).** Approximately 20-30 register-voiced templates across the four registers, per GDD §57.6. The reduction from v14.0's 80-120 templates reflects the structural simplification: v14.0's 30-second gesture phase needed a dense voice system to sustain the interior; v14.0.3's 20-second hold needs one primary line and one optional second line, which requires fewer templates for comparable coverage. Estimated 1-2 weeks narrative designer + voice casting + audio director time.

`[v5.41 INTERPRETIVE CALL — review needed]` The v5.41 proposal: the primary voice line at 8-12s arrives at a specific randomized moment within that window per ritual, not at a fixed time across all rituals. This small variation prevents the ritual from acquiring a mechanical "8-second mark" feel that would develop if the voice always landed at the same moment. The 40% second-line probability similarly fires at a randomized 16-18s moment if it fires. The randomization is small (4-second window for the primary, 2-second window for the optional) and serves the ritual's *"argument that was made, not an event that fires on a schedule"* register.

**Fragment construction audio (v14.0.3 NEW per GDD §57.7a step 2).** The 4-second Fragment construction sequence (1s white flash + 4s dissolve/reassembly + 1s black) carries specific audio treatment:

- **White flash (1s):** audio cuts to complete silence. Hard cut, not fade. The transition from the held drone to complete silence is the ritual's first loud audio event — the silence itself is loud because it displaces the drone that has been continuous for 20+ seconds. This matches the v14.0 white-flash silence discipline preserved here: 1-2 seconds of silence at the transition between hold and Fragment construction.
- **Fragment construction (4s):** a low sustained pad in the player's pole color register fades in over the first 0.5 seconds and holds for approximately 3 seconds as the particles dissolve and reassemble. Volume: low, approximately -20dB. Timbre: pole-register-specific — Light pole pad is warm and steady; Dark pole pad is deep and slow-pulsing at the same period as the Fragment's visual pulse; Eclipse-warm pole pad drifts subtly between warm and deep; Eclipse-reserved pole pad carries a precisely-held harmonic boundary. The pad is the audio register of *the holding becoming permanent* — it is held, not built. It does NOT crescendo toward the Fragment's completion; it holds at a steady volume across all 3 seconds of construction, then fades over 0.5 seconds as the chamber cuts to black.
- **Held black (1s):** silence again. One second of pure black with no audio. The silence lets the pad's trailing decay clear before the Naming begins.

**The Naming — perfect-fifth sustained note (v14.0.3 PRESERVED from v14.0 addendum, recovered from v13 Final Echo Stone audio).** Per GDD §57.7a step 4 and §8.17 HISTORICAL RECORD (below), the Naming plays the canonical sentence for six seconds in pure black with perfect-fifth sustained-note audio underneath. The perfect-fifth interval is the v13 Final Echo Stone audio treatment, canonicalized for this moment.

Four pole voicings of the perfect-fifth interval:

- **Light pole voicing.** Warm brass timbre — french horn or warm synth pad at the perfect-fifth interval, with a warm sustained quality that holds for the full 6-second duration without vibrato.
- **Dark pole voicing.** Deep string timbre — low cello or deep synth pad at the perfect-fifth interval (one octave below the Light voicing), with a slow sustained quality that carries weight without declaiming.
- **Eclipse-warm pole voicing.** Shimmer-flecked timbre — a bell or vibraphone resonance with a subtle shimmering high-harmonic component that drifts slightly over the sustain duration. The interval is held precisely; the shimmer is in the upper harmonics.
- **Eclipse-reserved pole voicing.** Precise-edged timbre — a clean synth or processed string at the interval, with sharp attack and sustained held tone. No vibrato, no shimmer; the sound is architecturally clean and held exactly.

The sentence's typography appears at the moment the perfect-fifth begins (per §8.15 v14.0.3 direction). The typography holds for 6 seconds of sustained note, fades over 2 seconds as the note fades. One additional second of pure black with pure silence follows. Total Naming duration: 9 seconds from note attack to silence clearing.

Audio authoring: the perfect-fifth interval is well-defined musically; the authoring work is in the four per-pole timbral voicings. Estimated 1-2 weeks audio director time.

**The sky flash audio (v14.0.3 NEW per GDD §57.8).** At the instant of the white flash at ritual completion, every outdoor player server-wide hears a soft perfect-fifth in their current zone's key, harmonized with the local ambient. The audio cue is keyed to the listener's zone, not the completer's zone — a Frost-zone listener hears the fifth in a cooler register than a Flame-zone listener.

Volume: low, approximately -28dB (20% of standard combat audio per GDD §57.8). Duration: 2 seconds, fading over the final half-second.

Scope rules:
- Players indoors (dungeons, buildings, any interior) do not hear the fifth.
- Players outdoors hear the fifth regardless of whether they are looking at the horizon (audio is not gated by visual attention; visual is gated by attention per §57.8).
- The fifth does not interrupt any other audio system — combat audio, zone ambient, NPC dialogue all continue unchanged. The fifth layers over them.
- The fifth fires unconditionally at any completion regardless of whether any players are online or outdoors.

`[v5.41 INTERPRETIVE CALL — review needed]` The v5.41 proposal: the perfect-fifth is tuned to the completer's pole color (Light-pole completions produce a brighter fifth, Dark-pole completions a deeper fifth, Eclipse completions a more complex voicing) but rendered in the listener's zone key. This means a Light-pole completion playing into a Frost zone produces a Light-tuned fifth transposed to the Frost zone's key — preserving both the completer's register and the listener's zone's harmonic context. Alternative approaches (fifth ignores completer pole, fifth ignores listener zone, fifth is fixed across all completions and zones) should be evaluated against §57.8's intent — the aggregate sky flash palette over server years is meant to carry server-culture readability, and per-completer pole tuning in the fifth's timbre supports this. Per-listener zone tuning in the fifth's key supports the §57.8 framing that *"the audio cue is keyed to the listener's zone."* Both layers can coexist; the proposal preserves both.

**Per-Crossed-player ambient shifts in zones (preserved from v14.0).** When a Crossed player is within 25u of another player, the other player's zone audio gains a subtle pole-specific tinge matching the Crossed player's pole. Light: slightly warmer harmonic; Dark: slightly deeper frequency emphasis; Eclipse-warm: slightly more porous element-signature boundaries; Eclipse-reserved: slightly sharper element-signature clarity. The shifts are small (within 3-5% of the canonical zone ambient mix) and do not interrupt combat or quest audio. The shifts exist as ambient texture — the Crossed player's continued presence in the world has an audible trace.

**Voice echoes near Crossed players (preserved from v14.0).** Per GDD §9.5.23.5, other players hear faint voice echoes near Crossed players at approximately 600s average frequency. Audio treatment: voice at approximately -30dB (quieter than chamber voices), moderate reverb (more than dialogue, less than chamber voices), pitch-adjacent to the Crossed player's register voice pool. The echoes are positionally ambiguous — they come from a 2D stereo position roughly matching the Crossed player's bearing, but with enough positional ambiguity that the listener cannot pinpoint the source. No accompanying visual cue. The echoes are ambient texture, not conversation.

**Hall of Fragments ambient (preserved from v14.0 with v14.0.3 Keeper-pole variation from v14.0.2).** A sustained harmonic drone combining all ten elemental signatures at very low volume — close to the ritual chamber's drone but slightly warmer (the Hall is a place of completion rather than threshold). Volume: approximately -42dB. The drone is subtly colored by the server's Keeper pole per §8.15b: Light-Keeper Halls have slightly warmer drone tuning (consonant overtones favored); Dark-Keeper Halls have slightly deeper-register drone; Eclipse-warm-Keeper Halls have drone with subtle harmonic shifts; Eclipse-reserved-Keeper Halls have precisely-tuned drone with sharpest element separation. Each variation is small (within 3-5% of the canonical drone mix) but recognizable. Occasional faint voice echoes pass through the Hall at approximately half the frequency of zone voice echoes (1200s average vs 600s) — quieter, older, more distant.

**Authoring scope — v14.0.3 audio.**

- **Chamber drone composition (preserved from v14.0).** Ten element signatures layered at -45dB. No new authoring required for the drone itself; the v14.0 composition carries forward.
- **Eight-light Rise audio with intensity scaling.** Per-element signature tones at volumes scaled 30-100% by player's lifetime usage share. Uses existing zone audio motifs as source material; authoring work is in the intensity-scaling pipeline. Estimated 1 week audio director time.
- **Grip gentle-descent audio cue.** Three per-material notes (bronze, iron, copper) with decay profile. Estimated 2-3 days audio director time.
- **Voice system — 20-30 templated lines (reduced from v14.0's 80-120).** Recording with multiple voice actors for source ambiguity, processing for unlocated positioning and reverb/pitch variation. Estimated 1-2 weeks audio director + voice casting + actor time.
- **Fragment construction audio (1s silence + 4s pole pad + 1s silence).** Four pole-register pad voicings (Light warm steady, Dark deep slow-pulsing, Eclipse-warm drifting, Eclipse-reserved precisely-held). Estimated 1-2 weeks audio director time.
- **Naming note — four pole voicings (preserved from v14.0 addendum).** Perfect-fifth interval recovered from v13, extended into four pole voicings (Light warm brass, Dark deep strings, Eclipse-warm shimmer-flecked, Eclipse-reserved precise-edged). Estimated 1-2 weeks audio director time.
- **Sky flash perfect-fifth — per-pole-tuned, per-listener-zone-keyed.** Pipeline for generating the listener's zone-key perfect-fifth tuned to the completer's pole. Estimated 1-2 weeks audio director + audio engineering time.
- **Per-Crossed-player ambient shifts in zones (preserved from v14.0).** Per-pole frequency-shift specifications applied to existing zone ambient compositions. Estimated 1 week implementation + audio director validation per zone. Approximately 10-20 zones require validation; total audio director time ~2-3 weeks spread across zones.
- **Hall of Fragments ambient (preserved from v14.0 with v14.0.2 Keeper-pole variation).** No new authoring required; carries forward.

**Total v14.0.3 audio authoring estimate.** Approximately 8-12 weeks of audio director time plus voice casting/recording. **Reduced from v14.0's 16-22 week estimate** because the ten-element harmonic chord (which was 3-4 weeks of the v14.0 estimate) is deprecated, and the voice system is reduced from 80-120 templates to 20-30. The saved authoring budget should be redirected to the Fragment construction audio's four pole voicings and the sky flash's per-pole-per-zone tuning, which are the v14.0.3 ritual's most audibly load-bearing authoring deliverables per GDD §57.13 Gate 4 (Naming timing) and Gate 3 (Fragment construction VFX, which includes audio).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v5.41): The v14.0.3 audio direction is structurally simpler than v14.0's but more load-bearing per element. v14.0's ten-element harmonic chord was the ritual's climax and carried most of the audio emotional weight; v14.0.3 has no single climactic audio moment, and the emotional weight distributes across multiple smaller moments: the grip's soft material-note at descent, the near-silence of the 20-second hold, one or two voice lines arriving in that silence, the white-flash hard-cut silence, the Fragment construction's held pad, the Naming's perfect-fifth, the sky flash's distant perfect-fifth heard by other players. Each of these is a smaller moment than v14.0's chord but collectively they carry the ritual's audio weight more distributed-ly. This matches the v14.0.3 structural unification: fewer discrete moments, each doing more specific work. The silence of the 20-second hold is the hardest authoring discipline — production pressure will want to "fill" the hold with a building ambient layer, a subtle tension build, a musical crescendo toward the completion. All such notes must be refused. The hold is meant to feel structurally held, not temporally built. The chamber drone continues at -45dB, the player breathes audibly, the voices arrive once or twice, and the rest is silence. Creative authors reviewing production must defend this silence specifically — it is the v14.0.3 ritual's most vulnerable audio register and the most likely to be eroded under production pressure.]`

---

#### §8.17 HISTORICAL RECORD — v14.0 Ten-Phase Audio Direction (SUPERSEDED by v14.0.3)

The v14.0 ritual audio direction is preserved below as historical record per Editing Protocol step 6. The v14.0 direction addressed a ten-phase ritual structure with per-register gesture audio (Opening suffused, Consuming compressed, Dancing reactive, Watching chromatic) and a ten-element harmonic chord at Phase 7 as the ritual's audio climax. v14.0.3's structural simplification (universal 20-second chest-cradle hold replacing the three-phase recombination; eight-light Rise with intensity scaling replacing the ten-element rising; Fragment construction through grip-and-weapon dissolving together replacing the recombination chord climax) eliminates the targets of most of the v14.0 audio direction. The v14.0 per-register gesture audio treatments contained strong composition ideas that do not carry forward because their target (register-specific visible gestures during the hold) no longer exists in v14.0.3. The ten-element chord's composition brief (an E-major tenth chord or equivalent voicing with per-element harmonic tuning) is deprecated along with the chord itself.

The v14.0 audio direction's most preserved element is the Naming perfect-fifth interval, recovered from v13 Final Echo Stone audio and canonicalized for the Naming delivery. This is preserved in the v14.0.3 direction above. The v14.0 chamber drone composition (ten element signatures layered at -45dB) is also preserved in the v14.0.3 direction above; it continues to serve as the chamber's ambient register across the threshold, Rise, grip descent, and 20-second hold.

**Pre-v14.0.3 content begins below (v14.0 canonical, now historical):**

The v14.0 ritual's audio direction is the game's most ambitious single-scene audio work. Per GDD §57, the ritual's ten phases each carry distinct audio treatment; the ten-element harmonic chord at Phase 7 (recombination) is the game's key audio moment. This direction covers chamber ambient, gesture-phase audio per register, the voices system, the recombination chord, the white-flash silence, the Phase 8 Naming with its sustained-note treatment, and the beach transition. It also covers the Hall of Fragments ambient drone and the server-wide voice-echo system's rendering register.

**Chamber ambient (Phases 2-4 — entry, framing, rise).** A sustained low-frequency drone combining all ten elemental signature tones at very low volume. Each element has a canonical audio signature (per §10.1 elemental audio design); the drone is those ten signatures layered at approximately -45dB below unity, producing a barely-audible harmonic bed. The drone does not change during Phases 2-4; it is the chamber's sound register, held constant as the Ferryman speaks, as the ten elements rise, as the player crosses to the center. Over the drone: the Ferryman's dialogue delivered at conversational volume (standard NPC dialogue register, no reverb, no ceremonial processing); the player's footsteps on raw stone with natural echo matching the chamber's 60u diameter acoustic space.

**The Rise (Phase 4 — ten elements appear).** As each element rises from the floor, its canonical audio signature plays at a louder volume (approximately -15dB, discrete from the ambient drone) for about 2 seconds, then fades back into the drone. The ten elements rise in sequence rather than simultaneously — approximately 3 seconds between each element's rise — producing a 30-second Rise phase where the drone is successively enriched by each element's distinct sonic identity emerging briefly and settling. By the end of the Rise, the ambient drone has been audibly enriched — the player can hear that all ten element signatures are present.

**The Gesture (Phases 5-6 — register-specific, 30 seconds).**

Each register has distinct audio treatment during the 30-second gesture phase. All four share the voice system (below) and build toward the same recombination chord, but the audio signature of "what the player is doing" is register-specific:

- **Opening (Responsible → Light).** As the elements drift inward and pass through the player's silhouette, each element's audio signature fades into a unified tone at the player's position. The cumulative effect is the ten signatures gradually coalescing into one sustained tone near the player. The register is *suffusion* — the audio swells softly, without percussive events, without peaks and valleys; it simply becomes more present, more full, more unified over 30 seconds.
- **Consuming (Ruthless → Dark).** As the player reaches toward each element in sequence and absorbs it, each element's audio signature is *pulled* toward the player's position and then cut off at the moment of absorption — a sharp termination of each element's tone, not a fade. The cumulative effect is the drone's element signatures being successively silenced as each is taken. The register is *compression* — audio density increases at the player's position (where absorbed elements are stored) while the chamber becomes quieter around them. By the end of the gesture, the chamber itself is near-silent; only the player's position carries dense audio.
- **Dancing (Mischievous → Eclipse-warm).** As the player moves through the ring, their footsteps trigger element-signature trills in the elements they pass closest to — a playful audio response where the elements "respond" to the player's movement in real time. The element signatures weave together in varying configurations as the player's path brings different elements closer or farther. The register is *call-and-response* — the audio is playfully reactive, and a player who changes their path mid-gesture changes the audio composition. By the end of the gesture, the element signatures have been woven into a composition shaped by the player's specific dance, unified through the shared movement pattern.
- **Watching (Cool → Eclipse-reserved).** As the elements converge on a focused point in front of the player, each element's audio signature is precisely stepped — approximately 3 seconds per element, in sequence, each settling to a clean note before the next begins. The register is *chromatic* — each element's audio presence is held distinct until the sequence completes, producing a "scale" of ten tones played one at a time at the focal point. By the end, the ten notes resolve to a held chord. The register is *architectural* — every audio event happens exactly when it should, in exactly the order required, with no overlap or improvisation.

**The Voices (throughout Phases 5-6).** Unlocated voices speaking register-specific lines drawn from the player's Journey Log at ritual time. Audio treatment: voices are NOT positioned in 3D space — they appear to come from nowhere and everywhere simultaneously. Processing: slight reverb (matching the chamber's acoustic space but not quite), variable pitch (different voices for different lines — some lower, some higher, none identifiable as a specific NPC the player has met), variable volume (some lines loud, some barely audible, varying unpredictably). The voices are NOT separated from the ambient drone — they exist *in* the drone, rising and falling within it.

**Voice volume and spacing.** Individual lines are 5-15 seconds apart. Some lines repeat. Some are new each time. Volume variation is critical — a loud line followed by a whisper followed by a line at conversational volume produces the "unsettled pressure" register the design requires. No line concludes or argues itself out; the pressure does not resolve.

**Voice source ambiguity.** No two voices in a single ritual should sound alike in a way that would let the player identify "that was [specific NPC]." The designer has a pool of pitched/processed voice actors for the 80-120 authored templates, and the templates draw from multiple actors such that a single ritual's voice content is cast from 4-6 distinct voices. The player cannot track which voice said what; by ritual end, the player remembers the arguments but not the speakers. This is the ambiguity GDD §57.4 requires.

**Phase 7 — the recombination (the game's key audio moment).** When the 30-second gesture completes, the ten elements fold into a single point. For three seconds, the chamber renders the **pre-elemental whole** visually; the audio renders it as a single sustained chord containing all ten element signatures at equal weight. This is the game's moment of audio resolution.

**The chord itself.** `[OPEN ITEM v14.0]` The composition brief for the ten-element harmonic chord is the audio director's single most important deliverable. The ten element audio signatures (Flame, Frost, Water, Stone, Wind, Flora, Storm, Venom, Dark, Light) were authored individually with no requirement that they harmonize; the chord requires authoring the ten signatures *jointly* such that their simultaneous presence produces harmonic resolution rather than cacophony. This is substantial authoring work, likely requiring iteration with the zone ambient compositions (§10.1) to ensure backward compatibility. Estimated 3-4 weeks audio director time. The deliverable is: the ten signatures' frequencies and harmonics tuned such that their simultaneous presence at equal weight forms an extended chord (recommended framing: an E-major tenth chord with element-specific overtones, but the audio director's judgment determines the specific key and voicing). The chord plays for exactly 3 seconds. It is the longest single sustained moment of resolved-harmony in the entire game.

**Phase 7 end — the white flash silence.** Immediately after the 3-second chord, the screen goes white and audio cuts to complete silence for 2 seconds. Not fade-out — a hard cut. The transition from dense chord to complete silence is itself the loudest audio event in the scene; the silence carries more weight than any sound would. 2 seconds is chosen carefully: long enough for the silence to register, short enough that it doesn't feel like a bug.

**Phase 8 — The Naming (v14.0 addendum).** The white fades to black over approximately 1-2 seconds. One second of pure black in silence. Then the canonical sentence appears in Echo Stone typography (see visual direction below), and the **sustained-note audio** plays beneath it: a perfect-fifth interval held for five seconds, then fading over the next two seconds as the sentence fades. The note is pitched in the player's pole register — slightly warmer for Light, slightly deeper for Dark, shimmer-flecked for Eclipse-warm, precisely-tuned for Eclipse-reserved. No other audio plays during Phase 8: no drone, no voice, no ambient bed. The perfect fifth in the pure black is the audio event. Then one further second of pure black and complete silence before the beach ambient rises.

This sustained-note treatment is the **v13 Final Echo Stone audio treatment canonicalized for the Naming moment**. See §8.17 HISTORICAL RECORD below for the original v13 direction. The v14.0 Naming preserves the perfect-fifth interval, the 5-second hold, and the fade shape exactly. The v13 direction said "the player knows, from the sound alone, that this is the last one" — in v14.0 the sentence the note accompanies is the game's last written word, delivered once to its player in the pure black between white flash and beach.

**Pole-specific pitch variation for the Naming note.** The perfect-fifth interval's specific voicing varies by pole:

- **Light** — warm-tuned: the interval is scored with slight string-like resonance and air, landing in a register that feels inhabited rather than hollow. The note is warm the way candlelight is warm — not bright, not cold.
- **Dark** — deep-tuned: the interval is scored with low-frequency body and slow attack, landing in a register that has weight. Substantial rather than heavy; present rather than oppressive.
- **Eclipse-warm** — shimmer-flecked: the interval carries subtle irregularity in its tone, as if the note is alive and slightly varying moment to moment. The pitch is steady but the texture breathes.
- **Eclipse-reserved** — precise: the interval is played without vibrato, without variation, held mathematically. The pitch is clean and the envelope is exact. The boundary between note and silence is sharp.

All four variants share the perfect-fifth interval and the five-second hold and the two-second fade. The pole-specific voicings are the audio director's authoring work — each pole gets approximately equal authoring time to produce a voicing that specifically feels like the pole. The four voicings together comprise the game's audio of "the world naming you" across all four possible recombinations.

**Phase 9 — the beach.** The 1-second final silence resolves into the tutorial beach's canonical ambient: water lapping, distant gull cry, gentle wind. No music. No ceremonial audio treatment. Exactly the opening-scene ambient the player first heard when they created the character. Mayor Bro's canonical line plays at standard NPC dialogue volume after approximately 3 seconds of beach ambient — *"Another one washed up. They never last."* The audio register at this moment is *exactly identical* to the tutorial opening, which is the ending's audio rhyme. Players who pay attention recognize: this is the beach I started on, rendered at the same fidelity the tutorial rendered it.

**The Unfinished Fragment walkout.** If the player walks out of the ritual chamber (Phase 5-6 voices active), the voices warm up and speak in a noticeably different register — lower volume, gentler pitch, slower pacing. The Ferryman's line "Some don't. That's part of it too. Take this." plays at standard dialogue volume. The Unfinished Fragment inventory grant plays the standard "item received" sound (no Fragment-specific audio variant). The walk back to the Convergence plays a soft ambient transition with no ceremonial treatment. The register is *gentle release* — the walkout is not punished with somber audio; the game lets the player go quietly.

**The Hall of Fragments ambient.** A sustained harmonic drone combining all ten elemental signatures at very low volume (approximately -48dB — even quieter than the chamber drone). The drone is *warmer* than the chamber's — the element signatures are tuned toward their consonant overtones rather than dissonant ones, producing a more settled bed. The Hall is a place of *completion*, not threshold; the audio register reflects this. Volume level is designed such that conversation between players in the Hall is not drowned by ambient — the Hall is a walkable space that players should linger in.

**Hall voice echoes.** Occasional echoes of the ritual voices' lines pass through the Hall at approximately half the frequency of in-zone voice echoes (1200s average vs 600s per GDD §9.5.23.5). The Hall echoes are quieter than zone echoes (-35dB vs -25dB) and are more processed — additional reverb, slight pitch shifting downward, producing a "distant memory" register. The echoes here are the arguments made against past rituals, fossilized in the Hall's ambient. A player in the Hall for 30 minutes might hear 1-2 distant voice echoes; they pass through and fade without attribution.

**Crossed player ambient shifts in other zones.** Per GDD §57.6, when a Crossed player is present in a zone, the zone's ambient shifts per the player's pole:

- **Light-Crossed presence.** Zone's ambient music gains approximately +2dB on warm frequencies (low-mid), producing subtle warmth. Duration of shift matches the player's presence within CROSSED_AMBIENT_RADIUS.
- **Dark-Crossed presence.** Zone's ambient music gains approximately +2dB on low frequencies (sub-bass), producing subtle weight.
- **Eclipse-warm-Crossed presence.** Zone's ambient music gains approximately ±1dB variable shimmer — moving between warm and cool frequency emphasis slowly, producing subtle living motion.
- **Eclipse-reserved-Crossed presence.** Zone's ambient music gains approximately +1dB on high-mid frequencies with sharper attack envelopes, producing subtle clarity.

These shifts are small enough to be subliminal to players who don't know they are happening and consistent enough to be recognizable to players who do.

**Voice echoes near Crossed players in zones.** Per GDD §9.5.23.5, other players hear faint voice echoes near Crossed players at approximately 600s average frequency. Echo audio treatment: voice at -30dB (quieter than chamber voices), moderate reverb (more than dialogue, less than chamber voices), pitch-adjacent to the Crossed player's register voice pool. The echoes are NOT positioned on the Crossed player specifically — they come from a 2D stereo position roughly matching the Crossed player's bearing, but with positional ambiguity so the listener can't pinpoint the source. Echoes play without any accompanying visual cue (no overhead text, no speech bubble, no Fragment pulse animation) — the echoes are ambient texture, not conversation.

**Authoring scope — audio.**

- **Chamber drone composition.** Ten element signatures layered at unity, with the Rise sequence choreography. Estimated 1-2 weeks audio director time.
- **Four gesture audio treatments.** Four distinct 30-second audio signatures (suffusion / compression / call-and-response / chromatic). Estimated 3-4 weeks audio director time.
- **Voice system — 80-120 templated lines.** Recording with multiple voice actors for source ambiguity, processing for unlocated positioning and variable reverb/pitch/volume. Estimated 4-6 weeks audio director + voice casting + actor time. Shared with GDD §57.4 authoring scope.
- **Ten-element recombination chord.** The game's key audio moment. `[OPEN ITEM v14.0]` 3-4 weeks audio director time. This is the deliverable that determines whether the ending lands.
- **White-flash silence and beach transition.** Existing audio infrastructure; estimated 1-2 days.
- **Naming note — four pole voicings (v14.0 addendum).** Perfect-fifth interval at five-second hold with two-second fade, voiced in four pole registers (Light warm, Dark deep, Eclipse-warm shimmer-flecked, Eclipse-reserved precise). Recovered from v13 Final Echo Stone audio direction and extended for pole variation. Estimated 1-2 weeks audio director time — the interval is well-defined, so the authoring work is in the four per-pole timbral voicings rather than composition.
- **Unfinished Fragment walkout audio.** Gentler voice-shift programming, no new recorded content. Estimated 1 week.
- **Hall of Fragments ambient and voice echoes.** Drone composition (shared with chamber drone, with warmer tuning), voice-echo processing variants. Estimated 1-2 weeks audio director time (shared authoring with chamber drone).
- **Crossed player ambient shifts in other zones.** Per-pole frequency-shift specifications applied to existing zone ambient compositions. Estimated 1 week implementation + audio director validation per zone. Approximately 10-20 zones require validation; total audio director time ~2-3 weeks spread over the zones.

**Total v14.0 audio authoring.** Approximately 16-22 weeks of audio director time plus voice casting/recording. This is a substantial audio investment — comparable to the v12.49 Listening Mode TTS infrastructure ship but in creative rather than technical direction. The audio deliverable is what makes the ending work or not work; investment scales accordingly.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v14.0): The v14.0 audio direction is the game's most ambitious single-scene audio work because the ending's success depends on audio as much as visual. The recombination chord is the game's key audio moment, and getting it right requires the audio director to author the ten element signatures as a harmonic system rather than ten independent sonic identities — this is significant rework of existing zone audio signatures (§10.1), and is flagged as an open item because it is the specific authoring deliverable without which the ending falls flat. The voice system's source-ambiguity discipline is the most important authoring choice for the voices — a single identifiable NPC voice would collapse the voices' ambiguous-source design, and the multi-actor casting with pitch/processing variation is the structural enforcement of that design. The white-flash silence is the ending's loudest audio event — the transition from ten-element chord to complete silence is itself the climax of the audio scene. The Naming note (v14.0 addendum) is the audio that fills the silence after the silence has done its work — the perfect-fifth interval from v13 Final Echo Stone direction, recovered and extended into four pole voicings, delivered beneath the canonical sentence in the pure black. The note does not compete with the silence; it follows it, at lower intensity than the chord, and its job is to be the audio register of the game's own voice speaking its last written word once, clearly. The four pole voicings are the authoring discipline — a single generic voicing would make the Naming feel like a stock cutscene sting; four specific voicings make the moment feel like the game knows which kind of ritual just happened. The beach audio rhyme (exactly identical to tutorial opening) is the ending's most subtle audio move — players who pay attention recognize it as recognition, and the moment becomes quietly devastating on first recognition. The Crossed player ambient shifts across zones are the audio-design equivalent of the visual ambient shifts — small enough to be subliminal, consistent enough to be recognizable, producing a world that audibly remembers its completers. The Hall's warmer drone and distant-voice-echo treatment is the memorial-library register rendered in sound — the Hall is a place to stay in, not a place to process, and the audio register supports lingering. The investment scale is correct — audio authoring is usually under-invested in relative to visual, and the v14.0 ending specifically inverts this ratio because the moments that matter most in the ending (recombination chord, white-flash silence, Naming note, beach rhyme) are primarily audio events with supporting visuals rather than visual events with supporting audio. The audio director leads the ending's creative realization more than any other single creative role. The Naming addendum specifically closes a gap in the initial v14.0 spec — the canonical sentences were authored as the ending's last words but had been demoted to inscription options; the Naming restores them AND restores the v13 audio treatment that was authored to accompany them.]`

---

#### §8.17 HISTORICAL RECORD — Sound: The Primordial (SUPERSEDED by v14.0)

The pre-v14.0 Primordial audio direction is preserved below as historical record per Editing Protocol step 6. The Primordial entities do not exist in v14.0; their audio signatures (the Darkness sub-bass + distant breathing, the Light high-frequency shimmer, the Eclipse collision tone) are superseded by the ritual chamber's audio direction (above).

**Primordial of Darkness ambient:** Near-silence. The deepest sub-bass the speakers can produce — felt more than heard. Occasional distant sounds that might be breathing or might be nothing. The absence of sound IS the sound design. When the player lights an area, a faint warm tone fades in for that zone — the sound of the world remembering it exists.

**Primordial of Light ambient:** High-frequency shimmer that's almost painful. A constant tone that the player wants to escape. When the player creates a shadow zone, the shimmer dampens to a tolerable hum within that zone — the sound of relief, of darkness as shelter.

**Primordial attack sounds:** The Darkness tendril attack is a low wet swipe — organic, intimate, close. The Light beam is a high sharp crack — clinical, distant, precise. Neither sounds like a game enemy. They sound like forces.

**Arbiter Eclipse in the Primordial arena:** The biggest sound in the game gets even bigger here. Sub-bass rumble + high shimmer collapsing into a single resonant tone at the exact frequency where the dark ambient and light ambient would meet if they could. The sound of wholeness. Plays for 3 seconds. The only time in the game where the audio feels *resolved*.

**Final Echo Stone sound:** Not the standard crystalline chime. A single sustained note — a perfect fifth interval held for 5 seconds, then fading into the zone's ambient. Warm. Final. The player knows, from the sound alone, that this is the last one.

`[DESIGN INTENT PRESERVATION NOTE (v14.0): The pre-v14.0 Primordial audio direction contained two load-bearing design ideas that carry directly into v14.0 — and one of them is not just inherited but fully canonicalized. First: the Arbiter Eclipse's "sub-bass rumble + high shimmer collapsing into a single resonant tone at the exact frequency where dark and light would meet if they could" is the DIRECT ANCESTOR of the v14.0 ten-element recombination chord. The v14.0 chord generalizes the idea from "two opposed forces resolving" to "ten element signatures resolving" and extends it from a moment within a combat encounter to the game's key audio moment at ritual completion. Second, and more directly: the Final Echo Stone's "perfect fifth, held for 5 seconds, fading into the zone's ambient" is preserved and canonicalized in full as the **Naming note** (Phase 8, v14.0 addendum; see §8.17 above). The Naming note's authoring discipline is the v13 Final Echo Stone's authoring discipline — same interval, same hold duration, same fade shape — extended with four pole-specific voicings (Light warm, Dark deep, Eclipse-warm shimmer-flecked, Eclipse-reserved precise). The v13 sentence "the player knows, from the sound alone, that this is the last one" remains true in v14.0 at the Naming moment: the note is the game's audio of speaking its last written word, played once, delivered beneath the sentence in the pure black. This is the most complete preservation of a superseded audio direction into the canonical replacement — the Naming IS the Final Echo Stone sound, recovered from the v13 direction and given the authored role the v14.0 initial spec had inadvertently taken from it.]`

### 8.18 The Emotional Arc — Final Update (v13 REVISED, v14.0.5 UPDATED)

| Act | Levels | Player feels | World says | Combat expression |
|-----|--------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|
| I Arrival | 1–10 | Small, uncertain | "You won't last" | Walk in, swing, dodge away. Weapon glow starts cycling. |
| I.5 First Lunge | 10–15 | Aggressive, discovering | (no NPC comment) | First accidental lunge. "Wait, what was that?" |
| II Pattern | 11–40 | Growing, curious | "Huh. You're still here." | First collision. First resonance pulse noticed. Combo burst discovered — "that swipe hit harder." |
| II.5 Timing | 25–35 | Deliberate, calculating | (no NPC comment) | First intentional resonance trigger. First retreat-shot-to-collision chain. Combo spread discovered — "the status jumped." |
