| III Deepening | 41–70 | Capable, invested | "I stopped betting against you." | Resonance timing consistent. Lunge-to-swipe combos fluid. Retreat shot automatic. Now-Wide-Next becomes instinct. |
| IV Duality | 71–90 | Powerful, questioning | "The opposite isn't your enemy" | Eclipse resonance — holding Dark+Light collision for peak timing. Combo investment cycles compound. |
| V Truth | 91–100 | Tested, refined | "Not everyone can see this" | Every collision resonance-timed. Lunge opens, retreat seeds, swipe detonates. |
| VI Guardian | 100+ | Masterful, purposeful | "The world needs what you became" | Resonance on Apex bosses during phase transitions. The pulse timing becomes meditative. |
| VII Ending | Completion | Defined by register — five outcomes (Hero / Trickster / Arbiter / Sovereign / Weaver, v14.0.5) | (reflected back at you) | — |
| VIII Ritual (v14.0.3) | Post-True-Guardian | Held, resolved | (silence; then the sky flash) | The twenty-second chest-cradle hold. Both hands. No combat. |
| IX New Life | Post-ritual | Stripped-and-returning | "You came home. Everything is where you left it. You are not." | No combat — the Meadow-to-town-to-farm walk. |
| X Convergence (ongoing) | Server-lifetime | Committed, interdependent | "Your opposite has what you need" | Eclipse resonance is the game's highest timing challenge. Weaver characters carry the Complete Eclipse sequential cycle per §8.61. |

Per GDD §53.1 v14.0.5, §53.4 v14.0.5, and §57 v14.0.3, Act VII has five outcomes: Hero (Responsible-dominant), Trickster (Mischievous-dominant, v13 NEW), Arbiter (Cool-dominant), Sovereign (Ruthless-dominant), and Weaver (balanced-register, v14.0.5 NEW). Act VIII is the Last Recombination ritual introduced at v14.0. Act IX is the v14.0.3 walk-home beat. Act X is the ongoing post-completion Convergence + Hall of Fragments presence. The pre-v14.0 Primordial act that occupied what is now Act VIII–IX is superseded by the ritual; see historical record below.

**HISTORICAL RECORD — pre-v14.0 nine-act table with Primordial (preserved per Editing Protocol step 6):**

> | Act | Levels | Player feels | World says | Combat expression |
> |-----|--------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|
> | I Arrival | 1–10 | Small, uncertain | "You won't last" | Walk in, swing, dodge away. Weapon glow starts cycling. |
> | I.5 First Lunge | 10–15 | Aggressive, discovering | (no NPC comment) | First accidental lunge. "Wait, what was that?" |
> | II Pattern | 11–40 | Growing, curious | "Huh. You're still here." | First collision. First resonance pulse noticed. Combo burst discovered — "that swipe hit harder." |
> | II.5 Timing | 25–35 | Deliberate, calculating | (no NPC comment) | First intentional resonance trigger. First retreat-shot-to-collision chain. Combo spread discovered — "the status jumped." |
> | III Deepening | 41–70 | Capable, invested | "I stopped betting against you." | Resonance timing consistent. Lunge-to-swipe combos fluid. Retreat shot automatic. Now-Wide-Next becomes instinct. |
> | IV Duality | 71–90 | Powerful, questioning | "The opposite isn't your enemy" | Eclipse resonance — holding Dark+Light collision for peak timing. Combo investment cycles compound. |
> | V Truth | 91–100 | Tested, refined | "Not everyone can see this" | Every collision resonance-timed. Lunge opens, retreat seeds, swipe detonates. |
> | VI Guardian | 100+ | Masterful, purposeful | "The world needs what you became" | Resonance on Apex bosses during phase transitions. The pulse timing becomes meditative. |
> | VII Ending | Completion | Defined, complete | (reflected back at you) | — |
> | VIII Convergence | Post-ending | Committed, interdependent | "Your opposite has what you need" | Eclipse resonance as Arbiter is the game's highest timing challenge |
> | IX Primordial | Final | Alone, transcendent | (one sentence that contains everything) | Every lunge is a choice to engage fear. Every retreat shot seeds light/shadow. |

The pre-v14.0 nine-act table placed the Primordial encounter as the final act (IX) with the note "every lunge is a choice to engage fear. Every retreat shot seeds light/shadow." The v14.0 ship replaced the Primordial encounter with the Last Recombination ritual, which is non-combat and does not involve lunge or retreat shots; the v14.0.3 ship collapsed the ritual structure into the 20-second uniform chest-cradle hold. The new-life walk-home beat (Act IX above) is a v14.0.3 addition not present in the pre-v14.0 table. The pre-v14.0 table is preserved here for design-provenance.

### 8.19 Resonance — Creative Direction

#### The Held Moment

Resonance is the moment between knowing you can detonate and choosing to wait. It's the inhale before the swing. Mechanically it's a 25% damage window at the end of a status duration. Emotionally it's the game asking: *are you patient enough to be powerful?*

**What the player sees:** They've landed frost on a Brute. The hexagonal Freeze icon appears above its head, steady and blue. They have their flame weapon ready. They could swipe now — guaranteed collision, standard damage, move on. But they've learned. They wait. The icon starts pulsing. Slowly at first — a gentle throb, like a heartbeat. Then faster. The blue brightens toward white. The hexagon swells and contracts. The Brute is still frozen, still dangerous when it thaws. The pulsing accelerates. The icon is almost strobing now, bleaching to near-white, the element's color barely visible. The player swipes. Steam erupts. The ground mark has a bright ring around its edge that standard collisions don't. The damage number is 25% larger. The mana restore chimes at a higher pitch.

That held moment — the three-quarter second between "I could" and "I did" — is the skill ceiling of the collision system made visible. Every other player in the zone can see the pulsing icon. They know what the player is doing. They know the risk. When the resonance collision lands, the bright ring on the ground mark is a signature.

#### Visual Philosophy

**The pulse is the signal.** It must be readable at a glance, even in chaotic multi-enemy fights. The pulsing should feel organic — like the status itself is straining to resolve, like the two elements are pulling toward each other. Not a UI blink. A physical tension.

**Pulse curve:** Not linear. Ease-in — starts slow, accelerates. The first pulse feels like a nudge. The last pulse before expiry feels urgent, almost frantic. The player's gut tells them "now or never" before their brain calculates the math.

**Color shift:** The element's signature color drains toward white as resonance deepens. This communicates "time is running out" through the language the game has already taught — white is neutral, elemental color is potency. The icon losing its color is the status losing its life. Triggering at peak resonance means triggering when the icon is almost gone.

**Resonance ring on ground marks:** The ring is subtle but distinctive — a thin bright halo around the standard collision ground mark. Not flashy. Just present enough that a player who didn't time their collision notices the absence. Over time, players learn to associate the ring with mastery. Seeing someone else's resonance rings on the ground is like seeing clean brushstrokes — you know someone skilled was here.

**No UI indicator for the window itself.** No progress bar, no timer overlay, no "PERFECT!" popup. The pulse IS the indicator. The game teaches timing through feel, not through UI. A player who watches the pulse and learns its rhythm is a better player than one who watches a countdown timer. Resonance rewards attention to the game world, not attention to the HUD.

#### Resonance by Archetype Feel

**Greatsword Bruiser — Resonance feels like:** Coiling a spring. The big slow weapon wants to hit hard. Waiting through the resonance window with a Greatsword feels like pulling back a bowstring — the tension is physical. The release is devastating. This is the archetype where resonance damage bonus matters most because each hit is so infrequent.

**Bow Ranger — Resonance feels like:** Patience from distance. The Ranger can afford to wait — they're out of melee range, kiting safely. Resonance rewards the Ranger's natural playstyle: stay back, observe, fire at the perfect moment. The frost arrow's hex icon pulsing on a distant enemy while the Ranger circles is the game's most aesthetically clean combat moment.

**Staff Mage — Resonance feels like:** Orchestration. Three enemies tagged with status, all pulsing, all approaching expiry at different times. The Mage tries to time the swipe cone to catch two or three resonance windows simultaneously. When it works — multiple resonance collisions in one cone — it's the Mage's peak fantasy. The ground is covered in ringed marks.

**Tank — Resonance feels like:** An afterthought. Tanks don't chase resonance — they're too busy blocking and absorbing. When a Tank accidentally triggers a resonance collision during a counter-attack window, it's a pleasant surprise, not the plan. This is correct — resonance is an offensive skill expression, and the Tank's skill expression is defensive.

**Battle Healer — Resonance feels like:** Compassionate timing. A Light weapon's Bless heal scales with the swipe. A resonance-timed Bless collision (Baptism, Salvation) produces a larger heal. The Healer's resonance skill isn't about damage — it's about timing the heal for when the party needs it most AND getting bonus healing for the patience.

**Control Specialist — Resonance feels like:** The widest window. High Influence extends status durations, which extends the resonance window proportionally. The Control Specialist has the most forgiving resonance timing in the game — and they deserve it, because their collision payoff is CC and debuffs rather than raw damage. The window width is their reward for investing in control over destruction.

#### The Primordial and Resonance

The Primordial encounter (§57) gains additional depth from resonance. The stability bar mechanic operates on large time scales (phase transitions, zone coverage). But within each phase, individual combat exchanges involve collision timing. A Hero who resonance-times their Light collisions during Phase 3 (when the Primordial is cornered and desperate) is threading the needle between the Primordial's rapid attacks and the resonance window's closing pulse. The pulsing status icon on a 320×320 void-entity while shadow tendrils are flying — that's the game's most demanding timing challenge, and the resonance ring on the resulting collision ground mark is the most earned ring in the entire game.

The Arbiter's Eclipse resonance in the Primordial arena is the narrative peak expressed as a timing challenge. Curse on the Dark Primordial, Reveal on the Light Primordial, both statuses pulsing as the Arbiter positions at the center boundary, both entering resonance window simultaneously — the Arbiter triggers Eclipse at the exact moment both icons are strobing white — the biggest sound in the game plays with the ascending resonance harmonic on top — the stability bar jumps — the two Primordials are forced closer together.

That is the game's best moment. It cannot be bought, optimized, or shortcut. It can only be earned through hundreds of hours of learning to read the pulse.

#### Resonance Streak — Tireless Flow State (v12.38)

The v12.38 Resonance Streak (GDD §5.7.7) layers a mana-restoration bonus onto consecutive resonance-timed collisions within a 10-second rolling window. Each streak step amplifies the mana restored by the next resonance-timed collision, capping at 5 steps (+50% mana). The creative register for this mechanic is *tireless flow state*, not *devastating escalation* — a deliberate register choice that distinguishes the streak from the damage-amplification frame most games would reach for.

**Why "tireless" and not "devastating."** Most action games conflate flow state with increasing raw power — the longer the player sustains combo, the harder they hit. This register produces a "chasing a damage number" feel that rewards precision in terms of *output magnitude*. Bro Town's flow state is different. The resonance window already rewards precision with bonus damage (up to +30% at peak, §5.7.2). The streak layer instead rewards *sustained precision* with resource refill — a player in flow isn't hitting harder, they're *never running out*. This captures what ARPG flow actually feels like in the hand: the sense that your tools keep their edge as long as you keep your focus. The streak makes the game feel *tireless* the longer you're engaged, which is the authentic psychological state flow induces.

**The streak badge — quiet confidence, not excitement.** The streak counter displays as a small numeric badge next to the combo count (§5.9 UI area). The badge's visual register is calibrated to *confirmation* rather than *celebration*. No pulsing outer ring. No color escalation at high streaks. No particle emission when streak increments. The badge simply shows the current streak count and fades slightly as the 10-second window approaches expiry. A player in high streak should feel the confirmation without feeling the *announcement* of their own flow state — announcing the flow state would break it. The fade-on-window-expiry is the one subtle signal that communicates "hurry if you want to keep this going" without using a timer number.

**The silence of streak loss.** Losing the streak is *quiet*. When the 10-second window expires or a non-resonance collision fires, the badge disappears. No audio sting, no screen effect, no popup saying "streak broken." This discipline is load-bearing for the flow-state register — narrating the loss of flow state is worse than the loss itself. A player who drops out of flow notices it because they were *in* flow; the absence of the positive sensation is the feedback. The game's silence at the moment of loss respects the player's capacity to feel the drop without being told about it. Players who care about streaking will rebuild their streak immediately; players who weren't paying attention to their streak won't notice its absence. Both outcomes are correct.

**The mana-flow channel as flow-state surface.** The streak's effect is felt in the mana-bar dynamics rather than in the damage-number layer. A chained-resonance player watches their mana bar stay fuller than baseline during extended combat — the bar's *behavior* becomes the flow state's visible signature. This is a subtle but intentional channel choice: the mana bar is already part of the HUD, already visible peripherally, and already associated with sustain. Streak amplifies the bar's fullness without adding a new visual element. A veteran player learns to read mana-bar behavior as flow-state telemetry in a way that reads as lived-in mastery rather than as numeric optimization.

**Interaction with §3.5 mana entropy grace period.** The grace period (v12.33 planned for later ship) reduces mana decay rate during active engagement; the streak increases mana restoration amount per collision. Together they produce a multi-channel flow-state affordance: less decay, more restoration, felt in the same mana-bar behavior. The two mechanisms compound without overlapping because they operate on different sides of the mana ledger (loss rate vs gain amount). A player in full flow (sustained engagement triggering grace period + resonance streak) experiences the mana bar as more durable than the base design parameters would suggest — *tireless*.

**What streak is NOT.** Not a damage multiplier. Not a new HUD element beyond the small badge. Not celebrated through audio, particles, or screen effects. Not carried across exploration (the 10s window ensures streak is about single-engagement flow, not across-session compounding). Not a party-shared resource (each player builds their own, preserving individual skill expression). Not a mandatory mechanic (a player who never resonance-times their collisions still plays the game exactly as before — they just never feel the tireless layer). The streak is an additive feel-state for players who sustain precision; its absence is not a penalty.

### 8.20 Contextual Dodge — Creative Direction

#### The Lunge

**What the player sees (melee):** They're approaching a Brute. Normally they'd walk into range and start swinging. But they have the Brute locked on — red rectangle framing it. They swipe toward it. Instead of a sideways dodge roll, the character dashes forward — a committed, forward thrust with weapon extended. They close the gap in a blink. The weapon connects on arrival. The Frost icon appears on the Brute. They're already in auto-attack range with status ticking. The collision setup began the instant they entered the fight.

**The lunge feels like:** Initiative. Not the stat — the feeling. The difference between "I walked into this fight" and "I chose the exact moment this fight began." The lunge is a declaration. It says: I see you, I'm coming, and by the time I arrive, the clock is already ticking on your defeat.

**Animation direction:** The lunge is NOT a dodge roll toward the enemy. It's a distinct animation — a forward lean, weapon-first, weight committed. The character's body language communicates intent. A dodge roll says "escape." A lunge says "attack." The silhouette must be different: dodge is curled and compact, lunge is extended and aggressive. Even at pixel-art scale, the distinction should be readable.

**Lunge by weapon type:**

*Greatsword lunge:* Heavy, ground-covering. The character slides forward with the greatsword extended horizontally — a charging slash. The weight of the weapon carries the movement. Feels like a freight train.

*Sword lunge:* Quick, precise. A fencer's thrust — weapon tip leading, body following. Faster animation than greatsword lunge, tighter movement arc. Feels like a striking snake.

#### The Retreat Shot

**What the player sees (ranged):** They're kiting a pack with their frost bow. Enemies are closing in. They swipe away — their character rolls backward, and mid-roll, snaps off a frost arrow at the locked target. The arrow flies backward from a moving archer. The Freeze icon appears on the pursuing enemy. The player is already at a new position, already swapping to their storm bow, already planning the Hailstorm collision. The retreat wasn't a retreat — it was the opening move of the next attack.

**The retreat shot feels like:** Competence. The character is a better fighter than the player's raw input would suggest. All the player did was "dodge away." The character turned that into a tactical repositioning with a free status application. Over time, players start to feel that their character has learned to fight — every retreat seeds the next engagement. Running away stops feeling like failure and starts feeling like strategy.

**Animation direction:** The retreat shot is a dodge roll with a backward glance. Partway through the roll, the character's upper body twists toward the locked target — briefly — and the projectile fires from that twisted position. The twist is fast, subtle, and communicates "I'm leaving, but I'm not done with you." It should look reflexive, not deliberate. The character does this because they're good, not because they planned it.

*Bow retreat shot:* A backward-fired arrow. The character's bow arm extends behind them mid-roll. The arrow's trajectory is a flat line from the roll's midpoint to the target — no arc, no travel time ambiguity. Clean and immediate.

*Staff retreat shot:* A backward burst from the staff's tip. The cone is narrower than a standard staff AOE — a focused blast, not a sweeping wave. The staff crackles briefly at the tip during the roll. The projectile(s) fire from behind the rolling character.

#### The Retreat Shot and the Battlefield Read

The retreat shot fundamentally changes how ranged players see space. Without it, backing away from enemies is dead time — you're not attacking, not setting up, just surviving. With it, every retreat is a brush stroke. A Ranger who retreat-shots three different enemies during a kiting sequence has just painted three status effects across the battlefield. When they stop running and start swiping, three collisions fire in sequence. The retreat shots were the setup. The fight was won while they were running away.

### 8.21 Combo Chain — Creative Direction

#### The Heartbeat

The combo chain is the pulse underneath everything. Resonance is the held breath. The lunge is the leap. The retreat shot is the pivot. The combo chain is the heartbeat — the steady one-two-three that the player's body learns before their mind names it.

**What the player feels at Level 5:** They're auto-attacking a Fodder. They notice their sword glowing faintly, then brighter, then brightest. They don't know why. They swipe. The collision feels a little stronger than last time. Maybe. They're not sure. They keep playing. Over the next hour, their subconscious associates the bright glow with better collisions. They start waiting for it without deciding to.

**What the player feels at Level 30:** They know the count. They can feel hit 1, hit 2, hit 3 without looking at the weapon glow. They know that swiping at full glow makes the next status last longer. They're starting to chain cycles — invest, cash in, invest, cash in. Against a Brute, they wait for bright glow. Against Fodder, they swipe on faint glow and move on. The count is a language they speak fluently without having studied it.

**What the player feels at Level 80:** The combo chain is invisible. Not because they stopped tracking it — because it's been absorbed into their combat rhythm so completely that it's no longer a separate thought. The weapon glow cycles and their hands respond. Hit 1, hit 2, hit 3 — the decision to swipe at each position is instantaneous and contextual. They don't think "should I swipe at count 2?" They think "there's a cluster" and their hands swipe at count 2 because the spread is the right tool. The combo chain has become fluency.

#### Now, Wide, Next — The Three Words

The combo positions have names that the player community will invent their own versions of. But the design intent maps to three temporal horizons:

**Now** is this collision, this moment, this enemy. The player needs damage and they need it fast. Hit 1 swipe. The weapon was barely glowing. The collision burst is the reward for decisiveness. Now feels *sharp*.

**Wide** is this collision plus the next target. The player sees the cluster and thinks one step ahead. Hit 2 swipe. The collision detonates and the status arcs to a nearby enemy — a brief connecting line of element color, there and gone. The battlefield just expanded. The player didn't have to auto-attack that second enemy. The collision reached for them. Wide feels *generous*.

**Next** is the next cycle, the next collision, the next resonance window. The player is thinking two steps ahead. Hit 3 swipe. The collision detonates with burst and spread, and somewhere in the player's peripheral awareness, the knowledge that their next status will last longer. They can't see it yet. They'll feel it on the next cycle when the resonance window opens earlier and holds longer. Next feels *patient*.

Three feelings. Three time horizons. The player oscillates between them based on what the fight asks for. Against a lone Brute with a deep HP pool, the rhythm is: Next, Now, Next, Now — invest, cash in, invest, cash in. Against a Swarm, the rhythm is: Wide, Wide, Wide — every collision seeds the next target. Against a boss phase transition, the rhythm is: Next, Next, Next — stack investment for the hardest phase. The combo chain doesn't prescribe a rhythm. It gives the player a vocabulary for the rhythms they discover.

#### The Weapon Glow

The glow is the combo chain's only visual language. It must be readable without demanding attention.

**Design principle:** The glow should feel like the weapon is *charging* — not mechanically, not like a progress bar, but like the weapon is accumulating intent. Faint glow: the weapon remembers it just hit something. Medium glow: the weapon is warm with sustained contact. Bright glow: the weapon is ready for something bigger than an auto-attack.

**Glow per weapon type:**

*Greatsword:* The glow runs along the blade edge. At faint, it's a hairline of element color. At bright, the entire edge is lit and the flat of the blade reflects the color faintly. The Greatsword's glow is heavy and solid — it looks like heated metal.

*Sword:* The glow concentrates at the tip and runs partway down the blade. At bright, the tip is a point of intense element color. The Sword's glow is precise and focused — it looks like a pen about to write.

*Bow:* The glow lives on the bowstring. At faint, the string has a tint. At bright, the string hums with element color and the next arrow visibly carries a brighter trail. The Bow's glow is taut — it looks like tension.

*Staff:* The glow lives at the head of the staff — the gem or crystal or focal point. At faint, a dim inner light. At bright, the head radiates element color in a soft halo. The Staff's glow is arcane — it looks like accumulated knowledge.

#### The Spread Arc

When a count 2+ collision spreads status to a nearby enemy, a brief visual arc connects the two enemies for 0.3 seconds. The arc is a thin line in the collision's element color — not a lightning bolt, not a chain, just a line. A connection. It says "the collision reached further than the point of impact."

The arc should feel incidental, not dramatic. The spread is a bonus, not a spectacle. The player notices it in their peripheral vision: "oh, that one has Freeze now too." The arc confirmed what happened. It didn't celebrate it.

**Sound:** The spread has no unique sound. The collision sound plays normally. The status application chime plays on the spread target 100-200ms after the collision sound — the slight delay creates a "and also..." feeling. The collision landed... and also reached the next one.

#### The Extended Status Signal

When the count 3 investment applies an extended-duration status on the next auto-attack, the status icon above the target renders slightly larger than normal for 0.5 seconds before settling to standard size. This brief size pulse is the only visual distinction between a normal status and an extended one.

**Why subtle:** The extended status feeds into the resonance window, which has its own visual language (pulsing icon, color drain toward white). The extended status signal should not compete with resonance's visual language — it should set the stage for it. The brief size pulse says "this one is going to last." The resonance pulse that follows later says "and now it's ready." Two sequential signals, not two simultaneous ones.

#### Combo Chain and the Primordial

In the Primordial encounter, auto-attack windows are short and dangerous. The Primordial attacks constantly. Each opening for auto-attacks is 2-3 seconds long. The combo chain transforms these windows from "swing as many times as possible" to "how many hits can I fit, and what does that mean for my swipe?"

A 2-second window against the Primordial might only allow hit 1 and hit 2. The player swipes at count 2 — burst + spread. The spread is wasted (the Primordial is alone in the arena). But the burst matters. Every collision against the Primordial is precious.

A rare 3-second window allows hit 3. The player reaches bright glow and swipes — burst + spread (wasted) + investment. The next status application has extended duration. The next resonance window will be wider. The player just turned a generous opening into an advantage that compounds into the next cycle.

The combo chain in the Primordial fight becomes a risk assessment of opening length. Short opening: hit 1 swipe, take what you can get. Long opening: commit to hit 3, invest in the future. The player reads the Primordial's attack patterns not just to dodge but to *count how many auto-attacks they can fit*. The combo chain gives the dodge timing a second layer of meaning.

#### Visual Combo Posture — Character as Combo Signal (v12.42)

The v12.42 combo posture (GDD §5.9.8) extends the weapon-glow combo indicator into the character's body itself. Weapon glow tiers communicate count through the tool; posture tiers communicate count through the wielder. The two channels compose — at count 3 a player sees their weapon bright with element color *and* their character body braced in a weapon-specific flourish pose. The creative register for posture specifically: *the character looks like they're about to deliver a payoff*, not "the character is showing off."

**The four flourish poses as character-identity signatures.**

**Greatsword count-3 flourish — blade planted, weight coiling.** The Greatsword Bruiser plants the blade tip in the ground between their feet, both hands gripping the hilt, shoulders squared. Weight shifts back slightly as though drawing the bow of the body. The pose reads as *physically coiled* — the character is gathering the energy of the earth through the planted blade, preparing to release it in the next swing. This is not a showy ready-stance; it is a working pose. A Greatsword player approaching a difficult enemy at count 3 *looks* ready, and the enemy's aggro arrow (per §11.1) pointing at the player creates a compound signal: the character is braced and the threat knows it.

**Sword count-3 flourish — ready, narrow, precise.** The Sword Duelist raises the blade to shoulder height, pointed forward along the enemy's likely approach line. Off-hand extends behind the body for balance and to present a narrower silhouette. The torso angles slightly sideways — the classic fencer's ready pose. The character's weight is forward on the front foot, ready to lunge or retreat with equal ease. This is *precise readiness* — the opposite of the Greatsword's planted weight. The Sword wielder at count 3 communicates, through posture alone, that they're tracking the enemy's movement and preparing to intercept.

**Bow count-3 flourish — already drawn, already aimed.** The Bow Archer draws the arrow to full tension at the anchor point, with draw-hand fingers just behind the ear. The off-hand holds the bow at full extension. Unlike the Sword's dynamic ready pose or the Greatsword's braced stance, the Bow's count-3 pose is *static tension* — the shot is formed and held, waiting for release. A Bow player at count 3 is visibly *not shooting*, which is the point. The arrow is ready; the release will happen when the player decides. This static quality makes the Bow's count-3 pose read as a moment of held breath.

**Staff count-3 flourish — the spell is formed.** The Staff Mage suspends the gem or crystal visibly above the staff tip — ~30cm above the tip with faint particles orbiting the gem. The character's off-hand is raised with palm open toward the gem, channeling. Unlike the other three weapons where the flourish visibly *prepares*, the Staff's flourish visibly *holds* — the spell is already formed, already gathered, already at full power; what remains is the release. Particles around the gem orbit slowly, indicating sustained magical potential rather than accumulating buildup. A Staff player at count 3 is visibly carrying magical weight, which matches the mage archetype's narrative identity of "power reservoir" over "power accumulator."

**Weapon-identity signatures in body language.** The four poses carry each weapon type's personality into the combo-state signal:
- Greatsword: *weight, commitment, earth*
- Sword: *precision, mobility, air*
- Bow: *stillness, focus, held breath*
- Staff: *magic, gathered power, sustained potential*

A player who switches between weapon types across their career learns their own character's flourish vocabulary across all four. A Greatsword main who picks up a Bow for a specific encounter sees their character adopt the Bow's held-breath pose and feels the tonal shift from weight to stillness — the weapon change is read through body, not just through the weapon swap itself.

**Posture as public character identity.** Per §5.9.5's existing "visible to all players" rule, combo posture is public information. A party of four entering a dungeon with all players at count 3 for their respective weapons produces a diverse tableau: the Greatsword braced, the Sword ready-stance, the Bow drawn, the Staff's gem suspended. The team's combat readiness is legible to each member through everyone's body language simultaneously. A spectator watching a high-skill player reach count 3 in Arena play sees the flourish pose resolve and understands *"this player has played the full combo chain and is about to deliver"* before the next swipe lands. This public-visibility discipline treats combat skill as a spectator-friendly property.

**Respect for the mechanical constraint — posture never compromises responsiveness.** The posture is purely visual; a character in count-3 flourish who starts running shifts smoothly between the flourish pose and the run animation without stutter. Combat inputs — attack, dodge, block, swipe — remain fully responsive throughout posture states. This discipline is load-bearing: if any part of the flourish pose locked the character even briefly, combat feel would degrade, and the posture would become a cost rather than a layer. The 0.2s interpolation rule applies only to the *visual* posture transition; the character's mechanical state (position, velocity, ability availability) never pauses for posture animation.

### 8.22 Journey Log — Creative Direction

The Journey Log is the player's autobiography written in game events. Not prose — a chronological list of milestones with timestamps. First kill. First death. First zone discovery. Each quest completion with the moral choice noted. Each collision discovered. Level milestones. The ending. The Primordial.

**Tone:** Clinical, not sentimental. "Day 3: First visited Frozen Shore." "Day 12: Completed Mayor Bro quest (Good path)." "Day 45: Reached Level 50." The log doesn't interpret. It records. The sentiment comes from the player reading back through their own history and feeling the arc that emerged from hundreds of small choices.

**The return moment:** A player who stopped playing for two weeks opens the game. They don't remember where they were. They open the Journey Log. Last entry: "Day 34: Cleared Depth 5 Frost Dungeon. Reached Level 52." They know exactly where they are, what they were doing, and what comes next. The log is a re-onboarding tool disguised as a narrative feature.

**The social moment:** Two players comparing Journey Logs. "You went Evil on the Beastmaster quest? I couldn't sacrifice my pet." The log turns alignment choices from a final score into a visible sequence of decisions across time. The two Evil choices aren't a number — they're moments in a timeline where something changed.

**The final read:** A player who defeats the Primordial scrolls back through their entire Journey Log. First kill. First death. First collision. First dungeon. The NPC tone shifts. The alignment choices. The Convergence. The corridor. The Primordial. One sentence at the end — the Final Echo Stone text, logged as the last entry. The log is the game's story, and the story is the player's life in this world.

### 8.23 Death Screen — Creative Direction

**One line. The killing blow.** "Killed by Ember Brute (Flame)." "Killed by Burn DoT." "Killed by Magma collision splash." Displayed below the respawn timer in the same font as damage numbers. No analysis, no blame, no detailed breakdown. Just the thing that actually ended you.

**Tone:** Matter-of-fact. The death itself is whimsical (limbs detach in a comical pile). The killing blow line is dry and informational — a clinical footnote beneath the comedy. The tonal contrast makes both work: the animation says "that was unfortunate," the text says "here's why."

**The teaching pattern:** The player doesn't learn from one death. They learn from five. The first "Killed by Burn DoT" is information. The third is a pattern. The fifth is a lesson: "Burn keeps killing me during recovery — I need fire resistance or faster healing." The death screen doesn't teach. It plants seeds that become understanding over repetition.

**What the line should never do:** Blame party members. Reference other players' actions. Show the damage pipeline. Explain what the player could have done differently. The line is what happened, not what should have happened. The player draws their own conclusions.

### 8.24 Codex Tactical Hints — Creative Direction

**One question per collision. The player answers it by playing.**

The hints are written in the same voice as Echo Stones — short, present-tense, oblique. They don't tell the player what to do. They ask a question whose answer is a strategy the player discovers through experimentation. "What happens to a Brute that can't see?" The player tries Steam against a Brute and discovers the miss chance area. The Codex asked the question. The game answered it.

**Rules for writing Codex hints:**
- One sentence. Always a question. Never a statement.
- Present tense. No conditional ("what would happen if..."). Direct: "What happens when..."
- The question should be answerable by triggering the collision against the right target type. The answer is in the gameplay, not in the text.
- Emotional over tactical where possible. "Who else is hurting right now?" (Baptism) points toward party healing through empathy, not through a damage formula.
- Eclipse's hint is the only one that matches its Echo Stone significance: "What if destroying and healing were the same thing?" This is the game's central question expressed as a Codex entry.

### 8.25 Archetype Death Behaviors — Creative Direction

**Brute Collapse:** The Brute was the enemy that tested your patience. Its death should feel like the release of tension, not just a health bar reaching zero. The stagger-hold-fall sequence gives the player a beat to breathe between "it's dead" and "it's gone." The ground shake on impact is the Brute's last statement — a reminder that this thing had mass, had presence, had weight. At higher depths, the 176×176 endgame-scale Brute's collapse (v12.40) should make the screen feel momentarily unstable. The dust cloud in the element's color is the Brute's final exhale.

**Sound direction for collapse:** The stagger is silent. The hold is a creak — structural stress, like a building about to fall. The impact is a deep, resonant thud that scales with sprite size. Small Brutes: a tap. Large Brutes: a boom you feel in your chest. The dust cloud has a soft hiss — particles settling.

**Swarm Flinch Chain:** The Swarm is the enemy that teaches spatial awareness. Their death behavior should make the pack feel like an organism, not a collection of individuals. When one dies, the others *notice*. The flinch is tiny — a 0.1 second visual recoil, like a wince. But five Swarm members flinching simultaneously when you kill their leader looks like a ripple through a school of fish. The flinch makes the pack feel alive, which makes killing the pack feel like dismantling something that was cooperating against you.

**Sound direction for flinch:** No sound on the flinch itself. The flinch is visual only. Adding a sound per flinching Swarm member would create audio chaos in a group of five. The silence of the flinch is the point — it's a reaction, not an event. The kill sound plays on the dead member. The living members react in silence. The contrast between the kill's sound and the flinch's silence makes the flinch more noticeable, not less.

### 8.26 Flora/Thorn — Creative Direction

#### The Living Element

Flora is the only element that fights back *passively*. Every other status does something to the enemy directly — burns them, freezes them, slows them, shocks them. Thorn does something to the enemy *when the enemy does something to you*. The status turns the afflicted target into a hazard. Hit someone with Thorn active, and you take damage for the privilege.

This inverts the game's fundamental combat grammar. The player has spent dozens of hours learning that applying status = setup, triggering collision = payoff. Thorn says: applying status = the payoff already started. The enemy has to decide whether to keep attacking a Thorn-afflicted target and eat the retaliatory damage, or switch targets and let the Thorn expire. Monsters don't make this decision (they follow threat tables). But in PvP — in the Lawless Land, in Clan Wars, in the Arena — Thorn creates a genuine tactical dilemma that no other status produces.

**What the player feels with a Flora weapon:** Patient. Inevitable. The Flora player isn't chasing burst damage. They're building a situation where the enemy damages itself. Thorn on the target, auto-attacks building combo count, wait for the vine network to do its work. Flora collisions lean toward sustain — heals, roots, regen zones. The Flora player is the one still standing when everyone else has burned through their resources.

**What the enemy feels fighting a Flora player:** Frustrated in the right way. Every hit they land costs them something. They can see the Thorn icon — the leaf glyph pulsing green above the target. They know they're paying for every swing. The correct response is to disengage, wait for Thorn to expire, and re-engage. But in the heat of combat, most players keep swinging. Thorn punishes impatience. That's its design identity.

#### Thorn Visual Language

**Status icon:** The Leaf glyph (§10.1) — Flora's element shape. Green (#2ECC71) with the standard Grow animation (a subtle expanding-contracting cycle, like breathing). When Thorn is active on a target, thin vine-like lines trace outward from the icon for 2–3 pixels in random directions, shifting slowly. The vines are the visual cue that this status is *reactive* — it reaches out.

**Thorn retaliatory hit:** When an enemy strikes a Thorn-afflicted target and takes retaliatory damage, a brief green thorn-spike visual flashes at the point of contact — a tiny sharp shape, 3–4 pixels, lasting 0.1 seconds. The direction points from the afflicted target toward the attacker. It's small enough to be subliminal during fast combat but visible in replays or careful observation. The sound is the Flora/Thorn collision sound from §2 — the organic creak-and-snap, but at reduced volume (30% of full collision volume). Thorn retaliation is a background tax, not a foreground event.

**Thorn on the ground (post-collision):** Flora collision ground marks use the Grow animation — green patches that expand slightly before fading, as if briefly alive. Heal-type Flora collisions (Bloom, Sanctuary, Thaw Bloom) have a softer, warmer green with tiny flower-like particles. Damage-type Flora collisions (Scorched Earth, Blight Garden, Decompose) have a darker, thornier green with sharp angular particles. The visual distinction between "Flora that heals" and "Flora that hurts" should be readable at a glance.

#### Verdant Wilds — Zone Personality (see also §3.2 planned mechanics)

The Verdant Wilds is where Flora's identity is strongest. It is the only zone where the environment itself uses Thorn — pollen clouds apply Thorn status to the *player*, making the zone fight back the same way a Flora weapon does. The zone teaches Thorn's mechanic through environmental exposure before the player ever equips a Flora weapon.

**The Wilds feel like:** A place that doesn't want you there but isn't hostile — just aggressively alive. Vines close paths behind you (must be cut or burned to reopen). Carnivorous plants grab and hold (mash to escape — a brief CC that isn't Freeze or Root, just the environment asserting itself). Bioluminescent fungi reveal hidden paths in deeper depths — the zone rewards curiosity. The overall impression is lush, overgrown, slightly suffocating. Life didn't just survive here. It won.

**The Tank's home zone:** A Power/Vitality/Fortification build with a Flora weapon in the Verdant Wilds is Flora's peak fantasy. Fortification Thorns (§8.3) trigger on blocked hits. Thorn status triggers on all hits to the afflicted target. The zone's environmental Thorn applies to the player, so enemies that attack the Tank take retaliatory damage from *three* sources: Fortification Thorns, Thorn status on the Tank, and the environment's Thorn on the Tank. The Tank stands in the middle of the Verdant Wilds and everything that touches them regrets it.

#### Flora Codex Hints

These follow the same rules as §8.24 — one question per collision, present tense, emotional over tactical.

| Collision | Hint |
|-----------|------|
| Wildfire (Flame+Flora) | *"What if the fire had roots?"* |
| Thaw Bloom (Frost+Flora) | *"What grows when the ice finally melts?"* |
| Overgrowth (Water+Flora) | *"What happens when you water something that doesn't know when to stop?"* |
| Decompose (Venom+Flora) | *"What eats poison and asks for more?"* |
| Charged Canopy (Storm+Flora) | *"What conducts lightning better than metal? A forest."* |
| Reclaim (Stone+Flora) | *"What turns cracks into gardens?"* |
| Scorched Earth (Flora+Flame) | *"What burns greener than wood?"* |
| Brittle Thorn (Flora+Frost) | *"What's sharper — a thorn, or a frozen thorn?"* |
| Bloom (Flora+Water) | *"Who else needs this right now?"* |
| Blight Garden (Flora+Venom) | *"What grows in the place nothing should?"* |
| Lightning Rod (Flora+Storm) | *"What if the tallest thing in the field was on your side?"* |
| Petrified Wood (Flora+Stone) | *"What's stronger than stone? Stone that was alive."* |
| Scatter Seed (Flora+Wind) | *"What if the wind carried more than dust?"* |
| Stranglehold (Dark+Flora) | *"What tightens when you struggle?"* |
| Withering (Flora+Dark) | *"What happens to a garden that never sees light?"* |
| Sanctuary (Light+Flora) | *"Where do you go when everywhere else hurts?"* |
| Purifying Bloom (Flora+Light) | *"What blooms when the truth finally reaches the roots?"* |

### 8.27 Reverse Pairs — Creative Direction

#### Two Stories, Same Ingredients

Every element pair now tells two stories. Steam (Flame→Frost) is fire attacking ice — aggressive, explosive, damage-forward. Meltwater (Frost→Flame) is ice surrendering to heat — passive, pooling, control-forward. Same two elements. Different initiator. Different emotional register.

**The design principle:** The original direction (upper triangle) expresses what the *trigger* does to the setup. The reverse direction expresses what the *setup* does when disrupted. Steam is Flame's story. Meltwater is Frost's story. The two collisions share ingredients but have completely different authors.

**What this means for weapon orientation:** A Flame/Frost Fusion weapon in one orientation is a burst machine (auto-attack applies Burn, swipe triggers with Frost = Steam). In the other orientation it's a control tool (auto-attack applies Freeze, swipe triggers with Flame = Meltwater). The player choosing element orientation is choosing between two playstyles. This was always mechanically true — now it's emotionally distinct.

#### Directional Volatile — The Orientation Bet

The volatile bonus (+30%) now applies only when the strong element is the trigger. For a Flame/Frost weapon, Flame beats Frost on the effectiveness circle. So Meltwater (Freeze setup + Flame trigger) gets the volatile bonus. Steam (Burn setup + Frost trigger) does not.

**What this feels like in practice:** The player choosing orientation isn't just choosing between burst and control — they're choosing between a 30% damage bonus on the control collision and standard damage on the burst collision. Meltwater with volatile bonus might actually outdamage Steam despite being a "control" collision. The orientation choice becomes a genuine decision with competing incentives, not a solved problem.

**The visual cue:** Volatile Fusion weapons crackle at the element boundary. The crackle subtly favors the strong element's side — a Flame/Frost weapon crackles more on the Flame edge. This whispers "Flame is dominant here" without the game ever stating it. Players who notice the asymmetric crackle and experiment with orientation discover the volatile direction on their own. The crackle teaches.

**Dark↔Light is the exception:** Both Eclipse and Umbra are volatile. Mutual destruction, mutual +30%. The only weapon where both orientations get the bonus. A Dark/Light weapon — the game's rarest and most thematically loaded combination — rewards both directions equally. This is mechanically unique and narratively correct: neither darkness nor light dominates the other.

**Visual direction for reverse collisions:** The ground mark uses the *setup* element's color palette, not the trigger's. Steam (Flame→Frost) leaves a red-tinged steam cloud. Meltwater (Frost→Flame) leaves a blue-tinged puddle. Same collision pair, different visual author. Players can tell which direction the collision was triggered from the ground mark alone.

**Sound direction:** Reverse collisions use a reversed envelope of the original sound — same frequencies, swapped attack/decay. Where Steam has a sharp explosive attack, Meltwater has a slow pooling swell. The ear recognizes the kinship without consciously analyzing it. The reverse pairs sound like echoes of the originals.

#### Wind as Setup — The Orchestrator

Wind had zero setup collisions for the entire early and mid game. Slow was a dead end — it slowed targets and expired. The 9 new Wind-as-setup collisions turn Slow into a *launchpad*. Every trigger element transforms the Slow into something different: Updraft displaces, Frostbite bites, Downpour spreads, Plague Wind infects, Cyclone pulls, Sandstorm blinds, Overgrow roots, Dread Wind terrifies, Zephyr liberates.

**What the Wind player feels:** Like a conductor choosing which instrument plays next. The Slow is on the target. The Wind player's weapon swap — or their party member's trigger — determines what happens. Wind doesn't destroy. Wind *redirects*. The Control Specialist with a Wind setup weapon has the most options per status application of any build in the game.

**What Wind-as-setup pays for versatility:** The lowest average collision damage of any setup element (INV-19). Wind doesn't burst. It enables. A Wind/Flame weapon can Updraft for displacement or Firestorm for spread — but neither hits as hard as a raw Flame/Frost Steam. The tradeoff is choice vs power.

### 8.28 Dark Corruption & Light Redemption — Creative Direction

#### Two Directions

The 14 palette→Dark and palette→Light collisions create the game's clearest emotional gradient. The same setup status — Burn, Freeze, Soak, Root, Shock, Fracture, Slow — becomes something entirely different depending on whether darkness or light touches it.

**Palette→Dark = Corruption.** The status becomes twisted, hidden, fear-amplified. Shadowfire hides the Burn icon from the target — they're taking damage and can't see why. Dread Chill lingers after Freeze ends — the fear outlasts the ice. Nightshade feeds on missing HP — the weaker the target, the harder it hurts. Abyss doubles Soak vulnerability — the target drowns in amplified damage. Every Dark corruption collision should make the player think: "that's *mean*."

**Palette→Light = Redemption.** The status is purified, converted, redirected into something generous. Sacred Flame turns Burn into a party damage buff. Holy Water turns Soak into an AOE cleanse. Antidote turns Root into a heal. Diamond Dust turns Freeze into a crit chance field. Every Light redemption collision should make the player think: "that's *kind*."

**Exception — Judgment (Storm→Light):** Not all Light collisions heal. Judgment is the hardest-hitting single-target burst in the palette→Light category. Light isn't only merciful. It's also righteous. Sometimes truth is a hammer. Judgment sounds different from other Light collisions — no ascending healing chime. Just impact.

**Exception — Mend (Stone→Light):** Mend doesn't heal the enemy — it protects the attacker. Fracture stacks consumed, burst damage dealt, and the attacker gains temporary damage resistance per stack. The light that fills the cracks fills *your* cracks. A Stone/Light weapon on a Tank is a self-buff loop: Fracture the enemy with auto-attacks (building stacks), swipe with Light to trigger Mend, gain resistance that makes the next exchange easier. The Tank becomes harder to kill by fighting, not by hiding. The sound reflects this self-directed benefit — the Stone collision crack followed by a warm settling hum, like armor knitting itself back together.

**Weapon identity through collision direction:** A player with a Flame/Dark weapon makes Shadowfire — invisible, cruel, hidden damage. A player with a Flame/Light weapon makes Sacred Flame — fire that blesses allies. The element pairing tells you who the player is. This is alignment expressed through gear, not through quests.

### 8.29 Umbra — Creative Direction

#### Eclipse's Shadow

Eclipse (Dark→Light) is the game's thesis: destruction and restoration are the same act. Massive burst, heals allies, cleanses all statuses. It's the capstone. It's the moment the game's duality theme becomes a single mechanic. Eclipse is wholeness.

Umbra (Light→Dark) is the antithesis. Reveal (Light's status) is consumed by Dark. The resistances that Reveal stripped are permanently consumed — the target can never regain them for the rest of the fight. Moderate burst. No heal. No cleanse. Just exposure without mercy.

**What Umbra feels like:** A sentence. Not a kill — a judgment that the target carries for the rest of the encounter. The boss that lost fire resistance to Umbra takes +25% fire damage for the entire remaining fight. Every player in the room benefits. The Sovereign who triggered Umbra — through a *Light* weapon setup, the element opposite to their alignment — just made everyone else's job easier. And they did it by embracing truth (Reveal) and then drowning it in darkness (Dark trigger).

**The irony:** Eclipse requires moral complexity to unlock its True cosmetic variant (§33.5). Umbra requires *elemental* complexity — the Sovereign must equip a Light weapon, the element of truth, to access their signature collision. The game's most selfish path requires carrying its opposite. The game's most destructive alignment produces the most generous raid debuff.

**Visual:** Umbra's ground mark is Eclipse's inverted — gold center with dark edges, where Eclipse is dark center with gold edges. Same size. Same duration. Players who know both can tell at a glance which one fired. The Arbiter triggers Eclipse. The Sovereign triggers Umbra. Both leave marks that other players stop to look at.

**Sound:** Eclipse's sound played in reverse. The harmonic resolution that normally ends Eclipse instead *begins* Umbra, then decays into the Dark sub-bass shadow tone. Where Eclipse resolves into harmony, Umbra unravels from it. Three seconds. The most unsettling sound in the game. It should linger in the room like a question that was answered wrong.

### 8.30 Codex Hints — New Collisions (37)

Following §8.24 rules: one question per collision, present tense, emotional over tactical.

#### Reverse Palette Pairs

| Collision | Hint |
|-----------|------|
| Meltwater (Frost→Flame) | *"What does ice leave behind when it surrenders?"* |
| Scald (Water→Flame) | *"What's worse — cold water or hot?"* |
| Ash Cloud (Venom→Flame) | *"What does poison become when it burns?"* |
| Ignition (Storm→Flame) | *"What happens when the spark finds the fuel?"* |
| Forge (Stone→Flame) | *"What gets stronger in the fire?"* |
| Updraft (Wind→Flame) | *"What lifts you when the ground is burning?"* |
| Ice Shell (Water→Frost) | *"What protects better — water or the ice it becomes?"* |
| Frozen Vein (Venom→Frost) | *"What happens to poison when it can't flow?"* |
| Static Freeze (Storm→Frost) | *"What conducts cold as well as it conducts lightning?"* |
| Shale (Stone→Frost) | *"What breaks apart when the cold gets inside?"* |
| Frostbite (Wind→Frost) | *"What bites harder — the cold or the wind that carries it?"* |
| Dissolve (Venom→Water) | *"What washes clean?"* |
| Short Circuit (Storm→Water) | *"What happens when the current has nowhere to go?"* |
| Washout (Stone→Water) | *"What fills every crack?"* |
| Downpour (Wind→Water) | *"What falls when the wind finally stops?"* |
| Toxic Shock (Storm→Venom) | *"What happens when poison and lightning share a body?"* |
| Corrode (Stone→Venom) | *"What eats stone from the inside?"* |
| Plague Wind (Wind→Venom) | *"What carries sickness to everyone?"* |
| Tremor (Stone→Storm) | *"What shakes the ground when the sky already cracked it?"* |
| Cyclone (Wind→Storm) | *"What happens when the wind finds a center?"* |
| Sandstorm (Wind→Stone) | *"What blinds you with what it tore from the ground?"* |

#### Dark Corruption

| Collision | Hint |
|-----------|------|
| Shadowfire (Flame→Dark) | *"What burns when you can't see the flame?"* |
| Dread Chill (Frost→Dark) | *"What's left after the ice thaws and the fear doesn't?"* |
| Abyss (Water→Dark) | *"How deep does it go when you can't see the bottom?"* |
| Nightshade (Venom→Dark) | *"What grows in the dark that feeds on the weak?"* |
| Blackout (Storm→Dark) | *"What happens when all the lights go out at once?"* |
| Crumble (Stone→Dark) | *"What finds every crack you thought was sealed?"* |
| Dread Wind (Wind→Dark) | *"What makes you walk away from something you haven't seen?"* |

#### Light Redemption

| Collision | Hint |
|-----------|------|
| Sacred Flame (Flame→Light) | *"What burns for everyone instead of against them?"* |
| Diamond Dust (Frost→Light) | *"What does ice become when light passes through it?"* |
| Holy Water (Water→Light) | *"What blesses instead of drowning?"* |
| Antidote (Venom→Light) | *"What turns poison into medicine?"* |
| Judgment (Storm→Light) | *"What strikes once and never needs to strike again?"* |
| Mend (Stone→Light) | *"What fills cracks instead of making them?"* |
| Zephyr (Wind→Light) | *"What does wind become when it stops being angry?"* |

#### Remaining

| Collision | Hint |
|-----------|------|
| Overgrow (Wind→Flora) | *"What catches you when you're too slow to run?"* |
| Umbra (Light→Dark) | *"What can't hide after the light has already seen it?"* |

### 8.31 Collision Preview — Creative Direction

90 collisions is too many to memorize. The game doesn't ask the player to. The Codex is the reference. The collision preview in the loadout menu (§15.7) is the at-a-glance tool.

**What the player sees:** When they open element orientation (choosing which element is auto-attack vs swipe), two collision names and one-line effects display. The volatile indicator appears on whichever direction gets the +30% bonus. The player doesn't need to know the full matrix. They need to see the two collisions *their current weapon produces right now*.

**Design philosophy:** The preview answers "what does my weapon do?" not "what do all weapons do?" The Codex answers the second question. The preview answers the first. The preview is a lens. The Codex is a library.

**The discovery moment:** A player who has been using Steam for 20 levels swaps their element orientation and sees "Meltwater — AOE slow field (volatile +30%)" for the first time. They didn't know their weapon had a second collision. They didn't know the reverse direction was volatile. The preview taught them both in one glance. They try it. They feel the difference. They start thinking about orientation as a choice. The preview is a silent teacher.

**Collision name as tappable link:** Each collision name in the preview opens the Codex entry. The Codex hint — the single question — appears. "What does ice leave behind when it surrenders?" The player goes back to combat and tries Meltwater against different enemies. The hint's question becomes their experiment. The preview → Codex → combat loop is the full learning path for the 90-collision system.

### 8.32 NPC-Witnessed Lock Ceremony (v13 REVISED — replaces central Vows Shrine)

Per GDD §1.5.1 and §14.3.1 v13, stat locks are committed at the relevant NPC's location — not at a single central Shrine. The ceremony's creative register remains **formal without being sacred**, **deliberate without being heavy**: a lock is not a religious act but a craftsman's decision, made in a recognized place, witnessed by the community. v13 distributes the witnessing across the game's NPCs rather than concentrating it at a central Shrine, so the decision is witnessed *by someone specific* who knows the context of the commitment.

**Which NPC witnesses which lock.** The lock happens at the NPC most relevant to the locked stat or build. The Blacksmith witnesses weapon-hardening commitments and Power-stat locks. The Gem Cutter witnesses elemental-loadout commitments and ElementalMastery locks. The Arena Herald witnesses Ferocity and Agility locks (skill-expression stats). The Guardian Zone Keeper witnesses Fortification and Restoration locks (role-anchoring stats). The Mayor witnesses Vitality and Mind locks (capacity-anchoring stats). The Farmer witnesses Endurance locks (endurance is accumulated through farming and life-skill play). Each NPC carries a per-NPC VowsLedger / NPCVowLog tracking the locks they have witnessed, displayed publicly at their location. See §1.9.3 / GDD §14.3.1 for the full witness-to-stat mapping.

**Visual register — distributed shrinelets, not a central Shrine.** Each witness NPC has a small ceremonial space near them — not a shrine but a *recognized place*. The Blacksmith has a small anvil-side plaque where hardening commitments are carved. The Gem Cutter has a small stone panel beside their worktable. The Arena Herald has a small pillar at the arena entrance. Each location is small enough to feel domestic to its NPC rather than monumental. Stone base, simple carved patterns, warm neutral lighting. The space does not glow or pulse in idle state; it is unremarkable until activated.

**The ceremony animation.** A player approaching the witness NPC and committing to a lock enters interaction range. The character kneels on one knee (not fully prostrate — committed but upright), hands resting on the thigh, head bowed. The animation takes approximately 3 seconds total: 0.5s kneel, 1.5s hold with glyph formation, 1.0s rise. No UI overlays interrupt this. The glyph appears in the air roughly 2 meters in front of the character — warm ochre-gold color, ~6 geometric segments forming a recognizable but non-specific pattern. The glyph dissipates upward into light as the character rises. The NPC holds a still witness pose for the duration of the ceremony, without speaking. The witnessing is active but wordless — the NPC's presence is the acknowledgment.

**The affirmation line.** The fixed line *"This is who I am."* appears on screen roughly 1.5 seconds into the animation, held for ~1 second, fades. Typography: plain sans-serif, warm white, centered. No quotation marks. The line is not voiced — it is the player's own thought, rendered in text. Voicing it would turn it into an NPC declaration; leaving it as text keeps it internal.

**Per-NPC local acknowledgment (v13 REVISED — replaces golden light column).** Simultaneous with the glyph's formation, a brief golden halo rises from the witnessing NPC's location, visible within ~30 units but not across the town. The halo is soft-edged, semi-translucent, not blinding — a small lighthouse-beam catching dust motes, localized to the NPC's immediate area. Other players in the NPC's vicinity see the halo and understand that a commitment has been witnessed here. Players anywhere else in town see nothing. The acknowledgment is *local* to the community of people who are near this NPC at this moment, matching the intimacy of having a specific NPC witness the lock. The halo does not identify the player; observers must read the NPC's local VowsLedger panel to find out who committed.

**Per-NPC VowsLedger panel.** Each witness NPC has a local display panel — a small wooden or stone board — showing the 20 most recent lock and unlock entries for commitments that NPC has witnessed. Entries chisel themselves in real-time during the ceremony. Older entries visibly weather; the chisel marks fade as the entry scrolls toward the bottom. Tapping a name on the panel opens that player's inspect card. Players visiting a particular NPC can see "who recently committed through this NPC" as local community texture rather than as a server-wide ranking.

**The unlock ceremony.** Deliberately different in register from the lock. No kneeling — the character stands at the NPC's ceremonial space, turns slightly *away* from the glyph site, and watches the glyph dissipate rather than forming. The glyph that appears and immediately fades is the same ochre-gold color but fainter, less defined, as if already half-gone. No affirmation line. The "Vow Broken" entry appears on the NPC's VowsLedger panel with the same chiseling effect. The whole ceremony takes ~2 seconds. The mood is *release*, not *regret* — breaking a vow is not shame, it is a different kind of decision. The NPC's witness pose during unlock is visibly different: they turn slightly to the side, not directly facing the player. The NPC does not judge the unlock, but they also do not pretend it is the same weight as the lock.

**Audio.** The lock ceremony is accompanied by a single sustained note — a warm low-frequency hum, like a bell struck and held. The note begins as the character kneels, swells during the glyph formation, fades as the character rises. Audible within ~10m of the NPC; softly present within ~30m. The unlock ceremony plays a different note — slightly higher in pitch, with a small descending inflection at the end. The descending inflection is the audible signal that the ceremony is *release*, not *commitment*.

**Tone inspiration.** Japanese tea ceremony formality without any specific cultural appropriation. The care with which each motion is performed; the deliberate slowness; the presence of a recognized audience (the NPC stands in for the witnessing community, and the NPC's specific knowledge of the commitment's context — blacksmith on hardening, cutter on gems — adds weight that a generic central shrine could not provide). The affirmation line *"This is who I am"* is assertive but quiet — not a prayer, not a boast, a statement.

**What the ceremony is NOT.**
- Not religious in any specific cultural sense — no crosses, no lotuses, no specific iconography.
- Not melancholic — committing to your identity is assertive, not sad.
- Not performative — the ceremony happens whether or not anyone else is watching.
- Not elaborate — it should feel doable frequently without ever becoming tedious.
- Not centralized (v13) — there is no single Shrine; the ceremony happens at the relevant NPC's location.

**Creative thesis.** Players make dozens of menu-toggle decisions in any game session. The NPC-witnessed lock ceremony takes one specific decision — stat locking — and gives it the weight it deserves by *marking it* with a brief acknowledgment from a specific NPC who knows the context of the commitment. The witnessing is the value, not the gate. Distributing the witnessing across the game's NPC roster rather than concentrating it at a central Shrine (v13 reframing) has two effects: (1) each lock becomes personal to the NPC who witnesses it, producing specific social texture rather than generic ceremony; (2) the VowsLedger panels accumulate per-NPC records that become part of each NPC's social presence ("the Blacksmith who has witnessed the most hardening commitments"), integrating the lock system into the named-NPC social fabric the game has been building since v12.25.

**HISTORICAL RECORD — pre-v13 central Vows Shrine ceremony (superseded, preserved per Editing Protocol step 6):**

> *The Vows Shrine (GDD §1.5.1 and §14.3.1) is where players publicly commit to their character's identity. The ceremony's creative register is formal without being sacred, deliberate without being heavy. A lock is not a religious act — it is a craftsman's decision, made in a recognized place, witnessed by the server.*
>
> *Visual register.* The shrine itself is small — roughly the size of a large NPC stall, positioned near town center but not central. Stone base, carved with simple geometric patterns (not elaborate). A flat top holds the Recent Vows display (described below). Warm stone color, neutral lighting. The shrine does not glow or pulse in idle state; it is unremarkable until activated.
>
> *The ceremony animation.* A player approaching the shrine enters interaction range. On commit to a lock, the character kneels on one knee (not fully prostrate — committed but upright), hands resting on the thigh, head bowed. The animation takes approximately 3 seconds total: 0.5s kneel, 1.5s hold with glyph formation, 1.0s rise. No UI overlays interrupt this. The glyph appears in the air roughly 2 meters in front of the character — element-inflected but not attached to a specific element (locks aren't elemental). Color: a warm ochre-gold, similar to candlelight but slightly deeper. The glyph has ~6 geometric segments forming a recognizable but non-specific pattern. The glyph dissipates upward into light as the character rises.
>
> *The affirmation line.* The fixed line "This is who I am." appears on screen roughly 1.5 seconds into the animation, held for ~1 second, fades.
>
> *The golden light column.* Simultaneous with the glyph's formation, a golden light column extends upward from the shrine, visible across the town map for approximately 2 seconds. The column is soft-edged, semi-translucent, not blinding. Think of a lighthouse beam catching dust motes, not a stage spotlight. Other players anywhere in town can see the column — it is a server-wide "someone just committed" signal. The column does not identify the player; observers must approach the shrine to read the Recent Vows display to find out who.
>
> *The Recent Vows display.* A flat stone panel at the shrine's base, roughly waist-height, covered in inscribed text. The 20 most recent lock and unlock entries are visible as chiseled lines, freshest at top.

The pre-v13 central-Shrine design used a single location in town with a server-wide golden light column for commitment signaling. The v13 rework distributed the witnessing across the game's NPC roster for two specific reasons: (1) the central Shrine's generic-ceremony framing did not produce specific social texture — every lock looked the same regardless of what was locked; (2) the server-wide column signal, while elegant, was architecturally a cross-zone broadcast that did not fit the v13 per-NPC VowsLedger model where locks accumulate in the witness NPC's local record rather than in a server-wide ranking. The pre-v13 body is preserved here for design-provenance. Both designs shared the ceremony-animation pattern, affirmation line, and audio treatment; the v13 revision localizes the witnessing and replaces the server-wide column with per-NPC local halo while preserving these shared elements.

### 8.33 Per-Fight Role Title Flavor

The role titles assigned post-fight (GDD §7.1) are meant to feel like *characterizations a friend would make about you* — slightly heroic, slightly dry, never sycophantic. Think hardboiled nicknames from noir fiction ("The Closer," "The Ghost") more than RPG class titles ("Grand Marshal," "Mythic Hero"). The titles observe the player's play rather than celebrating it.

**Tone guidelines for the ~30-title pool.**

- **Observational, not evaluative.** "The Spearhead" is what you *did* in that fight, not a judgment that you're great. Contrast: "Damage Master" (evaluative, avoid) vs. "The Spearhead" (observational, keep).
- **Singular noun with a definite article.** "The [noun]" pattern is consistent across the pool. "The Closer" not "Closer" or "Fight Closer." The article gives each title a small dramatic weight — you were *the* one who did this thing in that fight.
- **Body-action or role-metaphor nouns, not abstract concepts.** "The Bulwark" (a physical wall) is good; "The Protector" (an abstract role) is weaker. "The Scalpel" (a precise tool) is good; "The Precisionist" (an abstract trait) is weaker.
- **Rare titles should reward rare behavior.** "The Lifeline" firing on a single decisive healing action in a critical moment is powerful because that specific situation is rare. A party member earning The Lifeline produces a memorable fight story.
- **Dry humor is welcome when the contribution pattern is unusual.** A player whose entire contribution was one massive crit against a Brute with no other action might earn "The Hail Mary" — dry, observational, slightly funny without being mocking.

**Sample titles with flavor text.**

- **The Spearhead** — *"You went first. You went hardest."*
- **The Closer** — *"You put the last nail in."*
- **The Bulwark** — *"Nothing got past you."*
- **The Ghost** — *"They couldn't land a hand on you."*
- **The Scalpel** — *"Every hit mattered. Every hit landed."*
- **The Anchor** — *"You were the reason they swung at you."*
- **The Healer** — *"You were the difference between a win and a funeral."*
- **The Opportunist** — *"You found every crack in their armor."*
- **The Tactician** — *"You layered them in problems."*
- **The Lifeline** — *"One critical moment. You were there."*

(Full pool of ~30 titles + flavor text authored in `role-titles.json` as v12.25.1 content ship.)

**Display typography.** Title and flavor render in a warm off-white sans-serif against a subtle dark overlay, centered in the lower third of the screen. The title is ~24pt; the flavor line is ~16pt in a lighter weight. The popup fades in over 0.3s, holds for 2.5s, fades out over 0.3s — never interrupts combat input. In party play, each player's title popup is positioned near their character portrait on the party panel.

**Long-term signature display.** The signature line on the inspect card (GDD §7.2) renders as *"Fighting style: The Spearhead (primary), The Closer (secondary)"* — primary title bold, secondary in normal weight. Tapping the line opens an expanded view with the full 50-fight title frequency distribution. The expanded view presents as a simple bulleted frequency list, not a chart or graph — the data is already characterized by the titles themselves; visualization would be over-design.

### 8.34 Crafter Attribution and Portfolio

Crafter attribution (GDD §18.3) is a persistent label on every crafted item. The creative goal: make every item carry its maker's name visibly but tastefully, and make the portfolio view feel like *stepping into an artisan's workshop* rather than browsing a catalog.

**Attribution line on inspect cards.**

Format: *"Crafted by: Bro Mason"* — single line, warm typography, positioned below the item name and above the stats. For Transcendent crafters: *"Made by Transcendent Craftsman Bro Mason"* — same position, prismatic text treatment matching the §1.7 Godly quality border color (not identical — Godly items have radiant prismatic borders; Transcendent attribution is prismatic text only, a quieter register of sacred).

The attribution is a tappable link on mobile, hover-revealable citation on desktop. Tap opens the portfolio view.

**Portfolio view aesthetic.**

The portfolio opens as a full-screen modal with the crafter's name prominently displayed at top, their rank badge for relevant skills, and the 20-item recent-work grid. The grid is sparse — generous whitespace, one item per grid cell with thumbnail + name + quality grade. The impression should be "this is this person's body of work," not "this is a merchandise page." Think gallery wall, not online store.

For Transcendent crafters, the portfolio additionally shows:
- A prominent "Signature Specialty" line: *"Primarily forges greatswords"* or *"Specializes in Frost composites"* — warm italic text
- The shop note, if the crafter has written one, rendered as a brief quoted block at the top of the portfolio. Format: *"Commissions welcome for Tier 20+ hardening attempts. Contact via mail. — Bro Mason"* — the em-dash author attribution is a small typographic flourish that marks the shop note as *the crafter's voice*, not a system-generated line.
- Production counts as small text: *"Lifetime: 4,209 weapons forged. 312 hardened. 18 at Tier 20+."* Factual, not boastful.

Non-Transcendent portfolios show only the 20-item grid + rank badges. The simplicity is its own statement.

**Milestone notification format.**

Notifications (GDD §18.3.2) appear as ambient text on the crafter's next login, in a small "Since you were away" block on the inventory screen. Format:

*Since you were away:*
*— Bro Alice won the Arena Tournament wielding your Tier 8 Steel Greatsword.*
*— Bro Juno's first Godly was revealed last Tuesday. She was eating your Harmony Stew at the time.*

The em-dash bullet style is consistent. Player names in bold. The notification tone is *"I thought you'd want to know"* — personal, warm, slightly understated. Not *"CONGRATULATIONS, YOUR ITEM WAS USED IN A MILESTONE!"* — notifications should feel like a friend mentioning something nice, not a gamification system firing reward dopamine.

The Milestone Ledger (the permanent archive) renders as a scrolling chronological list with the same em-dash format, viewable from the inventory screen. Older entries fade slightly with age — a gentle visual weathering that suggests *these are from a while ago* without erasing them.

**Creative thesis.** Attribution is the opposite of anonymous commodity crafting. Every weapon, every dish, every gem carries its maker forward through the game's economy. A hundred-hour player's inventory, inspected, becomes a web of relationships — *this weapon from this crafter, this food from that cook*. The game's social fabric becomes visible in the objects players hold. The portfolio view makes any single crafter knowable as an individual with a body of work, not just a name on a marketplace listing. Transcendent rank earns a meaningfully different presentation — the prismatic text, the shop note, the signature specialty — because Transcendent crafters are named individuals in the server's mythology (§8.12a), and their presentation should match.

### 8.35 Clan Hall Atmosphere

The Clan Hall (GDD §29.1) is the clan's shared interior — a place where clanmates gather, where collective achievements accumulate visibly, where clan culture takes physical form. The creative register is **lived-in**, not grand. A clan hall should feel like a favorite tavern where the regulars' names are carved into the tables, not a throne room.

**Architectural baseline.** The initial hall is a simple rectangular stone hall, roughly 40×30 units. Wooden ceiling beams, stone walls, warm torchlight. Central table with mismatched benches — the kind of place where people actually sit and talk. A fireplace at one end with comfortable worn chairs near it. The overall impression: *this is a gathering place, not a monument.*

**Accumulation philosophy.** As members earn accumulation events, the hall fills with their material records. The key creative principle: accumulated items should feel like *they were placed there by clanmates*, not generated by a system. A trophy from an Arena win is on a shelf, not on a pedestal with a plaque. A Godly weapon shrine is a wooden case leaning against a wall, lit by torches, not a glowing dais with light beams. Donated crafted items hang from pegs or rest on shelves as if the crafter walked in and said "here, I made this for the hall."
