**v14.0 creative direction's deepest thesis.** The ending's success depends on audio as much as visual. The recombination chord is the game's key audio moment. The Naming is the game's only direct authorship at the climax — one sentence, once, in the dark. The Fragment's visual register must be register-congruent and sustainable-at-quality-through-thousands-of-Fragments. The Hall is a library not a cemetery. The inscription is a memorial not a trophy. Every creative choice in the v14.0 ending is in service of the game's core thesis — identity earned through accumulated behavior, memorialized publicly in the server's living record.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v5.39): The v5.39 ship is the creative-direction counterpart to the GDD's most ambitious single-section rewrite. The v14.0 ending requires creative-direction work at every layer — chamber visuals, Fragment visuals, Hall spatial design, inscription authoring, and the audio scene that ties them together. The audio scope is particularly ambitious (16-22 weeks audio director time) because the ending's climactic moments are primarily audio events with supporting visuals — the recombination chord, the white-flash silence, the Naming note, the beach rhyme — rather than visual events with supporting audio. The creative direction explicitly inverts the usual audio-investment ratio for this single scene. The Naming addendum is the ship's most important late corrective: the v14.0 initial spec had accidentally buried the v13 canonical sentences as filler inscription options; the Naming restores them to their authored role as the ending's last words, delivered once in the game's voice, accompanied by the v13 sustained-note audio treatment extended with four pole voicings. This is the most complete preservation-and-restoration of superseded content in any ship — the sentences AND their audio direction AND their authored-as-last-words role AND the third-person pronoun discipline are all preserved across the v13→v14.0 transition, with the Naming as the mechanism of restoration. The historical-record preservation of pre-v14.0 §8.15/§8.15a/§8.16/§8.17 content is thorough because the prior direction contained genuinely strong authoring (the Primordial of Darkness's anti-color palette, the Final Echo Stone's four canonical sentences and perfect-fifth sustained-note treatment, the Arbiter Eclipse's "single resonant tone at the exact frequency where dark and light would meet" — this last is the direct ancestor of the v14.0 recombination chord). The design-intent-preservation-notes in each superseded section explicitly document which creative ideas carried forward and which were discarded, serving as a design provenance record for future creative work. The open items (recombination chord composition, zone placement designations, Keeper dialogue authoring, voice-echo frequency tuning) are flagged in-doc because they are the specific deliverables without which the ending falls flat, and visibility of those flags prevents the common "important work deferred indefinitely without explicit tracking" failure mode. The "library not cemetery" register for the Hall is the ship's most quietly radical creative choice — the default framing for a memorial zone would be somber/reverential, and the deliberate rejection of that framing in favor of warmth-and-continued-life is what makes the Hall feel like a place players want to visit rather than a place players feel obligated to visit. The Naming is the ship's most quietly devastating creative choice — one sentence, delivered once, in the pure black, in the game's own voice, naming what the ritual did to the player who performed it. Those two choices — Hall as library, Naming as delivered statement — are the ship's two structural inventions layered on top of the v13 sentence authoring and audio direction inherited from prior generations.]`

---

### CR v5.38

Design-ship — companion to GDD v13.0 (three cross-cutting design principles + ~95 scoped changes). Creative-direction counterparts for the v13 design ship, authored as new §0 "v13 Creative Cross-Cutting Principles" plus targeted additions to existing sections for four-register voice guides, Trickster visual language, Growing Crystal aesthetic, emergent marks, and Listening Mode audio direction.

**§0 v13 Creative Cross-Cutting Principles (NEW top-level section).**

- **§0.1 Four-Register Voice Guide.** Each of the four alignment registers (Responsible/Mischievous/Cool/Ruthless) gets a distinct creative voice with register-specific speech rhythm, humor origin, and specificity patterns. Content authors should identify which register a line belongs to from voice alone. Full examples across all registers for NPC dialogue, Echo Stones, Journey Log, achievements.

- **§0.2 Progressive UI Creative Direction.** First-appearance animation spec (0.4s soft pulse fade-in), contextual hint line tonal variants across all four registers per UI element. Hint examples authored for Quest Log, Alignment display, Title showcase unlocks.

- **§0.3 Tonal Rule — Epic AND Absurd.** Standing creative rule: for every somber line, sibling funny one. Applied across NPC dialogue, Echo Stones, Journey Log, achievements, flavor text, system messages, Primordial dialogue. Explicit narrow exceptions: Final Echo Stone per register, Ferryman Crossing completion, death instant.

**Four-register voice treatments (extensions to §8 Narrative Direction).**

- Per-NPC register voice tables — 6 most-visible town NPCs (Mayor, Blacksmith, Ferryman, Enchanter, Tavern Keeper, Herbalist) gain authored voice direction across all three phases × four registers = 72 voice cells.
- Mischievous-register specific: warm-wit delivery; affectionate trouble; quick asides. Mischievous voice is *not* snarky sarcasm — it's genuine delight in the absurd.
- Ruthless-register specific: dark comedy; sharp edges; uncomfortable truths. Ruthless voice is *not* villainy — it's the Bro who names the hard thing when everyone else is soft.

**Trickster ending visual language (extension to §8 Narrative + §1 Visual Direction).**

- Trickster ending's spoke transformation: elements visibly *trick* each other. Flame gently warms Frost instead of destroying it. Storm plays tag with Stone. Palette stays warm; motion stays playful. No ironic register — warm playfulness as its own serious mode.
- Winking Final Zone Keeper sprite: one-frame eye close + open. Brief. Not a gag animation. The Keeper's wink is affectionate, not comedic.
- Trickster Convergence perception: "carnival" palette — kinetic shadow-and-light, colors shifting unexpectedly, playful motes. Distant laughter-like tones in ambient audio. The Convergence reads as a place of play rather than cosmic weight. **Load-bearing creative rule:** carnival visual language must never tip into gag comedy. The register is *delight*, not *joke*.

**Growing Crystal aesthetic (extension to §56 Convergence Shrine).**

- Visual growth proportional to pool gem count. Year 3 crystal visibly massive compared to Day 3 crystal. Growth/shrink animated on contribution/purchase events.
- Refraction varies per register perception (white-gold Hero, dark-red Sovereign, pale Arbiter, prismatic-shifting Trickster).
- The crystal is the only Convergence object whose visuals change per viewer's register. Reinforces that the Convergence itself is one place with four perceptions.

**Emergent mark visual treatments (extension to §1 Visual Direction + §8 Narrative).**

- Scarred visage: facial scars accumulate additively. Not heavy or disfiguring — visible but character-preserving. Tonal register: dignified; the scars look *earned*, not *wounded*.
- Ferryman's mark: subtle prismatic glint in eyes. Only visible at close range (inspect card, close-player views). Not broadcast.
- Silvered streak in hair: single silver streak appearing at 1,000+ hours. Characters with multiple eligible marks (scars + Ferryman + silvered) show all three layered.
- Visual treatment per mark is reversible through the "Show Marks" settings panel. Players choose what to display. Default-on for newly-earned marks with opt-out.

**Listening Mode audio direction (extension to §2 Audio).**

- Text-to-speech voice profiles per NPC: each named NPC gets a TTS voice identity matching their register (Mayor = mid warm baritone; Blacksmith = gruff tenor; Ferryman = low measured; Enchanter = crisp mid; Tavern Keeper = wry mid; Herbalist = soft alto).
- Spatial audio positioning scale widens in Listening Mode — directional cues stronger, distance attenuation recalibrated for audio-only navigation.
- Combat audio priority hierarchy: attack telegraph > collision resolution > status application > ambient. Priority layer raises critical audio above zone ambient when Listening Mode active.
- UI narration tone register: neutral-observational ("Inventory opened. Weapon slot highlighted."). No tonal variants in UI narration — predictability is accessibility.

**Primordial opposition-pairing dialogue direction (extension to §8 Narrative).**

- Each opposition pairing gets ~20-30 authored lines with tonal range per §0.3. Examples:
  - *Fighting your inner Ruthless (you are Hero/Steadfast):* lines reference specific capstone choices the player refused.
  - *Fighting your inner Mischievous (you are Arbiter/Unmoved):* playful-dry lines noting the humor the player avoided.
- **Load-bearing creative rule:** Primordial dialogue should feel like a voice *inside* the player, not an external enemy. The Primordial knows the player's specific history. Authoring requires knowing the player's Journey Log event categories so lines can reference the right events.

**Crafter-made cosmetics visual direction (extension to §48 Cosmetic Masks).**

- Crafter-made cosmetics bear visible crafter signatures (per GDD §18.3). Small sigil at cosmetic edge indicating source.
- Visual quality tier: crafter-made cosmetics match or exceed developer-authored catalog quality. No visual hierarchy between sources — a Transcendent Blacksmith's mask looks as complete as a developer-authored one.
- Marketplace display integration: crafter-made cosmetics appear alongside developer catalog with a "Crafter: [Name]" metadata line.

**Dissolved clan memorials visual treatment (extension to §4 Social Design).**

- Year+ dissolved clans: old hall locations become memorial sites. Specific visual treatment: slightly faded palette, sparse decorations, no banners, a single plaque with the clan's player-authored epitaph (under 200 chars per GDD §14.3.2 v13).
- Ambient NPC dialogue references dissolved clans in gossip (per GDD §23.9). NPC tone for dissolved clans is *remembering*, not *mourning*. Past tense, acknowledgment of what was.

**Anniversary ritual + per-year thematic identity (extension to §50 Anniversary).**

- v13 preserves canonical per-year thematic identity principle — Year 1 Phoenix Trail visual identity differs from Year 2 anniversary item visual identity. Per §0.1, anniversary NPC reference lines now exist across four register variants (each year's anniversary event gets Responsible/Mischievous/Cool/Ruthless variants in NPC ambient dialogue).

**Register-specific Eclipse variants visual direction (extension to §33.5).**

- **Anchored Eclipse** (Responsible): steady symmetrical cosmetic circle, sharp-edged gold-and-dark boundary, slow rotation. Feels *held*.
- **Playful Eclipse** (Mischievous): shimmer animates, edge-tricks light into motion, occasional playful distortions. Feels *alive*.
- **Still Eclipse** (Cool): minimal animation, clean geometry, reserved. Feels *observed*.
- **Severe Eclipse** (Ruthless): sharp edges, dramatic dark-gold contrast, no softness. Feels *decided*.
- **Complete Eclipse** (balanced 3+ registers): all four variants layered together, prismatic. The rarest cosmetic; earned only by exploring the register space. Feels *unified*.

**Twenty-ninth consecutive CR ship.** v5.38 is the creative companion to the GDD's largest design revision since v11.8. Scope spans narrative direction (four-register voices, Trickster ending), visual direction (emergent marks, Growing Crystal, Eclipse variants), audio direction (Listening Mode, carnival ambient), and social design (dissolved clan memorials, crafter signatures). All canonical creative registers (social-layer, cosmetic-identity, environmental-narrative, outward-sharing, audience-layer, behavioral-expression) preserved and extended.

---

### CR v5.37

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.54 Mask-Conditional Emotes (Entry 57, §48.1 new canonical subsection under §48 Cosmetic Masks stub). One creative-direction addition: new **§8.60 Mask-Conditional Emotes — Personality in Motion**. Covers masks-inflect-personality-not-just-appearance register, 1-sentence authoring briefs as creative constraint discipline, per-variant authored animations (not procedural blending), the Wave as diagnostic for authoring success, audio variants paralleling animation authoring, accessibility discipline (core meaning preserved across variants), behavioral-expression framing, mask-switching as persona commitment, and explicit creative rejection list.

**The load-bearing creative choice — masks inflect personality, not appearance alone.** A mask sitting on a character's face is a static visual layer seen once and tuned out. A mask that inflects how the character *behaves* is a continuous personality expression across the session. Skull's wearer is quiet and deliberate because Wave/Laugh/Bow/Dance/Shrug all carry slow-deliberate-menacing tone. Cat's wearer is playful because variants carry paw-first tone. Royal Crown's wearer performs regality. Mask choice becomes creative choice about the character the player commits to being this session.

**Per-mask personality profiles — 1-sentence authoring briefs.** 15 masks × 1-sentence authored brief in `mask-personality-profiles.json` (authoring data, never surfaced to players). Load-bearing authoring discipline: single-sentence constraint forces specificity — longer profiles allow drift toward generic interpretations; 1-sentence briefs produce concrete embodiment. Examples: Clown (exaggerated, theatrical, big gestures committing fully), Skull (slow, deliberate, gravity-present, subtle menace in small motions), Cat (playful, mischievous, paw-first, body-stretch punctuation), Royal Crown (regal, stately, performative dignity, slight hauteur), Ninja Cloth (quick, precise, economical, no wasted motion). Animator reads profile, produces animations interpreting the brief across five launch emotes with own authorial choices shaping specifics.

**Per-variant authored — not procedural.** 75 variants individually authored, not parameter-generated from base animations. Procedural generation produces muddy middle-ground results (a "Clown Wave" generated from "playful" + "theatrical" parameters feels like a wave with playful tint rather than a Clown's wave). Authored animations commit fully to mask's persona. Animation reuse across emotes is intentional-limited — shared secondary motion elements (shoulder twist, head-tilt) encouraged to produce consistent motion vocabulary per mask; reusing same animation across emotes with parameter tweaks is not allowed.

**The Wave as diagnostic.** Highest-frequency emote and therefore the most-visible test of per-mask personality profile. Player who equips mask for first time should feel the personality through their first Wave — before using Dance/Bow/Laugh/Shrug, Wave should already communicate character choice. If Skull-Wave feels indistinguishable from Clown-Wave, authoring has failed. Each mask's Wave is the creative-direction proof-point.

**Audio variants — personality in sound.** Each mask-variant carries authored audio layer paralleling animation. Clown-laugh = high-pitched cackle; Skull-laugh = low dry chuckle; Cat-laugh = giggle; Royal Crown-laugh = soft polite. Audio NOT procedurally pitch-shifted from base — each mask+emote pair has authored audio asset. Audio doubles character-voice surface: hearing Clown-laugh and Skull-laugh back-to-back experiences two distinct characters without dialogue.

**Accessibility discipline.** Core meaning preserved across all variants — Wave is Wave regardless of mask; observers understand "this player is waving" without needing to recognize specific mask. Matches §58 shape-first discipline — personality inflection layers *on top of* legible base meaning, not replacing it. Audio cues preserve emote intent; silhouette preserves emote shape; personality is additive richness, not primary information. No procedural tone-shifting runtime — authored audio variants only.

**Behavioral-expression framing.** v12.54 reframes cosmetic masks from accessory items to performance-persona commitments. Player equipping Skull mask this session says "I'm playing a Skull-masked character." Every emote reinforces commitment through variant animation + audio. Lightweight roleplay infrastructure — players treating masks as cosmetic-only still receive variants automatically, enjoying visual/audio variety without behavioral commitment; players who lean into persona find mask choice rewarding across session. Mask switching during play is non-ceremonial but each swap is a persona shift.

**Explicit rejection list.** No procedurally generated variants. No personality-parameter configuration. No runtime animation blending (pure lookup). No mask-combo variants (one active mask). No player-customizable variants (authoring centralized). No Supporter Pass gating (gold-purchased masks include variants automatically). No "variant discovery" surface (variants automatic, not achievements). No variant spectator-feed broadcast (observers see through standard emote visibility). No audio-only or visual-only variants (every variant authors both — half-authoring produces inconsistent persona).

**Twenty-ninth consecutive CR ship (v5.9-v5.37).** v5.37's contribution: articulating **masks inflect personality, not appearance alone** as governing register, **1-sentence authoring briefs as creative constraint** as discipline for per-mask profiles, **per-variant authored not procedural** as discipline for animation/audio authoring. Extends creative register vocabulary into **sixth variant: behavioral-expression register** — cosmetic items affecting how character *performs* rather than how it *looks*, requiring authored-not-procedural commitment, with the load-bearing test that first-use should communicate persona through highest-frequency expressive action (Wave for masks). Complements five prior register variants (social-layer v5.29, cosmetic-identity v5.31, environmental-narrative v5.33, outward-sharing v5.34, audience-layer v5.35). Sixth register completes creative-register vocabulary Block 6/7 has established — social / cosmetic-identity / environmental / outward / audience / behavioral — positioning integration sequence's creative infrastructure as broadly applicable to future design work across all social and cosmetic surfaces. **Final creative-direction ship in Block 7 marathon.** v12.55 closing hygiene pass includes no new CR content.

**Zero content changes to existing sections** — pure addition, §8.60 new subsection after §8.59. All existing §8.1-§8.59 content remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.54 (Entry 57 §48.1 Mask-Conditional Emote Variants — targeted Phase 3 elevation; core §48 Cosmetic Masks catalog/pricing/purchase remains stub). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.53 → v12.54; companion architecture reference bumped v2.25 → v2.26 (§9.5.22 client-side variant lookup pipeline + content-pipeline variant authoring).

### CR v5.36

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.53 Arena Training Mode + Public Bet Outcomes (Entry 55 + Entry 56, combat transparency bundle landing as targeted Phase 3 elevations §43.1 and §44.1). One creative-direction addition: new **§8.59 Arena Training + Public Bet Outcomes — Combat Transparency Bundle**. Covers Training Mode's practice-not-performance register (no ceremony, no announcement, no completion texture), AI opponent feel (human-plausible not frame-perfect — Master tier feels like skilled opponent not robot), Public Bet Outcomes' transparency-without-spectacle register (full disclosure without broadcast flourishes — scoreboard-style presentation, not sports-betting dashboard), the privacy-publicity spectrum as complementary-not-contradictory, and explicit creative rejection list.

**The load-bearing creative choice — privacy and publicity as complementary.** v12.53 bundles two opposite-register subsystems under one creative direction because the Arena ecosystem benefits from having both extremes. Training Mode is radically private (no rewards, no counters, no social visibility) and serves personal preparation; Public Bet Outcomes is radically public (no anonymization, no opt-out, full stakes displayed) and serves momentary public stakes. These registers are not contradictions — they are complementary surfaces covering the full range from personal preparation to public accountability. A player practices privately at Training Mode, then enters the tournament where spectators can bet on them and outcomes display publicly. The private-practice-to-public-stake arc is the Arena's narrative for every tournament participant.

**Training Mode register — practice, not performance.** Training sessions have no ceremony. Entering Training Mode is a menu selection at the Arena entrance, not a dramatic threshold. AI opponents spawn without fanfare. Matches end without victory screens (just "match complete, continue training?"). No announcement plays "You have completed training session 47." The functional register matches the purpose — training is practice, and practice is quiet time-on-task, not performative achievement. Training UI treatment: minimal HUD, no progress indicators beyond the current match's state, no "training progress" panel, no training-specific visual flourishes. Players who finish a session should feel they spent time practicing, not that they completed content.

**AI opponent feel — human-plausible, not frame-perfect.** The load-bearing AI discipline is that Master tier opposition should feel like a skilled human opponent, not like a robot playing optimally. Specific direction: Master AI uses TRAINING_AI_LATENCY_MASTER(50ms) simulated reaction lag (not 0ms — frame-perfect would feel mechanical), makes occasional tactical mistakes (not errors — misreads that skilled players make under pressure), varies timing slightly across matches (not deterministic — human opponents don't execute identically every match), and uses cognitive-plausible targeting (punishes visible mistakes, but doesn't react to information only the server knows). The overall effect: a player fighting Master AI should think "I was beaten by someone good," not "I was beaten by an algorithm." Novice and Skilled tiers are simpler but apply the same discipline at their level — Novice mistakes are the mistakes a new player makes, not the mistakes a broken algorithm makes.

**Training Mode AI opponent presentation.** AI opponents appear as Bro-archetype characters (standard character visual, random name generation from Bro naming pool — "Bro Kai," "Bro Lin," "Bro Marcus") rather than as labeled NPCs or visually-distinct robots. Their build varies across sessions per the metagame-sampling discipline. The AI is not disclosed visually as AI — players who want the roleplay experience of fighting practice opponents can read their Training Mode opponents as representing rival Bros; players who want the mechanical experience read them as AI. Both interpretations work because the AI is implemented with human-plausible feel. No "[AI]" label, no robot avatar, no "Training Dummy" naming.

**Public Bet Outcomes register — transparency without spectacle.** The 8-second post-match display is factual and quiet. No victory music swell, no cash-register sound effects, no coin-shower visual, no "BIG WIN" text treatment for large payouts. Display typography is identical across wins and losses — same font, same color, same layout — so losses don't visually cringe away from wins. The presentation should feel like a scoreboard, not a casino payout screen: information delivered cleanly without emotional manipulation. The transparency is the point; the spectacle would undermine it.

**Display ordering discipline.** Bets sort by stake size largest-first because stake size is the most salient information — a 5,000g bet carries more narrative weight than a 50g bet regardless of outcome. Within equal-stake entries, sort alphabetically by bettor name (no "winners first" / "losers first" / "friends first" secondary sorting that would impose editorial framing). The display is data, not narrative.

**Player attribution in full — no euphemism.** Bettor names display as their full Bro names ("Bro Sam," "Bro Mason"). No anonymization, no "A player" framing, no truncation to first letter. Stake amounts shown as full numbers (500g, not "medium bet" or "< 1000g"). Outcomes shown as full amounts (won 1,500g, not "won big" or "3x"). The specificity is what makes the display meaningful as a social record — "Bro Sam bet 500g on Bro Alice, won 1,500g" is a sentence that tells a small story about a specific bet; "A player bet medium on a fighter, won big" is not.

**Ephemeral display — no persistence discipline.** The 8-second window is the entire lifetime of the display. It does not save to player inspect cards, does not appear in Arena history panels, is not queryable later. Players who want to remember a bet outcome remember it themselves or reference a §34.3 Replay Library clip of the match (though bet outcomes don't ride the clip pipeline). Ephemerality is the load-bearing discipline — persistent bet history would become a harassment surface ("Bro Casey has lost 50,000g on Arena bets this month"); the 8-second visibility window keeps the social record at the match-moment scale without enabling cross-match aggregation.

**What this rejects.**

- **Training Mode rejections:** No "Training Graduated" cutscene. No "Training Complete!" popup. No AI difficulty unlock progression (all three tiers available immediately). No "practice partner of the week" featured opponent. No training-themed visual skins, flags, or banners. No training-specific music or audio cue.
- **Public Bet Outcomes rejections:** No victory music / cash-register SFX / coin-shower visuals. No "BIG WIN" / "WHALE BET" / "UPSET" text labels. No emotional-differential typography (wins and losses same font/color/layout). No secondary sort imposing narrative framing. No bettor-anonymization, stake-amount-bucketing, or outcome-rounding. No persistent bet-history surface. No leaderboard compilations. No "betting streaks" tracking. No aggregation dashboards ("Bro Alice has been bet on 47 times this month").

**Twenty-eighth consecutive CR ship (v5.9-v5.36).** v5.36's contribution: articulating **practice not performance** as governing register for Training Mode, **human-plausible not frame-perfect** as discipline for AI opponent feel, and **transparency without spectacle** as register for Public Bet Outcomes. v5.36 distinctive as **first ship to bundle two opposite-register subsystems under single creative direction** — the privacy-publicity spectrum as complementary rather than contradictory. This bundling pattern (opposite-end registers unified by shared ecosystem context) may apply to future ships where subsystems within a single system warrant distinct registers — Arena ecosystem's preparation-to-stake arc is the template.

**Zero content changes to existing sections** — pure addition, §8.59 new subsection after §8.58. All existing §8.1-§8.58 content remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.53 (Entry 55 §43.1 Arena Training Mode + Entry 56 §44.1 Public Bet Outcomes Display — combat transparency bundle; two targeted Phase 3 elevations). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.52 → v12.53; companion architecture reference bumped v2.24 → v2.25 (§9.5.21 Training Mode AI pipeline + stats-suppression session flag + Public Bet Outcomes rendering pipeline).

### CR v5.35

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.52 Clan War Spectator Drone-View (Entry 54, §42.1 new canonical subsection under §42 Clan Wars stub). One creative-direction addition: new **§8.58 Clan War Spectator Mode — Audience at a War**. Covers the quiet-engagement-not-spectacle register, camera-as-cinematographer discipline, HUD-minimum treatment, audio-as-war's-own-ambience register, reactive-not-expressive emote palette discipline, visibility scoping (own clan's spectators only), spectator count as quiet presence readout, post-war quiet-settle transition, and explicit creative rejection list.

**The load-bearing creative choice — quiet engagement, not spectacle.** Sports broadcasts are authored for neutral audiences — multiple camera angles, commentary overlays, stat pop-ups, graphics packages, highlight replays. That register is wrong for clan war spectating. The spectator is not a neutral observer — they are a clan member watching their own clan fight. Experience should feel *personal*, not produced. Minimal UI. One thoughtfully-controlled camera. No commentary, no graphics, no highlight injection. Spectator sees their clan's war the way a supporter sees their team play from the sideline — invested, focused, quietly reacting.

**Camera register — cinematographer's restraint.** Auto-drift follows center-of-mass smoothly (breathing-pace, not instant). Manual retarget is immediate-but-soft (~0.4s acceleration, decelerate on arrival, settle). Multi-action-center buttons are named, not numbered ("North Engagement" / "Mid-Zone" not "Action 1/2/3") — authored at zone-design time in `war-zone-regions.json`. Free-roam is an option, not a feature to encourage — default auto-drift should feel natural enough that spectators rarely reach for manual camera. Cuts feel like broadcast editing; smooth transitions feel like presence.

**HUD — essentials only.** Elapsed war time, score (kill counts per clan), spectator count ("7 clan members watching"), action-center buttons. Nothing else. No minimap (camera is spatial awareness; minimap would add broadcast register). No participant health bars above heads (spectators watching without intervention need atmospheric focus, not tactical clarity). No kill feed (events communicated through combat visuals — death animations, reactions — the same vocabulary participants use). No commentary, no voice-over, no authored "key moment" annotations.

**Audio — the war's own ambience.** Spectator audio is war zone's own audio from observed position — combat, footsteps, abilities firing, distant zone ambient. Positionally spatial based on camera focus point (not spectator's nonexistent in-world body), subtly attenuated ~15% below normal gameplay levels. No music added specifically for spectators. No war theme stinger. No epic orchestral track layered over combat. **The war's own audio is the score.**

**Emote palette — reactive, not expressive.** Eight spectator emotes calibrated as *reactive* (responses to witnessed events) rather than *expressive* (statements spectator wants to make). Distinction matters because reactive emotes cannot carry tactical information (even with 5-second delay); expressive emotes could. Palette: **Cheer** (shared celebration — kill/duel win), **Lament** (shared loss — fall), **Go go go** (encouragement during pushes), **Brilliant play** (skill recognition), **Oof** (hard-hit acknowledgment), **Hold** (defensive encouragement), **Close one** (narrow-escape/near-win acknowledgment), **Watching** (base "I am here" presence signal with no specific reaction). "Watching" emote matters — lets spectators signal presence without reacting to specific event, producing clan-cohesion texture during lulls. All render as small icons with player name, fade ~4s, appear in Spectator Feed sidebar, invisible to participants.

**Visibility discipline — own clan's spectators only.** Spectator sees own clan's Feed but NOT enemy clan's Feed. Invisibility both ways — enemy spectators don't see this clan's emotes either. Preserves two principles: (1) no cross-clan taunting where spectators harass opposing fighters through emote spam, (2) no tactical-information leakage where enemy spectator activity patterns could indicate morale or expectations.

**Spectator count — quiet presence.** "7 clan members watching" small text readout, corner-placed, low visual weight. Knowing fellow clan members are watching creates "we are watching this together" register, but doing so visually loud would shift focus from war to audience. Present but peripheral — spectators feel shared attention without attention becoming the subject.

**Post-war — quiet settle, not broadcast wrap-up.** ~5-second transition: 5-second stream delay shrinks to 0 (spectator view to real-time), final scoreboard fades in with clan winner and participant kill/death counts, Spectator Feed enters 30-second reaction window. **No "and that's a wrap!" graphics, no victory fanfare music, no MVP spotlight animation.** Scoreboard appears, spectators emote reactions, session ends when window closes. Emotional register matches war's own gravity — spectators process outcome quietly with their clan, don't receive broadcast package.

**Explicit rejection list.** No multi-camera broadcast angles, picture-in-picture, split-screen. No commentary/voice-over/authored narration. No music added for spectators — war audio is the score. No minimap, kill feed, health bars, or broadcast-style graphics packages. No "Clan War Spectator of the Week" ranking. No cross-clan emote visibility. No text chat in Spectator Feed (emotes only per §42.1). No visibility of spectator activity to war participants (participants fight unobserved by their audience). No archived replay of prior wars within game — wars are their moment, not re-watchable in-game. §34.3 Replay Library clips exist for sharable highlights, not full-war archival.

**Twenty-seventh consecutive CR ship (v5.9-v5.35).** v5.35's contribution: articulating **quiet engagement not spectacle** as governing register for clan war spectating, and **reactive not expressive emotes** as discipline for Spectator Feed palette. v5.35 extends creative register vocabulary into **fifth variant: audience-layer register** — distinct from v5.29's social-layer, v5.31's cosmetic-identity, v5.33's environmental-narrative, v5.34's outward-sharing registers. Audience-layer applies wherever players observe other players' ongoing gameplay without interfering: mandates observer-feels-like-witness-not-broadcaster discipline across camera, audio, UI, social-affordance decisions.

**Zero content changes to existing sections** — pure addition, §8.58 new subsection after §8.57. All existing §8.1-§8.57 content remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.52 (Entry 54 — §42.1 Spectator Drone-View targeted Phase 3 elevation; core §42 Clan War mechanic remains stub). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.51 → v12.52; companion architecture reference bumped v2.23 → v2.24 (§9.5.20 server-side read-only observer stream pattern + clan-scoped Spectator Feed).

### CR v5.34

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.51 Desktop Power-User + Screenshot Mode + Replay Clip Library (Entry 51 + Entry 52 REVISED, three new §34 subsections). Three creative-direction additions extending existing §1.5 Desktop UX Direction: new **§1.5.1 Power-User Controls — Flow, Not Menu**, new **§1.5.2 Screenshot Mode — Framing as Creative Intention**, new **§1.5.3 Replay Library — Sharing the Moment, Not the Match**. First CR ship in the integration sequence to focus on desktop platform identity + social-sharing infrastructure rather than in-game system creative registers.

**The load-bearing creative choice — keybinds as continuation of combat flow.** A weapon-swap via menu breaks combat flow — attention shifts from fight to menu, and even a fast menu-swap feels like a pause. Keybinds 1-8 must feel like *continuation*: player decides to switch, presses key, swap happens, combat continues. No menu flash, no inventory overlay, no "Swapped to Bow" toast, no cooldown animation. Visual feedback is the swap itself — weapon changes in character's hand, that is the entire acknowledgment. Audio feedback: single soft swap-click, identical per slot, present but not distracting from combat audio.

**Tab target cycling — surgical, not circular.** Players should not need to think about which enemy Tab will pick; they press Tab and know. When Tab picks, lock-on indicator appears immediately — no transition animation, no interstitial "cycling" state. Each Tab is a direct target selection, not rotation through a list. Shift+Tab reverses with same immediacy.

**Hover-tooltips — information on patience.** HOVER_TOOLTIP_DELAY(0.6s) calibrated to moment where player has *decided* to inspect item, not where they are still scanning inventory. Instant-on-hover feels aggressive; never-appearing feels broken. 0.6s captures decision-to-commitment moment. Visual treatment: floating panel, dark translucent matching inspect-card style, compact two-column stats, subdued crafter attribution at bottom, 0.15s fade-in/out — information, not UI event.

**Screenshot Mode framing as authored composition, not optimization presets.** Four framing positions presented as composition choices — centered as portraiture (character is subject), left-third as action-implied (arriving from off-frame), top-third as landscape-emphasis (environment dominates, character is figure in it), free-roam as player-as-photographer (deliberate composition within 40-unit bound). Each positioning appropriate to different moments; player choice reflects what they want capture to say. **Filter register — restrained intensification.** Four filters as register shifts, not dramatic transformations: vintage (desaturated/warm, reflective moments), high-contrast (pushed darks/lights, dramatic moments), dreamy (soft bloom/gaussian blur, peaceful moments). Filters never distort beyond recognition — Greatsword should read as Greatsword regardless of filter. **Mobile parity through tap-and-hold respecting platform conventions** — phone screenshots are OS-level; adding in-app pipeline fights OS's existing capability. Creative direction: mobile-provides-capture-surface, game-provides-clean-frame.

**Replay Library — clips are moments, not footage.** Most in-game recording produces undifferentiated footage requiring player curation. Replay Library produces *moments* — auto-captured, auto-trimmed, event-specific. Each clip is *about something specific*; player knows what it is without watching through irrelevant gameplay. Every clip has a subject; subject is the reason clip exists. **Clip trim windows authored per-moment.** Godly reveal -5/+10 (lead-up establishes drop, full reveal animation, post-reveal reaction). Apex kill -10/+5 (fight context before killing blow). Arena win -15/+5 (match-deciding sequence). Hardness 5 -3/+9 (minimal lead-up, full "yes, it worked" reaction). Collision -5/+5. Grand Slam -7/+3. Not generic windows — each calibrated to what the specific moment needs. **Watermark discipline — unobtrusive but present.** Thin-weight text, ~60% white opacity with subtle shadow, 14pt font, fixed bottom-right corner ~16px margin. Text: `"{GAME_NAME} · {PLAYER_NAME} · Day {PLAYER_DAY_COUNT}"` — game first for brand, player second for attribution, day third for context. Text-only, no logos/emblems/graphics. NOT removable through any in-game path. Balance: present-but-quiet — game's presence visible in shared content, that presence is the point. If watermark too prominent, players stop sharing. If removable, most would remove it. If absent, organic marketing value evaporates.

**What players want to share — observed behavior.** Trigger list (Godly reveal, Apex kill, Arena win, Hardness 5, first collision, Grand Slam) reflects observed ARPG social-media behavior: players overwhelmingly share *peak moments*, not general gameplay, and prefer *motion* over *state*. Auto-capture list calibrated to these preferences. Future trigger expansions should ask: peak moment? Motion? Coherent small story? If not, trigger isn't worth storage cost. **Library UI — personal archive, not social dashboard.** Clips Tab under inspect panel, visible only to player themselves. No broadcast to friends, no ranking, no upvoting, no commenting within game. Matches identity-acknowledgment discipline (§8.51 / §8.54 / §8.56 / §8.57) — game notices player's moments, player notices them, social visibility is optional and player-initiated through share flow.

**Explicit rejection list.** No clip-rating system (no thumbs-up, "best clip of week"). No automated highlight reels or "top moments" compilations — players curate own sharing. No branded overlay kits (sponsor logos, ad placements, affiliate watermarks) — watermark is game-presence only. No real-time clip notifications to friends — sharing is player-initiated, not auto-broadcast. No "private-only" clips below library level — all library clips private by default; sharing is explicit public action. No "premium clip quality" upsell — uniform across free-tier and Supporter per §34.3 discipline.

**Twenty-sixth consecutive CR ship (v5.9-v5.34).** v5.34's contribution: articulating **keybinds as continuation of combat flow** for desktop power-user controls, **framing options as authored composition choices** for Screenshot Mode, and **clips as moments not footage** for Replay Library. v5.34 is distinctive within the sequence as the first explicit focus on **desktop platform identity + social-sharing infrastructure** — prior ships focused on in-game systems and creative registers; v5.34 opens how game's presence extends *outward* into player's own social-media sharing. The watermark-as-unobtrusive-presence discipline is the creative infrastructure for this outward extension, specified with the same care as in-game registers.

**Zero content changes to existing sections** — pure addition, three new subsections (§1.5.1 / §1.5.2 / §1.5.3) inserted within existing §1.5 Desktop UX Direction. All existing §1.5 content (detection, visual changes, aim reticle, feel target) remains authoritative unchanged. All other CR content unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.51 (Entry 51 §34.1 Power-User Controls + Entry 52 §34.2 Screenshot Mode + Entry 52 REVISED §34.3 Replay Clip Library — three new canonical §34 subsections). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.50 → v12.51; companion architecture reference bumped v2.22 → v2.23 (§9.5.19 client-side rolling buffer + WebCodecs H.264 encoding + Cloudflare R2 clip hosting + keybind registry expansion).

### CR v5.33

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.50 Contemplative Underwater Diving (Entry 45, §46 rewritten from Phase 3 stub to canonical). One creative-direction addition: new **§8.57 Contemplative Underwater Diving — The Zone's Memory**. Covers the mode-change-not-combat-variant framing, swelling-ambient audio register in the Final-Echo-Stone / Convergence-shrine sacred-object vocabulary, parallax-as-gradient visual treatment with no UI depth indicator, creature silhouettes as aliveness-without-combat, air-rhythm-as-breathing discipline, underwater Echo Stone voice (things forgotten / sunk / drowned — the zone's memory), Discovery Journal quiet acknowledgment, and explicit creative rejection list.

**The load-bearing creative choice — diving is a mode change, not a combat variant.** The critical register question was whether underwater exploration would feel like "surface combat with different scenery" or "a different kind of time entirely." v5.33 commits fully to the latter: underwater is a mode change, not an extension of surface activity with new visuals. Every creative decision serves this framing — audio register, visual treatment, creature behavior, air-management framing. A player descending through surface-to-deep should feel the shift from one kind of experience to another, not just camera adjustment.

**Audio register — swelling ambient in the sacred-object vocabulary.** Underwater audio follows Final Echo Stone (§8.16) and Convergence-shrine (§8.52) register. Single swelling tone per Tidal Caves variant, different per zone-instance. Swell cycle ~30-45 seconds, unsynchronized with any game event. Surface audio carries through low-pass filter muted and distant. Player action sounds present but softened. Effect: aural isolation — underwater as separate acoustic space with surface audible only as memory. Sacred-object register is deliberate; diving is contemplative, and the game's other contemplative surfaces set the vocabulary.

**Visual register — parallax as gradient, not discrete zones.** Four depth layers (surface / mid-water / deep / bottom) as continuous rendering-depth gradients, NOT zones with visible boundaries. Player swimming from surface to deep experiences continuous transition — increasing darkness, particulate shifting, color deepening, Echo Stone glows becoming primary illumination. Camera exposure adjusts gradually. Water light-scatter always present, varying in density/color with depth. **No UI depth indicator.** Depth is felt through rendering — the load-bearing choice for underwater visual register. Numeric depth would break contemplative immersion.

**Creature silhouettes — aliveness without combat.** Mid-water and deep-layer creatures rendered as silhouettes only, not detailed species. Player sees *shape* of something large moving but cannot identify or track. Detailed creatures would introduce combat expectations (hostile? attackable? drops?) conflicting with contemplative register. Silhouette-only communicates aliveness and scale while signaling "not interactive." Silhouettes do not react to player presence or produce gameplay events.

**Air-rhythm narrative — breathing, not failure.** Air meter visual shifts color slowly (clear-blue → light-teal → faint-amber → faint-red) with subtle pulse at final threshold — NEVER warning indicator, alarm, red flash, or failure-imminent audio. Forced-resurface ascent is *graceful* (character floats upward, camera follows, audio muffles to near-silence) — same vocabulary as voluntary resurface, slightly slower. Player experience: *surfacing to breathe*, not *failing*. 1.5-second decompression pause on surface is the rhythmic heart of the system — character settles, ambient register shifts, breath happens. Prevents frantic dive-resurface optimization.

**Underwater Echo Stone voice — things forgotten, sunk, drowned.** Surface stones describe what players see; underwater stones describe **what is no longer seen**. Content: things that existed and are gone, things submerged, things remembered only by the water, things the current carries. Voice is elegiac — neither mournful nor sentimental, but quietly aware that something has passed. Specific enough to feel real (a daughter, a lighthouse keeper, walls, a tide) without resolving into narrative. §33 Echo Stone discipline applied to underwater register: *specificity without exposition, fragment without resolution.* Examples: *"The lighthouse keeper's daughter never learned to swim." / "These waters remember names the land forgot." / "They built walls against the tide. The tide remembered."*

**Discovery Journal quiet acknowledgment.** Echo Stone discovery updates Journal silently — counter increment, no notification, no popup, no audio cue. Journal visible to player in own UI; no broadcast, no social-surface visibility. Matches identity-acknowledgment discipline (§8.51 / §8.54 / §8.56) — game notices, player notices, no one else needs to. Underwater-stone count is quiet personal record of attention to zone's memory.

**Explicit rejection list.** No drowning damage / animation / status effect (air depletion → graceful forced-resurface). No combat underwater — creature silhouettes are decoration only. No "first diver" / "deepest explored" / competitive surface. No sonic-boom audio on surface / "Fresh Air!" status / surfacing exclamation. No UI depth indicator or "You are at 12m" readout. No surface↔underwater Echo Stone content crossover (pipeline enforces exclusivity). No underwater boss music, combat music, or encounter-prep register. No tutorial popup on first dive — players learn through doing.

**Twenty-fifth consecutive CR ship (v5.9-v5.33).** v5.33's specific contribution: articulating **diving as mode change, not combat variant** as governing framing for underwater experience, and **air as breathing rhythm, not failure threat** as discipline for air-management visual treatment. Block 6/7's four Phase 3 elevations (v5.29 / v5.30 / v5.31 / v5.33) now share expanded creative vocabulary with fourth register variant: **social-layer surfaces must be invited, not imposed; cosmetic-evolution systems must express, not optimize; identity-artifact surfaces must acknowledge, not celebrate; environmental-narrative surfaces must reward attention, not optimization.** Environmental-narrative register (v5.33) complements social / personal-identity / foundational-legibility registers established in prior ships.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §8.57 is a new subsection after §8.56 Pet Evolution. All existing §8.1-§8.56 content remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.50 (Entry 45 — §46 Contemplative Underwater Diving rewrite from Phase 3 stub to canonical). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.49 → v12.50; companion architecture reference bumped v2.21 → v2.22 (§9.5.18 Echo Stone placement field + client-side diving state machine).

### CR v5.32

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.48 Training Moment Feedback (Entry 1, §1.6.1 new subsection extending stat legibility infrastructure with continuous-fill bars + threshold celebration). One creative-direction addition: new **§1.7 Training Moment Feedback — Visual Identity**. Covers acknowledgment-not-reward register, character-glow as presence-not-spectacle, floating label quiet typography, first-productive-use trigger discipline, stat-name pedagogy teach-then-trust, continuous-fill animation restraint, and the explicit creative rejection list.

**The load-bearing creative choice — acknowledgment, not reward.** A threshold crossing in a use-trained stat system is not a *reward* in the loot-box sense — the player already earned the progression through play. The celebration is acknowledgment of what already happened, not a prize arriving from outside. No popup, no modal, no screen interruption. The player fights through the moment; the game quietly marks it. **Character-glow register — presence, not spectacle.** 0.5-second accent-color halo treats the character as the subject of the acknowledgment. Red for Ferocity, blue for Mind, gold for Power, matching §1.7 archetype accent palette exactly. Glow pulses once softly; no flashing or strobing. Other nearby players see the glow with the same intensity — no privacy asymmetry. **Floating label — quiet typography.** "FEROCITY 150" label ~1 unit above character's head, stat accent color, floating-damage-number size with slight weight bump. Fades in 0.15s, holds 0.35s, fades out 0.15s; total duration ≈0.65s. Gentle upward drift ~0.3 units. Reads once, then gone — no lingering, no combat log persistence. **The first-productive-use trigger discipline.** Celebration fires when the new tier is *used*, not when it is *crossed*. Ferocity 150 during idle → no effect; next crit → glow + label. Trigger must be invisible-until-used — no preview tooltips, no waiting-period notifications, no "your next crit will celebrate" predictions. The moment arrives when it arrives. **Stat-name pedagogy — teach, then trust.** First 10 celebrations per stat show stat-name alongside value ("FEROCITY 150"); after 10th trigger, label drops to value-only ("150"). Teaches new players color-to-stat mapping through explicit naming; veterans get cleaner signature. Transition is silent. **Continuous-fill bars — animation restraint.** Inspect-card fill bars animate over TRAINING_FILL_BAR_SMOOTHING(0.3s) windows using ease-out curves. Combat-generated rapid XP events absorbed into continuous fill motion rather than discrete jumps. Bar-completion transition is soft snap (brief pause at full, then reset). No celebration fires from bar completion itself — celebration is separate event driven by productive-use trigger. **Explicit rejection list.** No popup/modal acknowledgment, no achievement chime / level-up jingle / audio sting (celebration is visual-only), no broadcast beyond local view, no leaderboard/ranking tied to celebration count, no predictive "your next action" tooltips, no persistent log entry beyond Journey Log session recap.

**Twenty-fourth consecutive CR ship (v5.9-v5.32).** v5.32's specific contribution: articulating **acknowledgment-not-reward** as governing register for use-trained stat milestones, and **invisible-until-used** as trigger discipline for milestone-moment feedback. Closes legibility coverage for the §1 stat system — all five time scales now explicitly articulated: moment-to-moment micro-feedback (§1.6), sub-integer fill bars (§1.6.1 continuous), threshold celebration (§1.6.1 discrete), session recap (Journey Log), lifetime archetype emergence (§1.7). The identity-acknowledgment thread extends to its eleventh surface — the player's own stat training becomes visible as its own identity register, complementing weapons (v12.25), weapon history (v12.30), Mastery certifications (v12.33), title showcase (v12.36), pool contribution (v12.41), derivatives (v12.42), Stability Fragments (v12.44), minigame badges (v12.45), furniture gift history (v12.46), and Mythic pet lineage (v12.47).

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — the new §1.7 training-moment subsection is inserted between existing §1.7 Stat-Based Visuals and §1.7a Weapon Quality and Hardness Visuals. All existing §1.7 archetype visual direction, §1.7a weapon visuals, and all other CR content remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.48 (Entry 1 — §1.6.1 Training Moment Feedback new subsection extending §1.6 Training Visibility infrastructure). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.47 → v12.48; no architecture bump (pure UI/UX work with zero schema change; client-side productive-action detection; server-validated celebration broadcasts use existing event pipeline).

### CR v5.31

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.47 Expressive Pet Evolution (Entry 43, §40 rewritten from Phase 3 stub to canonical). One creative-direction addition: new §8.56 Expressive Pet Evolution — Your Pet Is Yours. Covers the inheritance-not-upgrade semantic, the quiet merge moment, the bittersweet register of consuming parents, the legible (non-randomized) visual inheritance, the Mythic arrival without ceremony, the auto-generated lineage sentence's specificity discipline, the prismatic nameplate treatment consistency with other sacred-object surfaces, the emergent-not-enforced uniqueness of Mythics, the non-tradeable-as-statement register, and the explicit creative rejection list.

**The load-bearing creative choice — inheritance, not upgrade.** Most evolution systems use upgrade language; the v12.47 register is different — evolved forms are *inherited children*, not upgraded versions. A Flame Imp merged with a Frost Imp doesn't "upgrade into" a Purple Imp; the Purple Imp *inherits from* the Flame and Frost parents. The creative vocabulary matters because it determines how players narrate what's happening. "My Flame Imp got upgraded" is a completion thought; "I merged my Flame and my Frost — look what came out" is a creative thought. Every UI surface, every audio cue, every animation reinforces inheritance-not-upgrade.

**The merge moment — brief, small, lived-in.** Two pets selected. Merge preview shows deterministic result. Confirm + optional rename. Both parents fade gently over ~1.5 seconds; child fades in. No fanfare, no particle explosion, no "EVOLUTION!" overlay. Parents settle quietly; child appears already-formed, not completing a ritual. Emotional weight comes from what the player knows (irreversible consumption), not from how the game performs the moment.

**The bittersweet register.** Merge consumes both parents permanently. The creative direction treats this honestly — merge confirmation explicitly names what's being lost: *"This action will consume [Flame Imp's name] and [Frost Imp's name] permanently."* No euphemism ("send to the heavens"), no softening. Matches Stability Fragments (§8.15a v12.44) and Act-Transition Acknowledgments (§8.51 v12.36) observational-warm voice — the game tells you what happened without reframing it.

**Visual inheritance legible, not randomized.** Merge preview shows the specific color, shape, and (bounded-random) pattern result before commit. Why legibility matters: randomized merges produce gambling dynamics, and gambling dynamics break the expressive/aesthetic framing. Deterministic main channels mean every merge is a deliberate aesthetic decision — the player is choosing the child's appearance, not rolling for it.

**The Mythic moment — arrival, not victory.** First Mythic appears in inventory with prismatic nameplate + full lineage sentence already attached. No "Mythic Unlocked!" screen, no achievement chime, no broadcast. The pet sits in inventory; the player notices; the player inspects to read the lineage sentence. Matches Final Echo Stone register (§8.16) — the game's most personally-meaningful moments are delivered without ceremony, because ceremony would compete with the meaning the player has already earned.

**Lineage sentence — specificity over poetry.** *"This Mythic Imp was born from your first Flame Imp captured at Ember Fields on [date], merged across 7 evolutions with Frost, Storm, and Venom companions, culminating on [date]."* Names actual zones, actual dates, actual elements — not generic narrative language. Specificity is what makes the sentence personal: "your first Flame Imp captured at Ember Fields" is *your* story in a way "forged in the crucible" is not.

**Non-tradeable as statement, not restriction.** When a player tries to list a Mythic on marketplace or gift via mail, UI language is *"Mythic pets are bound to you. They cannot leave your character."* Identity-semantic, not policy-restriction — the reason Mythics don't trade is that they *are* their owner's in a way other pets aren't.

**Explicit rejection list.** No "Evolution!" popups, no particle explosions on merge, no victory music stings, no Mythic-creation broadcast to nearby players, no rarity tiers beyond the four canonical ones (no "Shiny Mythic"), no breeding mechanics producing new Base pets, no "favorite pet" UI surface.

**Twenty-third consecutive CR ship (v5.9-v5.31).** v5.31's specific contribution is articulating **inheritance-not-upgrade** as the governing semantic for cosmetic-evolution systems, and **specificity-over-poetry** as the lineage-sentence authoring discipline. The thread through v5.15 / v5.25 / v5.27 / v5.28 / v5.29 / v5.30 continues. Block 6's three Phase 3 elevations (v5.29 / v5.30 / v5.31) now share a consistent creative vocabulary: **social-layer surfaces must be invited, not imposed; cosmetic-evolution systems must express, not optimize; identity-artifact surfaces must acknowledge, not celebrate.**

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §8.56 is a new subsection after §8.55. All existing §8.1-§8.55 content remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.47 (Entry 43 — §40 Expressive Pet Evolution rewrite from Phase 3 stub to canonical). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.46 → v12.47; companion architecture reference bumped v2.20 → v2.21 (§9.5.17 Pet schema extension for lineage + visual_traits fields, merge_pets RPC, Mythic non-tradeable enforcement at transfer RPC boundaries).

### CR v5.30

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.46 Gift-Economy Furniture (Entry 44, §41 rewritten from Phase 3 stub to canonical). One creative-direction addition: new §8.55 Gift-Economy Furniture — Your House Is a Social Map. Covers the tonal register across all attribution surfaces (three attribution formats by provenance, peripheral-readable typography, inspection-triggered display), the gift reception and gift-giving feel (small/warm/private), the absence-of-display discipline (crafters must see the room the recipient chose, not a shadow of the undisplayed gift), the emergent social-network read through walking the house, and the explicit creative rejection list.

**The load-bearing creative choice — decorated space first, social record second.** A player walking into a friend's house should see a *decorated room*, not a wall of attribution displays. The room reads as a home. When the visitor wants to know "who made this chair?", they can inspect a piece and the crafter's name appears. The social-map information is present throughout the house but *inspected rather than displayed*. A furniture system that put "Made by Bro Alice" floating above every chair would look like an achievement wall; the v12.46 register is closer to a museum curator's quiet labels beside exhibits — available when asked, invisible when not.

**Three attribution formats by provenance.** Self-crafted displays *"By [Owner Name]"* (shortest, most modest). Marketplace-purchased displays *"Made by [Crafter]"* (standard attribution without relationship context — neutral informational register). Gifted displays *"Made for [Recipient] by [Crafter]"* (warm dedication register — names both parties, positions the piece as a relationship). Transcendent-crafted pieces use prismatic text for the crafter's name matching §18.3 discipline, producing a visual signature where a visitor sees most labels in warm off-white with occasional prismatic flickers marking Transcendent work.

**Gift reception — small, warm, private.** Arrival notification: *"Bro Alice sent you a Crafted Dining Chair."* Accompanied by a soft unobtrusive audio cue (closer to a soft knock at the door than a victory chord). The gift sits in inventory with a small gift-ribbon icon. The crafter's message, if included, renders in a lightly-informal typeface distinct from system text — reads as a handwritten note rather than a formal caption.

**Gift giving — quiet confirmation, no fanfare.** *"Gift sent to Bro Alice."* No popup, no giver-badges. The giving itself is the reward; the game trusts the player to know that. The crafter is not notified when a gift is placed; they either see it when they visit the recipient's house or they don't. **This preserves both parties' dignity** — the recipient isn't pressured to display gifts they don't want, and the crafter isn't publicly rejected when their work stays in inventory.

**The absence-of-display discipline — invisible by design.** If a crafter visits a friend's house and their gifted piece is not visible, the creative direction is explicit: **the crafter must see the room the recipient has chosen, not a placeholder or shadow of the undisplayed gift.** No "your gift is in their storage" indicator, no faded icon, no subtle signal that the piece exists somewhere. The recipient's aesthetic choices are theirs; the crafter sees what the recipient displays.

**Walking through the house — the emergent social read.** Four pieces, each a different provenance (self-crafted bookshelf, gifted bed from Bro Mason, marketplace-purchased Transcendent dining set from Bro Priya, gifted armchair from the visitor themselves). A visitor interested in the social network can spend minutes exploring and build a real picture of the friend's relationships; a visitor passing through sees a decorated space and moves on. Both modes are valid; the creative direction supports both.

**Explicit rejection list.** No "Best Decorated" rankings or leaderboards, no floating attribution text above every piece (inspection-triggered only), no placement achievements or badges, no Supporter Pass furniture tier (gift-economy framing depends on all furniture being earnable through the shared crafting system), no visit tracking visible to the owner, no "complete the collection" prompt (the catalog is a catalog, not a goal set).

**Twenty-second consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.30).** Observational, warm-without-ceremony voice continues. v5.30's specific contribution is articulating **decorated space first, social record second** as the governing visual principle, and **inspection-triggered over always-visible** as the attribution-display discipline. v5.29's background-not-foreground principle for minigames and v5.30's decorated-space-first principle for furniture share a common discipline: **the social layer must be invited, not imposed**. The thread through v5.15 (Gambling Den), v5.25 (Convergence shrine), v5.27 (accessibility as baseline), v5.28 (Stability Fragments), v5.29 (Farm Minigames) continues — mechanical additions articulated through tonal register that matches the narrative gravity of their surface.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §8.55 is a new subsection inserted after §8.54 Farm Minigames. All existing §8.1-§8.54 content remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.46 (Entry 44 — §41 Gift-Economy Furniture rewrite from Phase 3 stub to canonical). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.45 → v12.46; companion architecture reference bumped v2.19 → v2.20 (§9.5.16 PlayerState extension for `house_furniture_layout: Array<PlacedFurniture>` field with gift-metadata pointers).

### CR v5.29

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.45 Farm Minigames (Entry 46, §47 rewritten from Phase 3 stub to canonical v12.45 specification). One creative-direction addition: new §8.54 Farm Minigames — Hangout Material. Covers the tonal register across all three minigames (Fishing as meditative register, Cooking as active-chat register, Matching as shared-problem-solving register), the farm-as-social-space framing that positions minigames as activities within hangout visits rather than destinations for content, the completion-moment quiet acknowledgment matching the identity-acknowledgment thread, and the explicit creative rejection list.

**The load-bearing creative choice — minigames are background, not foreground.** A player's attention during a farm visit is primarily on their guest (or on their own drifting thoughts during solo play), not on the minigame. The minigame provides something for hands and eyes to do while the social or solitary moment happens — the same way a board game at a dinner party is a *vehicle* for conversation rather than the *point* of the evening. Every creative decision follows from this: no cutscenes before rounds (would pause conversation), no forced tutorials (would require attention-diversion to read), no victory fanfare (would interrupt whatever was just being discussed), no failure punishment (would introduce stakes the hangout register rejects). The minigames announce themselves gently, run gently, conclude gently.

**Per-minigame tonal direction.** Fishing's pond is quiet water with reflection and occasional ripple; the waiting period between casts is filled with ambient sound; a successful reel produces a gentle *flup* rather than fanfare music; a missed reel produces nothing at all. If fishing feels "boring" in playtest — that's not a problem to fix, because fishing in isolation *should* feel boring as the companion activity whose rhythm fills silence between chat messages. Cooking's kitchen is small and warm with scrolling recipe prompts; hits produce satisfying audio clicks, misses produce comedic splat effects; the co-play mode turns the rhythm into call-and-response dialog between players. Matching's puzzle table sits near the flower garden with element-glyph tiles at player-seated height; taps clear with soft poof rather than triumphant flash, cascades produce sequences of soft clears rather than a dramatic sting; the shared-puzzle co-play mode produces friendly "you take it — I'll get the next one" coordination in chat.

**The farm as social space — not arcade, not content park.** The three minigame locations (pond, kitchen, puzzle table) are distributed across existing farm geography rather than clustered into a designated "arcade area." This is deliberate. A dedicated minigame zone would read as a content destination; distributed locations read as parts of a lived-in space that happen to support activity. The farm is a home; homes have activities scattered throughout. This is not a theme park.

**Completion moment — quiet acknowledgment.** Badge earning produces a single line of observational text on the post-round summary (*"Your Fisher's Badge has been added."*) — no fanfare music, no particle effect, no screen-dim moment, no achievement-unlocked popup. The badge appears silently on the inspect card, visible the next time the player opens their card. Matches the established identity-acknowledgment thread across v12.25 crafter attribution, v12.30 weapon history, v12.33 Mastery certifications, v12.36 title showcase, v12.41 pool contribution credit, v12.42 derivatives revealed, v12.44 Stability Fragments — all surface player identity through small persistent records rather than celebration events.

**Explicit rejection list.** No victory music stings (per-minigame), no failure punishment (missed reels/expired timers/splat misses don't punish), no leaderboard UI anywhere, no minigame-specific character cosmetics (only the three earn-once badges, no unlockable fishing rods or chef's hats), no time-limited seasonal events in the launch spec (three games / three badges / one farm location only; future ships can add seasonal variations only within the same hangout-framing discipline).

**Twenty-first consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.29).** The observational, warm-without-ceremony voice continues — farm minigames extend the discipline into the social-hangout surface. v5.29's specific contribution is articulating **background-not-foreground** as the governing principle for secondary content types — a principle that should propagate to future ships touching other hangout-adjacent surfaces (farm visiting mechanics, personal farm decoration, gift-economy furniture per §41). The thread through v5.15 (Gambling Den as neutral presence in commercial contexts), v5.25 (shrine as zone-native structure), v5.27 (accessibility as baseline not mode), v5.28 (Stability Fragments as compound effort without celebration) continues — mechanical additions articulated through tonal register that matches the narrative gravity of their surface. Minigames' tonal register is lightness and conversation; the creative direction delivers exactly this.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §8.54 is a new subsection inserted at the end of the §8 creative-direction sequence (after §8.53 Accessibility Modes). All existing §8.1-§8.53 content remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.45 (Entry 46 — §47 Farm Minigames rewrite from Phase 3 stub to canonical). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.44 → v12.45; companion architecture reference bumped v2.18 → v2.19 (§9.5.15 PlayerState extension for `minigame_badges_earned: Set<MinigameBadge>` field + ephemeral daily-completion tracker).

### CR v5.28

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.44 Stability Fragments (Entry 41, opens §57 Primordial as a design surface). One creative-direction addition: new §8.15a Stability Fragments — Compound Effort Made Visible. Covers the tonal discipline governing every surface where Stability Fragments appear — earning silence (no celebration on failure), portal-entry prompt observational language, visual treatment of the stability-bar prefill, inventory minimalism, post-success silence, and the explicit list of creative treatments the direction REJECTS (no "Compound Effort!" explanatory text, no cumulative-counter surface, no social-comparison surface, no fragment art/icon/sound).

**The load-bearing creative choice — no celebration on earn.** When a player fails a Primordial attempt, the failure is not reframed. No "Stability Fragment Earned!" popup, no particle effect, no Echo Stone commentary on the fragment's existence. The fragment appears in the player's count silently, visible only on the next portal-entry prompt. This is the sharpest edge of the creative direction: any framing that says "don't feel bad about failing — you earned something!" would transform the encounter from "narrative conclusion I work toward" into "farming surface with participation rewards," which is exactly what the no-monetization / no-loot / no-retry-on-success disciplines are protecting against. **The fragment is not a reward for failing. It is an acknowledgment that you tried.**

**Prompt language — observational, not encouraging.** *"You have [N] Stability Fragments. Apply [0-N] to this attempt?"* No exclamation marks, no emoji, no color-coded urgency, no "hard-earned" framing. Matches Mayor Bro's §53.6 act-transition discipline — a statement of what's available, not a framing of how to feel about it. The player reads the prompt, decides, confirms. The decision is theirs.

**Visual treatment — the prefill is visible but not announced.** When the player applies fragments and enters the arena, the stability bar opens with the prefill already present as filled bar segment — no "filling up" animation, no "Fragments Applied!" overlay. The prefill is simply the encounter's starting state for this attempt, reinforcing the fragment's conceptual framing (prior learning has already happened, prefill represents progress already made).

**Inventory minimalism.** Fragments are not displayed in any standard inventory surface — no slot in the inventory panel, no entry in the Codex, no mention on the inspect card. Visible exclusively on the portal-entry prompt. The fragment count is encounter-local; persistent inventory-slot framing would misrepresent them as "items the player owns" rather than encounter-local state.

**Post-success silence.** When a player stabilizes the Primordial and reads the Final Echo Stone, the game does not acknowledge unspent fragments. They are not refunded, not converted, not displayed in a summary. They remain orphaned in the player's count — a silent record of the path taken. The silence is the correct creative treatment for an ending.

**Explicit rejection list.** The creative direction names specific treatments it rejects: "Compound Effort!" explanatory text anywhere, cumulative-counter surface, social-comparison surface, fragment icon/sprite/art, earn/spend sound effects. Each rejection preserves a specific aspect of the encounter's gravity that the corresponding celebration framing would undermine.

**The framing discipline, stated directly.** A player who fails five attempts and reaches the fragment cap has invested roughly five days of attention on this encounter and learned something from each attempt. The game acknowledges this with a maximum 50% stability prefill on their next attempt — meaningful mechanical shortening, bounded to preserve the encounter's difficulty floor. The creative direction's job is to deliver this acknowledgment *without reframing the player's experience*. The failures were failures. The encounter remains the game's hardest content. The Final Echo Stone still requires the present attempt's completion. The fragment mechanic is small because the acknowledgment should be small — just large enough that persistence is honored, small enough that the encounter's gravity is untouched.

**Twentieth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.28).** The observational, warm-without-ceremony voice continues — Stability Fragments extend the discipline into the endgame-encounter surface. The thread through v5.15 (Gambling Den as neutral presence in commercial contexts), v5.25 (shrine as zone-native structure), v5.27 (accessibility as baseline not mode) continues — mechanical additions articulated through tonal register rather than ceremony. v5.28's contribution is the explicit statement that mechanical acknowledgment of persistence does not require celebration of persistence, and the game's voice at the endgame-encounter surface must match the surface's narrative gravity.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §8.15a is a new subsection inserted between §8.15 The Primordial — Creative Direction and §8.17 Sound — The Primordial. All existing §8.15 content (Primordial visual philosophy, the two Primordials' creative direction) and §8.17 content (Primordial ambient sound design) remain authoritative unchanged. §-Reference Map gains one row for §8.15a.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.44 Stability Fragments (Entry 41, opens §57 Primordial as a design surface). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.43 → v12.44; companion architecture reference bumped v2.17 → v2.18 (§9.5.14 PlayerState extension for `stability_fragments` scalar field).

### CR v5.27

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.43 Accessibility Compilation Bundle (Entries 47, 48, 49, 50). Three creative-direction additions: (1) §1.1.1b DO/DON'T Quick Reference extension with shape-first UI authoring rules — a seven-row table articulating the shape-first discipline as a design-gate check for all future UI authoring; (2) new §1.9.11 One-Handed Auto-Attack Feel covering the creative direction for the v12.43 One-Handed Control Preset, specifically how auto-attack, auto-lock, and block-long-press should register to preserve full character presence under single-thumb input constraints; (3) new §8.53 Accessibility Modes — Creative Direction covering the tonal register across the Accessibility settings panel (observational labels not medical framing), per-mode creative direction for Visual-Enhanced / Reaction-Assist / One-Handed / Shape-First, privacy defaults across all accessibility modes, and the unifying thesis of accessibility as universal UX quality.

**Unifying creative thesis: accessibility is baseline, not a mode.** The v12.43 creative additions share a single discipline — accessibility surfaces must work without signaling themselves as accessibility surfaces. Shape-first is not a "colorblind mode" the player enables; it is the baseline UI grammar that every element in the game follows. One-Handed auto-attack is not a "this is auto-attack" visual register; it produces visuals identical to manual attack. Reaction-Assist is invisible to self, visible only in outcome. The underlying principle: a player using any accessibility accommodation should experience the same game, the same combat register, the same character presence as a player who doesn't need the accommodation. The accommodation reshapes the input or amplification layer; it never reshapes the shared visual or mechanical scene. This matches the privacy register established across all §58 accessibility modes — accessibility is personal, not broadcast.

**§1.1.1b extension — shape-first as design-gate discipline.** The new seven-row DO/DON'T table articulates the specific authoring rules: element identification through unique geometric shape glyphs (not color-only), quality tiers through border treatment scaling (not hue ranges), status effects through distinctive silhouettes (not color-tinted variants of a shared icon), directional indicators through arrow/triangular shapes (not color-coded arrows), state differentiation through shape + position changes (not color-only state flips), resistance reads through typography/size/treatment (not color-only tier indication), and a meta-rule: shape-first is verified in monochrome preview *before* color is specified.
