The discipline is stated as a design-gate check rather than a pipeline stage — a new UI element must pass shape-first verification before approval, which forces designers to think about shape-distinguishability as the *first* question rather than a late-stage accessibility audit. The alignment with existing CR discipline is deliberate: §1.9.9 Collision Opportunity Glow already included shape-edge texture supplements for colorblind-palette players; §1.9.10 Threat Direction Arrows already treated triangular shape as the primary directional channel; the v12.43 extension formalizes what the recent CR ships were already practicing and extends the discipline as the baseline for all future UI.

**§1.9.11 One-Handed Auto-Attack Feel — character handles routine, player handles interesting decisions.** The creative direction for auto-attack under One-Handed mode explicitly rejects the "idle game" register where automation signals that the player has stepped back. Instead, the mode's feel is that the character is handling the routine rhythm input so the player can focus on movement, positioning, and swipe-timed abilities. Attack visual cadence is identical to manual attack — same animation, same damage numbers, same combo-count progression, same collision opportunity glow. Auto-lock is framed as character initiative ("the character picks a target") through subtle orientation and weapon-ready stance changes on target transfer, not as system automation. The swipe layer carries the full expressive weight — dodge direction, lunge choice, retreat shot timing — because automating the input with the least tactical depth (tap rhythm) preserves full fidelity on the input with the most (directional swipes). Block long-press produces a "committed stance" register distinct from manual tap-to-block, leaning into held-pressure feel rather than tap-toggle feel. What the mode does NOT do: no "AUTO-ATTACK" UI indicator, no distinct color/aura/animation variant marking the mode, no audio cue announcing the preset, no difficulty adjustment. The result: One-Handed play reads as a different *input style* for the same character in the same game, not as a "lite" version.

**§8.53 Accessibility Modes — settings tonal discipline + per-mode creative direction + privacy defaults.** The Accessibility settings panel uses observational language, not medical or remedial framing — "Visual Enhancement" not "For Hearing-Impaired Players," "Reaction-Assist Dodge" not "For Players with Motor Impairments," "One-Handed Layout" not "Simplified Controls." Descriptions state mechanics, not justifications. A player enabling Visual Enhancement for preference reasons gets the same label framing as a hearing-impaired player enabling it for accessibility reasons. Per-mode creative direction: Visual-Enhanced as *amplification with motion discipline* (scalar on existing cues, not new signals); Reaction-Assist as *invisible to self, visible in outcome* (the feel of successful dodge identical across modes); One-Handed as *full fidelity on core loop, constrained on sub-tactics* (auto-attack register as character fighting competently, not game playing itself); Shape-First as *the quiet refactoring* (designers internalize the discipline rather than adding it post-hoc). Privacy as default: no accessibility mode is publicly visible to other players — a player using Reaction-Assist has no visible indicator, a player in One-Handed mode doesn't appear differently to party members, Arena opponents cannot tell whether the other uses Assist. This privacy default is the correct ethical register: accessibility is personal, and broadcasting mode usage would create social pressure that undermines the modes' purpose.

**Nineteenth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.27).** The observational, warm-without-ceremony voice continues — accessibility is articulated as creative discipline rather than feature-list accommodation. v5.27's specific contribution across three additions is (1) the formalization of shape-first as a design-gate check, (2) the articulation of accessibility-as-equivalent-presence for the One-Handed preset, and (3) the explicit thesis that accessibility features built correctly improve the experience for everyone — shape-first UI reads better in sunlight, Visual-Enhanced serves muted-audio contexts, Reaction-Assist helps anyone with occasional fatigue, One-Handed lets any player switch to single-thumb play when circumstances require. The thread through v5.24 (sprite scaling), v5.25 (shrine as zone-native structure), v5.26 (peripheral-vision combat awareness) continues — creative direction articulates the same experience across mechanical articulation, and v5.27 extends this into the accessibility surface.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions and extensions — §1.1.1b gains the shape-first table (inserted after the existing DO/DON'T material-rendering table), §1.9.11 is a new subsection, §8.53 is a new subsection. All existing §1.1.1b reference material, existing §1.9.1-§1.9.10 UI/UX direction, existing §8.1-§8.52 content, and all other CR content remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.43 (Accessibility Compilation Bundle — Entries 47, 48, 49, 50). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.42 → v12.43; companion architecture reference bumped v2.16 → v2.17 (§9.5.11 extension — three PlayerPreferences flags: `visual_enhanced_mode`, `reaction_assist_dodge`, `one_handed_mode`, matching the v12.38 `collision_glow_enabled` + `haptics_enabled` pattern).

### CR v5.26

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.42 Combat Legibility Compilation Bundle #4 (Entries 4, 11, 20). Three creative-direction additions matching the "combat legibility" thread: (1) §8.21 Combo Chain extension covering Visual Combo Posture — per-weapon flourish pose creative direction (Greatsword planted weight, Sword ready precision, Bow held breath, Staff gathered magic); (2) §1.9.3 Inspect Card Hierarchy extension for the progressive derivative reveal animation and "earned visibility" tonal register; (3) new §1.9.10 Threat Direction Arrows visual direction covering the muted-orange color choice, peripheral-grade rendering discipline, shape-based direction, fade-on-transition feel.

**Unifying creative thesis: peripheral-vision combat awareness.** All three additions extend the peripheral-vision discipline established by v12.38's weapon edge glow. Information is placed in the peripheral zone where expert players already attend, rendered at intensities that register without demanding focal attention, and structured so that the information's meaning is legible at a glance without decoding. Combat at v12.42 reads across multiple peripheral channels simultaneously: weapon edge for collision compatibility, character body for combo tier, above-enemy for threat direction, inspect card for stat-investment depth. Expert ARPG play perceives all channels peripherally while foveal attention stays on the active combat focus; new players learn each channel as they encounter situations that require it.

**§8.21 Combo Chain extension — flourish poses as weapon-identity signatures.** The per-weapon count-3 flourish pose carries each weapon type's personality into the combo-state signal: Greatsword *weight/commitment/earth* (blade planted with weight coiling), Sword *precision/mobility/air* (ready-stance, narrow silhouette), Bow *stillness/focus/held breath* (fully drawn, static tension), Staff *magic/gathered power/sustained potential* (gem suspended above staff tip, orbiting particles). A player who switches between weapon types across their career learns their character's flourish vocabulary across all four — the weapon change is read through body language, not just weapon swap. Posture as public character identity extends §5.9.5's visible-to-all-players rule: a party of four at count 3 produces a diverse tableau of flourish poses legible to each member simultaneously. Spectators watching high-skill play read the posture and understand "this player has worked the full combo chain and is about to deliver" before the next swipe lands. The discipline: posture as visual overlay that never compromises mechanical responsiveness — a character in count-3 flourish who starts running shifts smoothly between poses without stutter.

**§1.9.3 Inspect Card — progressive reveal animation as earned visibility.** The v12.42 reveal (0.3s slide-from-right + 0.5s subtle highlight) fires the next time the player opens their inspect card after a stat crosses a derivative-visibility threshold. No audio, no popup, no notification — the animation is the sole acknowledgment, happening inside the inspect card the player chose to open. Tonal register is *earned visibility* rather than *unlocked content* — observational framing ("Status Duration is now visible on your inspect card") not congratulatory ("You've unlocked Status Duration!"). Typography parity: newly-revealed rows render in the same style as always-visible primary derivatives, reinforcing that the derivative was always applying mechanically — only the inspect-card presence was gated. **No opt-out toggle by design** — configuring the mechanic away would destroy what it's configuring (the small recognition of the card growing richer with investment).

**§1.9.10 Threat Direction Arrows — muted orange as threat-neutral attention.** The color choice sits between Flame red (would read as urgency) and Flora green (would read as safety) — muted orange signals *attention* in the peripheral sense without triggering alarm response. 70% alpha keeps the arrow catchable peripherally while never competing with the enemy sprite itself. Shape-based directional information is independent of color (triangle shape works in all colorblind palettes and high-contrast sunlight conditions). Fade-on-transition (~0.3s) makes aggro shifts *legible as events* rather than silent snaps — a Tank playing against a boss whose aggro flips to the DPS sees the arrow fade away and re-resolve at the DPS, providing the window needed to re-taunt before damage lands. No audio component (audio layered on threat arrows would produce constant clicking during active combat). Solo play: 5 aggroed enemies produce 5 converging arrows, visceral peripheral signal of threat concentration. Party play: Tank's aggro-management loop becomes tight and responsive because transitions are visible.

**Eighteenth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.26).** The thread continues — observational, warm-without-ceremony, respects player discovery over system presentation. v5.26's specific contribution is the extension of peripheral-vision combat-awareness vocabulary into three new channels simultaneously. Together with v5.22 (weapon edge glow + haptic feedback + resonance streak badge) and v5.23 (entropy overlay + grace tick), the combat-scene creative direction now articulates roughly nine peripheral channels reading specific kinds of state without overlap.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions and extensions — §8.21 gains the flourish-pose subsection at end, §1.9.3 gains the progressive-reveal subsection at end, §1.9.10 is a new subsection. All existing §8.21 combo-chain content (resonance interaction, Primordial fight examples), existing §1.9.3 inspect card hierarchy content (nameplate + full card + showcase rules), and existing §1.9.1-§1.9.9 remain authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.42 (Combat Legibility Compilation Bundle #4). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.41 → v12.42; companion architecture reference bumped v2.15 → v2.16 (§9.5.10 extension for `derivatives_revealed` Set field on PlayerState).

### CR v5.25

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.41 Communal Gem Pool at Convergence Shrine (Entry 40 REVISED variant). One new §8 entry: §8.52 The Convergence Communal Pool Shrine — Creative Direction. Covers the shrine's visual identity, material direction, lighting discipline, accumulation visualization, contribution and purchase moment registers, audio presence, and the tonal thesis of persistence-through-trace rather than transaction-hub.

**The load-bearing creative choice — the shrine is of the zone, not deployed to the zone.** The Convergence is the game's thematic endgame zone where alignments meet. A transactional NPC vendor would introduce commercial machinery into the space and turn the zone into "the shop for Tier 7 gems when direct trade fails." The shrine instead extends the zone's existing lore ("two alignments meet here and both leave traces") into the economic mechanic itself: gems from both alignments commingle in shared neutral inventory, accumulating and depleting through the zone's own activity without external management. The shrine belongs to the Convergence rather than being deployed to it.

**Material and color discipline — pre-game neutral.** The shrine is a weathered stone altar, waist-high, roughly 2m wide, positioned precisely at the geometric midpoint between the Shadow and Radiant halves of the zone. Material read: unadorned cool grey-brown stone with hairline fractures, a patina of age, and subtle weathering textures. The color register refuses to align with either alignment — neither Shadow-dark nor Radiant-light. This is load-bearing: any directional lighting or color bias favoring one side would make the altar feel aligned, which contradicts its neutral function. The altar reads as having been present before either alignment claimed the Convergence.

**Element motes drift from the contents, not from the structure.** The one concession to visual interest is a faint multicolored glow rising from the basin itself, colored by the elements of the gems currently held in the pool. A Fire-heavy pool produces faint red motes drifting upward; a Frost-heavy pool, faint blue; a balanced pool, drifting multicolored particles. The light comes from the accumulated traces, not from the stone. When the basin is empty, the drift dies and the shrine becomes ambient-dormant visual until the next contribution arrives. The basin is always honest about its current state — no deceptive "infinite inventory" hiding; the visible pile is the real inventory.

**Contribution moment — a single faint mote and one observational line.** When a player dies in the Convergence and one of their equipped gems transfers to the pool, a single faint element-colored mote lifts from the player's body and drifts toward the shrine, fading as it crosses the zone. Small (~15px at standard zoom), soft, missable if not looking. No audio, no screen effect, no text popup beyond one observational line on the post-death summary: *"Your [Element] gem is now in the Communal Pool."* The register matches Mayor Bro's §53.6 act-transition discipline — a statement of what happened, not a ceremony. The dying player's competence to understand the mote's meaning is respected without further explanation.

**Purchase moment — persistence becomes transmission.** When another player purchases a contributed gem from the pool, the contributor receives a soft notification: *"Your [Element] gem from [Day N] has been taken up by another."* Not "Your gem has been used!" Not "Someone paid for your contribution!" Just *taken up by another* — which captures the correct feeling. The contributor lost the gem when they died; another player has now given it continuation. That's the entire emotional content: a death mark becomes a continuation mark for someone else.

**What the shrine is NOT.** Not an NPC vendor (no person, no speech, no salesman figure). Not a merchant stall (no banners, no signage, no "open for business" language). Not gold-sink UI design (prices present but deliberately understated; display emphasizes the gem and contributor credit over the cost). Not aligned (no Shadow or Radiant iconography anywhere). Not permanent-empty (accumulates over time even on dead servers). Not a "low population indicator" (always there regardless of population; behavior adapts to activity, but presence is constant).

**Seventeenth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.25).** Voice continues unchanged — observational, warm-without-ceremony, respects player discovery over system presentation. v5.25's contribution extends the register into physical zone infrastructure: a structure that behaves as the zone already behaves, rather than a UI element dropped into the zone. The thread through v5.15 (Gambling Den creative direction), v5.19 (late-depth hybrid zones), v5.21 (zone-paced archetype arrivals), v5.24 (endgame sprite-size references) continues — the zone system is articulated at the creative level in parallel with its mechanical articulation.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §8.52 new subsection only. The existing §8.50 Gambling Den and §8.51 Act-Transition Acknowledgments content remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.41 (Communal Gem Pool at Convergence Shrine — Entry 40 REVISED). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.40 → v12.41; companion architecture reference bumped v2.14 → v2.15 (§9.5.13 Convergence Communal Gem Pool DO).

### CR v5.24

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.40 Non-Linear Monster Size Jumps (Entry 13 REVISED variant). Reference-consistency ship — small and surgical. Four existing references to the pre-v12.40 endgame sprite sizes (128×128 at depth 8, 144×144 at depth 9, 160×160 at depth 10) are updated to match the v12.40 endgame-scale plateau (176×176 across depths 8-10): §1.1.1 Visual Identity sprite-size range updated to the v12.40 depth-8-jump framing; §1.1.2 Imp visual evolution updated from `128×128+` to `176×176 endgame scale`; §2 Brute collapse audio sizing reference updated from `128×128` to `176×176 endgame-scale`; §8 Brute Collapse creative direction updated from `128×128` to `176×176 endgame-scale`. The CR ship is pure reference maintenance — no new subsections, no new creative direction content, no new tonal material beyond what v5.23 established.

**Why this ship is a CR version bump rather than a hygiene correction.** The v12.40 GDD ship is a canonical design-ship (rewrites §6.8 Monster Sizes with new progression, adds design intent, changes cross-referenced content across §14.1, §55.2, §56.2, §6.3a). The CR edits reference concrete sprite sizes that v12.40 changed; they must be synchronized with the GDD at the design-ship level rather than deferred to the next hygiene pass. CR v5.24 is the smallest possible design-ship that still needs to be a design-ship — a reference-consistency update triggered by a canonical content change, documented inline so future readers understand why the Brute-collapse description says "176×176" and what that ties to.

**Sixteenth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.24).** The run continues, though v5.24 is lighter in design-weight than any CR ship since v5.16. The voice continues unchanged — observational, warm-without-ceremony — but the content is reference-maintenance rather than new creative direction. No new §8 entry, no new tonal register, no new channel opened.

**Zero content changes beyond the four surgical edits.** §1.1.1, §1.1.2, §2 Brute audio, and §8 Brute Collapse each have one sentence updated to reflect the new endgame size. The surrounding creative direction is preserved exactly — the Imp's mischievous-to-demonic progression register is unchanged, the Brute-collapse three-phase sound is unchanged, the "Brute as tension-release" creative thesis is unchanged. Only the specific numeric references are updated.

**Zone-as-pedagogy presentation thread.** v5.24 is a small continuation of the thread v5.21 (zone-paced archetype arrivals) and v5.13 (zone collision curricula) have been building. Where v5.21 addressed *when* each archetype arrives in each zone, v5.24 implicitly addresses *what it looks like* when it arrives at endgame scale — a depth-8 Hexer (archetype introduced at depth 7 per v12.37, now at endgame sprite size per v12.40) is a meaningfully larger visual presence than a depth-7 Hexer. The combination reads as "the endgame has arrived" more decisively than either ship could produce alone.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.40 (Non-Linear Monster Size Jumps — Entry 13 REVISED). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.39 → v12.40. Architecture unchanged at v2.14 (sprite size is content data, no runtime architecture impact).

### CR v5.23

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.39 Visible Mana Entropy + Grace Period (Entry 21 REVISED variant, closing the v12.25 deferred item). Two creative-direction additions covering the mana-flow visibility layer: (1) §1.4.3 Mana Entropy Overlay and Grace Tick — full visual direction for the red entropy overlay and green grace-tick countdown-arc; (2) §2.4 extension adding the v12.39 grace-collision haptic micro-tick. The CR ship is smaller than v5.22 because v12.39's design weight is mechanical (GDD §3.5a grace-period mechanic) rather than atmospheric — the CR work articulates the HUD surface that makes the mechanic legible without adding new sensory-channel direction beyond what v5.22 established.

**§1.4.3 — resource dynamics as flow-state signature.** The load-bearing creative idea is that the mana bar's *behavior* becomes the flow-state surface a player reads peripherally during combat. The red entropy overlay grows with spam-paced play and shrinks as recent collisions drop out of the 15-second window; the green grace tick appears after every collision and depletes its countdown arc over 2 seconds, resetting whenever a grace collision extends the window. A rhythm-paced player watches the green tick appear and reset smoothly, the red overlay stay absent, and the mana fill stay consistently high — a visual rhythm that reads as "you're in flow" without any explicit flow-state indicator. A spam-paced player sees the inverse pattern. The mechanic self-teaches through the visibility: rhythm-paced play looks different than spam-paced play at the mana-bar level, and players learn which pattern they want to cultivate through observation alone.

**Right-aligned overlay, not full-bar tint.** The red overlay renders on the right portion of the mana bar rather than covering the left-aligned fill or tinting the full bar. This is a deliberate visual choice — right-alignment reads as *"your mana's effective cap for the next collision's restore is reduced,"* which accurately describes the mechanic (per-collision restore is scaled down by entropy). A full-bar tint would read as *"your mana is contaminated,"* which would imply a different mechanic (mana pool degradation). Visual positioning carries semantic weight; the right-aligned approach preserves the left-fill as the primary mana indicator while layering entropy cost as a secondary ceiling.

**Green grace tick as "ready," not as "safe."** Green in most games signifies low-threat or healthy state; in the grace-tick context green signifies *available opportunity*. The same player who sees green as "healthy HP" elsewhere in the HUD now sees green as "next collision gets full restore." The color re-mapping works because shape (pulse-ring with countdown arc) and position (above mana bar) contextualize the green to the mana system specifically, not to general health state. The 2-second arc depletion unfolds as a *breath rhythm* rather than as a strict timer, which matches the "tireless" flow-state register established in v5.22.

**Reset-pulse discipline.** When the player triggers another collision within the grace window, the tick's arc resets to full with a brief +10% scale bounce (~100ms) that confirms the reset. The reset-pulse catches attention peripherally without demanding it. A rhythm-paced player chaining grace collisions sees a steady sequence of pulse-resets that communicates "you're in flow" through the icon's behavior. The pulse is deliberately small and quiet — no audio, no particle effect, no screen shake. Just the small bounce. This matches the v5.22 streak-badge discipline: confirmation over celebration.

**§2.4 extension — grace-collision haptic micro-tick.** The v12.38-authored Haptic Feedback Design gains a 13th event: a subtle 5ms light-intensity micro-tick that fires ~20ms after the main collision haptic when the collision is a grace collision. The double-event rhythm reads as "collision + confirmation" in the fingers — the main collision triple-pulse lands, followed by a small tactile echo that confirms the grace bypass. Pattern-coherence is preserved because the micro-tick extends the existing collision haptic rather than replacing it with a distinct pattern; players feel the additional state through rhythm extension rather than through pattern substitution. Hearing-impaired players benefit — during extended sessions where visual HUD scanning fatigue sets in, the haptic micro-tick provides a secondary confirmation channel that the grace bypass applied.

**Fifteenth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.23).** The tonal voice continues — observational, warm-without-ceremony, respects player instrument-awareness over system-broadcasting. v5.23 extends the mana-flow design surface opened in v5.22: the Resonance Streak badge + mana-restore amplification (v5.22) plus the entropy overlay + grace tick + micro-tick (v5.23) together produce a fully articulated mana-region HUD that reads flow-state through resource dynamics alone. Together with the v12.26 atmospheric audio (§2.1-§2.3) and v12.27 combat-feedback visibility, the sensory vocabulary for combat now spans visual focal (Dynamic Reticle), visual peripheral (Weapon Edge Glow), visual resource-dynamics (entropy overlay + grace tick), audio, tactile (Haptic Feedback + grace micro-tick), and the resonance-streak counter — a sensory layering that makes flow state felt and seen through multiple simultaneous channels.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions — §1.4.3 new subsection, §2.4 v12.39 extension paragraphs. The existing §1.4.1 Resource Bar Behaviors, §1.4.2 Damage Number Resistance Tier Presentation, and §2.4 12-event haptic registry remain authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.39 (Visible Mana Entropy + Grace Period — Entry 21 REVISED, closing v12.25 deferred item). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.38 → v12.39; companion architecture reference bumped v2.13 → v2.14 (combat-state `grace_window_start` field extension).

### CR v5.22

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.38 Combat-Sensory Compilation Bundle #3 (Entries 17, 5, 29). Three creative-direction additions, each small and matching the "combat-sensory recognition" thread: (1) §8.19 Resonance extension covering the Resonance Streak "tireless flow state" register for v12.38's mana-restore streak mechanic; (2) new §1.9.9 Collision Opportunity Glow visual direction covering the peripheral-signal weapon-edge-glow treatment; (3) new §2.4 Haptic Feedback Design covering the 12-event haptic registry with per-event tonal rationale.

**The unifying creative thesis: tool responsiveness, not system broadcasting.** All three additions share a register — the player's instruments and their own resource dynamics become the information display rather than adding new broadcast elements on the screen. The Resonance Streak is felt in mana-bar behavior (a tool-state the player already tracks) rather than in a new damage number. The Collision Opportunity Glow is felt in the weapon's edge glow (the hand in the foreground) rather than in markers on enemy sprites. The Haptic Feedback is felt in the device itself (the phone in the player's grip) rather than in new HUD elements. Each channel treats something the player already holds or possesses as the information surface, which reads as empowering and instrument-aware rather than as training-wheels or system-explanation.

**§8.19 Resonance extension — the "tireless, not devastating" flow-state register.** The load-bearing register choice distinguishes Bro Town's flow state from the damage-escalation framing most ARPGs reach for. When a player is in flow, they feel *tireless* (never running out of resources) rather than *devastating* (hitting harder and harder). The streak's mana-restore amplification captures this exactly — a chained-resonance player watches their mana bar stay fuller than baseline during extended combat, and the bar's *behavior* becomes the flow state's visible signature. The streak badge itself is deliberately quiet — confirmation rather than celebration, no pulsing ring, no color escalation at high streaks. Streak loss is silent; narrating the loss would be worse than the loss itself. The mana-flow channel works as flow-state surface because mana is already part of the HUD, already associated with sustain, and already watched peripherally by veterans. Streak amplifies the bar's fullness without adding new visual elements. A veteran learns to read mana-bar behavior as flow-state telemetry in a way that reads as lived-in mastery rather than as numeric optimization.

**§1.9.9 Collision Opportunity Glow — subtle tool awakening.** The weapon-edge glow treats the player's own tool as the information display instead of cluttering the battlefield with markers. Rendering is a 2-pixel outer edge ring in the setup element's color (§10.1 palette), following the weapon's sprite contour. Intensity scales with the most-urgent nearby compatible status's remaining duration — a peripheral vision signal that encodes both element (color) and urgency (intensity) in one channel. Max 60% alpha never competes with central-vision combat elements. Shape-edge texture supplements color for colorblind-palette players, preserving information through the §10.1 shape-first design. The framing discipline is load-bearing: a glowing weapon reads as "my tool is coming alive" (empowering), while a marker on an enemy reads as "the enemy has been identified for you" (training-wheels). The entry articulates why the weapon-on-player approach beats the not-shipped status-icon-arrow alternative (clutter scaling, information richness, interpretability, combat-density constant signal).

**§2.4 Haptic Feedback Design — accessibility-first tactile layer.** Full creative direction for the 12-event haptic registry with per-event rationale: why Perfect Block is a double-pulse "click" (matches the mental register of precise contact, like a latch snapping; critically provides a tactile confirmation for hearing-impaired players who can't hear the Perfect Block audio cue); why Godly reveal is wave-modulated over 3.5s (device *holds* the moment in the player's hands, matching the reveal's transcendent register); why Death is a single heavy pulse followed by silence (the silence-after carries the dramatic weight of losing the character's run); why Hardness 5 has a 4-pulse forge-hammer rhythm (constructive rather than gifted, paralleling the §8.12a grace-vs-grit audio motif distinction). The accessibility-first framing ensures the layer serves hearing-impaired players genuinely — audio-critical signals all have tactile counterparts. The "never primary signaling" discipline preserves the haptic's role as additive confirmation rather than required channel. Three duration tiers (combat-event 10-120ms, moment 50-120ms, cinematic up to 3.5s) prevent fatigue through per-tier intensity and frequency calibration.

**Fourteenth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.22).** The tonal voice continues — observational, warm-without-ceremony, respects player instrument-awareness over system-broadcasting. v5.22 extends the voice into three sensory channels that most design-reference docs neglect: the resource-ledger channel (mana flow telemetry), the peripheral-visual channel (weapon edge glow), and the tactile channel (haptic feedback). Together with v12.26's atmospheric audio (§2.1-§2.3) and v12.27's combat-feedback visibility, v12.38 produces a combat feel that reads across visual focal, visual peripheral, audio, resource-ledger, and tactile channels simultaneously.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions — §8.19 extension paragraphs, §1.9.9 new subsection, §2.4 new section. The existing §8.19 resonance content (tier-by-class feel, The Primordial and Resonance, Eclipse moment) remains authoritative unchanged. §1.9.7 and §1.9.8 marketplace subsections unchanged. §2.1-§2.3 audio direction unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.38 (Combat-Sensory Compilation Bundle #3 — Entries 17, 5, 29 from the experience-first integration plan). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.37 → v12.38; companion architecture reference bumped v2.12 → v2.13 (combat-state streak field + preferences schema `collision_glow_enabled` + `haptics_enabled` additions).

### CR v5.21

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.37 Progressive Archetype Introduction. Adds a single new subsection: §3.4 Archetype Arrival per Zone, extending §3 Zone Personality with per-zone creative direction for how each of the 6 non-Fodder archetypes (Brute, Swarm, Sentinel, Volatile, Stalker, Hexer) arrives at its introducing depth in each of the 8 elemental spokes. Also specifies the first-encounter notification tonal register matching the v12.33 Surfaced Mastery Ladder discipline.

**"The archetype belongs to the zone" as the design principle.** The load-bearing creative choice for v12.37's creative work is that each archetype × zone combination should feel native rather than universal. Brutes in Ember Fields should read as volcanic-geology-producing-a-massive-creature; Stalkers in Frozen Shore should read as "the silence always had something moving in it; now we see it"; Hexers in Deep Hollows should read as "the rock itself applying Fracture." The archetype's combat identity (Brute's mass, Stalker's stealth, Hexer's status-application) connects to the zone's atmospheric identity (Ember Fields' geology, Frozen Shore's silence, Deep Hollows' claustrophobic stone). Each encounter is designed rather than procedural.

**Per-zone arrival framing — eight zones × six archetypes = 48 arrival moments.** Each of the 8 elemental spokes (Ember Fields, Frozen Shore, Mistwood, Thunder Peaks, Deep Hollows, Sky Reaches, Tidal Caves, Verdant Wilds) gets a full depth-2-through-depth-7 arrival specification, with each archetype's first appearance framed through the zone's atmospheric identity. Key patterns that emerge across the 48 combinations:

- **Brutes** (depth 2) embody each zone's *mass* — Ice-Titan in Frozen Shore, Fungal-Mass in Mistwood, Stone-Colossus in Thunder Peaks, Tidal-Mass in Tidal Caves, Growth-Mass in Verdant Wilds. The Brute's patience-teaching slow combat pacing fits zones that already reward patience.
- **Swarms** (depth 3) embody each zone's *coordinated motion* — Ember Swarm in heat channels, Ice-Shard in frost waves, Spore Swarm in fog currents, Static-Flit in storm flashes, Crystal-Scuttle in rock, Wind-Rider on air currents, School Swarm in water curves, Pollen-Spire from ground.
- **Stalkers** (depth 6) use each zone's *native cover* — heat-shimmer in Ember Fields, the silence in Frozen Shore, fog in Mistwood, cloud shadows in Thunder Peaks, near-dark in Deep Hollows, silence gaps in Sky Reaches, underwater cover in Tidal Caves, canopy density in Verdant Wilds. Every zone already has cover-producing atmosphere; Stalkers make that cover suspicious.
- **Hexers** (depth 7) combine each zone's primary-element status with the §14.4 hybrid zone's secondary-element status. Depth 7 is the *status-application peak* in hybrid zones — two status types applied at range, leveraging the §14.4 late-depth hybrid system.

**Shadow Sanctum and Radiant Heights exclusion.** The §3.4 entry explicitly documents that depth-9 Shadow Sanctum and depth-10 Radiant Heights receive no per-archetype arrival moments because all seven archetypes are already established by the time a player reaches these endgame zones. Instead, the zones' creative framing is that all archetypes take on the zone's alignment-aesthetic — Shadow Sanctum archetypes are shadow-cast, Radiant Heights archetypes are light-saturated. This preserves the zone-identity principle established by §14.4's own Dark/Light exclusion (v12.35) and by §1.2c (the pure-element endgame zones don't blend).

**First-encounter notification register matches v12.33 discipline.** §3.4 specifies that the GDD §6.3b one-time Codex reveal fires with the same *brief, observational, informational rather than celebratory, opt-in hint rather than instruction* register established by v12.33 Surfaced Mastery Ladder notifications. Example phrasing for the Stalker reveal: *"New archetype discovered: Stalker. Invisible until attacking. Lock-on is the counter — it reveals position early. The Codex has a new entry."* The notification is not a tutorial popup, not a cutscene, not a celebratory banner — it is an acknowledgment that new Codex content is available and a single-sentence tactical hint.

**Thirteenth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.21).** The tonal voice continues — observational rather than announcing, warm-without-ceremony, respects player discovery over system presentation. v5.21's specific contribution extends the voice into the monster-archetype system: the game teaches through zone-atmospheric native-arrival rather than through announcement popups, and each archetype's arrival in each zone is a designed atmospheric moment rather than a random-encounter mechanical event.

**Completes the zone-as-pedagogy creative thread.** With v5.13 (§8.48 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery creative direction), v5.15 (Gambling Den creative direction), v5.17 (Surfaced Mastery Ladder creative direction), v5.19 (§1.2c Late-Depth Hybrid Zones creative direction), and now v5.21, the zone-as-pedagogy creative thread has full articulation: zones teach collision curricula (v5.13), the learning curve is visible as Mastery Level tiers (v5.17), zones deepen at late depth rather than changing (v5.19), and now monster archetypes arrive natively zone-by-zone as a pedagogical curve (v5.21). The zone system's creative direction is fully specified across combat pedagogy, atmospheric deepening, and archetype introduction.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §3.4 new subsection only. The existing §3.1, §3.2, §3.3 content (implemented and planned zone mechanics, zone-specific lunge/retreat-shot interactions) remains authoritative unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.37 (Progressive Archetype Introduction — Entry 12 from the experience-first integration plan). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.36 → v12.37. Architecture unchanged at v2.12 — archetype availability is `zone-config.json` content data and first-encounter notification state is a Set field on existing character-state storage with the same pattern as v12.33's `certifications_earned`. No Durable Object, no schema migration, no runtime infrastructure.

### CR v5.20

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.36 Small-Entry Compilation Bundle #2 (Entries 38, 39, 53 — Act-Transition Acknowledgments, Title Showcase, Supporter Pass Ferryman Acknowledgment). Three creative-direction additions, each small and matching the "recognition moments" thread: (1) new §8.51 Act-Transition Acknowledgments — Creative Direction for Mayor Bro's tonal register across 6 transitions; (2) §1.9.3 Inspect Card Hierarchy extension for the dual-title showcase display typography; (3) §8.5a expansion adding the Ferryman's Supporter Pass line alongside his existing v12.5 Godly-weapon line.

**The unifying creative thesis: noticing, not celebrating.** All three additions share the same register — the game observes what the player has done without celebrating it. Act-transition lines from Mayor Bro are specific and observational ("You cleared the frost caves. That's real.") rather than congratulatory ("Amazing work!"). The dual-title showcase reads as story ("Hero · The Unlikely One") rather than as résumé. The Ferryman's Supporter Pass line is brief and plain ("Thank you. The lights stay on because of you.") rather than theatrical. This discipline continues the v5.10-v5.19 voice established across the integration sequence and extends it into narrative, social, and emotional recognition registers simultaneously.

**§8.51 is the largest authored piece.** The entry specifies Mayor Bro's tonal register per transition (watchful → respectful-with-reservation → weighty → awed-quietly → invested → collegial), mirroring the tonal progression his §33.7 tone-phase system already produces but keyed to high-weight accomplishment moments rather than quest counts. The entry articulates the difference between *congratulatory* and *observational* delivery with a paired example ("Amazing work clearing the frost caves! You're a true adventurer!" vs "You cleared the frost caves. That's real. The Shore doesn't give those up easily."), explaining why the second register treats the player as an adult participant in a world rather than as a protagonist the game cheers for. The "what Mayor Bro's lines are NOT" list (not cutscenes, not quest markers, not rewards, not mandatory, not repeatable, not broadcast) establishes the discipline that makes the feature texture-not-announcement.

**§1.9.3 dual-title typography is subtle but load-bearing.** The two-slot ceiling is deliberate — showing 3+ titles produces a résumé, showing 1-2 titles produces a story. The active title renders in its canonical weight; the showcase title renders at ~80% weight below the active title. Horizontal rendering uses middot separator ("Hero · The Unlikely One"); vertical space uses two-line rendering. Selection interaction is frictionless (no confirmation dialog, immediate swap from character-info panel). The single most important typographic property: the dual-title read should feel *narrative* rather than *accumulative* — a pairing of identities rather than a collection of achievements.

**§8.5a extension frames the Ferryman as the game's "fourth-wall thins" voice.** The entry adds the v12.36 Supporter Pass line as the Ferryman's second unique out-of-band trigger (after v12.5's Godly-weapon line). The entry articulates why the Ferryman rather than any other NPC: he is already the game's sole acknowledger of external forces (the gods), and that positioning extends naturally to acknowledging the real-world support that sustains the game. Mayor Bro saying the same line would read as intrusive; Bro Dealer saying it would read as salesy. From the Ferryman's mouth, acknowledging external support fits his established register. The two Ferryman unique lines together bracket the game's two kinds of external forces — divine gifts and real-world support — both acknowledged in the same voice, both brief, both unrepeated on the same trigger.

**"The fourth wall thins, not breaks" as the Ferryman's register.** The entry articulates that the Ferryman's acknowledgment of real-world support does not name the developer, does not reference Anthropic or hosting infrastructure, does not announce itself as breaking character. It just thanks the player plainly, once. This is the smallest possible acknowledgment of the real-world player-game relationship, fitted into an established voice rather than added as a separate narrative layer. The register preserves game immersion while acknowledging the reality of who keeps the servers running.

**Twelfth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence** (v5.9-v5.20). The tonal voice continues — observational, warm-without-ceremony, respects player discovery over system presentation — with v5.20's addition of *multi-register recognition* (narrative via Mayor Bro, social via dual-title, emotional via the Ferryman's Supporter Pass line) as the unified addition. Together these three registers complete the "recognition moments" design thread that v12.33 Surfaced Mastery Ladder started mechanically.

**Mayor Bro joins the designer-authored dialogue-specialization pattern.** The v12.34 §28.3.7 work established a framework: Mayor Bro handles tone progression; the Ferryman handles gods and now Supporter Pass; Bro Dealer handles Gambling Den modes. v12.36 confirms this pattern by extending Mayor Bro's specialization to include act-transition acknowledgments — a second dialogue system under his voice without introducing a new named NPC. Each designer-authored NPC now has a distinctive dialogue register; the roster is stable.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** The three additions are purely additive — §8.51 new entry, §1.9.3 extension paragraph, §8.5a new subsection. The existing §1.9.3 Inspect Card content through pre-v12.36 titles, the existing §8.5a Godly-weapon line and its v12.7 clarification, and all other §8 entries remain exactly as authored.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.36 (Small-Entry Compilation Bundle #2). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.35 → v12.36. Companion architecture reference bumped v2.11 → v2.12 (§9.5.6 PlayerState extension with nullable `showcase` field).

### CR v5.19

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.35 Late-Depth Elemental Hybrid Zones bundle. Adds a single new subsection: §1.2c Late-Depth Hybrid Zones, extending the §1.2a Zone Mood Guides with per-zone hybrid creative specifications for all 8 elemental spokes at depths 7-8.

**"Extension, not replacement" as the cross-zone design principle.** The load-bearing creative choice for v12.35 is that each hybrid zone's secondary element *makes the primary element more itself*, not less. Ember Fields depth 7-8 is not "Flame diluted with Stone" — it is "Flame revealing its geological bedrock." Frozen Shore depth 7-8 is not "Frost diluted with Storm" — it is "Frost gaining weather, adding violence to its indifference." Each of the 8 hybrid combinations is framed as the zone deepening into its own identity rather than losing it. This framing is what preserves the zone-as-curriculum thesis that v12.32 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery established — a player whose preferred zone is Ember Fields can encounter Flame+Stone collisions natively in Ember Fields without the zone feeling like it has become "Flame+Stone hybrid zone." It's still Ember Fields. Depth 7-8 is just where Ember Fields teaches the geology underneath its lava.

**Eight per-zone creative specifications.** Each hybrid zone (Ember Fields, Mistwood, Frozen Shore, Thunder Peaks, Deep Hollows, Sky Reaches, Tidal Caves, Verdant Wilds) receives a four-part creative direction at depth 7-8: palette blend (how the secondary element's colors layer into the primary palette), ambient audio layering (the secondary element's sonic texture added underneath the primary soundscape), signature depth 7-8 detail (the one visual element that defines the hybrid moment), and thematic framing (the one-phrase characterization of what the hybrid reveals about the zone's identity). Together these specifications give each hybrid zone a distinctive experiential register while preserving continuity with the depth 1-6 zone the player already knows.

**Transition choreography is deliberately gradual.** The entry specifies that depth 6→7 transitions should unfold over 60-90 seconds of traversal rather than as discrete boundaries. The player crosses into depth 7 and gradually notices the secondary element's presence — first as occasional environmental details, then as ambient audio additions, then as monster-encounter variations. By the time the player has been in depth 7 for 5-10 minutes, the hybrid atmosphere is fully established. This gradient preserves the "zone deepening" thesis; a hard transition would feel like entering a different zone.

**Shadow Sanctum and Radiant Heights exclusion reaffirmed.** The entry makes the Dark/Light exclusion explicit at the creative level: both zones' alignment-endgame identity depends on elemental clarity, and hybrid treatment would dilute the endgame's thematic weight. The exclusion is thematic, not mechanical — Dark and Light zones remain pure-element through all depths because their narrative role requires that purity.

**Zone-pedagogy infrastructure completion.** With v12.32 (Codex as map) and v12.35 (zones complete their curricula at late depth), the zone system now has a fully articulated pedagogical infrastructure: the Codex maps what each zone teaches, depth-graded encounters at depths 1-6 teach the primary-element collisions, and late-depth hybrid content at depths 7-8 teaches the secondary-element collisions that complete the curriculum. A player's preferred zone can now be their complete combat classroom. The zone-pedagogy arc that started with v12.32's Codex structuring reaches full expression in v12.35's zone-content structuring.

**Eleventh consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence** (v5.9-v5.19). The tonal voice continues — observational rather than announcing, warm-without-ceremony, respects player discovery over system presentation — with the v5.19 addition of *deepening-through-layering* as a zone-design principle. This complements the v5.18 atmospheric-texture-in-gambling-contexts dimension and extends the v5.10 town-tonal-phases work into the deeper zone-atmosphere design space.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §1.2c new subsection only.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.35 (Late-Depth Elemental Hybrid Zones bundle). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.34 → v12.35. Architecture unchanged at v2.11 — hybrid is content-data additions (monster-config.json gains 16 new entries, zone-config.json gains `late_depth_hybrid` field per zone). No runtime infrastructure, no Durable Object, no schema migration.

### CR v5.18

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.34 Gambling Den Time-of-Day Events bundle. Adds a single new §8 entry: §8.50 Gambling Den — Creative Direction, specifying the atmospheric register for each of the four time-of-day event modes (morning's quiet individual gambling, afternoon's Dealer's Hour with Bro Dealer, evening's House Tournament, late night's Quiet Room) and the voice of Bro Dealer as the Den's named designer-authored NPC. Tenth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence (v5.9-v5.18).

**The Den as a place with temporal texture.** The load-bearing creative idea in v12.34 is that a single space can have four distinct atmospheres across the server day without being mechanically fragmented. The pre-v12.34 Den was a single-atmosphere gold-sink widget behind a counter; the v12.34 Den is a *place* that changes feel across morning contemplation, afternoon observation, evening community, and late-night intimacy — four emotional registers that align with the town's §14.3.3 tonal phases. The atmospheric variation is the entire addition; the 60/40 house edge and underlying gambling mechanics are preserved identically across all modes.

**Bro Dealer's voice is deliberately narrow.** Short sentences, present tense, wryly observational without being mocking. The §8.50 entry provides a specific sample line set (fourteen example lines covering common situations across four categories — opening, post-win, post-loss, first-time wager) demonstrating the voice — *"Didn't see that coming"* for a winner, *"Bad run. Happens to the best of us."* for a loser, *"First time? The rules are simple. Luck isn't."* for a new player. The range is intentionally limited: Bro Dealer speaks little but what he says is distinctive enough that players will recognize his voice quickly and remember it. This produces the kind of NPC that generates spontaneous community quoting (*"Bro Dealer said..."*) without the game forcing the recognition.

**Gambling-without-spectacle as the register.** Most games handle gambling with high theatrics — cheering crowds for wins, dramatic music stings for losses, flashing lights and coin-cascade visuals. The §8.50 entry rejects this vocabulary entirely. The Den's atmospheres across all four modes share a restraint aesthetic: winners feel real joy, losers feel real disappointment, and the *space* doesn't amplify or suppress either emotion. The Quiet Room's late-night presentation is the most distilled version of this thesis — two players alone in a dim room, a single flickering lantern, silence broken only by a wooden-click token reveal. This *restraint creates the tension* in a way that dramatic embellishment couldn't.

**Named-NPC classification reinforced.** The §8.50 entry explicitly reinforces GDD §28.3.7's distinction between designer-authored NPCs (Bro Dealer, Mayor Bro, Ferryman, Tavern Keeper) and player-emergent mythology (the six §8.12a layers). Bro Dealer joins the designer-authored roster without adding to the player-emergent count. This keeps the two categories unblurred — a new Den NPC is not a seventh mythology layer; it's a seventh line on the designer roster. The distinction matters because the player-emergent framework's scaling properties (content generated by player action, different on every server) don't apply to designer-authored NPCs (same on every server).

**The town-as-place arc extends.** v12.26 Town Time-of-Day gave the *town* temporal texture (morning/afternoon/evening/late-night tonal phases across the whole town). v12.34 Gambling Den gives *one specific building within that town* its own temporal texture aligned with the town's phases. The creative pattern is now proven and portable — future ships may apply the same treatment to other town buildings (the Tavern during different phases, the Marketplace, the Blacksmith's forge) if desired. The §8.50 entry names this as an explicit design precedent, calling out that the Den's high-atmosphere-low-mechanical-complexity profile makes it an ideal test case for the pattern.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §8.50 new entry only.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.34 (Gambling Den Time-of-Day Events). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.33 → v12.34. Architecture unchanged at v2.11 — time-of-day event modes are server-time-computed branches on existing gambling infrastructure, no new Durable Object or persistent state required.

### CR v5.17

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.33 Surfaced Mastery Ladder bundle. Adds a single new §8 entry: §8.49 Surfaced Mastery Ladder — Creative Direction, specifying the creative register for tier-crossing notifications, certification notifications, Inspect Card display, and the opt-in hint voice. Ninth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence.

**The "acknowledgment, not celebration" thesis is the load-bearing creative choice.** Most games announce progression loudly — level-up jingles, achievement popups with badge art, celebratory banners that interrupt play. Experience-first design rejects this vocabulary. Progression becomes quieter, smaller, and deeper: a small line of text in the corner of the screen, a named tier on the Inspect Card, a count of certifications alongside character level. The §8.49 entry articulates the register specifically: no fanfare audio, no screen dim, no "Achievement Unlocked!" presentation, typography identical to standard UI text, 4-second lifetime non-blocking upper-left placement. The notification is an acknowledgment that the player just did something — not a ceremony around it.

**Tier-crossing vs certification size hierarchy.** The entry specifies a two-level visual hierarchy: tier crossings are larger events (entering a new pedagogical chapter, two-line notifications with opt-in hints), certifications are smaller marks within a tier (single-line notifications, no hints because the player just demonstrated the skill). This calibrates visual weight to event meaning without requiring explanation — players who see both over time develop intuitive sense of the event sizes.

**Never during combat — firm rule.** The entry makes explicit that Mastery Level notifications and certification notifications must never fire during active combat. If the trigger event occurs mid-combat, the notification queues and displays when combat ends. This protects the "never steal attention from gameplay" principle that has been a consistent thread across the v12.27, v12.29, v12.32, and now v12.33 pedagogical infrastructure ships.

**Context string voice as smallest narrative surface.** Each earned certification's context string ("triggered Steam in Ember Fields," "defeated Imp King," "timed Flash Freeze in the resonance window") is the smallest narrative surface in the game — a single past-tense clause that ties an abstract certification to a specific moment in the player's play. The relative-time phrasing ("47 days ago") consistent with §8.47 Weapon History timestamps gives certifications a sense of lived history: it happened, time has passed, now looking back at it.

**The pedagogical-infrastructure arc capstone.** §8.49 explicitly names v12.33 as the capstone of a four-ship pedagogical arc (v12.27 Combat Feedback Visibility, v12.29 Encyclopedia Flavor Text, v12.32 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery, v12.33 Surfaced Mastery Ladder). The creative voice across all four ships is identical: observational, warm-without-ceremony, respects player discovery over system presentation, invitation without instruction. Together the four ships form a complete pedagogical infrastructure that teaches through structure rather than through tutorials. The Mastery Ladder specifically makes the entire learning curve legible — a player sees not just combat feedback (v12.27) or zone curricula (v12.32) but the game's whole progression through mechanics over time.

**"Progression as recognition, not reward" thesis.** The entry's closing creative thesis articulates the Mastery Ladder's philosophical position: the player knows they just did something meaningful because they felt the mechanic fire; the notification is a small friendly confirmation, nothing more. The feeling of the mechanic is the reward; the notification is the acknowledgment. This framing is the game's clearest articulation yet of experience-first design principle — the mechanic itself is the meaningful content, and progression surfaces should recognize and name the experience, not manufacture celebration around it.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §8.49 new entry only. The existing §8.47 (Weapon History), §8.48 (Zone-Paced Collision Discovery), and earlier entries are unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.33 (Surfaced Mastery Ladder bundle). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.32 → v12.33. Architecture v2.10 → v2.11 (§9.5.10 character-state schema extension for `mastery_level`, `mastery_level_timestamps`, and `certifications_earned` fields).

### CR v5.16

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.32 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery bundle. Adds a single new §8 entry: §8.48 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery — Creative Direction, specifying the creative register for the Codex's zone-clustered presentation.

**The "Codex as map, not checklist" thesis is the load-bearing creative choice.** The entry articulates why map is the right metaphor — maps reward wandering through territory, checklists reward completion of items. Zone-clustered presentation treats each zone as a pedagogical place with content, making the player's discovered collisions feel like places they've been rather than items they've collected. This register shift is subtle but consequential: it preserves the "discovery is its own reward" design intent (no Master of Collisions achievement, no completion titles, no unlocks) while giving players a structured way to pursue the collision system intentionally.

**The teaser-register design for undiscovered entries is the most careful piece.** Undiscovered collisions are visible in the Codex (their existence is revealed) but their effect is not (discovery is preserved). The entry details how this register is achieved typographically: same typography as discovered entries, differentiated only by the open-circle vs checkmark glyph, no gray-italic-placeholder or silhouette-hidden-treasure treatment that would signal "here is an achievement to unlock." The teaser invites without instructing — players see element pairs and can pursue them purposefully, but first-trigger surprise is preserved.

**Eighth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence.** The tonal voice continues — observational rather than announcing, warm-without-ceremony, respects player discovery over system presentation. v12.32's specific contribution is extending the voice into *directed discovery* — the game surfaces what exists so players can pursue it, while preserving the discovery moment for what it does. This builds on v5.14's collaboration-across-ownership thesis and v5.15's neutral-presence-in-commercial-contexts thesis, adding "invitation without instruction" as the new v5.16 thematic dimension.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure addition — §8.48 new entry only.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.32 (Zone-Paced Collision Discovery bundle). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.31.1 → v12.32. Architecture unchanged at v2.10 — zone-paced discovery is entirely client-side rendering on existing state.

### CR v5.15

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.31 Marketplace Price Guidance and Flip Disclosure. Adds two new §1.9 subsections: §1.9.7 Price Guidance UI (typography register, layout specification, neutral-numeric delta presentation with explicit rejection of editorial labels like "good deal"/"overpriced", no-color treatment for delta lines, no-data state rendering, fallback-match annotation) and §1.9.8 Flip Indicator (↻ glyph treatment, past-tense text register, hover/tap popover content, explicit "what the flip indicator is NOT" list).

**Neutrality as load-bearing creative choice.** The two subsections share a single core thesis: *the marketplace presents information; it does not advocate.* Price guidance and flip disclosure could easily slip into editorial territory — colored text for above/below-average listings, warning icons for flips, "best price" labels for bargains. The CR direction deliberately rejects all of these because a neutral order book must remain neutral between buyers and sellers to preserve long-term marketplace trust. Factual-numeric framing ("15% below recent average") respects buyer judgment; editorial labels ("good deal!") implicitly pick winners and erode trust over time. The visual register is correspondingly restrained: no color, no highlight, no alarm-adjacent typography. Information is presented; conclusions are drawn by readers.

**Completes the marketplace-UI arc.** The v12.25 CR ship added §1.9.5 Friend List Activity Display and §1.9.6 Leaderboard Scope Presentation. The v12.31 ship adds §1.9.7 Price Guidance UI and §1.9.8 Flip Indicator. The §1.9 subsection now spans four consecutive marketplace/social UI entries (§1.9.5 through §1.9.8) that share the observational-neutral register — helping players see information relevant to their activity without advocating one way or another. This is consistent with the broader CR voice established across v5.9-v5.14.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions to §1.9 — §1.9.7 and §1.9.8 as new subsections. Existing §1.9.1 through §1.9.6 content unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.31 (Price Guidance and Flip Disclosure). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.30 → v12.31. Companion architecture reference bumped v2.9 → v2.10 for the Marketplace DO extensions.

### CR v5.14

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.30 Weapon History bundle. Extends §8.12a Player-Emergent Mythology — The Lottery Layer with the weapon-as-artifact phenomenon subsection (and updates the "layers of player-emergent content" summary from five to six), and adds §8.47 Weapon History — Creative Direction as a new full entry.

**The sixth mythology layer is structurally novel.** Previous mythology layers (Godly recipients, Hardness 5 owners, named blacksmiths, named gem-cutters, Tempered-weapon crafters) all centered on *named players*. The weapon-as-artifact layer is different: it centers on the *weapon itself* as an identity-carrying object whose content is co-authored by successive owners through starring. A high-History weapon carries social weight across ownership changes, accumulating layered curation as each successive wielder stars the entries they felt defined their time with the weapon. This is the most purely emergent of the six mythology categories — no single named player is at its center; the weapon is the named entity, and its biography is generated by play rather than authored by designers.

**§8.47 specifies the creative register** for Weapon History's inspect card presentation: humanized counter rendering (rounded to appropriate scale), relative timestamps ("47 days ago" rather than absolute dates, making the weapon's biography feel lived-in), minimal star typography (no color, no glow, no animation — just the ★ glyph), tap-to-toggle star interaction with no confirmation friction, and the "weapons accumulate meaning" thesis articulating the design intent. The entry also includes an explicit "what Weapon History is NOT" list (not achievement, not progression, not broadcast, not in-combat visible) consistent with the restraint-as-aesthetic-weight pattern established by Temper in §8.43.

**Tonal thread continues.** v5.14 is the sixth consecutive CR ship in the integration sequence. The voice established across v5.9-v5.13 — observational, warm-without-ceremony, respects player discovery over system presentation — continues in v5.14 with the added dimension of *collaboration across ownership*. Weapon History is the first CR entry that specifies content generated collaboratively by multiple players over time through an object they pass between them. This is a new design pattern, and §8.47 calls it out as such.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions — §8.12a extension paragraphs updating the framework from five layers to six, plus §8.47 as a new full entry. The existing §8.12a content through "Tempered-weapon crafters (v12.28)" remains exactly as authored in v5.12.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.30 (Weapon History bundle). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.29 → v12.30. Companion architecture reference bumped v2.8 → v2.9 for the `history` field on Item schema.

### CR v5.13

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.29 quiet surfaces compilation bundle. Adds three new creative direction entries to §8: §8.44 Encyclopedia Flavor Text (Fracture-lore-adjacent tonal register, length discipline, fragmentary-over-complete principle, per-category register variations for zone/material/collision flavor, good-vs-bad contrast examples), §8.45 Quiet Archetype Emergence (peripheral-vision discovery arc, 0.4-second interpolation during active combat, Journey Log as memory infrastructure, ambient other-player visibility), §8.46 Feedback Culture (author display typography, Voice title amber color anchor and award chime, developer-voice implementation credit register, opt-in anonymity without visual stigma, cumulative social-fabric effect over time).

**Tonal thread completion.** v5.13 is the fifth consecutive CR ship in this integration sequence, and the thematic thread is now unambiguous. v5.9 (accumulated lived-in presence), v5.10 (atmospheric layered ambient), v5.11 (legible combat-state through feel), v5.12 (quietest mythology surface), and now v5.13 (quiet surfaces compilation) all push in the same direction: **content that accumulates through engagement rather than arriving through announcement.** The creative voice is observational, not announcing; warm-without-ceremony; respects player discovery over system presentation. This voice is now load-bearing across ~14 new §8 entries shipped in five consecutive CR bumps, and future ships should default to this voice unless there's a specific reason to deviate (dramatic mythological moments like Godly reveals and Hardness 5 successes, which live in a different register entirely).

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions — §8.44, §8.45, §8.46. The existing §8 content is unchanged.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.29 (quiet surfaces compilation bundle). Companion GDD reference bumped v12.28.1 → v12.29. Architecture unchanged at v2.8.

### CR v5.12

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.28 Temper bundle. Extends §8.12a Player-Emergent Mythology — The Lottery Layer with two new paragraphs (the tempered-weapon phenomenon subsection and a "five layers of player-emergent content" summary extension) and adds §8.43 Temper — Creative Direction as a new full entry. The CR work for v12.28 is more significant than the surface size suggests because the named-craftsman mythology is the load-bearing thematic framing for the entire Hardness / crafter economy, and Temper's integration into that mythology required careful reframing of the existing grit-vs-grace / outcome-vs-process axes. The revised framing shifts the named-Blacksmith mythology from purely outcome-based ("Bro Mason's weapons are high-Hardness because Mason has Transcendent rank") to dual-layered (outcome *and* process — "Bro Mason's weapons are high-Hardness AND high-Temper"). This is additive to the existing mythology, not a replacement, and it produces a fifth distinct category of player-emergent content alongside Godly recipients, Hardness 5 owners, named blacksmiths, and named gem-cutters.

**Tonal discipline maintained.** The v5.12 CR work continues the observational-not-announcing register that has carried through v5.9/v5.10/v5.11. Temper is specifically designed as the game's *quietest* mythology surface — no audio cue, no broadcast, no in-world visual, inspect-only visibility with plain typography. The creative direction's central thesis in §8.43 is that Temper's aesthetic weight is precisely its restraint. This maintains the thematic coherence: every socially-visible system shipped in this integration sequence has been designed to acknowledge rather than celebrate, to let the content speak for itself rather than demand attention. Temper in particular fits this register because it is metadata-about-persistence, and persistence is exactly the kind of quality that loud celebration would undermine.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions — §8.12a extension paragraphs and §8.43 new entry. The existing §8.12a content (Godly/Hardness 5 cosmology, named-blacksmith, named-gem-cutter, sound rules, design intent block) is unchanged. §8.30 Crafter Attribution and Portfolio extends naturally under v12.28 with the example shop-note text for Temper philosophy, but the §8.30 section itself is not modified — the Temper-relevant shop note text is contained within §8.43 as an example of how named-crafter portfolios could use the extension.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.28 (Temper bundle). Companion GDD version bumped v12.27 → v12.28. Also triggered architecture bump v2.7 → v2.8 for the `temper` field on Item schema (companion to this CR ship, see `architecture.md`).

### CR v5.11

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.27 combat feedback visibility bundle. Adds two new creative direction entries to §8: §8.41 Active Block — Creative Direction (metallic chime + gold flash for perfect block, muted thud + brace for standard block, silence for miss; PvP audibility intent; explicit list of what Active Block is not — not parry, not counter, not stance, not AoE), §8.42 Dynamic Reticle — Creative Direction (three reticle visual registers, continuous transitions, accessibility via shape+position beyond color, auto-retirement farewell message tone and typography). The CR carries the felt-register half of the v12.27 combat-mechanics change — the GDD specifies the timing outcomes and mechanic pseudocode; the CR specifies what each outcome *feels like* through sound, animation, and visual register.

**Tonal thread continuity.** v5.9's accumulated-lived-in-presence thesis and v5.10's atmospheric-layered-ambient thesis both carry forward. v5.11 adds a third: **legible combat-state through feel.** The three Active Block outcomes are designed to be distinguishable by sound alone so skilled players read their own success acoustically. The Dynamic Reticle's auto-retirement mechanic and farewell tone reinforce the dry-congratulatory register the game uses for milestone acknowledgments without ceremony. Across v5.9/v5.10/v5.11, the CR's consistent voice is observational rather than announcing — the game acknowledges what the player is doing rather than celebrating it.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions to §8. No revisions to prior CR content.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.27 (combat feedback visibility bundle). Companion GDD version bumped v12.26 → v12.27.

### CR v5.10

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.26 environmental persistence + atmospheric polish bundle. Adds four new creative direction entries to §8 (§8.37 Bless Heal Wave visual, §8.38 Thorns Pack-Flash visual and audio, §8.39 Ground Mark tiers decay register, §8.40 Anniversary Ritual ceremony specification), two additions to §1 (§1.4.1 Resource Bar Behaviors, §1.4.2 Damage Number Resistance Tier Presentation), one new subsection to §1.7 (§1.7b Zone Marks on Players per-zone specification), one new subsection to §1.2 (§1.2b Town Time-of-Day Phases), and two new subsections to §2 (§2.2 Zone Emotional Audio Layer per-zone specification, §2.3 Combat Audio Cues for resistance tier and Bless heal).

The CR is carrying the substantial creative-direction load for the v12.26 ship, matching the GDD-side pattern where GDD changes are brief references and CR changes are full specifications. The unifying thesis: **atmospheric presence through layered ambient** — ground marks become tiered zone history, zones mark the characters who pass through them, town phases produce temporal shared memory, resource bars communicate body-state through motion, damage numbers carry tier information through shape and sound, and each zone develops a felt emotional identity through its audio layer. No single entry is large; together they densify the game's moment-to-moment presence considerably.

**Tonal discipline maintained.** The same "observation not announcement" register that governed v5.9's social layer entries governs v5.10's atmospheric entries. Anniversary Ritual closes with a single low chime, not fanfare. Zone Emotional Audio Layer is pianissimo-to-mezzo-piano throughout — present but never dominant. Zone marks on players are subtle enough not to obstruct combat readability. Ground mark tiers escalate visual persistence, not visual brightness. The creative thesis in each entry: *the world acts on the player ambiently, and the player notices through feel rather than announcement.*

**Authoring follow-ups in v12.26.1 content ship.** Three content pools are tone-directed in v5.10 but authored in a follow-up content ship: (1) per-zone emotional audio tracks for all 10 zone types (~10-15 tracks at ~30-60 seconds each), (2) phase-exclusive NPC lines for the Town time-of-day system (~80-120 lines across ~10 town NPCs), (3) thematic visual and audio assets for the Year 1 and Year 2 Anniversary Rituals (banner designs, ambient layers, closing chime pitches — per anniversary item).

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions to §1.4, §1.7, §1.2, §2, and §8. No revisions to prior CR content.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.26 (environmental persistence + atmospheric polish bundle).

### CR v5.9

Design-ship — companion to GDD v12.25 social layer bundle. Adds five new creative direction entries to §8 (§8.32 Vows Shrine ceremony, §8.33 role title flavor, §8.34 crafter portfolio presentation, §8.35 Clan Hall atmosphere, §8.36 NPC gossip tone per archetype) and two new subsections to §1.9 (§1.9.5 friend list activity display visual direction, §1.9.6 leaderboard scope presentation). Collectively, these creative entries define the tonal register for seven new socially-visible systems shipping in GDD v12.25. The unifying creative thesis: **accumulated lived-in presence over gamified display.** Ceremonies, halls, attributions, and gossip should all feel like they belong in a world inhabited by characters with histories, not in a game interface announcing accomplishments. The tonal discipline is load-bearing — a Vows Shrine that feels like a religious monument would break the game's tone; a Clan Hall that feels like a trophy room would undermine the "clan is a gathering place" thesis; NPC gossip that sounds like a news feed would convert the server pulse back into UI chrome. Each entry specifies not just the visual and audio direction but the *kind of feeling* the system should produce. Together they establish the creative vocabulary for future social-layer ships in the experience-first integration sequence (v12.26 combat feedback, v12.27 Temper system, and beyond).

**Authoring follow-ups in v12.25.1 content ship.** The ~30-title role title pool (tone-directed in §8.33), the ~200-line initial NPC gossip library (tone-directed per NPC archetype in §8.36), and creative-direction refinements that emerge during implementation will ship as a content update immediately following the v12.25 infrastructure ship.

**Zero content changes to existing sections.** Pure additions to §8 and §1.9. No revisions to prior CR content. Note on numbering: the originally-drafted patch used §8.28–§8.32 placeholders; actual integration assigned §8.32–§8.36 to avoid collisions with existing §8.28 Dark Corruption & Light Redemption, §8.29 Umbra, §8.30 Codex Hints New Collisions, and §8.31 Collision Preview. Similarly §1.9.5 and §1.9.6 follow the existing §1.9.1–§1.9.4 sequence.

**Triggered by:** GDD v12.25 (social layer bundle).

### CR v5.8

§6 Monetization rewritten to align with GDD §32 v12.11. Drift-correction ship.

- **What this corrects.** Prior to v5.8, §6 Monetization carried pre-v12.11 framing — `$10 ARPU target`, `$2 or less per item` with tiered $1–$2 pricing, a `30-day non-transferable hold` rule for discontinued drops, a `7.5% royalty on all secondary sales` structure, and a "secondary market royalties are the long-term play" revenue model. GDD §32 had rewritten the monetization model to $2/month Supporter Pass + $1 flat Pass-gated cosmetics + earned-in-game prestige drops with no royalty layer in v12.11, but the CR §6 content was not updated at the time. The drift persisted through v5.6.2 → v5.7 (nine versions) because the v5.6.2 changelog entry noted that GDD v12.11 "explicitly stated no creative implications" and the CR team took that assessment at face value. **That assessment was wrong.** Looking at §6's actual content — the philosophy framing, the prestige-drop experience rules, the revenue-model framing — each has real creative-direction implications for how monetization surfaces feel to the player. v5.8 corrects the drift.
- **Why the drift wasn't caught by tooling.** `check_cross_doc_refs.py` (Ship C-1, v12.19) verifies that cross-document filename references resolve to real files. It does not verify content consistency across the doc set. The GDD §32 v12.11 rewrite updated the GDD cleanly and the CR-side drift lived entirely inside CR §6's body content — no filename references, no broken links, nothing a reference-resolving tool would find. The class of drift ("canonical section rewritten in one doc, companion section in another doc carries stale framing that isn't automatically caught") was explicitly flagged in GDD v12.13's changelog as a pattern the Editing Protocol doesn't catch. v5.8 is the §6 instance of that pattern.
