- **§6 full rewrite.** The stale 32-line §6 (§6.1 Philosophy, §6.2 Item Categories, §6.3 Discontinued/Limited Drops, §6.4 Revenue Model) is replaced with a new ~130-line §6 structured as: §6.0 Authority Note (explicit split of concerns with GDD §32 — mechanics vs experience), §6.1 Philosophy — The Small Machinery (emotional tone of the purchase flow, what the model is NOT), §6.2 The Supporter Pass — Feel (purchase-flow register, Supporter marker visual treatment, expiry UX), §6.3 The Cosmetics Store — Feel (store feel, visual hierarchy rule, owned-cosmetics-persist rule), §6.4 Discontinued Prestige Drops — The "I Was There" Mythology (earned-in-game principle, tradeable-with-no-royalties rule, explicit removal of prior 30-day hold + 7.5% royalty framing with rationale, no-NFT framing), §6.5 The Godly / Hardness 5 Distinction (how the earned-visual peaks enable a modest cosmetics store), §6.6 Revenue Feel — Sustainability, Not Extraction (cost-recovery framing, published margin, Paying-Player-Never-Worse principle as creative anchor), §6.7 What Players Should Take Away (intended first-impression register). The expansion from 32 to ~130 lines is deliberate — the v12.11 model has more creative direction implications than the prior framing did, and the CR's job is to make those implications explicit for future designers implementing the monetization surfaces.
- **What changes in actual creative direction.** The visual hierarchy rule in §6.3 ("cosmetic store items should be visibly less prestigious than any earned-from-gameplay visual") is the single most important creative-direction update and is load-bearing. It is the operational defense against the Paying-Player-Never-Worse failure mode — if a $1 store cosmetic looked cooler than an OG Cape or a Hardness 5 badge, paying players would visually outclass earned-prestige players, which would violate GDD §32.11 and §32.9 item 12. §6.3 makes the hierarchy explicit, names the specific earned-prestige items the store must measure against, and specifies that it is enforced through the store's art direction (simple 1-2 color palettes for store trails vs multi-color elemental palettes for earned-prestige trails, modest soft glows for store auras vs build-specific directional intensity for archetype auras). Every future cosmetic item the store adds is measured against this rule. The §6.5 Godly/Hardness 5 subsection reinforces the hierarchy by explicitly placing the earned lottery items *outside* the monetization model entirely — they are the visual peaks that allow the store to be modest without feeling cheap.
- **§6.4 Prestige drop framing changes.** The prior framing ("30-day non-transferable hold", "7.5% royalty on all secondary sales", "one-time drops create urgency and secondary market value") is removed and replaced with the v12.11 canonical framing: drops are **earned through play during their acquisition window**, not purchased with real money; they are marketplace-tradeable after the window with only the standard 5% §39 listing fee; scarcity is enforced by the developer's commitment to never mint additional copies, and that commitment is part of the same trust model the rest of the game runs on. The v12.11 rationale for removing royalties — "operationally complex and player-perceived as extractive in a way the rest of the model is structured to avoid" — is preserved in the §6.4 prose. The prior framing is noted explicitly as "removed in v12.11" so future readers can see both what the framing was and why it changed, per the preserve-historical-record principle.
- **§6.1 Philosophy framing changes.** The four-bullet pre-v12.11 philosophy (`No pay-to-win` / `$2 or less per item` / `$10 ARPU target` / `Stablecoin transactions`) is replaced with a cost-recovery framing that carries the v12.11 "small machinery" language from GDD §32.0. The $10 ARPU target is removed — ARPU is no longer a design target under v12.11 (cost recovery is). The $2-or-less-per-item framing is replaced with the $1-flat-Pass-gated pricing from GDD §32.3. The "impulse-buy territory" framing is removed — the v12.11 model is explicitly not optimized for impulse purchases (the monthly spending warning at $30, the $10 per-purchase cart cap, the no-auto-renewal rule, the absence of FOMO timers are all anti-impulse design choices). The stablecoin-transactions point is preserved but recontextualized: under v12.11, stablecoins are used because they allow the published cost math to be verified by anyone (GDD §32.8), not because "crypto-native but accessible" is a marketing angle.
- **§6.6 Revenue Feel framing changes.** The prior "Secondary market royalties are the long-term play — A $2 cape that trades at $20 generates $1.50 per resale. 10 resales over its lifetime = $15 from one $2 mint. This compounds with player growth." framing is removed. Under v12.11 there is no royalty layer, no compounding from secondary sales, no "long-term play" around resale extraction. The revenue model is: Supporter Pass at $2/month plus optional $1 cosmetics gated behind the Pass, break-even at 1-2% conversion, modest margin that scales roughly linearly with the player population (GDD §32.10). The §6.6 rewrite states this openly and frames the revenue posture as "small sustainable indie operation" rather than "optimize for maximum ARPU" — which is the creative direction for every monetization surface the player interacts with.
- **What does NOT change.** No other creative direction is edited by this ship. Zone mood guides (§1.2a), visual reference sheets (§1.1.1a), reveal animation direction (§1.7a), audio direction (§2), zone personality (§3), social design (§4), economy philosophy (§5), design-for-broad-appeal (§7), narrative direction (§8.1–§8.31) are all byte-identical to v5.7. The monetization rewrite touches only §6 and the §6 row in the NAVIGATION map.
- **NAVIGATION map row updated.** The §6 row in the Social & monetization cluster reads *"Monetization — cost-recovery philosophy, Supporter Pass feel, cosmetics store, prestige drops, revenue feel"* instead of *"Monetization — vanity only, philosophy, revenue model"*. The "vanity only" framing was accurate under the prior model (where the entire store was cosmetic fluff sold directly) but misleading under v12.11 (where cosmetics are Pass-gated, earned-prestige items do real identity work, and the store is explicitly a side product rather than the main monetization path). The new routing language matches the new section structure.
- **Companion reference unchanged at Master GDD v12.24.** The v5.8 ship is CR-internal — it corrects a drift that existed from v5.6.2 forward and brings §6 into alignment with a GDD change that shipped in v12.11. No new GDD changes are triggered by this ship. The companion version line remains Master GDD v12.24.
- **Authority discipline note.** The §6.0 Authority Note added at the top of the rewritten section makes explicit the split of concerns with GDD §32: GDD §32 owns the model specifications (prices, time caps, payment architecture, the no-pay-to-win list, the cost FAQ, the Paying-Player-Never-Worse principle); this section owns how the model *feels* (tone of the purchase flow, visual hierarchy, social texture in the community). This split parallels the authority notes already present in §1.7a (weapon quality/hardness visuals vs GDD §4.6b/§4.6c mechanics) and §8.19 (resonance feel vs GDD §5.7 mechanics). Making the split explicit at the top of §6 reduces the chance of future drift in either direction — a future CR edit that tries to redefine pricing, a future GDD edit that tries to specify the emotional register of the purchase flow.
- **The v5.8 lesson about "no creative implications" assessments.** The v5.6.2 CR changelog entry that noted v12.11 "explicitly stated no creative implications" is the proximate cause of the drift this ship corrects. The assessment was made in good faith — v12.11's GDD changelog did include the phrase "no creative implications" for the CR-side. But "no creative implications" is a subtle judgment that requires looking at the actual CR content being assessed, not at the abstract category of the GDD change. The GDD §32 v12.11 rewrite had no implications for the zone mood guides or the collision creative direction, which is what the "no creative implications" assessment meant in context. It did have implications for the CR's own §6 Monetization section, which existed as a parallel creative statement of the monetization philosophy — and that section needed updating. **Going forward, "no creative implications" assessments for cross-doc coordination should be scoped to the specific CR sections affected, not to the CR as a whole.** If the GDD is rewriting its §X.Y mechanics section, the question to ask is "does the CR have a §X.Y-equivalent creative-direction section, and if so, does the GDD rewrite change what that section should say?" — not "does the GDD rewrite change the overall creative tone of the game?"



- **§8.13 Sovereign Convergence experience rewrite.** Under GDD v12.24, the Dark allies' friendly-fire mechanic (15% chance per cycle to damage the Sovereign) is removed and replaced with targeting chaos (25% chance per cycle to target a non-focus Light enemy). The §8.13 Sovereign experience paragraph is updated to match: the chaos framing shifts from "occasionally they hurt you" to "they pursue enemies erratically — charging off to kill a weak Light being when you needed them focused on a priority target, overkilling corpses, missing obvious threats." The "powerful but untrustworthy" framing is preserved but narrowed to *untrustworthy-as-coordinators* rather than *untrustworthy-as-allies*. Dark allies won't hurt you; they just won't always kill the thing you wanted killed. This preserves the volatile-and-chaotic tonal identity the Sovereign path needs while removing the mechanical asymmetry (documented in GDD v12.24's §56 entry).
- **§8.13 Sound direction update.** The prior "Dark ally friendly fire" sound spec (same as Dark ally attack, pitched down, dissonant overtone, "the player should wince") is replaced with a "Dark ally off-focus attack" sound spec. The same base acoustic identity is preserved — the dissonant overtone still indicates Dark ally inefficiency — but the trigger changes from self-damage events to overkill/wrong-target kills. Players should still register the dissonance and wince at it, but what they're wincing at is wasted ally damage rather than personal injury.
- **§8.9 Sovereign ending line revised from "You could have been different." to "Everything is yours now."** The prior line gestured at an alternative path the Sovereign didn't take, which was a form of judgment that conflicted with the explicit commitment in §8.9 itself (the ending "is written with the same care, the same production quality, the same emotional weight as the Hero ending" and "the difference is in what the path reveals about the player, not in how the game judges them"). The new line is purely observational. Four words instead of five. The ending passage is rewritten to describe the new line's register: it matches the Hero's silent bow and the Arbiter's single word "Again" in refusing to moralize. The word "empty" is implicit in "everything is yours, and nothing else is here." The prior line is preserved in an inline v12.24 revision note below the main passage, per the preserve-historical-record principle — future readers can see both what the line was and why it changed.
- **§8.16 Final Echo Stone unchanged.** The Sovereign's Final Echo Stone (*"He stared into the light until it blinked."*) already meets the observational-register standard the Zone Keeper line now aspires to. The Zone Keeper line and the Final Echo Stone serve different moments in the ending sequence (the Zone Keeper speaks during the 60-90 second ending scene; the Final Echo Stone is discovered later, potentially in post-ending Convergence exploration) and both endings' equivalents remain distinct.

**Companion reference** updated from Master GDD v12.23 to Master GDD v12.24. No changes to visual direction for the rest of the game — zone mood guides, character progression arc, Primordial creative direction, Echo Stones, alignment-specific combat-feel sections, gem and composite visuals, named-craftsman mythology framing — all unchanged from v5.6.7.

**Named-craftsman mythology note.** GDD v12.24 moves the named-craftsman gate from life skill Level 100 to the §45 Transcendent rank system. This is a GDD-side mechanical change; the creative direction for named craftsmen in §8.12a does not need revision because the mythology framing ("Bro Tessa is the only player on the server who can cut Level 9 Storm gems") is still accurate — it just applies to Transcendent-rank cutters now rather than Level 100 cutters. The §8.12a passage remains the canonical creative direction for the phenomenon; if Phase 3 implementation surfaces creative-direction implications (for example, if the Transcendent rank gets its own visual treatment, NPC interaction, or sound identity), those would ship in a future CR version.

### CR v5.6.7

Navigation parity with the GDD. Adds a **NAVIGATION** block at the top of the file (immediately below the header metadata, before §1) containing a **§-Reference Map** and an **Authority note**.

- **What changes:** the §-Reference Map is grouped into five topic clusters (Visual direction, Audio direction, Zone personality, Social & monetization, Narrative direction, Combat feel) with a topic-per-row table under each cluster. The Combat feel table includes a third column linking each creative-direction section to its companion GDD section, making the split of concerns (feel here, mechanics there) visible at the routing layer. The Authority note is a one-paragraph statement that this doc owns *experience* and the GDD owns *mechanics*, with explicit guidance to flag rather than silently reconcile if the two appear to conflict.
- **Why:** CLAUDE.md routes AI assistants to this doc for "how does it feel / look / sound" questions. Prior to v5.6.7, an AI arriving here had no navigation aid equivalent to the GDD's §-Reference Map — section headers were the only index and scrolling through 1,500 lines was the default retrieval path. The cost fell on every lookup. A 60-line index at the top of the file is the single highest-leverage structural edit available for this doc.
- **Why grouped:** the GDD's Reference Map is flat and ~80 rows long, which works because the GDD is largely about one thing (game systems) with subsystems. The creative reference spans distinct concerns (visual, audio, narrative, social, economy, combat feel) and groupings make the first-pass scan faster. Groups are deliberately coarse — no sub-grouping beyond one level — to keep the index itself readable.
- **Why the GDD companion column for combat feel:** the combat-feel sections (§8.19–§8.31) are where cross-doc coordination is densest. An AI editing Resonance feel in §8.19 almost always needs to know what GDD §5.7 says; making the companion visible at the routing layer prevents the common failure mode where the AI treats the creative-reference description as authoritative on timing or stats.
- **Zero creative direction changes** — no visual, audio, narrative, alignment, monetization philosophy, or collision creative direction is edited by this ship. The state of §§1–8.31 is byte-identical to v5.6.6. Companion GDD reference unchanged at v12.22.

### CR v5.6.6

Changelog split — design ships inline, process/sync ships to `CHANGELOG.md`.

- **What changes:** the v5.6.5 compact summary table is replaced with full verbatim entries for every design ship below. Process entries (v5.6.5, v5.6.4, v5.6.3, v5.6.2, v4.8) moved to `CHANGELOG.md` in structured form. Top-of-doc `Version` line and bottom metadata updated.
- **Why:** same rationale as GDD v12.22 — design-ship rationale belongs adjacent to the creative direction it shaped, where an AI editing (for example) the §1.7a reveal animation direction can find the v5.5 rationale directly. Metadata-sync prose does not benefit from this adjacency and stays in the audit-trail file.
- **Zero creative direction changes** — no visual direction, audio, narrative, alignment, monetization philosophy, or collision creative direction is edited by this ship. The state at v5.6.6 is identical to v5.6.5.

### CR v5.6

Godly tradability, lineage, and mystery item trading — visual direction for v12.7. **§8.5a Ferryman line clarification added.** The line itself stays exactly as written (*"I take everything. Almost everything. The gods have left a mark on this. I do not touch what they have touched"*), but the cosmological framing is updated to make explicit that the line is about the *object*, not the *holder*. Under v12.5 the Godly weapon was bound to its original recipient and the line was unambiguously addressed to that recipient. Under v12.7 the weapon is tradeable, and the player carrying it to the Ferryman may be the divine recipient or may have acquired it through marketplace, trade, or gift. The line plays in either case — the Ferryman defers to the gods' touch on the weapon, regardless of whose hand currently carries it. A player who has *bought* a Godly is no less a witness to the line than the original recipient was. **§8.12a Player-Emergent Mythology updated** for the v12.7 reframing. The "bound to receiver" language in the Godly grace passage is replaced with the v12.7 framing: Godly weapons are tradeable, the protections travel with the object, but the original divine encounter is permanent and recorded forever in the lineage. The "named-recipient phenomenon" passage is rewritten — Godly weapons no longer create a "quieter" version of the named-weapon mythology because the binding is gone; instead, they create a *richer* version with the lineage system. Every Godly weapon now carries its full ownership history publicly, and the marketplace can display lineage as part of the listing. A 5-year-old server will have Godly weapons whose lineages stretch back to their original recipients on day 7 of the server, with multiple owners over the years, each transfer recorded forever. The story is in the lineage, and the lineage is permanent. The grace/grit design intent passage is updated to capture the v12.7 split: the *encounter* is sacred (and remains permanent in the lineage), and the *object* is a divine artifact that mortals can subsequently trade. Both categories of meaning survive at different layers of the system. **Marketplace economic implications.** Godly weapons become the marketplace's apex tier under v12.7, with prices likely in the millions of gold. Mystery items become a new gambling tier — players can sell unrevealed question-mark drops to buyers who reveal them themselves. The seller never learns what they sold (the post-sale reveal is private to the buyer), which prevents the cruel twist of selling a Godly for a pittance. These are creative direction implications, not new creative content — the Creative Reference does not add new sections for marketplace UI but does note that the §1.7a reveal animation now needs a "purchased mystery item reveal" UX path that is functionally identical to a personal pickup reveal but triggered by buyer interaction with the inventory item. **Cosmological reframing.** Under v12.5, the binding rule supported the "the gods chose you" framing — if you had a Godly, you were one of the few players the gods had visited. Under v12.7, the framing splits cleanly: the *original recipient* is still the player the gods visited (immutable, recorded forever), but the *current holder* may be a different player who acquired the weapon through wealth or trade. The cosmology adapts: the gods visit specific players occasionally, the gifts they leave subsequently circulate among mortals through normal economies, and the divine encounter is permanent and personal even after the resulting object has changed hands many times. **Companion reference** updated from Master GDD v12.6 to Master GDD v12.7. **No changes** to the visual direction for the rest of the game (zone mood guides, audio direction, character visual progression arc, Convergence and Primordial creative direction, alignment feel, NPC tone progression, Echo Stones, Hardness 5 reveal, the v12.6 gem and composite visuals, the named gem-cutter mythology — all unchanged from v5.5). **§39.7 Buy Intent System creative direction note (added in v5.6 amendment).** The marketplace's secondary mechanism for non-auto-matchable demand introduces a new social-information surface that has its own UX feel. When a player opens their inventory and an item they hold matches active buy intents, the inventory icon gains a small "Matching Buy Intents" badge — a subtle indicator, not a flashing call to action. The first time a player sees a buy intent badge on something they own is a quiet revelation moment: *"someone, somewhere on this server, wants this thing I have."* The marketplace is no longer just a place to dump loot — it is a *room of demand* where other players are visibly looking for the things you've accumulated. The intent badge feel should be **weight, not urgency** — it suggests value and possibility, not pressure to act. When the player taps the badge and sees the list of intents, the panel feels more like a *bulletin board* than a slot machine: each intent is a small piece of social information ("Bro Mason, intent reputation 94%, will pay 80,000g for any Hardness 4 Greatsword, notes: 'long-time hardener, please contact for Tier 18+ specifically'"), and the player makes a deliberate choice about whether to respond. The escrow vs negotiated distinction has its own visual treatment — escrow intents show a small lock icon (the gold is committed, the trade is guaranteed if the seller fills) and negotiated intents show a small handshake icon (the gold is not committed, the trade requires conversation). Sellers learn the difference visually within their first few interactions. The Buy Intent system is a *bidirectional* marketplace mechanism, and the UI should make that bidirectionality felt — buyers signaling, sellers responding, both parties visible to each other in a way the order book's anonymous matching doesn't permit. The marketplace becomes a *social space* through this feature, not just a transaction venue. v5.6 is a focused tradability + lineage update that touches only the Godly framing, plus the universal Buy Intent system as a marketplace UX addition.

### CR v5.5

Gem refactor and tiered RNG reveal — visual direction for v12.6. **§1.7a fully rewritten** for the v12.6 elemental system: weapons no longer have designer-authored creative names, identity is composed of material+type+quality+hardness+enchanted-state+applied-composite. The reveal animation is rewritten as a tiered RNG visualization that honestly shows the underlying probability distribution playing out: Stage 1 (~9s) cycles silver/gold at the actual ~9% conditional escalation rate, Stage 2 (~9s) cycles gold/prismatic at the actual ~1 in 3,600 conditional Godly rate, Stage 3 (~5s) is the screen-hijacking Godly finale. Each visible color tick is a real server roll, and prismatic flashes appear in the animation only at the rate the underlying RNG produces them — most Stage 2 reveals show 96 gold ticks in a row with no prismatic at all, and the rare prismatic flashes are the moments where the player notices and pays attention. The hybrid input model is canonical: Rare reveals happen in a non-blocking corner, Elite escalations move to a more prominent screen position, only Godly escalations fully hijack the screen with input lock. Visual prominence escalates with rarity. **New gem and composite visuals**: gems are first-class inventory items with element shapes from §10.1, level-indicating numerals, and intensity-scaling inner glow (Level 1 matte → Level 10 noticeable shimmer). Composite icons show both gems side by side within a unifying frame, with a faint crackle effect at the boundary for volatile composites. Shift gems are visually unique with prismatic cycling and small inset element icons showing the three rolled elements. **New Enchanter UI direction**: workbench-style interface for all gem operations with brief functional NPC narration; the first enchanting triggers the only thematic line the Enchanter ever delivers — *"A weapon holds. A gem speaks. The weapon doesn't speak until you give it something to say."* **§8.12a updated** with the named-gem-cutter phenomenon as the fourth pillar of player-emergent mythology, parallel to named blacksmiths but gating elemental endgame instead of hardness endgame. The full mythology framework now has four layers: Godly recipients (mythology around blessing), Hardness 5 owners (mythology around commitment), named blacksmiths (mythology around forging), and named gem-cutters (mythology around gem mastery). *(Historical note: v12.28 subsequently added a fifth layer — Tempered-weapon crafters — and v12.30 added a sixth — high-History weapons as identity-carrying objects. The current count is six; this v5.6 entry captures the framework as it stood at v12.6. See CR v5.12 for the fifth-layer extension and CR v5.14 for the sixth.)* All four scale with the player base for free, and all four create permanent fixtures in their server's social fabric. **§1.7a no longer references "Shift element tier"** — Shift is now a gem type, not a weapon tier, and the visual treatment is on the gem itself rather than as a weapon body effect. **Companion reference** updated from Master GDD v12.5 to Master GDD v12.6. No changes to the visual direction for the rest of the game (zone mood guides, audio direction, character visual progression arc, Convergence and Primordial creative direction, alignment feel, NPC tone progression, Echo Stones — all unchanged from v5.4).

### CR v5.4

Godly cosmology and lottery refinement to match GDD v12.5. **§1.7a Drop Moment** rewritten to reflect the new mystery reveal model: drops above Normal appear as question marks rather than bordered icons, and the reveal is the dramatic moment, not the appearance. Reveal animations escalate dramatically — Rare gets a quick silver shimmer, Elite gets a gold ring expansion, Godly hijacks the screen for 5 seconds with input lock and the dedicated motif. The mystery moment is private (only the recipient sees the reveal); the social broadcast follows. **§1.7a Hardness 5 reveal** updated with the v12.5 broadcast safety rules (zone-vague, 30s post-success PvP invulnerability) and the new shared-motif-with-differentiating-notes specification: Godly gets a high resolving note (chime from above, grace), Hardness 5 gets a deep resolving note (strike from below, grit), shared base motif lets players identify the lottery hit and the differentiating note tells them which kind. **New §8.5a The Ferryman and the Gods** added: the Ferryman has a single unique line triggered by carrying a Godly weapon into his presence — *"I take everything. Almost everything. The gods have left a mark on this. I do not touch what they have touched."* The line is the entire scripture of the game's only theistic element. The Ferryman is the only NPC who acknowledges the gods, and he does so once and only when forced to. **§8.12a Player-Emergent Mythology** rewritten to incorporate the grace-vs-grit cosmological asymmetry. Godly weapons are now framed explicitly as *grace* — sacred objects governed by special rules (bound, indestructible, untradeable, protected from the Ferryman) — while Hardness 5 weapons remain *grit*, governed by mortal rules. The two cosmologies are different and the game treats them differently, and the asymmetry is what makes the system feel honest. The named-weapon, named-blacksmith, and "the player who has a Godly" phenomena are reframed as three layers of player-emergent content. **New §8.12b The Gods — Cosmological Direction** added as the canonical worldbuilding statement for the gods. The gods exist *exclusively* through Godly drops — they never appear, never speak, never have temples, never have priests, never demand worship, never respond to prayer. They are present in the world through one mechanism only: occasional gifts placed into players' hands. The Ferryman is the only NPC who acknowledges them, and he confirms their existence only when standing in front of one of their gifts. The plural ("the gods") is intentional — it keeps the cosmology vague enough that the player projects their own theology onto it. The section explains why this works as worldbuilding: the gods are *more real* because they are *less visible*, and a player who has received a Godly weapon has a private theology built from a single 5-second moment of revelation. The cosmology is unfalsifiable, intimate, and unrepeatable. **Companion reference** updated from Master GDD v12.4 to Master GDD v12.5. No other content changes — the v5.3 player-emergent mythology framing is preserved and extended, the visual direction is unchanged, the zone mood guides are unchanged, the Convergence and Primordial creative direction are unchanged.

### CR v5.3

Quality and Hardness creative direction. Adds **§1.7a Weapon Quality and Hardness Visuals** covering quality borders (none/silver/gold/prismatic), hardness badges (1-5 with prismatic shimmer at 5), the drop moment escalation from Normal (silence) to Godly (screen flash + private chat + persistent shimmer), the hardening attempt as a deliberate ritual at the Blacksmith with success/failure animations and held suspense, and the Hardness 5 reveal as a server-room broadcast event sharing its musical motif with Godly drops. Adds **§8.12a Player-Emergent Mythology** as the creative anchor for the lottery layer — distinguishes Godly drops as "luck given form" (grace) from Hardness 5 weapons as "commitment given form" (grit), establishes the named-weapon and named-blacksmith phenomena as the game's most important social system (content the game does not author but makes visible through nameplates, animations, server broadcasts, and the shared musical motif), and explicitly connects this layer to the §8.1 unlikely-hero arc as the literal mechanism by which Bros earn stories other players can see. Companion reference updated from Master GDD v12.3 to Master GDD v12.4. No other content changes — the use-trained stat system framing from v5.2 carries forward unchanged, and the lottery layer is additive rather than displacing.

### CR v5.2

Stat system framing alignment with GDD v12.3 (use-trained stats replace allocation). **§1.7 Stat-Based Visuals** reframed: characters visually evolve based on the player's *trained* stat distribution, not their allocation. The archetype emerges as a visual readout of how the player has actually played — Berserker visuals come from hundreds of hours of aggressive melee play, not from a menu choice. **§8.1 identity-through-systems passage** strengthened: the trained stat distribution is now framed as "literally a record of how they've played — not a build they declared but a shape their hours produced." Added explicit philosophy line: "a character's identity cannot be optimized in a menu before play begins, because identity is what emerges from play. The Bros who become Heroes were not assigned that path. They walked it." This is the Creative Reference's most load-bearing identity statement and the use-trained system is the mechanic that makes it true. **§8.9c Channel 2** unchanged — its existing language ("the player notices the changes happening to their own character over time — 'wait, when did I get that glow?'") was already aligned with use-trained behavior and reads more naturally now than it did under allocation. **Companion reference** updated from Master GDD v12.0 to Master GDD v12.3. No other content changes — the visual direction, audio direction, zone mood guides, narrative direction, alignment feel, Convergence/Primordial creative direction, and collision creative direction are all unchanged. The use-trained stat system did not require any creative-side rework beyond reframing identity language; the existing creative direction was already aligned with "identity emerges from behavior."

### CR v5.1

Reference image embedding pass. All six visual reference sheets in §1.1.1a now embed inline via markdown image syntax pointing to `./references/`. Reference files copied into `references/` folder alongside this document. Added §2.1 Zone Soundscape Reference Table — quick-lookup table cross-referencing all 13 zone soundscapes to their full mood guides in §1.2a, addressing the gap where audio engineers had to read prose to find per-zone sound direction.

### CR v5.0

Major creative direction expansion grounded in reference art. **Visual reference sheets** (§1.1.1a): six annotated reference images establishing the canonical quality bar for monsters (Lava Toad, Venom Serpent, Venom Skeleton), inventory items (shields, food), and environments (town). DO/DON'T quick reference table (§1.1.1b). **Zone mood guides** (§1.2a): first-impression feel, ambient soundscape direction, and signature environmental detail for all 13 zone types including Convergence and Lawless Land. **UI/UX feel direction** (§1.9): panel motion language, Codex browsing feel, inspect card information hierarchy, loadout menu interaction. **Convergence visual direction** (§8.9a): boundary shimmer, alignment-based visual framing, gem glow. **Primordial visual direction** (§8.9b): Darkness as shifting void with migrating eyes, Light as blinding geometric form, Arbiter merge animation, Final Echo Stone fade. **Character visual progression arc** (§8.9c): four-channel visual identity accumulation from Level 1 to Level 100.

### CR v4.7

Full collision expansion creative direction. Added §8.27 (Reverse Pairs — two stories per element pair, directional volatile creative direction with crackle asymmetry and Dark↔Light exception, Wind as orchestrator, visual/sound direction for reverse collisions). Added §8.28 (Dark Corruption & Light Redemption — emotional gradient, Judgment and Mend exceptions, weapon identity through collision direction). Redesigned Mend creative direction: now self-buff (attacker resistance) instead of enemy heal, updated audio cue. Added §8.29 (Umbra creative direction — Eclipse's shadow, Sovereign irony, visual/sound). Added §8.30 (37 new Codex hints). Added §8.31 (Collision Preview creative direction — at-a-glance loadout tool, discovery moment, preview→Codex→combat learning loop). Prior: v4.6 added §8.26 Flora/Thorn creative direction.
